# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q.** List the components of the expanded key of Blowfish and state their sizes.

**Model Answer:**

The expanded key of Blowfish consists of:

1. **P-array**: 18 subkeys, denoted $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_{18}$, each of **32 bits** (total $18 \times 32 = 576$ bits).
2. **Four S-boxes**: $S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4$, each containing **256 entries of 32 bits** (total $4 \times 256 \times 32 = 32768$ bits).

**Total expanded key size = 576 + 32768 = 33344 bits = 4168 bytes.** *(3 marks: 1 for P-array, 1 for S-boxes, 1 for total size.)*

### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q.** Why does the Blowfish key expansion algorithm encrypt the all-zero block 521 times?

**Model Answer:**

The Blowfish key schedule needs to produce **18 subkeys** (P1..P18) and **4 S-boxes** (S1..S4, 256 entries each). Each encryption of a 64-bit block yields **64 bits = two 32-bit words**, which is sufficient to fill two subkeys or two S-box entries at a time.

- P-array: 18 subkeys / 2 = **9 encryptions**.
- S-boxes: $4 \times 256$ entries / 2 = **512 encryptions**.
- Total: $9 + 512 = \mathbf{521}$ encryptions.

This self-encryption ensures the expanded key depends nonlinearly on the user key via the **avalanche effect** of the Feistel network. *(3 marks: 1 for 9, 1 for 512, 1 for the avalanche justification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** In a 14-mark question, you must answer **both sub-parts** for full credit. Sub-parts typically carry **7 marks each**.

### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Blowfish algorithm** in detail with a neat block diagram. Show the F-function and the Feistel structure.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **key expansion algorithm** of Blowfish and explain why the hexadecimal digits of π are used for initialisation.

#### Model Solution to (a):

1. **Inputs**: 64-bit plaintext $x$, expanded key state $\Sigma = (P_1, \ldots, P_{18}, S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4)$.
2. **Initial split**: $L_0 = $ high 32 bits of $x$, $R_0 = $ low 32 bits of $x$.
3. **For $i = 1$ to $16$ (Feistel rounds):**

   $$
   L_i = R_{i-1} \oplus P_i
   $$

   $$
   R_i = L_{i-1} \oplus F(R_{i-1})
   $$

4. **Output whitening (no F, no swap):**

   $$
   R_{17} = R_{16} \oplus P_{17}, \quad L_{17} = L_{16} \oplus P_{18}
   $$

5. **Ciphertext**: $C = L_{17} \,\|\, R_{17}$.

6. **F-function**:

   $$
   F(x_L) = ((S_1[a] + S_2[b] \bmod 2^{32}) \oplus S_3[c]) + S_4[d] \bmod 2^{32}
   $$

   where $x_L$ is split into bytes $a, b, c, d$.

7. **Block diagram** (refer to Section 4.1 and 4.2 for the architecture).

*(7 marks: 1 each for split, Feistel equations, whitening, ciphertext, F-function, diagram justification, summary.)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

The key expansion algorithm of Blowfish is a **3-stage process**:

**Stage 1 — Initialisation from π.** The P-array $P_1, \ldots, P_{18}$ and S-boxes $S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4$ are initialised with the **hexadecimal digits of the fractional part of π**, most-significant digit first. The first 32 hex digits (i.e., 16 bytes / 128 bits) of $\pi_{\text{frac}}$ are:

$$
0x243F6A88\,85A308D3\,13198A2E\,03707344
$$

These give $P_1 = 0x243F6A88$, $P_2 = 0x85A308D3$, etc. The remaining digits fill the S-boxes.

**Why π?** Schneier chose the digits of π because they are:
- **Publicly verifiable** (anyone can check the initial values).
- **Aperiodic and statistically random-looking** in any base.
- **Free of any embedded backdoor** (unlike secret constants, which raise trust concerns).

**Stage 2 — XOR with the user key.** The variable-length key $K$ (32 to 448 bits, i.e., 4 to 56 bytes) is treated as a circular buffer and XORed cyclically into the P-array byte-by-byte:

$$
P_i = \pi\text{-init}_i \oplus K_{(4i-4) \bmod k} \,\|\, K_{(4i-3) \bmod k} \,\|\, K_{(4i-2) \bmod k} \,\|\, K_{(4i-1) \bmod k}
$$

**Stage 3 — Self-encryption of the all-zero block.** Starting with $B_0 = 0^{64}$, we iteratively encrypt:

$$
B_1 = E_{P,S}(B_0), \quad P_1, P_2 \leftarrow \text{split}(B_1)
$$

$$
B_2 = E_{P,S}(B_1), \quad P_3, P_4 \leftarrow \text{split}(B_2)
$$

…and continue for a total of **521 encryptions** (9 for P-array, 512 for S-boxes). The self-encryption step **nonlinearly mixes** the user key into the entire state.

*(7 marks: 2 for Stage 1, 1 for π justification, 1 for Stage 2, 2 for Stage 3, 1 for the 521 figure.)*

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **F-function** of Blowfish. Why is it designed with mixed operations (addition mod $2^{32}$ and XOR)?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain how **decryption** is performed in Blowfish. Why is the P-array order reversed but the S-boxes are not?

#### Model Solution to (a):

The **F-function** of Blowfish is the heart of its round transformation. It takes a 32-bit input $x_L$ and produces a 32-bit output.

**Step 1 — Byte decomposition:**

$$
x_L = a \,\|\, b \,\|\, c \,\|\, d, \quad a, b, c, d \in [0, 255]
$$

**Step 2 — S-box lookups:**

$$
S_1[a], \quad S_2[b], \quad S_3[c], \quad S_4[d] \;\; \text{(each 32 bits)}
$$

**Step 3 — Combined operations:**

$$
F(x_L) = ((S_1[a] + S_2[b] \bmod 2^{32}) \oplus S_3[c]) + S_4[d] \bmod 2^{32}
$$

The combined operations are:
- **Addition mod $2^{32}$** between $S_1[a]$ and $S_2[b]$.
- **Bitwise XOR** with $S_3[c]$.
- **Addition mod $2^{32}$** with $S_4[d]$.

(Refer to Section 4.2 for the block diagram of the F-function.)

**Why mixed operations?** Schneier deliberately used a combination of **nonlinear additions (mod $2^{32}$)** and **linear XORs** to defeat both:
- **Linear cryptanalysis** (which assumes purely linear operations).
- **Differential cryptanalysis** (which exploits predictable differences in purely additive or purely XOR-based structures).

The mixing ensures that the cipher has **no simple algebraic closure** under either operation alone, dramatically increasing the work factor for an attacker.

*(7 marks: 1 each for byte split, 4 S-box lookups, 3 operations, diagram, and 3 marks for the cryptanalysis justification.)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

Blowfish **decryption** uses the **same F-function, the same S-boxes, and the same round structure** as encryption, with **one critical difference**: the **P-array is used in reverse order**.

**Encryption P-order in rounds:** $P_1, P_2, P_3, \ldots, P_{16}, P_{17}, P_{18}$ (the last two used in output whitening).

**Decryption P-order in rounds:** $P_{18}, P_{17}, P_{16}, \ldots, P_3, P_2, P_1$ (the last two used in reverse whitening).

**Symbolic decryption round $i$ (counting backwards):**

$$
R_{i-1} = L_i \oplus P_{i+1}, \quad L_{i-1} = R_i \oplus F(R_{i-1})
$$

The F-function inputs are the same 32-bit values; only the XOR-with-$P$ step is reversed.

**Why is the P-array order reversed?**
The Feistel structure is **invertible** if and only if the round subkeys are applied in the exact reverse order. The P-array is XORed at the boundary of each round, so reversing its order unwinds the rounds.

**Why are the S-boxes NOT reversed?**
The S-boxes are **bijective 32-bit mapping tables** (each input byte maps to a fixed 32-bit value, and the mapping is one-to-one). They are **not** XORed with the round data — they are merely **looked up**. Therefore, applying the same S-box in the reverse direction is mathematically identical to applying it forward. Reversing them would change the lookup values and corrupt the decryption.

*(7 marks: 1 for stating reverse P-order, 2 for symbolic equations, 1 for whitening inversion, 1 for the F-function/S-box reasoning, 2 for the bijection argument.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the output whitening** ($P_{17}, P_{18}$ XOR) — it carries **1 to 2 marks** and is omitted in over 60% of student scripts.
> 2. **Confusing addition mod $2^{32}$ with XOR** in the F-function — these are **not interchangeable**, and a wrong operation invalidates the entire derivation.
> 3. **Stating "the key is XORed with the S-boxes"** in Stage 2 — this is wrong; the cyclic XOR is applied **only to the P-array**, not the S-boxes.
> 4. **Forgetting that the S-boxes are NOT reversed** in decryption — the examiner deducts 1 mark for this error.
> 5. **Citing 521 as "the number of rounds"** — it is the number of **key-schedule encryptions**, not rounds. The cipher itself has **16 rounds**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **Blowfish = 64-bit block, 32- to 448-bit key, 16 Feistel rounds, 18 P-subkeys, 4 S-boxes of 256 × 32 bits.**
- **F-function**: $F(x_L) = ((S_1[a] + S_2[b] \bmod 2^{32}) \oplus S_3[c]) + S_4[d] \bmod 2^{32}$ — **addition, XOR, addition**.
- **Feistel round $i$**: $L_i = R_{i-1} \oplus P_i$, $R_i = L_{i-1} \oplus F(R_{i-1})$ — for $i = 1, \ldots, 16$.
- **Output whitening** (no swap, no F): $R_{17} = R_{16} \oplus P_{17}$, $L_{17} = L_{16} \oplus P_{18}$.
- **Key Expansion Stage 1**: Initialise P1..P18 and S1..S4 with **hex digits of π**.
- **Key Expansion Stage 2**: Cyclic XOR of P-array with user key bytes (4 to 56 bytes).
- **Key Expansion Stage 3**: Self-encrypt the all-zero block **521 times** (9 for P-array + 512 for S-boxes) to fill the state.
- **Decryption**: Same F-function and S-boxes, but **P-array used in reverse order** ($P_{18}$ down to $P_1$).
- **Why π?** Publicly verifiable, no backdoor, statistically random-looking.
- **Total expanded state = 33,344 bits = 4,168 bytes.**
- **Designer: Bruce Schneier, 1993.**
- **Why expensive key schedule?** Defeats brute force on keys used in `bcrypt` password hashing.
- **Block diagram memorisation**: split → 16 rounds (each with F) → whitening (no F) → ciphertext.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Cipher Modes of Operation - Electronic Codebook Mode (ECB)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT501 - FOUNDATIONS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Block Cipher Modes of Operation - Electronic Codebook Mode (ECB)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electronic Codebook (ECB) Mode** is the most straightforward and intuitive mode of operation for a block cipher. In ECB, the plaintext message is divided into a sequence of independent blocks, and **each block is encrypted separately using the same secret key**. There is no chaining, no feedback, and no dependency between consecutive blocks.

Formally, if the plaintext $\mathbf{P}$ is segmented into $n$ blocks of fixed length $b$ bits (where $b$ is the underlying block cipher's block size, e.g., **64 bits for DES, 128 bits for AES**), then ECB produces ciphertext as:

$$\mathbf{C} = E_K(P_1) \;\Vert\; E_K(P_2) \;\Vert\; \cdots \;\Vert\; E_K(P_n)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** ECB is the *baseline* mode from which all other modes (CBC, CFB, OFB, CTR) are derived as enhancements. Understanding ECB's limitations is essential to grasp *why* the other modes exist.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Codebook" Intuition

Imagine a **diplomatic codebook** used during World War II. Each English word (or phrase) has a unique, pre-assigned code number. To encrypt a message, the sender simply looks up every word in the codebook independently and substitutes the code.

| English Word | Codebook Entry |
|--------------|----------------|
| ATTACK       | 7341           |
| AT           | 22             |
| DAWN         | 9056           |
| ATTACK       | 7341           |

Now consider the message: *"ATTACK AT DAWN ATTACK"*. Notice that the word **"ATTACK"** appears twice and produces the code **7341** *both times*. This is exactly how ECB behaves: **identical plaintext blocks always produce identical ciphertext blocks under the same key**. It is a *one-to-one lookup table* — hence the name "Electronic Codebook."

> [!NOTE]
> The analogy reveals the central weakness: an eavesdropper who notices the pattern "7341" appears twice can deduce the message contains a repeated word, even without breaking the cipher.

### 1.3 Operational Properties at a Glance

- **Block size $b$:** **128 bits (16 bytes) for AES**, 64 bits (8 bytes) for DES/3DES.
- **Key size $k$:** Independent of block size; e.g., 128/192/256 bits for AES.
- **Padding requirement:** If the final plaintext block is shorter than $b$ bits, **padding** (PKCS#7, ANSI X.923, or zero-padding) is mandatory.
- **IV (Initialization Vector):** **Not used** in ECB — this is a defining characteristic.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners frequently test the fact that **ECB is the only major mode that does NOT require an IV**. Confusing this in an exam costs easy marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The encryption engine executes a simple, repetitive, and **parallelizable** workflow:

1. **Padding Phase:** Append padding bits/bytes to the plaintext so its length becomes an exact multiple of $b$ bits.
2. **Segmentation Phase:** Slice the padded plaintext into $n = \lceil \vert P \vert / b \rceil$ blocks: $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$.
3. **Independent Encryption Phase:** For each $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$, compute $C_i = E_K(P_i)$ using the block cipher $E$ with shared secret key $K$.
4. **Concatenation Phase:** Concatenate ciphertext blocks: $C = C_1 \Vert C_2 \Vert \cdots \Vert C_n$.

Decryption is the exact inverse: split $C$ into $n$ blocks and compute $P_i = D_K(C_i)$ for each $i$, where $D_K = E_K^{-1}$ is the inverse block cipher.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "parallelizable"?** Because $C_i$ depends *only* on $P_i$ and $K$ — not on $C_{i-1}$ or any other block. A multi-core processor or hardware pipeline can encrypt all blocks simultaneously, yielding massive throughput advantages.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property | ECB Expression | Engineering Implication |
|----------|----------------|--------------------------|
| **Encryption** | $C_i = E_K(P_i)$ | Independent per-block operation |
| **Decryption** | $P_i = D_K(C_i)$ | Independent per-block operation |
| **Determinism** | $P_i = P_j \implies C_i = C_j$ | Same plaintext $\Rightarrow$ same ciphertext (vulnerability) |
| **Block size (AES)** | $b = 128$ **bits = 16 bytes** | Standard modern block size |
| **Number of blocks** | $n = \lceil \vert P \vert \,/\, b \rceil$ | Padding inflates last block if needed |
| **Error propagation** | 1 bit-flip in $C_i$ corrupts only $P_i$ | Localized damage, no avalanche across blocks |
| **Synchronization** | Block-boundary alignment is critical | Lost/skipped block $\Rightarrow$ all subsequent blocks garbled |
| **IV requirement** | **None** | ECB does **not** use an initialization vector |
| **Parallelism** | **Full** (encrypt + decrypt) | Hardware acceleration friendly |
| **Semantic security** | **Fails** | Vulnerable to pattern analysis & replay |
| **Padding schemes** | PKCS#7, ANSI X.923, Zero-padding | Required to handle non-multiple lengths |
| **Recommended uses** | Single random values (keys, session IDs) | **Not** for messages, images, or files |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Mnemonic — "ECB = Easy but Empty Brain"**: Easy to implement, but provides *no semantic security*, making it unsuitable for almost all real-world message encryption.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Despite its weaknesses, ECB still appears in **specialized engineering contexts**:

- **Key Wrapping (RFC 3394):** AES in ECB mode is used to wrap fixed-length cryptographic keys because the input is a single, high-entropy, random block — exactly the scenario where ECB's determinism is harmless.
- **Pseudo-Random Permutation (PRP) Construction:** ECB is the canonical construction of a PRP from a block cipher primitive.
- **Hash Function Building Block (Matyas-Meyer-Oseas):** Uses ECB-style compression in Davies-Meyer and Matyas-Meyer-Oseas hash constructions.
- **Educational Benchmarks:** ECB is the *first* mode taught in every cryptography course (including KTU PCCGT501) to establish the baseline before introducing chaining.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — ECB Encryption of a 16-Byte Message

Let us work through a complete, numerically explicit ECB encryption so you can reproduce it in an exam.

**Given:**

- Block cipher: **AES** (block size $b = 128$ bits $= 16$ bytes).
- Secret key $K$: (treated abstractly).
- Plaintext message $\mathbf{P}$: the ASCII string `"CRYPTOISFUN0000"` (16 characters, exact block).

> [!NOTE]
> For traceability, each character is one byte in ASCII. `"C" = 0x43`, `"R" = 0x52`, etc. This gives us a clean 16-byte block.

**Step 1 — Padding Verification**

The message length is exactly $16$ bytes, which equals $b$. Therefore:

$$n = \frac{\vert P \vert}{b} = \frac{16}{16} = 1 \text{ block (no padding required)}$$

**Step 2 — Block Segmentation**

$$P_1 = \text{"CRYPTOISFUN0000"} = \texttt{0x43 52 59 50 54 4F 49 53 46 55 4E 30 30 30 30}$$

**Step 3 — ECB Encryption**

$$C_1 = E_K(P_1)$$

We treat $E_K$ as a black-box function for this example. Suppose the AES encryption under key $K$ produces:

$$C_1 = \texttt{0xA7 3F B2 91 4C 08 6E D3 55 21 7A C4 89 0E F6 12}$$

**Step 4 — Ciphertext Output**

$$\mathbf{C} = C_1 = \texttt{0xA7 3F B2 91 4C 08 6E D3 55 21 7A C4 89 0E F6 12}$$

### 3.2 Multi-Block Worked Example with Padding

**Given:**

- Plaintext: `"HELLOWORLD"` (10 ASCII bytes).
- Block cipher: AES ($b = 16$ bytes).
- Encryption: Assume $E_K(\text{"HELLOWORLD\!\!XXXX"})$ is the encryption where `X` are pad bytes.

**Step 1 — Padding**

We need 6 more bytes. Using **PKCS#7 padding**, each pad byte has the value $0x06$ (since 6 bytes are missing):

$$P_{\text{padded}} = \texttt{H E L L O W O R L D 06 06 06 06 06 06}$$

**Step 2 — Segmentation**

$$n = 1 \text{ block (after padding)}$$

$$P_1 = \texttt{0x48 45 4C 4C 4F 57 4F 52 4C 44 06 06 06 06 06 06}$$

**Step 3 — Encryption**

$$C_1 = E_K(P_1)$$

Suppose the result is:

$$C_1 = \texttt{0x9B 22 7E 4A 88 1D 5C F3 07 B6 91 4D 5A E2 0C 73}$$

**Step 4 — Ciphertext**

$$\mathbf{C} = C_1 = \texttt{0x9B 22 7E 4A 88 1D 5C F3 07 B6 91 4D 5A E2 0C 73}$$

### 3.3 A Three-Block Example Demonstrating ECB's Deterministic Weakness

**Given:**

- Plaintext: `"BLOCKCIPHERSECURE"` (17 bytes) → padded to 32 bytes → **2 blocks**.
- Actually, use a longer example: `"ENCRYPTENCRYPTNOW"` (18 bytes) → padded to 32 bytes → **2 blocks**.

**Step 1 — Padding (PKCS#7, need 14 bytes)**

$$P_{\text{padded}} = \text{"ENCRYPTENCRYPTNOW"} + \texttt{0E 0E 0E 0E 0E 0E 0E 0E 0E 0E 0E 0E 0E 0E}$$

**Step 2 — Segmentation**

$$P_1 = \texttt{ENCRYPTENCRYPT} \quad (16 \text{ bytes})$$
$$P_2 = \texttt{NOW\textasciitilde 0E 0E \ldots 0E} \quad (16 \text{ bytes, last 13 are 0x0E})$$

**Step 3 — Encryption (assumed mappings)**

$$C_1 = E_K(\text{"ENCRYPTENCRYPT"}) = \texttt{0xD4 88 1F 7B A2 55 0C 39 6E B4 7D 21 90 4A F8 0C}$$
$$C_2 = E_K(\text{"NOW0E0E...0E"}) = \texttt{0x3A 1B 99 0E 71 5D 82 C6 44 A0 1F 5E B7 8C 02 9D}$$

**Step 4 — Final Ciphertext**

$$\mathbf{C} = C_1 \Vert C_2$$

### 3.4 Demonstration of the Deterministic Vulnerability

Consider a 3-block plaintext where $P_1 = P_3$:

$$P_1 = \text{"YES"}, \quad P_2 = \text{"NO"}, \quad P_3 = \text{"YES"}$$

With $b = 16$ bytes and PKCS#7 padding:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= \texttt{YES\_{}0D 0D 0D 0D 0D 0D 0D 0D 0D 0D 0D 0D 0D} \\
P_2 &= \texttt{NO\_{}0E 0E 0E 0E 0E 0E 0E 0E 0E 0E 0E 0E 0E 0E} \\
P_3 &= \texttt{YES\_{}0D 0D 0D 0D 0D 0D 0D 0D 0D 0D 0D 0D 0D}
\end{aligned}$$

Encryption yields:

$$C_1 = E_K(P_1), \quad C_2 = E_K(P_2), \quad C_3 = E_K(P_3)$$

Since $P_1 = P_3$, we **provably** have:

$$C_1 = C_3$$

The attacker immediately knows that the first and third plaintext blocks are identical — *without ever breaking the block cipher*. This is the **defining weakness** of ECB and the reason CBC, CTR, and other modes were invented.

### 3.5 Algebraic Summary of ECB Properties

| Property | Mathematical Statement | Proof Sketch |
|----------|----------------------|--------------|
| Determinism | $\forall i,j:\; P_i = P_j \implies C_i = C_j$ | Both are $E_K$ applied to equal inputs with equal keys |
| Injectivity | $E_K$ is a permutation $\implies P_i = P_j \iff C_i = C_j$ | Block ciphers are bijective on $b$-bit strings |
| Localized errors | Bit-flip in $C_i$ affects only $P_i$ | $P_i = D_K(C_i)$ — no other block uses $C_i$ |
| Parallelism | $\forall i:\; C_i = E_K(P_i)$ independent | No $i \neq j$ coupling in the formula |
| No IV dependence | $C_i = f(P_i, K)$ only | IV term absent from the equation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ECB Encryption Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the structural data flow of ECB encryption. Each plaintext block $P_i$ is independently fed into the same block cipher $E$ keyed with the shared secret $K$, producing a corresponding ciphertext block $C_i$. The blocks are then concatenated in order to form the final ciphertext $\mathbf{C}$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["PLAINBLOCK P1"] --> B1["BLOCK CIPHER E WITH KEY K"]
    A2["PLAINBLOCK P2"] --> B2["BLOCK CIPHER E WITH KEY K"]
    A3["PLAINBLOCK P3"] --> B3["BLOCK CIPHER E WITH KEY K"]
    A4["PLAINBLOCK P4"] --> B4["BLOCK CIPHER E WITH KEY K"]
    B1 --> C1["CIPHERBLOCK C1"]
    B2 --> C2["CIPHERBLOCK C2"]
    B3 --> C3["CIPHERBLOCK C3"]
    B4 --> C4["CIPHERBLOCK C4"]
    C1 --> D["CONCATENATION OF C1 C2 C3 C4"]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E["FINAL CIPHERTEXT C"]
    K1["SECRET KEY K"] -.-> B1
    K1 -.-> B2
    K1 -.-> B3
    K1 -.-> B4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** The dotted arrows from `SECRET KEY K` to each cipher box indicate that the *same* key is reused for every block. The four `BLOCK CIPHER E` boxes are logically identical instances — they can be run in parallel on separate hardware cores, which is why ECB achieves maximum throughput.

### 4.2 ECB Decryption Block-Level Functional Architecture

The decryption process mirrors encryption: the ciphertext $\mathbf{C}$ is split back into blocks $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n$, and each is fed into the inverse block cipher $D = E^{-1}$ using the same key $K$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["CIPHERTEXT C"] --> B["BLOCK SPLITTER"]
    B --> C1["CIPHERBLOCK C1"]
    B --> C2["CIPHERBLOCK C2"]
    B --> C3["CIPHERBLOCK C3"]
    B --> C4["CIPHERBLOCK C4"]
    C1 --> D1["INVERSE BLOCK CIPHER D WITH KEY K"]
    C2 --> D2["INVERSE BLOCK CIPHER D WITH KEY K"]
    C3 --> D3["INVERSE BLOCK CIPHER D WITH KEY K"]
    C4 --> D4["INVERSE BLOCK CIPHER D WITH KEY K"]
    D1 --> E1["PLAINBLOCK P1"]
    D2 --> E2["PLAINBLOCK P2"]
    D3 --> E3["PLAINBLOCK P3"]
    D4 --> E4["PLAINBLOCK P4"]
    E1 --> F["CONCATENATION OF P1 P2 P3 P4"]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G["RECOVERED PLAINTEXT P"]
    K1["SECRET KEY K"] -.-> D1
    K1 -.-> D2
    K1 -.-> D3
    K1 -.-> D4
```

### 4.3 ECB Determinism — Attack Vector Visualization

This diagram makes the **pattern-preservation flaw** visually explicit. When two plaintext blocks are identical, ECB leaks this identity to the eavesdropper.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M["PLAINTEXT P"] --> S["PADDING AND SEGMENTATION"]
    S --> P1["BLOCK P1 EQUALS YES WITH PADDING"]
    S --> P2["BLOCK P2 EQUALS NO WITH PADDING"]
    S --> P3["BLOCK P3 EQUALS YES WITH PADDING"]
    P1 --> E1["ENCRYPT E_K"]
    P2 --> E2["ENCRYPT E_K"]
    P3 --> E3["ENCRYPT E_K"]
    E1 --> R1["CIPHERBLOCK C1"]
    E2 --> R2["CIPHERBLOCK C2"]
    E3 --> R3["CIPHERBLOCK C3"]
    R1 --> OBS["OBSERVED CIPHERTEXT C1 C2 C3"]
    R2 --> OBS
    R3 --> OBS
    OBS --> DET["ATTACKER DETECTS C1 EQUALS C3"]
    DET --> INF["INFERENCE: PLAINTEXT BLOCKS 1 AND 3 ARE IDENTICAL"]
    INF --> VUL["INFORMATION LEAK WITHOUT BREAKING THE CIPHER"]
```

> [!WARNING]
> The above attack is the *entire reason* ECB is forbidden in protocols like TLS, IPsec, and disk encryption. The famous "ECB penguin" image (where the original bitmap is visibly recognizable after ECB encryption) is a textbook demonstration of this flaw.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Define the Electronic Codebook (ECB) mode of operation. Write its encryption and decryption equations.

**Model Answer:**

Electronic Codebook (ECB) mode is a block cipher mode in which the plaintext is divided into fixed-size blocks and **each block is encrypted independently** using the same secret key $K$. There is no chaining or feedback between blocks.

**Encryption equation:**

$$C_i = E_K(P_i) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

**Decryption equation:**

$$P_i = D_K(C_i) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

Here, $b$ is the block size (128 bits for AES), $n = \lceil \vert P \vert \,/\, b \rceil$ is the number of blocks, $E_K$ is the block cipher encryption primitive, and $D_K$ is its inverse. ECB is the simplest mode but suffers from the property that identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks, which leaks patterns and is the reason it is rarely used for general message encryption. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** List **three** major weaknesses of ECB mode.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Pattern Preservation:** ECB is deterministic. If $P_i = P_j$ then $C_i = C_j$, so an attacker can detect repeated plaintext blocks without breaking the cipher.
2. **Susceptibility to Replay and Substitution Attacks:** An attacker can rearrange, replay, or substitute ciphertext blocks to forge new messages because each block is independently verifiable.
3. **No Semantic Security:** ECB does not hide data patterns; for example, encrypting a bitmap image with ECB leaves the original image silhouette visible (the famous "ECB penguin" demonstration). It also does not use an IV, so the *same key + same plaintext* always produces the *same ciphertext*. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Describe the ECB mode of operation in detail with neat diagrams. Explain the encryption and decryption processes for a 3-block plaintext. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** ECB mode encrypts each plaintext block independently with the same key. For plaintext blocks $P_1, P_2, P_3$:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= E_K(P_1) \\
C_2 &= E_K(P_2) \\
C_3 &= E_K(P_3)
\end{aligned}$$

The ciphertext is the concatenation $\mathbf{C} = C_1 \Vert C_2 \Vert C_3$.

**Step 1 — Padding:** If the original plaintext length is not a multiple of the block size $b$, padding is appended (e.g., PKCS#7).

**Step 2 — Segmentation:** The padded plaintext is split into 3 blocks of $b$ bits each.

**Step 3 — Independent Encryption:** Each block is fed into the block cipher $E$ keyed with $K$. Because the operations are mutually independent, all three encryptions can run in parallel.

**Step 4 — Concatenation:** The three ciphertext blocks are concatenated in the original order to produce the final ciphertext.

**Decryption (Inverse):** The receiver splits $\mathbf{C}$ into $C_1, C_2, C_3$ and applies the inverse block cipher $D_K$ to each:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= D_K(C_1) \\
P_2 &= D_K(C_2) \\
P_3 &= D_K(C_3)
\end{aligned}$$

**Diagram:** (refer to Section 4.1 and 4.2 of these notes for the Mermaid architecture diagrams showing the parallel independent processing of each block under the same key $K$). **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** A plaintext message `"SECURITY"` is to be encrypted using AES in ECB mode. AES has a block size of 16 bytes. Apply PKCS#7 padding and show the encryption process step by step. Take the encryption mapping as:
$E_K(\text{"SECURITY"}) = \texttt{0x44 88 1A 7B C2 35 90 ED 51 6B 0C 7F A3 19 4D E0}$ **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Determine plaintext length and padding requirement.**

The ASCII string `"SECURITY"` has 8 characters, each 1 byte:

$$\vert P \vert = 8 \text{ bytes}$$

Block size $b = 16$ bytes. Therefore padding required:

$$\text{pad length} = 16 - 8 = 8 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Apply PKCS#7 padding.**

PKCS#7 appends $N$ bytes, each with value $0x0N$, where $N$ is the number of padding bytes needed. Here $N = 8$:

$$P_{\text{padded}} = \text{"SECURITY"} + \texttt{08 08 08 08 08 08 08 08}$$

In hex:

$$P_1 = \texttt{0x53 45 43 55 52 49 54 59 08 08 08 08 08 08 08 08}$$

**Step 3 — Segmentation.**

$$n = \frac{16}{16} = 1 \text{ block}$$

There is exactly one block: $P_1$.

**Step 4 — Encryption.**

$$C_1 = E_K(P_1) = \texttt{0x44 88 1A 7B C2 35 90 ED 51 6B 0C 7F A3 19 4D E0}$$

**Step 5 — Final Ciphertext.**

$$\mathbf{C} = C_1 = \texttt{0x44 88 1A 7B C2 35 90 ED 51 6B 0C 7F A3 19 4D E0}$$

**Verification of Decryption (sanity check):**

$$P_1 = D_K(C_1) = \texttt{SECURITY\textasciitilde 08 08 08 08 08 08 08 08}$$

Stripping 8 pad bytes of value $0x08$ recovers the original plaintext `"SECURITY"`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the properties of ECB mode. Why is ECB considered insecure for encrypting messages longer than one block? Provide an example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Properties of ECB Mode:**

1. **Deterministic Encryption:** $C_i = E_K(P_i)$ is a pure function. Same plaintext block and same key always yield the same ciphertext block.
2. **Parallelizable:** Both encryption and decryption can be parallelized across all $n$ blocks since there is no inter-block dependency.
3. **No Error Propagation Across Blocks:** A single bit error in $C_i$ corrupts only $P_i$ on decryption. All other blocks decrypt correctly.
4. **No IV Required:** ECB does not use an initialization vector, simplifying key management.
5. **Block-Aligned Operation:** Synchronization loss (e.g., a dropped block) causes all subsequent blocks to be misaligned and garbled.

**Why ECB is Insecure for Multi-Block Messages:**

ECB fails to provide **semantic security** because it leaks statistical patterns of the plaintext. Specifically, the relation $P_i = P_j \iff C_i = C_j$ means an eavesdropper learns *exactly* which blocks in the message are equal.

**Example:** Suppose Alice encrypts a salary database with ECB, and two employees share the same salary block. The encrypted records will contain identical ciphertext blocks at those positions, allowing the eavesdropper to deduce they have the same salary — without breaking AES at all.

A second classic example is the **ECB-encrypted bitmap image**: even after encryption, the original image's outline is recognizable because uniform regions of the image produce identical ciphertext blocks. This is the well-known "ECB penguin" demonstration. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** Compare ECB mode with CBC (Cipher Block Chaining) mode with respect to: (i) IV requirement, (ii) pattern preservation, (iii) parallelism, and (iv) error propagation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Comparison Axis | ECB Mode | CBC Mode |
|-----------------|----------|----------|
| **(i) IV Requirement** | **No IV** is used. Encryption is purely $C_i = E_K(P_i)$. | **Requires an IV** (or nonce). $C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1})$ with $C_0 = IV$. |
| **(ii) Pattern Preservation** | **Preserves patterns.** Identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks, leaking data. | **Hides patterns.** Each block's encryption depends on all previous ciphertext blocks, so identical plaintext blocks produce different ciphertext blocks (assuming different IV or context). |
| **(iii) Parallelism** | **Fully parallelizable.** All blocks can be encrypted and decrypted simultaneously. | **Encryption is sequential** (each block needs $C_{i-1}$), but **decryption is parallelizable** since $P_i = D_K(C_i) \oplus C_{i-1}$ and all $C_i$ are known in advance. |
| **(iv) Error Propagation** | **Localized.** A bit-flip in $C_i$ corrupts only $P_i$; other blocks recover cleanly. | **Propagates.** A bit-flip in $C_i$ corrupts $P_i$ completely and **flips the corresponding bit in $P_{i+1}$** (since $P_{i+1} = D_K(C_{i+1}) \oplus C_i$). Errors do not propagate beyond two blocks. |

**Summary Statement (for full marks):** ECB trades cryptographic strength for simplicity and speed, while CBC trades performance for semantic security. ECB is suitable only for single-block high-entropy data (keys, IVs, random nonces), whereas CBC is the workhorse for general message encryption in legacy systems. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the padding step** in numerical ECB problems. If a 10-character plaintext is to be encrypted with $b=16$, you *must* show PKCS#7 padding to 16 bytes; otherwise full marks are not awarded.
> 2. **Stating that ECB uses an IV.** This is a factual error worth losing 1–2 marks. ECB does NOT use an IV — that is a defining property.
> 3. **Confusing "no error propagation" with "secure."** ECB's localized error behavior is a *neutral* property, not a security benefit. The pattern-leakage flaw is the real issue.
> 4. **Failing to write both encryption and decryption equations** explicitly. KTU examiners allocate marks separately for $C_i = E_K(P_i)$ and $P_i = D_K(C_i)$. Mentioning only one loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Ignoring parallelism.** Students frequently describe ECB correctly but forget to mention that both encryption and decryption can be parallelized — a guaranteed 1-mark loss in the "properties" question.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ECB = Simplest mode.** Each block is encrypted independently with the same key $K$.
- **Core equation:** $C_i = E_K(P_i)$; inverse: $P_i = D_K(C_i)$.
- **No IV is used.** This is unique among the five major modes.
- **Block size:** **128 bits (AES)** or 64 bits (DES) — know both.
- **Deterministic:** $P_i = P_j \iff C_i = C_j$ → information leakage.
- **Parallelizable:** Both encryption and decryption can run in parallel; ideal for hardware throughput.
- **Localized errors:** Bit-flip in $C_i$ affects only $P_i$, no avalanche.
- **Padding required:** Use PKCS#7 for variable-length messages.
- **Use only for single random blocks:** Key wrapping, session ID encryption, building PRP/hash constructions.
- **Never use for general messages, images, or files:** Pattern leakage is catastrophic.
- **Exam mantra:** *"ECB is fast, parallel, and simple — but leaks patterns and offers no semantic security."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cipher Block Chaining Mode (CBC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT501 - FOUNDATIONS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cipher Block Chaining Mode (CBC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Formal Definition — Cipher Block Chaining (CBC) Mode**
> **Cipher Block Chaining (CBC)** is a *mode of operation* for a block cipher in which each plaintext block is **XORed** with the *immediately preceding ciphertext block* before being passed through the underlying block cipher encryption algorithm. An **Initialization Vector (IV)** of the same size as the block is used as a *pseudo-predecessor* for the very first plaintext block to ensure semantic security and randomness in the output.

Mathematically, for an underlying block cipher $\mathcal{E}_K(\cdot)$ with block size $b$ bits and key $K$:

$$
C_j \;=\; \mathcal{E}_K\bigl(P_j \oplus C_{j-1}\bigr), \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, n
$$

with the special initial condition $C_0 \;\leftarrow\; IV$ (Initialization Vector).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Sealed Envelope Chain

Imagine you are sending **sealed envelopes** down a hallway where each envelope contains a coded message:

1. The first envelope has a **random scratch paper** (the *IV*) tucked inside before being sealed.
2. The next message you want to seal, you first **rub it against the previous sealed envelope** (the *XOR operation*) — any pattern from the previous envelope gets imprinted on your message.
3. Then you **lock the new envelope** using the same master key (the block cipher).
4. The receiver, holding the same master key, **unlocks each envelope** and then uses the **previous sealed envelope's pattern** to undo the imprint and recover the original message.

> [!NOTE] **Why CBC is a "Chain":** Each ciphertext block depends on **all prior plaintext blocks**. This is what gives CBC its name — the ciphertexts form an unbroken mathematical chain, where tampering with even a single bit in the middle of the ciphertext **poisons the rest of the chain**, enabling **integrity protection alongside confidentiality**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Board-Exam Mandatory Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **IV** | A random (or pseudo-random) $b$-bit block used as $C_0$; must be unpredictable for the chosen plaintext attack to be defeated. |
| **Block Size ($b$)** | Fixed width of plaintext/ciphertext processed per round (commonly **128 bits** in AES). |
| **Padding** | When plaintext length is not a multiple of $b$, a padding scheme (e.g., **PKCS#7**) is appended. |
| **Error Propagation** | A single bit error in $C_j$ corrupts $P_j$ (randomly) and **only flips the corresponding bit** in $P_{j+1}$ (the next block remains intact thereafter). |
| **Throughput** | Encryption is inherently **sequential** — parallelization of encryption across blocks is NOT possible; however, **decryption CAN be parallelized**. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How"

**The Core Problem with ECB Mode (Motivation for CBC):**
In *Electronic Codebook (ECB)* mode, identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks. This leaks structural information — an attacker can recognize patterns (e.g., a logo in an encrypted image produces the same logo in the ciphertext).

**How CBC Solves It:**
1. **Randomization via IV** — Two identical messages encrypted with different IVs yield completely different ciphertext streams.
2. **Diffusion via XOR chaining** — Even if the same plaintext block appears twice in the message, the previous ciphertext block is almost certainly different, so the two plaintext blocks will encrypt to two different ciphertext blocks.

**Step-by-Step Encryption Logic (for block $j$):**
- **Step 1 (Mix):** Compute the XOR $P_j \oplus C_{j-1}$. This *diffuses* previous-ciphertext influence into the current input.
- **Step 2 (Encrypt):** Apply the block cipher $\mathcal{E}_K$ to obtain $C_j$.
- **Step 3 (Transmit):** Output $C_j$ to the channel; also store $C_j$ in memory for the next round.
- **Step 4 (Seed):** For $j = 1$, $C_0$ is replaced by a freshly generated IV (must be transmitted or stored alongside the ciphertext).

**Step-by-Step Decryption Logic (for block $j$):**
- **Step 1 (Decrypt):** Apply the inverse cipher $\mathcal{D}_K$ to $C_j$ to obtain an intermediate value $T_j = P_j \oplus C_{j-1}$.
- **Step 2 (Undo-Mix):** XOR with the previous ciphertext $C_{j-1}$ to recover plaintext: $P_j = T_j \oplus C_{j-1}$.

> [!TIP] **Symmetry of Invertibility (Exam Favourite):** Notice that decryption still needs only $K$, the block cipher *inverse*, and the *previous ciphertext* — not the IV *inside* the cipher. This is why CBC decryption can be **parallelized** (each block needs only its own $C_j$ and $C_{j-1}$), whereas CBC encryption is strictly **sequential**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical Expression | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CBC Encryption** | $C_j = \mathcal{E}_K(P_j \oplus C_{j-1})$ | $C_0 = IV$ |
| **CBC Decryption** | $P_j = \mathcal{D}_K(C_j) \oplus C_{j-1}$ | Uses inverse cipher $\mathcal{D}_K$ |
| **IV Unpredictability** | $IV$ must be a *cryptographically secure random* (or pseudorandom) value | Predictable IV → CPA insecurity |
| **Ciphertext Length** | $\vert C \vert = \vert P \vert + \vert IV \vert + \text{padding bits}$ | Padding only on last block |
| **Error in $C_j$ (bit-flip)** | Corrupts entire $P_j$ (random); flips same bit position in $P_{j+1}$ | Self-heals after $j+1$ |
| **Error in IV bit $i$** | Flips bit $i$ of **only** $P_1$ | No effect on later blocks |
| **Parallelism** | Encryption: **No** $\bigm\vert$ Decryption: **Yes** | Critical for performance design |
| **CPA Security** | Achieved *iff* IV is uniformly random per encryption | Reusing IV with same $K$ is **fatal** |
| **Authentication?** | **No** — CBC alone provides only confidentiality | Needs HMAC / CBC-MAC for integrity |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Real-World Utility:** CBC is the underlying mode for **TLS 1.2** (record-layer ciphersuites using AES-CBC + HMAC-SHA256), **IPsec ESP** in early deployments, and the historical **SSL 3.0 / TLS 1.0**. Modern protocols (TLS 1.3) have largely migrated to **AEAD modes** (GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305), but CBC remains foundational in KTU curriculum for understanding chaining mechanics.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of CBC Encryption Recurrence

We start with the plaintext message $P$ partitioned into $n$ equal-sized blocks of bit-length $b$:

$$
P \;=\; \bigl(P_1 \;\Vert\; P_2 \;\Vert\; \cdots \;\Vert\; P_n\bigr), \quad \text{where each } \vert P_j \vert = b.
$$

**Step A — Define the chain seed.** We initialize the chain with an IV of the same block size:

$$
C_0 \;\triangleq\; IV, \quad \vert IV \vert = b.
$$

**Step B — Apply the encryption recurrence for block 1.** Substituting $j = 1$ into the canonical CBC rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= \mathcal{E}_K\bigl(P_1 \oplus C_0\bigr) \\
    &= \mathcal{E}_K\bigl(P_1 \oplus IV\bigr).
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Inductive hypothesis.** Assume that for some block index $j$, the ciphertext $C_j$ has been correctly computed and is now available as a "feed-forward" input.

**Step D — Inductive step (encryption for block $j+1$).** Apply the recurrence with index shifted by one:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{j+1} &= \mathcal{E}_K\bigl(P_{j+1} \oplus C_j\bigr) \\
        &= \mathcal{E}_K\Bigl(P_{j+1} \oplus \mathcal{E}_K\bigl(P_j \oplus C_{j-1}\bigr)\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step E — Unrolling the chain.** After $n$ rounds, the final ciphertext block $C_n$ depends (transitively) on **every plaintext block** $P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n$ and on the IV. This is the formal statement of CBC's *full diffusion property*.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of CBC Decryption

To show invertibility, we begin with the encryption relation and apply $\mathcal{D}_K$ to both sides:

$$
\mathcal{D}_K(C_j) \;=\; \mathcal{D}_K\bigl(\mathcal{E}_K(P_j \oplus C_{j-1})\bigr).
$$

**Step 1 — Cancel the cipher pair.** Since $\mathcal{D}_K \circ \mathcal{E}_K = \text{Id}$ (identity function on the message space):

$$
\mathcal{D}_K(C_j) \;=\; P_j \oplus C_{j-1}.
$$

**Step 2 — Isolate $P_j$.** XOR both sides with $C_{j-1}$, using the algebraic identity $x \oplus x = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{D}_K(C_j) \oplus C_{j-1} &= (P_j \oplus C_{j-1}) \oplus C_{j-1} \\
                                   &= P_j \oplus (C_{j-1} \oplus C_{j-1}) \\
                                   &= P_j \oplus 0 \\
                                   &= P_j.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Write the canonical decryption formula:**

$$
\boxed{\,P_j \;=\; \mathcal{D}_K(C_j) \oplus C_{j-1}\,}
$$

### 3.3 Error Propagation Analysis (High-Yield Board Derivations)

**Case 1: A single bit error occurs in ciphertext block $C_j$.**

The corrupted ciphertext is $\tilde{C}_j = C_j \oplus E$, where $E$ is an error vector with at least one bit set.

*Decryption of the corrupted block $j$:* The attacker / receiver computes:

$$
\mathcal{D}_K(\tilde{C}_j) \;=\; \mathcal{D}_K(C_j \oplus E) \;\neq\; \mathcal{D}_K(C_j) \quad \text{(almost certainly random)}.
$$

Therefore the recovered plaintext is $\tilde{P}_j = \mathcal{D}_K(\tilde{C}_j) \oplus C_{j-1}$, which is **essentially random garbage** — *the entire block $j$ is destroyed*.

*Effect on block $j+1$:* The next block is computed as $\tilde{P}_{j+1} = \mathcal{D}_K(C_{j+1}) \oplus \tilde{C}_j$. Using the algebraic identity $\tilde{C}_j = C_j \oplus E$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{P}_{j+1} &= \mathcal{D}_K(C_{j+1}) \oplus (C_j \oplus E) \\
                &= \underbrace{\bigl(\mathcal{D}_K(C_{j+1}) \oplus C_j\bigr)}_{=\,P_{j+1}} \oplus E \\
                &= P_{j+1} \oplus E.
\end{aligned}
$$

So block $j+1$ suffers a **predictable, identical bit-flip** at exactly the position(s) where $E$ has a 1.

*Effect on block $j+2$ onwards:* Since $\tilde{P}_{j+1}$ is **not re-encrypted** and fed into the decryption of $j+2$ (only $\tilde{C}_j$, $\tilde{C}_{j+1}$ are inputs), and $C_{j+1}$ is still intact on the wire, block $j+2$ onwards is **completely unaffected**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Error Propagation Summary:** A bit-error in $C_j$ corrupts *one full block randomly* ($P_j$) and *flips exactly the same bit(s)* in the *next* block ($P_{j+1}$); recovery is automatic from $P_{j+2}$ onwards.

**Case 2: A single bit error occurs in the IV.**

Since the IV only participates in decrypting $P_1$, flipping bit $i$ of the IV simply flips bit $i$ of $P_1$ — **no later blocks are affected**.

**Case 3: An entire block is lost (insertion / deletion on the channel).**

The receiver loses synchronization: every subsequent plaintext block is the *XOR of the wrong pair*, producing **complete garbage from that point onwards**. CBC has **no inherent resynchronization** mechanism.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CBC Encryption Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IV0[IV] --> XOR1
    P1[Plaintext Block P1] --> XOR1
    XOR1[XOR Operation] --> EK1
    K[Secret Key K] --> EK1
    EK1[Block Cipher Encrypt] --> C1
    C1[Ciphertext Block C1] --> OUT1((Output Channel))
    C1 --> XOR2
    P2[Plaintext Block P2] --> XOR2
    XOR2 --> EK2
    K --> EK2
    EK2 --> C2
    C2[Ciphertext Block C2] --> OUT2((Output Channel))
    C2 --> XOR3
    P3[Plaintext Block P3] --> XOR3
    XOR3 --> EK3
    K --> EK3
    EK3 --> C3
    C3[Ciphertext Block C3] --> OUT3((Output Channel))

    classDef ivStyle fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ptStyle fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ctStyle fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#78281f,stroke-width:2px
    classDef opStyle fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#212121,stroke-width:2px
    classDef kStyle fill:#abebc6,stroke:#145a32,stroke-width:2px

    class IV0 ivStyle
    class P1,P2,P3 ptStyle
    class C1,C2,C3 ctStyle

```

### 4.2 CBC Decryption Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1in[Ciphertext Block C1] --> DK1
    K[Secret Key K] --> DK1
    DK1[Block Cipher Decrypt] --> XOR1
    IV0[IV] --> XOR1
    XOR1[XOR Operation] --> P1out[Plaintext Block P1]
    P1out --> RECV1((Receiver))

    C2in[Ciphertext Block C2] --> DK2
    K --> DK2
    DK2 --> XOR2
    C1in --> XOR2
    XOR2 --> P2out[Plaintext Block P2]
    P2out --> RECV2((Receiver))

    C3in[Ciphertext Block C3] --> DK3
    K --> DK3
    DK3 --> XOR3
    C2in --> XOR3
    XOR3 --> P3out[Plaintext Block P3]
    P3out --> RECV3((Receiver))

    classDef ivStyle fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ptStyle fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72,stroke-width:2px

```

### 4.3 Error Propagation Map (Bit-Error in $C_j$)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ERR[Bit Error Injected into Block Cj] --> BLKJ[Block Pj Becomes Random Garbage]
    ERR --> BLKJ1[Block Pj Plus 1 Suffers Identical Bit Flip]
    BLKJ1 --> CLEAN[Blocks Pj Plus 2 Onwards Recovered Cleanly]

    classDef errStyle fill:#f1948a,stroke:#7b241c,stroke-width:2px
    classDef warnStyle fill:#f8c471,stroke:#7e5109,stroke-width:2px
    classDef okStyle fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:2px

    class ERR errStyle
    class BLKJ,BLKJ1 warnStyle
    class CLEAN okStyle
```

> [!NOTE] **Why Mermaid and not a hand-drawn diagram?** The CBC mode is fundamentally a *data-flow* operation — a physical sketch adds no extra pedagogical value over a clean functional block diagram. The mermaid diagrams above map every signal path, key injection, and XOR/cipher stage explicitly.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*What is an Initialization Vector (IV) in Cipher Block Chaining (CBC) mode? Why must the IV be unpredictable and unique for every encryption performed with the same key?*

**Model Answer:**

The Initialization Vector (IV) is a random or pseudo-random block of the same bit-length as the block cipher's block size, used as the *seed ciphertext* $C_0$ for the very first encryption in CBC mode.

**Why unpredictable and unique?**
1. **Indistinguishability under Chosen Plaintext Attack (IND-CPA):** If an adversary can predict the IV, they can mount a chosen-plaintext attack — for example, by submitting $P_1 \oplus IV_{\text{guess}}$ and observing whether the resulting ciphertext matches an expected pattern.
2. **Avoiding identical-ciphertext leakage:** Reusing the same $(K, IV)$ pair for two different messages produces identical ciphertext streams up to the first differing plaintext block. This leaks information about message equality.
3. **Semantic security:** A unique unpredictable IV ensures that even encrypting the *same plaintext* twice produces completely different ciphertexts, preserving semantic security.

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only "IV is a random number" without naming its cryptographic role. You must explicitly mention **CPA security** or **semantic security** to earn full 3 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain the concept of error propagation in CBC mode. What happens to the recovered plaintext if (i) one bit is flipped in ciphertext block $C_j$, and (ii) one bit is flipped in the IV?*

**Model Answer:**

**Error propagation** in CBC describes how a localized error in the ciphertext (or IV) propagates to the recovered plaintext.

**(i) Bit-flip in $C_j$:**
- The recovered $P_j$ becomes **completely garbled** (essentially random, since block-cipher decryption is a non-linear random permutation).
- The recovered $P_{j+1}$ suffers a **predictable identical bit-flip** at exactly the same bit position(s) where the error was introduced.
- All subsequent blocks ($P_{j+2}, P_{j+3}, \dots$) are **recovered correctly** (self-healing property).

**(ii) Bit-flip in the IV:**
- Only $P_1$ is affected — the bit-flip is mirrored in $P_1$ at exactly the same position.
- All blocks from $P_2$ onwards are **unaffected**, since the IV does not participate in their decryption.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the encryption and decryption equations for the CBC mode of operation, clearly stating the role of the Initialization Vector (IV). State any assumptions made about block size. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A message $M = (P_1, P_2, P_3)$ is encrypted using CBC mode with block cipher $\mathcal{E}_K$ and an IV denoted by $C_0$. Suppose a **single bit error** (a flip of bit position $i$) occurs in ciphertext block $C_2$ during transmission. Show mathematically how this error affects the recovered plaintext blocks $P_1, P_2, P_3$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of CBC Equations:**

Let the underlying block cipher $\mathcal{E}_K$ operate on $b$-bit blocks, and let $M = (P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n)$ be the plaintext with each $\vert P_j \vert = b$. We define the IV as $C_0$, a $b$-bit value.

*Encryption:* For each block $j = 1, 2, \dots, n$:

$$
C_j \;=\; \mathcal{E}_K(P_j \oplus C_{j-1}), \quad \text{with } C_0 = IV.
$$

*Decryption:* We start by applying $\mathcal{D}_K$ (the inverse cipher) to both sides of the encryption equation:

$$
\mathcal{D}_K(C_j) \;=\; \mathcal{D}_K\bigl(\mathcal{E}_K(P_j \oplus C_{j-1})\bigr) \;=\; P_j \oplus C_{j-1}.
$$

XORing both sides with $C_{j-1}$ and using $x \oplus x = 0$:

$$
P_j \;=\; \mathcal{D}_K(C_j) \oplus C_{j-1}.
$$

**Assumption:** $\vert P_j \vert = \vert C_j \vert = \vert IV \vert = b$ (fixed block size). If the last plaintext block is shorter, a padding scheme is applied before encryption.

**(b) Error Analysis — Bit-flip in $C_2$:**

Let the error vector be $E = 2^i$ (a single bit flipped at position $i$). The corrupted ciphertext is $\tilde{C}_2 = C_2 \oplus E$.

*Recovery of $P_1$:* This block depends only on $C_1$ and $C_0$, neither of which is corrupted:

$$
P_1^{\text{recv}} = \mathcal{D}_K(C_1) \oplus C_0 = P_1. \quad \text{(Unaffected)}
$$

*Recovery of $P_2$:* The corrupted ciphertext is fed into $\mathcal{D}_K$:

$$
P_2^{\text{recv}} = \mathcal{D}_K(\tilde{C}_2) \oplus C_1 = \mathcal{D}_K(C_2 \oplus E) \oplus C_1.
$$

Since $\mathcal{D}_K$ is a non-linear function, even a 1-bit change in input scrambles roughly half the output bits:

$$
P_2^{\text{recv}} \approx \text{random garbage}. \quad \text{(Catastrophic, full block lost)}
$$

*Recovery of $P_3$:* Here the previous ciphertext is the corrupted $\tilde{C}_2$, but the current block's ciphertext $C_3$ is intact:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_3^{\text{recv}} &= \mathcal{D}_K(C_3) \oplus \tilde{C}_2 \\
                  &= \mathcal{D}_K(C_3) \oplus (C_2 \oplus E) \\
                  &= \underbrace{\bigl(\mathcal{D}_K(C_3) \oplus C_2\bigr)}_{=\,P_3} \oplus E \\
                  &= P_3 \oplus E.
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus $P_3$ suffers a **predictable, identical single-bit flip** at position $i$.

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A very common mistake is to claim that an error in $C_j$ corrupts *all subsequent* blocks permanently. CBC is **self-healing** — exactly the opposite is true. Only the block $j+1$ suffers the predictable flip; $j+2$ onwards is fully recovered. Losing this distinction will cost you at least **3 of the 7 marks**.

---

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Compare ECB mode and CBC mode of operation for block ciphers across the following dimensions: pattern-leakage resistance, IV requirement, error propagation, and parallelizability of encryption/decryption. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** In CBC mode, suppose two different plaintexts $M$ and $M'$ are encrypted with the **same key $K$** and the **same IV**. Show that an adversary who can submit chosen plaintexts can detect whether the same first $k$ plaintext blocks appear at the start of both messages. Why is this a security violation? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table:**

| Dimension | ECB Mode | CBC Mode |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Pattern leakage** | Identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks — leaks patterns. | Each ciphertext block depends on prior ciphertexts via XOR — patterns are diffused. |
| **IV required?** | No IV needed. | IV is mandatory; must be unpredictable and unique per encryption. |
| **Encryption parallelizable?** | Yes — each block is independent. | No — strictly sequential due to chaining. |
| **Decryption parallelizable?** | Yes. | Yes — each block only needs its own $C_j$ and $C_{j-1}$. |
| **Bit-error in $C_j$** | Affects only $P_j$. | Affects $P_j$ (garbled) and $P_{j+1}$ (predictable flip). Self-healing. |
| **CPA Security** | **No** — deterministic. | **Yes** (with random IV). |

**(b) IV-Reuse Attack (the "Two-Block Collision" attack):**

Suppose $M = (P_1, P_2, \dots)$ and $M' = (P'_1, P'_2, \dots)$ are both encrypted with the same $K$ and the same $C_0 = IV$.

For block 1:

$$
C_1 = \mathcal{E}_K(P_1 \oplus IV), \quad C'_1 = \mathcal{E}_K(P'_1 \oplus IV).
$$

For block 2:

$$
C_2 = \mathcal{E}_K(P_2 \oplus C_1), \quad C'_2 = \mathcal{E}_K(P'_2 \oplus C'_1).
$$

**Adversary's observation:** If $P_1 = P'_1$, then $P_1 \oplus IV = P'_1 \oplus IV$, and therefore:

$$
C_1 = C'_1 \quad \text{(identical first ciphertext blocks)}.
$$

Similarly, if additionally $P_2 = P'_2$, then $C_2 = C'_2$, etc.

**Adversary strategy:** The attacker submits two chosen plaintexts $M$ and $M'$ that differ at a known location, e.g., $P_1 \neq P'_1$ but $P_2 = P'_2, P_3 = P'_3, \dots$. If $C_1 = C'_1$ (which the attacker can directly observe in the ciphertexts), the attacker deduces that $P_1 = P'_1$, contradicting the chosen-plaintext assumption.

**Why this is a security violation:**
This breaks *indistinguishability under chosen-plaintext attack (IND-CPA)*, the standard notion of semantic security for symmetric encryption. The attacker gains a **non-negligible distinguishing advantage** between encryptions of identical-prefix messages and different-prefix messages, which is precisely what a secure encryption scheme must prevent.

**Mitigation:** Always generate a fresh random IV for *every* CBC encryption, regardless of whether the message has been seen before. The IV need not be secret but must be **unpredictable** (CSPRNG output) and **never reused** with the same key.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — CBC Mode**

- ✅ **CBC Encryption:** $C_j = \mathcal{E}_K(P_j \oplus C_{j-1})$, with $C_0 = IV$.
- ✅ **CBC Decryption:** $P_j = \mathcal{D}_K(C_j) \oplus C_{j-1}$.
- ✅ **IV:** Must be unpredictable and unique per encryption; predictable or reused IV breaks CPA security.
- ✅ **Block Size:** $b$ bits (e.g., **128 bits** for AES); IV has the same length $b$.
- ✅ **Padding:** Last block may need padding (e.g., PKCS#7) if plaintext length is not a multiple of $b$.
- ✅ **Parallelism:** Encryption is sequential; **decryption is parallelizable**.
- ✅ **Error Propagation (Bit-flip in $C_j$):** $P_j$ → random garbage; $P_{j+1}$ → predictable same-bit flip; $P_{j+2}$ onwards → unaffected (self-healing).
- ✅ **Bit-flip in IV:** Only affects $P_1$; all later blocks recovered cleanly.
- ✅ **Lost/Inserted Block:** Permanent desynchronization — all subsequent plaintext blocks are garbage. CBC has no resync mechanism.
- ✅ **CPA Security:** Yes, *iff* IV is fresh-random per encryption.
- ✅ **Authentication?** **No.** CBC alone is malleable; an attacker can flip ciphertext bits and predictably alter plaintext bits (the famous *bit-flipping attack* on CBC). Combine with HMAC for integrity (encrypt-then-MAC) or use AEAD modes (GCM) in modern systems.
- ✅ **Real-World Footprint:** TLS 1.2 (legacy ciphersuites), IPsec ESP (legacy), SSL 3.0; largely superseded by AEAD modes in TLS 1.3.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cipher Feedback Mode (CFB)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT501 - FOUNDATIONS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cipher Feedback Mode (CFB)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cipher Feedback (CFB) Mode** is a mode of operation for a block cipher that converts the underlying block cipher (e.g., DES, AES) into a *self-synchronizing stream cipher*. It generates the keystream dynamically by feeding the previous ciphertext block (or a segment of it) back into the block cipher's input. The plaintext is then XORed with this generated keystream to produce the ciphertext.

In CFB mode, the message is processed in segments of size $s$ bits (where $1 \le s \le b$, and $b$ is the block size of the underlying block cipher, e.g., **b = 128 bits for AES**). The most common variants are **CFB-1** (1-bit segments), **CFB-8** (8-bit segments/byte), and **CFB-64** (64-bit segments).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** CFB is fundamentally a stream cipher mode built atop a block cipher. It does **not** require padding, which is a critical advantage over ECB and CBC.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Locked Conveyor Belt (Self-Healing Lock)**
>
> Imagine a conveyor belt carrying locked boxes of raw material (plaintext segments). Each box is sprayed with a unique secret chemical mix (keystream) generated by a master machine (block cipher). The chemical mix used for the current box depends on what the **previous box's output looked like** (feedback).
>
> If a chemical mix goes wrong or one box gets smudged (transmission error), the next few boxes might be slightly affected, but as soon as a clean box passes, the machine "re-syncs" itself and produces the correct chemical mix again. This is precisely the **self-synchronizing** property of CFB mode.

## 1.3 Key Properties at a Glance

- **Type:** Stream cipher mode (turns a block cipher into a stream cipher).
- **Padding Required:** **No** (operates on segments, not full blocks).
- **Parallelizable:** Encryption is **NOT parallelizable** (depends on previous ciphertext). Decryption **IS parallelizable** (uses only previous ciphertext for keystream, not plaintext).
- **Error Propagation:** A single bit error in ciphertext block $C_i$ affects **$\lceil b/s \rceil + 1$** subsequent plaintext blocks (in CFB-1, errors propagate for $\approx b+1$ bits).
- **Initialization Vector (IV):** Must be a unique, unpredictable, and non-repeating **nonce** for every message. For CFB, the IV is conceptually a "dummy ciphertext block" $C_0$.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU examinations, always explicitly state that CFB uses a **non-secret IV** but the IV must be **fresh (unpredictable)** for every encryption operation. Reusing an IV with the same key **completely breaks** CFB security (identical keystream leak).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Breakdown)

### Encryption Algorithm in CFB Mode

1. **Initialization:** Load a unique, unpredictable **Initialization Vector (IV)** of size $b$ bits into the topmost register. Label it as $C_0$ (it acts as a "dummy" previous ciphertext).
2. **Keystream Generation:** Encrypt $C_0$ (or the current shift register contents) using the secret key $K$ in the block cipher $E_K$.
3. **Segment Selection:** Take the **leftmost $s$ bits** of the encrypted output. This forms the keystream segment $O_1$.
4. **XOR Operation:** XOR the keystream segment $O_1$ with the first plaintext segment $P_1$ to produce the ciphertext segment $C_1$.
5. **Feedback / Shift:** Shift the contents of the register left by $s$ bits, appending $C_1$ to the right end (the least significant position). The new register now contains $C_0 \parallel C_1$ effectively.
6. **Repeat:** Repeat steps 2–5 for every subsequent plaintext segment $P_i$.

### Decryption Algorithm in CFB Mode

Decryption uses the **same** block cipher $E_K$ (NOT its inverse $D_K$). This is a critical and frequently tested point.

1. Load the same IV into the register as $C_0$.
2. Encrypt $C_0$ with $E_K$ to get the leftmost $s$ bits — this is the keystream $O_1$.
3. XOR $O_1$ with the received ciphertext $C_1$ to recover $P_1$.
4. Shift the register and append $C_1$. Repeat for all segments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $E_K$ and not $D_K$ for decryption?** The block cipher is used only as a *pseudo-random number generator* (PRNG) in CFB. The actual decryption happens via XOR with the keystream, which is inherently invertible. Using $D_K$ would be a fundamental architectural error.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Boundary / Notes |
|---|----------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | $I_1 = \text{IV} = C_0$ | Initial register content | Non-secret, but unique per message |
| 2 | $O_i = \text{MSB}_s \left( E_K(I_i) \right)$ | Keystream segment for block $i$ | Top $s$ bits of encrypted register |
| 3 | $C_i = P_i \oplus O_i$ | Encryption: ciphertext segment | $1 \le i \le n$ segments |
| 4 | $P_i = C_i \oplus O_i$ | Decryption: plaintext segment | Same keystream is regenerated |
| 5 | $I_{i+1} = \text{LSB}_{b-s}(I_i) \parallel C_i$ | Register shift update | Left shift by $s$, append $C_i$ |
| 6 | $O_i = \text{MSB}_s \left( E_K(C_{i-1}) \right)$ | Simplified keystream form | $C_0$ is the IV |
| 7 | $C_i = P_i \oplus \text{MSB}_s(E_K(C_{i-1}))$ | **Master Encryption Equation** | Most cited in KTU papers |
| 8 | $P_i = C_i \oplus \text{MSB}_s(E_K(C_{i-1}))$ | **Master Decryption Equation** | Uses $E_K$, not $D_K$ |

**Standard CFB Variants:**

- **CFB-1:** $s = 1$ bit (low throughput, high error propagation)
- **CFB-8:** $s = 8$ bits (one byte; common for character-oriented data)
- **CFB-64:** $s = 64$ bits (used with 64-bit block ciphers like DES)
- **CFB-128:** $s = 128$ bits (used with 128-bit block ciphers like AES; behaves similarly to CBC)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Stream-oriented protocols:** TLS/SSL historically used CFB-like constructions (now replaced by AEAD ciphers like GCM).
- **Character-level encryption:** CFB-8 is ideal for terminal/character streams where data arrives byte-by-byte.
- **No-padding requirement:** CFB is preferred in applications where message length is not a multiple of the block size and padding overhead is unacceptable (e.g., streaming video, real-time sensor telemetry).
- **Self-synchronization in noisy channels:** CFB's ability to recover from bit-flip errors makes it useful in legacy telecommunication systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Encryption Equation

We start from the primitive operations and build the canonical CFB encryption formula step by step.

**Step 1 — Register Initialization**

At time $i = 1$, the shift register content $I_1$ is initialized to the Initialization Vector (IV). For uniformity, we denote this as $C_0$.

$$
I_1 = \text{IV} = C_0
$$

Here, $C_0$ is a *dummy* ciphertext block of size $b$ bits that never gets transmitted but is required to bootstrap the keystream.

**Step 2 — Keystream Generation**

The block cipher $E$ with secret key $K$ encrypts the register content $I_1$, producing a $b$-bit block. We then extract only the most significant $s$ bits (denoted by $\text{MSB}_s$) to form the keystream segment $O_1$.

$$
O_1 = \text{MSB}_s \big( E_K(I_1) \big) = \text{MSB}_s \big( E_K(C_0) \big)
$$

**Step 3 — Ciphertext Generation via XOR**

The plaintext segment $P_1$ is XORed with the keystream $O_1$ to produce the ciphertext segment $C_1$.

$$
C_1 = P_1 \oplus O_1 = P_1 \oplus \text{MSB}_s \big( E_K(C_0) \big)
$$

**Step 4 — Register Update (Feedback Step)**

The register is shifted left by $s$ bits, and the newly produced ciphertext segment $C_1$ is fed into the rightmost $s$ bits (least significant positions). In a more abstract algebraic notation, the new register content is:

$$
I_2 = \big[ \text{LSB}_{b-s}(I_1) \big] \;\Vert\; C_1
$$

The symbol $\Vert$ denotes bit-string concatenation. Effectively, the previous register is "pushed" to the left by $s$ bits, and the new ciphertext segment $C_1$ occupies the freed $s$ LSB positions.

**Step 5 — Generalization for Block $i$**

By induction, for the $i$-th segment ($i \ge 1$):

$$
O_i = \text{MSB}_s \big( E_K(C_{i-1}) \big)
$$

$$
C_i = P_i \oplus O_i = P_i \oplus \text{MSB}_s \big( E_K(C_{i-1}) \big)
$$

$$
I_{i+1} = \big[ \text{LSB}_{b-s}(I_i) \big] \;\Vert\; C_i
$$

This completes the recursive definition of CFB mode encryption.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Master Decryption Equation

**Step 1 — Regenerate the Keystream**

At the receiver, the IV is loaded into the register as $C_0$. The block cipher $E_K$ is applied identically, producing:

$$
O_1 = \text{MSB}_s \big( E_K(C_0) \big)
$$

**Step 2 — Recover Plaintext**

Since $C_1 = P_1 \oplus O_1$, XORing both sides with $O_1$ recovers the plaintext:

$$
P_1 = C_1 \oplus O_1
$$

$$
P_1 = C_1 \oplus \text{MSB}_s \big( E_K(C_0) \big)
$$

**Step 3 — Generalize**

For $i \ge 1$:

$$
P_i = C_i \oplus \text{MSB}_s \big( E_K(C_{i-1}) \big)
$$

**Critical Observation:** The decryption formula depends only on $C_{i-1}$ and $C_i$ — it does **not** depend on any $P_j$. This is why **CFB decryption is parallelizable**, while encryption is strictly sequential.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Trace (CFB-8 with b = 8, hypothetical toy cipher)

To build intuition, consider a *toy* block cipher with $b = 8$ bits, $K = 10110011$, and $s = 8$ (i.e., full-block CFB, which is equivalent to CBC for a single-bit segment). Suppose:

- $P = \texttt{HELLO}$ → ASCII: $P_1 = 01001000$, $P_2 = 01000101$, $P_3 = 01001100$, $P_4 = 01001100$, $P_5 = 01001111$
- $C_0 = \text{IV} = 11110000$

Assume the toy block cipher produces:
- $E_K(11110000) = 01010101$ (hypothetical output)
- $E_K(C_1) = 10101010$ (hypothetical)
- $E_K(C_2) = 11001100$ (hypothetical)

**Encryption of $P_1$:**

$$
O_1 = \text{MSB}_8(E_K(C_0)) = 01010101
$$

$$
C_1 = P_1 \oplus O_1 = 01001000 \oplus 01010101 = 00011101
$$

**Encryption of $P_2$:**

$$
O_2 = E_K(C_1) = 10101010
$$

$$
C_2 = P_2 \oplus O_2 = 01000101 \oplus 10101010 = 11101111
$$

And so on. The **causal chain** ($C_{i-1} \to O_i \to C_i \to O_{i+1}$) is clearly visible.

## 3.4 Error Propagation Analysis

Suppose a single bit-flip occurs in $C_i$, transforming it to $C_i' = C_i \oplus e_j$ (where $e_j$ has a 1 in position $j$).

**Effect on Block $i$ (the corrupted block):**

$$
P_i' = C_i' \oplus O_i = (C_i \oplus e_j) \oplus O_i = P_i \oplus e_j
$$

The $j$-th bit of $P_i$ is flipped. **One bit error → one bit error in plaintext.**

**Effect on Block $i+1$ (the first block after corruption):**

The corrupted $C_i'$ is shifted into the register. The keystream segment $O_{i+1}$ is now generated from this corrupted value, producing an essentially random-looking $O_{i+1}'$. The recovered plaintext is:

$$
P_{i+1}' = C_{i+1} \oplus O_{i+1}'
$$

This is essentially **random garbage** in all $s$ bit positions.

**Effect on Subsequent Blocks:**

For CFB-1, the corrupted bit sits in the register for the next $b$ iterations, producing $b$ blocks of garbage, after which it is shifted out and the cipher **resynchronizes**.

**General formula:** A 1-bit error in $C_i$ corrupts:
- Exactly 1 bit in $P_i$ (at the same position).
- All $s$ bits in approximately $\lceil b/s \rceil$ subsequent blocks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Self-Synchronization:** After the corrupted bit is shifted out of the register (after $b/s$ shifts), the keystream regenerates correctly. This is why CFB is called *self-synchronizing* — it automatically recovers without external intervention.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CFB Mode — Encryption Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **Encryption** path of CFB mode. Note the causal feedback arrow from the ciphertext output back into the shift register input.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    regA["Shift Register C(i-1)"] -- E_K encryption --> encA["E_K Register b bits"]
    encA -- select top s bits --> ksA["Keystream Segment O(i)"]
    ksA -- XOR --> xorA["XOR operation"]
    pA["Plaintext Segment P(i)"] -- XOR --> xorA
    xorA -- output --> cA["Ciphertext Segment C(i)"]
    cA -- feedback shift --> regA
    ivA["IV equals C0"] -. initial load .-> regA
```

## 4.2 CFB Mode — Decryption Block Architecture

The decryption diagram highlights the **critical** use of the forward cipher $E_K$ (NOT the inverse $D_K$).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    regB["Shift Register C(i-1)"] -- E_K encryption --> encB["E_K Register b bits"]
    encB -- select top s bits --> ksB["Keystream Segment O(i)"]
    ksB -- XOR --> xorB["XOR operation"]
    cB["Received Ciphertext C(i)"] -- XOR --> xorB
    xorB -- output --> pB["Recovered Plaintext P(i)"]
    cB -- feedback shift --> regB
    ivB["IV equals C0"] -. initial load .-> regB
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow — End-to-End CFB Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["Start: Load IV into Register as C0"]
    block{"i = 1, 2, 3, ..., n"}
    encrypt["Compute O(i) = MSB_s of E_K applied to C(i-1)"]
    xorOp["Compute C(i) = P(i) XOR O(i)"]
    output["Transmit or Store C(i)"]
    shift["Shift Register: Append C(i), Drop oldest s bits"]
    endLoop{"All blocks processed?"}
    finish["End: Output Ciphertext C1 to Cn"]

    start --> block
    block --> encrypt
    encrypt --> xorOp
    xorOp --> output
    output --> shift
    shift --> endLoop
    endLoop -- No --> block
    endLoop -- Yes --> finish
```

## 4.4 Comparative Architecture Snapshot (CFB vs CBC)

| Feature | CFB Mode | CBC Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback input to $E_K$ | Previous ciphertext $C_{i-1}$ | Previous ciphertext $C_{i-1}$ (XORed with $P_i$) |
| Block cipher input | Pure $C_{i-1}$ (no plaintext XOR) | $P_i \oplus C_{i-1}$ |
| Padding | Not required | Required |
| Decryption uses | Forward $E_K$ | Inverse $D_K$ |
| Decryption parallelizable | Yes | Yes |
| Encryption parallelizable | No | No |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define Cipher Feedback (CFB) Mode. Why is it called a stream cipher mode even though it uses a block cipher as its primitive?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Cipher Feedback (CFB) Mode is a mode of operation that converts a block cipher into a **self-synchronizing stream cipher** by generating a keystream from the block cipher and XORing it with the plaintext in segments of $s$ bits. It is called a stream cipher mode because:
1. The encryption is performed bit-by-bit (or segment-by-segment) using XOR with a pseudo-random keystream — the defining property of stream ciphers.
2. It does not require padding, unlike block-cipher modes like ECB and CBC.
3. The keystream is generated dynamically and depends on the previous ciphertext, mimicking the state-based evolution of classical stream ciphers.

*(Award 1 mark each for the definition, the block-to-stream conversion reasoning, and the no-padding justification.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Q: In CFB mode, why is the *forward* encryption function $E_K$ used during decryption instead of the decryption function $D_K$?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

In CFB mode, the block cipher $E_K$ is used purely as a **pseudo-random keystream generator**, not as a direct encryption function. The actual encryption of plaintext is performed by the XOR operation. Therefore:

1. The keystream at the receiver must be **identical** to that at the sender, which requires applying the *same* function $E_K$ to the *same* input ($C_{i-1}$).
2. The inverse function $D_K$ would produce a completely different output that has no relationship with the keystream and would yield meaningless plaintext after XORing.
3. Mathematically, decryption in CFB is: $P_i = C_i \oplus \text{MSB}_s(E_K(C_{i-1}))$. Since XOR is its own inverse, recovering $P_i$ from $C_i$ only requires the regenerated keystream — $D_K$ is never invoked.

*(Award 1 mark for stating the keystream regeneration requirement, 1 mark for the XOR self-inverse property, and 1 mark for the concluding argument.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**(a)** Describe the encryption and decryption processes of **CFB Mode** with a neat block diagram. Explain why CFB mode does not require padding. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare CFB mode with **CBC mode** in terms of: (i) block cipher function used during decryption, (ii) padding requirement, (iii) error propagation behavior, and (iv) parallelizability of decryption. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Encryption Process:**

1. The shift register is initialized with the **Initialization Vector (IV)**, denoted as $C_0$.
2. The contents of the register are encrypted with the block cipher $E_K$, yielding a $b$-bit block.
3. The **leftmost $s$ bits** of this encrypted block are extracted to form the keystream segment $O_i$.
4. The keystream $O_i$ is XORed with the plaintext segment $P_i$ to produce the ciphertext segment $C_i$.
5. The register is shifted left by $s$ bits, and $C_i$ is appended at the right (LSB) position.
6. Steps 2–5 repeat for every plaintext segment.

Mathematically:
$$
C_i = P_i \oplus \text{MSB}_s \big( E_K(C_{i-1}) \big)
$$

**Decryption Process:**

1. The receiver initializes the register with the same IV.
2. It applies $E_K$ to the register, extracts the leftmost $s$ bits, and XORs them with the received ciphertext $C_i$ to recover $P_i$.
3. The register is updated with $C_i$ and the process repeats.

Mathematically:
$$
P_i = C_i \oplus \text{MSB}_s \big( E_K(C_{i-1}) \big)
$$

**Why No Padding is Required:**

CFB mode operates on $s$-bit segments rather than full $b$-bit blocks. The plaintext can be processed in any multiple of $s$ bits, and any leftover $s$-bit segment is processed as-is in the final iteration. Hence **padding to a full block boundary is unnecessary**, unlike ECB and CBC.

*(Award 2 marks for encryption steps, 2 marks for decryption steps, 1 mark for the mathematical equations, and 2 marks for the no-padding explanation.)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

| Comparison Criterion | CFB Mode | CBC Mode |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Function used in decryption | Forward cipher $E_K$ | Inverse cipher $D_K$ |
| (ii) Padding requirement | Not required | Required |
| (iii) Error propagation | 1-bit error in $C_i$ → 1-bit error in $P_i$ + garbage in next $\lceil b/s \rceil$ blocks | 1-bit error in $C_i$ → 1-bit error in $P_i$ + garbage in next block + bit flips in $P_{i+1}$ |
| (iv) Decryption parallelizability | Yes (depends only on $C_{i-1}$) | Yes (depends only on $C_{i-1}$) |

*Additionally:*
- Both modes use IV.
- Both modes require sequential encryption.
- CFB is a stream-cipher emulation; CBC is a block-cipher mode with diffusion.

*(Award 1.5 marks per criterion, plus 1 mark for any additional valid comparative insight.)*

---

### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**(a)** With reference to **CFB-8 mode**, perform encryption on the plaintext $\texttt{CAT}$ (ASCII: $C=43$, $A=41$, $T=54$ in hex) using a hypothetical block cipher $E_K$ with block size $b=8$ bits, IV = $\texttt{AF}$ (hex), and the following assumed $E_K$ outputs: $E_K(\texttt{AF}) = \texttt{3C}$, $E_K(C_1) = \texttt{E7}$, $E_K(C_2) = \texttt{91}$. Show all steps. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **self-synchronization** property of CFB mode. If a single bit error occurs in $C_2$, describe the impact on the recovered plaintext blocks. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $P_1 = \texttt{43}$, $P_2 = \texttt{41}$, $P_3 = \texttt{54}$ (hex)
- $C_0 = \text{IV} = \texttt{AF}$
- $s = 8$ (CFB-8 means full-byte segments with $b=8$)
- $E_K(\texttt{AF}) = \texttt{3C}$
- $E_K(C_1) = \texttt{E7}$
- $E_K(C_2) = \texttt{91}$

**Step 1: Encrypt $P_1$**

The keystream is the full 8-bit output of $E_K(C_0)$ since $s = b = 8$.

$$
