O_1 = E_K(C_0) = E_K(\texttt{AF}) = \texttt{3C}
$$

$$
C_1 = P_1 \oplus O_1 = \texttt{43} \oplus \texttt{3C}
$$

Performing the XOR bitwise (hex: $4 \oplus 3 = 7$, $3 \oplus C = F$):

$$
C_1 = \texttt{7F}
$$

**Step 2: Encrypt $P_2$**

$$
O_2 = E_K(C_1) = E_K(\texttt{7F})
$$

But the problem provides $E_K(C_1) = \texttt{E7}$ directly. So:

$$
O_2 = \texttt{E7}
$$

$$
C_2 = P_2 \oplus O_2 = \texttt{41} \oplus \texttt{E7}
$$

Performing the XOR: $4 \oplus E = A$, $1 \oplus 7 = 6$:

$$
C_2 = \texttt{A6}
$$

**Step 3: Encrypt $P_3$**

$$
O_3 = E_K(C_2) = E_K(\texttt{A6})
$$

The problem provides $E_K(C_2) = \texttt{91}$:

$$
O_3 = \texttt{91}
$$

$$
C_3 = P_3 \oplus O_3 = \texttt{54} \oplus \texttt{91}
$$

Performing the XOR: $5 \oplus 9 = C$, $4 \oplus 1 = 5$:

$$
C_3 = \texttt{C5}
$$

**Final Ciphertext:** $C = C_1 C_2 C_3 = \texttt{7FA6C5}$ (hex).

*(Award 2 marks for setting up the equations, 3 marks for correct XOR computation, 1 mark for the keystream regeneration using feedback, and 1 mark for the final ciphertext output.)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Self-Synchronization Definition (2 Marks):**

CFB mode is called *self-synchronizing* (or *ciphertext autokey*) because after a transmission error, the receiver automatically re-establishes correct decryption after a bounded number of blocks — **without requiring explicit resynchronization signaling** from the sender.

**Mechanism (2 Marks):**

- When a bit error occurs in ciphertext block $C_i$, the corrupted bit enters the shift register.
- For the next $\lceil b/s \rceil$ shifts, the corrupted bit participates in keystream generation, producing random-looking (garbage) keystream segments.
- Once the corrupted bit is shifted out of the register (i.e., after $b/s$ iterations), the register contains only correctly received ciphertext, and the keystream regenerates correctly.
- The receiver then recovers the correct plaintext from that point onward.

**Impact of a 1-Bit Error in $C_2$ (3 Marks):**

Assume $C_2$ has a bit-flip at position $j$ (i.e., $C_2' = C_2 \oplus e_j$).

1. **Effect on $P_2$:** During decryption of block 2, the corrupted $C_2'$ is XORed with the keystream $O_2$ (which was correctly generated from $C_1$). The recovered plaintext is $P_2' = C_2' \oplus O_2 = P_2 \oplus e_j$. **Exactly one bit of $P_2$ is flipped.**

2. **Effect on $P_3$:** The corrupted $C_2'$ is now in the shift register. The keystream for block 3, $O_3' = E_K(C_2')$, is completely different from the intended $O_3$. The recovered $P_3' = C_3 \oplus O_3'$ is essentially **random garbage** across all $s$ bits.

3. **Effect on subsequent blocks:** The corrupted bit propagates through the register for $\lceil b/s \rceil$ blocks. For CFB-8 with $b=8$ and $s=8$, this is exactly **1 block** (block 3). For CFB-1 with $b=128$, this would be **128 subsequent blocks** of garbage.

4. **Resynchronization:** After the corrupted bit is shifted out, decryption becomes correct again from block 4 onwards.

*(Award 2 marks for self-synchronization concept, 2 marks for mechanism explanation, and 3 marks for the structured impact analysis on $P_2$, $P_3$, and subsequent blocks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Using $D_K$ in decryption:** Students often mistakenly apply the inverse block cipher during CFB decryption. This is wrong and will cost you 2–3 marks immediately. Always remember: CFB uses the **forward cipher $E_K$** in BOTH encryption and decryption.
>
> 2. **Confusing CFB with OFB:** OFB (Output Feedback) feeds the *output of the block cipher* back into the register, while CFB feeds the *ciphertext* back. They are NOT the same! A common KTU question tests this distinction.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to label $C_0 = \text{IV}$:** Examiners explicitly look for the initialization step. If you write $C_i = P_i \oplus E_K(C_{i-1})$ without specifying $C_0 = \text{IV}$, you may lose 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Wrong error propagation count:** For CFB-$s$ with block size $b$, error propagation is $\lceil b/s \rceil + 1$ blocks (including the originally corrupted one). Students often forget the "+1" for the originally corrupted block.
>
> 5. **Padding confusion:** Some students write "CFB does not require padding" without explaining *why* (it processes $s$-bit segments, not full $b$-bit blocks). Always justify.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for CFB Mode:**

- **Definition:** CFB is a self-synchronizing stream cipher mode built from a block cipher; it processes data in $s$-bit segments.
- **Encryption Equation:** $C_i = P_i \oplus \text{MSB}_s(E_K(C_{i-1}))$, with $C_0 = \text{IV}$.
- **Decryption Equation:** $P_i = C_i \oplus \text{MSB}_s(E_K(C_{i-1}))$ — uses **forward $E_K$**, not $D_K$.
- **Padding:** **Not required** — works on segments of any size $s$.
- **IV:** Must be **fresh and unpredictable** for every message; non-secret but integrity-protected.
- **Encryption:** Sequential (NOT parallelizable) — depends on previous ciphertext.
- **Decryption:** Parallelizable — depends only on $C_{i-1}$, not on any $P_j$.
- **Error Propagation:** 1-bit error in $C_i$ → 1-bit flip in $P_i$ + garbage in the next $\lceil b/s \rceil$ blocks.
- **Self-Synchronization:** After corrupted bits are shifted out of the register, decryption automatically recovers.
- **Common Variants:** CFB-1, CFB-8, CFB-64, CFB-128.
- **Distinguish from OFB:** CFB feedback is **ciphertext-based**; OFB feedback is **keystream (output)-based**.
- **Decryption uses the SAME block cipher $E_K$** — this is the single most-tested CFB fact in KTU exams.
- **Real-world use:** Legacy TLS, character-oriented streams, applications needing self-recovery from bit errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Output Feedback Mode (OFB)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT501 - FOUNDATIONS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Output Feedback Mode (OFB)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Output Feedback (OFB) Mode** is a mode of operation for a symmetric block cipher that transforms the block cipher into a **synchronous stream cipher**. In OFB mode, the continuous feedback of the block cipher's *output* (rather than the ciphertext) is used to generate a **keystream**, which is then XORed with the plaintext blocks to produce the ciphertext.

Formally, given a block cipher $E_K(\cdot)$ with block size $b$ bits, the OFB mode is defined by the following recurrence relations:

$$O_0 = IV$$

$$O_i = E_K(O_{i-1}) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, n$$

$$C_i = P_i \oplus O_i \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, n$$

where $IV$ is the Initialization Vector, $O_i$ represents the **output block** (keystream block) at iteration $i$, and $P_i, C_i$ are the plaintext and ciphertext blocks respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** OFB is classified as a *stream-cipher-like* mode because the keystream $O_i$ depends **only on the key and the IV**, and is *independent* of the plaintext and ciphertext. This makes OFB a **synchronous stream cipher**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Infinite Number Generator":**
> Imagine you have a magical dice machine (the block cipher $E_K$) that, when fed a number, always produces a new pseudo-random number. You drop in an initial seed ($IV$), and the machine spits out a pseudo-random number. That random number becomes the *next* input to the machine, generating another random number, and so on. The sequence of random numbers forms your **keystream**. To encrypt, you simply mix (XOR) each keystream number with one chunk of your message. Since the keystream was generated *before* you even touched the message, encryption and decryption are identical operations.

### 1.3 Key Characteristics (Bullet-Form Overview)

- **Block-to-Stream Conversion:** Converts any block cipher (e.g., AES, DES) into a stream cipher.
- **No Plaintext Dependency:** The keystream is generated *independently* of plaintext/ciphertext.
- **Pre-Computable Keystream:** Since keystream depends only on $K$ and $IV$, it can be generated offline before plaintext is available.
- **No Padding Required:** Works on arbitrary plaintext lengths without padding (like a true stream cipher).
- **Synchronous Mode:** Sender and receiver must remain synchronized; a lost bit breaks the stream.
- **Error Propagation (Bit-Level):** A single bit error in ciphertext affects **only that bit** in the decrypted plaintext (no error expansion).
- **Standard:** Defined in **NIST SP 800-38A** (Recommendation for Block Cipher Modes of Operation).

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Comparison Hook:** OFB is structurally similar to **CFB (Cipher Feedback)**, but instead of feeding back the *ciphertext* $C_i$, OFB feeds back the *encryption output* $O_i$. This single change is the crux of the entire topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The OFB encryption process follows a strict iterative logic:

1. **Initialization:** A unique, non-secret **Initialization Vector (IV)** $O_0$ of block size $b$ bits is selected. For security, IV must be a **nonce** (Number used Once) — never reused with the same key.
2. **Keystream Generation:** The block cipher encrypts the previous output block:
   $$O_1 = E_K(O_0), \quad O_2 = E_K(O_1), \quad \ldots, \quad O_n = E_K(O_{n-1})$$
3. **XOR Encryption:** Each plaintext block is XORed with the corresponding keystream block:
   $$C_i = P_i \oplus O_i$$
4. **No Feedback of Ciphertext:** Crucially, $C_i$ is **NOT** fed back. Only the encryption output $O_i$ is fed back, breaking the dependency on plaintext.

For **decryption**, the *same* keystream must be regenerated (using $K$ and $IV$), and XORed with the ciphertext:

$$P_i = C_i \oplus O_i$$

Since $C_i = P_i \oplus O_i$, XORing both sides with $O_i$ recovers $P_i$.

### 2.2 Why Does OFB Behave Like a Stream Cipher?

A one-time pad uses a true random keystream of length equal to the message. OFB approximates this:

- The block cipher acts as a **pseudo-random number generator (PRNG)**.
- $O_i$ are the pseudo-random bits.
- The XOR operation is the encryption primitive.
- Since the keystream is **deterministic** given $(K, IV)$ and **independent of the message**, OFB is mathematically a **synchronous stream cipher**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Design:** By feeding back $O_i$ instead of $C_i$, we ensure that *errors in transmission* (flipped bits in $C_i$) do not corrupt the keystream. This is why OFB has *no error propagation* beyond the directly affected bit — a key advantage in **noisy channels** (e.g., satellite, voice communication).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Term** | **Meaning** | **Mathematical Form** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $E_K(\cdot)$ | Block cipher encryption with key $K$ | Maps $b$-bit input $\to b$-bit output | e.g., AES-128: $b = 128$ |
| $O_0$ | Initialization Vector (IV) / Initial seed | $b$ bits | Must be **unique per (K, message)** |
| $O_i$ | Output block (keystream) at step $i$ | $E_K(O_{i-1})$ | Independent of plaintext |
| $P_i$ | Plaintext block $i$ | $b$ bits | Padded if necessary for last block |
| $C_i$ | Ciphertext block $i$ | $P_i \oplus O_i$ | XOR with keystream |
| $b$ | Block size of underlying cipher | $\mathbf{64}$ (DES) or $\mathbf{128}$ (AES) bits | Standard metric |
| $n$ | Number of blocks | $\lceil L/b \rceil$ | $L$ = message length in bits |
| Decryption | Recovers plaintext | $P_i = C_i \oplus O_i$ | **Same operation** as encryption |
| Error propagation | Bit error in $C_i$ | Affects only corresponding bit in $P_i$ | **No error expansion** |
| Synchronization | Lost bit/IV mismatch | Entire stream desynchronizes | Requires reliable channel |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** In OFB, $\vert$ absolute value $\vert$ should never appear inside table syntax. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to prevent markdown table breaking.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Satellite & Wireless Communications:** Low error propagation makes OFB ideal for noisy environments.
- **Voice over IP (VoIP):** Stream-cipher property allows real-time encryption of variable-length voice packets.
- **Disk/File Encryption:** Pre-computable keystream enables faster bulk encryption when storage is ready before data arrives.
- **Deprecated Status:** OFB is **deprecated** for new cryptographic systems due to **keystream reuse vulnerabilities**; modern systems prefer **CTR (Counter) mode**, which offers the same advantages with better security proofs and parallelizability.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Keystream Recurrence

We begin with the foundational recurrence and derive the closed-form expression for the keystream $O_i$ in terms of the IV and the block cipher.

**Starting Definition (Recurrence):**

$$O_0 = IV$$

$$O_i = E_K(O_{i-1}) \quad \text{for } i \geq 1$$

**Step 1 — Unroll the first iteration:**

$$O_1 = E_K(O_0) = E_K(IV)$$

This is the block cipher applied once to the IV using key $K$.

**Step 2 — Unroll the second iteration:**

$$O_2 = E_K(O_1) = E_K(E_K(O_0)) = E_K^{(2)}(IV)$$

where $E_K^{(2)}(\cdot)$ denotes applying $E_K$ twice in succession. We can use the shorthand:

$$E_K^{(2)}(x) = E_K(E_K(x))$$

**Step 3 — Generalize to $n$ iterations (by mathematical induction):**

For the $i$-th output block, applying the recurrence $i$ times:

$$O_i = E_K(O_{i-1}) = E_K(E_K(O_{i-2})) = \ldots = E_K^{(i)}(IV)$$

So the closed form is:

$$O_i = E_K^{(i)}(IV)$$

**Step 4 — Derive the ciphertext from plaintext:**

By definition of XOR encryption:

$$C_i = P_i \oplus O_i = P_i \oplus E_K^{(i)}(IV)$$

**Step 5 — Verify decryption:**

Given $C_i$, regenerate the keystream (using shared $K$ and $IV$):

$$P_i' = C_i \oplus O_i = (P_i \oplus O_i) \oplus O_i$$

By the XOR identity $A \oplus A = 0$:

$$P_i' = P_i \oplus (O_i \oplus O_i) = P_i \oplus 0 = P_i$$

This confirms decryption recovers the original plaintext. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Error Propagation Analysis

**Assumption:** A single bit error occurs in ciphertext block $C_j$ (i.e., one bit is flipped during transmission). Let $C_j'$ denote the corrupted ciphertext.

**Step 1 — Corrupted block decryption:**

At the receiver, the keystream $O_j$ is regenerated (correctly, since keystream doesn't depend on $C_j$):

$$P_j' = C_j' \oplus O_j$$

**Step 2 — Effect of the bit flip:**

If $C_j' = C_j \oplus e_k$ where $e_k$ is an error vector with a single '1' bit, then:

$$P_j' = (C_j \oplus e_k) \oplus O_j = (C_j \oplus O_j) \oplus e_k = P_j \oplus e_k$$

**Step 3 — Propagation to subsequent blocks:**

The keystream $O_{j+1} = E_K(O_j)$ depends only on the previous output block $O_j$, which was correctly regenerated at the receiver. Therefore:

$$O_{j+1}, O_{j+2}, \ldots \text{ are unaffected.}$$

**Step 4 — Result:**

A single bit error in $C_j$ causes **exactly one bit error** in $P_j$. **No error propagation.** $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Numerical Example (Illustrative)

Let $b = 4$ bits, $IV = O_0 = 1010$, and suppose $E_K(1010) = 0110$, $E_K(0110) = 1101$, $E_K(1101) = 0001$. Suppose plaintext $P_1 = 1100$, $P_2 = 1011$, $P_3 = 0101$.

| **Step** | **Computation** | **Result** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $O_0 = IV$ | $1010$ |
| 2 | $O_1 = E_K(O_0)$ | $0110$ |
| 3 | $C_1 = P_1 \oplus O_1 = 1100 \oplus 0110$ | $1010$ |
| 4 | $O_2 = E_K(O_1)$ | $1101$ |
| 5 | $C_2 = P_2 \oplus O_2 = 1011 \oplus 1101$ | $0110$ |
| 6 | $O_3 = E_K(O_2)$ | $0001$ |
| 7 | $C_3 = P_3 \oplus O_3 = 0101 \oplus 0001$ | $0100$ |

**Final ciphertext:** $C = C_1 \vert C_2 \vert C_3 = 1010 \, 0110 \, 0100$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 OFB Encryption — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    ivNode[O sub 0 equals IV]
    enc1Node[Encrypt O sub 0 with key K]
    o1Node[O sub 1 keystream block 1]
    xor1Node[XOR operation]
    p1Node[P sub 1 plaintext block 1]
    c1Node[C sub 1 ciphertext block 1]
    enc2Node[Encrypt O sub 1 with key K]
    o2Node[O sub 2 keystream block 2]
    xor2Node[XOR operation]
    p2Node[P sub 2 plaintext block 2]
    c2Node[C sub 2 ciphertext block 2]
    enc3Node[Encrypt O sub 2 with key K]
    o3Node[O sub 3 keystream block 3]
    xor3Node[XOR operation]
    p3Node[P sub 3 plaintext block 3]
    c3Node[C sub 3 ciphertext block 3]

    ivNode --> enc1Node
    enc1Node --> o1Node
    o1Node --> enc2Node
    o1Node --> xor1Node
    p1Node --> xor1Node
    xor1Node --> c1Node

    enc2Node --> o2Node
    o2Node --> enc3Node
    o2Node --> xor2Node
    p2Node --> xor2Node
    xor2Node --> c2Node

    enc3Node --> o3Node
    o3Node --> xor3Node
    p3Node --> xor3Node
    xor3Node --> c3Node

    style ivNode fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style enc1Node fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style enc2Node fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style enc3Node fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style xor1Node fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style xor2Node fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style xor3Node fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style c1Node fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style c2Node fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style c3Node fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 OFB Decryption — Block-Level Functional Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** The decryption architecture is **identical** to encryption. Only the role of plaintext and ciphertext is swapped at the XOR gate. The keystream regeneration is the same.

```mermaid
graph LR
    ivNode2[O sub 0 equals IV]
    enc1Node2[Encrypt O sub 0 with key K]
    o1Node2[O sub 1 keystream block 1]
    xor1Node2[XOR operation]
    c1Node2[C sub 1 ciphertext block 1]
    p1Node2[P sub 1 recovered plaintext 1]
    enc2Node2[Encrypt O sub 1 with key K]
    o2Node2[O sub 2 keystream block 2]
    xor2Node2[XOR operation]
    c2Node2[C sub 2 ciphertext block 2]
    p2Node2[P sub 2 recovered plaintext 2]
    enc3Node2[Encrypt O sub 2 with key K]
    o3Node2[O sub 3 keystream block 3]
    xor3Node2[XOR operation]
    c3Node2[C sub 3 ciphertext block 3]
    p3Node2[P sub 3 recovered plaintext 3]

    ivNode2 --> enc1Node2
    enc1Node2 --> o1Node2
    o1Node2 --> enc2Node2
    o1Node2 --> xor1Node2
    c1Node2 --> xor1Node2
    xor1Node2 --> p1Node2

    enc2Node2 --> o2Node2
    o2Node2 --> enc3Node2
    o2Node2 --> xor2Node2
    c2Node2 --> xor2Node2
    xor2Node2 --> p2Node2

    enc3Node2 --> o3Node2
    o3Node2 --> xor3Node2
    c3Node2 --> xor3Node2
    xor3Node2 --> p3Node2

    style ivNode2 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style enc1Node2 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style enc2Node2 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style enc3Node2 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style xor1Node2 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style xor2Node2 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style xor3Node2 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style p1Node2 fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style p2Node2 fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style p3Node2 fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Architecture-Level Comparison: OFB vs CFB vs CTR

| **Property** | **OFB** | **CFB** | **CTR** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feedback source | Encryption output $O_i$ | Ciphertext $C_i$ | Counter value $T_i$ |
| Keystream depends on plaintext? | **No** | Yes | **No** |
| Parallelizable (encryption)? | **No** (sequential) | **No** (sequential) | **Yes** |
| Error propagation | 1 bit | Up to $b$ bits (CFB-1: 1 bit; CFB-64: 64 bits) | 1 bit |
| Random access decryption? | **No** | **No** (CFB-1 only: limited) | **Yes** |
| Stream cipher emulation? | **Yes** | Yes (with caveats) | **Yes** |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Pattern:** A common KTU question is to ask students to **compare OFB and CFB** with a diagram. The structural difference (feedback of $O_i$ vs $C_i$) is the most-marked feature.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define Output Feedback (OFB) mode. Why is it classified as a synchronous stream cipher?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 Mark) OFB is a mode of operation that generates a keystream by repeatedly encrypting an Initialization Vector (IV). The keystream is XORed with plaintext to produce ciphertext.
- (1 Mark) It is synchronous because the keystream is generated **independently of plaintext and ciphertext**, depending only on the key $K$ and IV.
- (1 Mark) The keystream can be pre-computed, and OFB emulates a stream cipher, hence classified as a synchronous stream cipher.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*How does OFB mode handle errors during transmission? Compare with CBC mode briefly.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 Mark) In OFB, a single bit error in ciphertext $C_i$ affects **only the corresponding bit** in recovered plaintext $P_i$. There is no error propagation.
- (1 Mark) In CBC, a single bit error in $C_i$ causes the **entire corresponding plaintext block** $P_i$ to be corrupted, plus **one bit** in the next plaintext block $P_{i+1}$.
- (1 Mark) Thus OFB is preferable in noisy channels, whereas CBC provides some self-healing but causes block-level corruption.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, Module 2 contains one 14-mark question with internal choice. Both Question A and Question B are provided below as full alternatives.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the OFB mode of operation with a neat block diagram. Show the encryption and decryption equations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

OFB mode transforms a block cipher into a stream cipher. It uses continuous encryption of an Initialization Vector to generate a keystream, which is XORed with the plaintext.

**Encryption Equations:**

$$O_0 = IV$$

$$O_i = E_K(O_{i-1}), \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

$$C_i = P_i \oplus O_i$$

**Decryption Equations:**

$$O_0 = IV$$

$$O_i = E_K(O_{i-1}), \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

$$P_i = C_i \oplus O_i$$

**Block Diagram (Text Representation):**

$$IV \rightarrow E_K \rightarrow O_1 \rightarrow \text{XOR} \rightarrow C_1$$
$$O_1 \rightarrow E_K \rightarrow O_2 \rightarrow \text{XOR} \rightarrow C_2$$
$$O_2 \rightarrow E_K \rightarrow O_3 \rightarrow \text{XOR} \rightarrow C_3$$

**Mark Distribution (7 Marks):**
- Diagram and explanation: **3 Marks**
- Encryption equations: **2 Marks**
- Decryption equations: **1 Mark**
- Stream cipher property justification: **1 Mark**

---

**(b)** With $IV = O_0 = 1100$, and given $E_K(1100) = 0110$, $E_K(0110) = 1011$, $E_K(1011) = 0001$, encrypt the plaintext $P = 1011 \, 0110 \, 1100$ using OFB mode. Show all steps. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the keystream.

$$O_0 = 1100$$
$$O_1 = E_K(O_0) = E_K(1100) = 0110$$
$$O_2 = E_K(O_1) = E_K(0110) = 1011$$
$$O_3 = E_K(O_2) = E_K(1011) = 0001$$

**Step 2:** Split plaintext into blocks.

$$P_1 = 1011, \quad P_2 = 0110, \quad P_3 = 1100$$

**Step 3:** XOR each plaintext block with the corresponding keystream block.

$$C_1 = P_1 \oplus O_1 = 1011 \oplus 0110 = 1101$$
$$C_2 = P_2 \oplus O_2 = 0110 \oplus 1011 = 1101$$
$$C_3 = P_3 \oplus O_3 = 1100 \oplus 0001 = 1101$$

**Final Ciphertext:** $C = 1101 \, 1101 \, 1101$

**Mark Distribution (7 Marks):**
- Keystream computation: **3 Marks**
- Block splitting: **1 Mark**
- XOR operations: **2 Marks**
- Final ciphertext: **1 Mark**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]`

**(a)** Compare OFB mode with CFB mode under the following heads: (i) Feedback mechanism, (ii) Error propagation, (iii) Stream cipher property, (iv) Parallelizability. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Feedback Mechanism:**
- **OFB:** Feeds back the *encryption output* $O_i = E_K(O_{i-1})$.
- **CFB:** Feeds back the *ciphertext* $C_i = P_i \oplus E_K(C_{i-1})$.

**(ii) Error Propagation:**
- **OFB:** A bit error in $C_i$ affects **only the corresponding bit** in $P_i$. No further propagation.
- **CFB:** A bit error in $C_i$ affects the corresponding bit in $P_i$ **and** propagates into the next plaintext block $P_{i+1}$ via the feedback register.

**(iii) Stream Cipher Property:**
- **OFB:** True stream cipher emulation; keystream is independent of plaintext.
- **CFB:** Stream-cipher-like, but the keystream depends on the ciphertext, which depends on the plaintext — so it is *self-synchronizing*, not purely synchronous.

**(iv) Parallelizability:**
- **OFB:** **Not parallelizable** during encryption, because $O_i$ depends on $O_{i-1}$. However, the keystream can be pre-computed.
- **CFB:** Also **not parallelizable** for the same reason.

**Mark Distribution (7 Marks):**
- Each head carries approximately **1.5–2 Marks** with proper explanation.

---

**(b)** Discuss the security strengths and weaknesses of OFB mode. Why is it deprecated for new applications, and what is its modern replacement? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Strengths:**
1. **Stream Cipher Property:** Allows encryption of variable-length messages without padding.
2. **Pre-computable Keystream:** Can be generated offline before plaintext is available.
3. **No Error Propagation:** Ideal for noisy channels.
4. **No Plaintext Dependency:** Keystream is independent of message content.

**Weaknesses:**
1. **IV Reuse Catastrophe:** If the same $(K, IV)$ pair is used for two different messages, the **same keystream** is generated. XORing two ciphertexts cancels the keystream and reveals $P_1 \oplus P_2$, a classical two-time-pad attack.
2. **Short Cycle Problem:** The keystream sequence $O_0, O_1, O_2, \ldots$ may enter a cycle shorter than the maximum possible $2^b - 1$ due to the structure of $E_K$. If the cycle is short, the keystream repeats and confidentiality is lost.
3. **No Integrity Protection:** OFB provides only confidentiality, not authentication. An attacker can flip ciphertext bits to flip the corresponding plaintext bits (malleability).

**Modern Replacement:**
- **CTR (Counter) Mode** is preferred over OFB because:
  - It allows full **parallelization** of both encryption and decryption.
  - It supports **random access** decryption.
  - It has well-established security proofs.
  - It avoids the short-cycle problem because counters never repeat within a message.

**Mark Distribution (7 Marks):**
- Strengths (any 2 well-explained): **2 Marks**
- Weaknesses (any 2 well-explained): **3 Marks**
- Modern replacement: **2 Marks**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in OFB Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing OFB with CFB:** Many students draw the CFB diagram (with $C_i$ feedback) instead of OFB ($O_i$ feedback). This costs **3–4 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the IV is unencrypted:** Unlike CBC, the IV in OFB is the *initial input* to the block cipher, not XORed with anything. Marking it as $E_K(IV \oplus \text{something})$ is a common error.
> 3. **Missing the "synchronous" justification:** Stating that OFB is a stream cipher *without* explaining the synchronous (plaintext-independent) nature loses the conceptual mark.
> 4. **Claiming OFB provides integrity:** OFB is confidentiality-only. Any answer claiming authentication/integrity is factually wrong.
> 5. **Ignoring IV uniqueness:** Failing to mention that IV must be a **nonce** in OFB loses 1–2 marks on the security part.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — OFB Mode**

- **Definition:** Block cipher mode that emulates a **synchronous stream cipher** by feeding back the encryption output.
- **Keystream Generation:** $O_i = E_K(O_{i-1})$, with $O_0 = IV$.
- **Encryption:** $C_i = P_i \oplus O_i$.
- **Decryption:** $P_i = C_i \oplus O_i$ (same operation; pre-generate keystream).
- **Key Properties:**
  - Keystream **independent** of plaintext.
  - **Pre-computable** keystream.
  - **No padding** required.
  - **No error propagation** (bit-level only).
  - **No parallelization** (sequential).
  - **Synchronous** (requires IV alignment).
- **Critical Security Rules:**
  - IV must be a **nonce** (unique per message under same key).
  - IV reuse ⇒ keystream reuse ⇒ two-time-pad vulnerability.
  - No integrity/authentication provided.
- **Standards:** NIST SP 800-38A.
- **Modern Preference:** Use **CTR mode** instead — parallelizable, random-access, no short-cycle issues.
- **Comparison Hooks:**
  - vs **CFB:** OFB feeds back $O_i$, not $C_i$ → no error propagation.
  - vs **CBC:** OFB has no plaintext chaining → no error propagation, but no avalanche from ciphertext either.
  - vs **CTR:** OFB is sequential; CTR is parallelizable.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Counter Mode (CTR), Stream Ciphers - Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT501 - FOUNDATIONS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Counter Mode (CTR), Stream Ciphers - Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Counter Mode (CTR) & Stream Ciphers — Structure

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Stream Cipher — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Stream Cipher (KTU Syllabus Definition)**
> A symmetric-key cipher in which the plaintext is encrypted one symbol (bit or byte) at a time, by combining each plaintext symbol with a corresponding pseudorandom keystream symbol, typically using the bitwise **XOR** operation. Mathematically: $C_i = P_i \oplus K_i$, where $K_i$ is the $i^{th}$ keystream symbol produced by a deterministic keystream generator driven by a secret key $K$ and (optionally) an Initial Value (IV) / nonce.

### 1.2 Counter (CTR) Mode — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CTR Mode Definition (NIST SP 800-38A aligned)**
> Counter Mode is a block-cipher mode of operation that converts a block cipher into a stream cipher. It encrypts each plaintext block $P_i$ by XOR-ing it with an encrypted counter value $T_i$. The encryption and decryption operations are **identical** (both are XOR with the encrypted counter), which is a defining structural property. Formally:
> $$C_i = P_i \oplus E_K(T_i), \qquad P_i = C_i \oplus E_K(T_i)$$
> where $T_i$ is a deterministic counter value that changes for every block.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Rotating Mailbox Lock"**
> Imagine a high-security mailbox with a numeric combination lock whose digits **rotate automatically** after every delivery. The mail carrier (sender) places a letter (plaintext block $P_1$) into the box and locks it with combination $T_1$ → output is the encrypted block $C_1$. The next delivery uses $T_2 = T_1 + 1$ (a *new*, predictable-but-unique counter value), and so on. The receiver knows the *initial* counter $T_1$ and the secret key (used to "run" the combination mechanism), so they can recompute the same sequence of combinations in reverse to unlock every letter. Because the counter increments deterministically, the receiver can also **skip ahead** to retrieve a specific message (random access) — just like jumping to mailbox number 47 without opening numbers 1 through 46.

This analogy captures the three structural pillars of CTR: (1) deterministic counter evolution, (2) identical encrypt/decrypt operations, and (3) random access.

### 1.4 Key Terminology (Bold-Defined for Exam Recall)

- **Keystream (KS):** The pseudorandom sequence of bits/bytes $\{K_1, K_2, \ldots, K_n\}$ produced by the keystream generator.
- **Initial Value (IV) / Nonce:** A non-secret, often unique value that initializes the keystream generator to ensure distinct keystreams across sessions.
- **Counter (T):** A monotonically changing value used as input to the block cipher in CTR mode.
- **Block Cipher (E_K):** The underlying symmetric primitive (e.g., AES) used to encrypt counter values.
- **Period (p):** The length of the keystream before it repeats; a fundamental security parameter (typically $\geq 2^{128}$).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact**
> A stream cipher does **not** preserve block structure in the ciphertext. The output is a pseudo-stream that looks random, of the **same length** as the plaintext. This is a key structural difference from CBC/CFB/OFB.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stream Cipher — Generic Architecture

A stream cipher is composed of **three functional blocks**:

1. **State Update Function (φ):** Maintains an internal state $S_i$ that evolves with every clock tick.
2. **Keystream Output Function (ψ):** Extracts one symbol $K_i$ of the keystream from the current state $S_i$.
3. **Combination Function (ξ):** Combines keystream symbol $K_i$ with plaintext symbol $P_i$ (almost always XOR) to produce ciphertext symbol $C_i$.

The recursive relationships governing any stream cipher are:

$$S_{i+1} = \phi(S_i, K, IV)$$
$$K_i = \psi(S_i, K, IV)$$
$$C_i = \xi(P_i, K_i) = P_i \oplus K_i$$

### 2.2 Classification of Stream Ciphers

Stream ciphers are broadly classified by the dependency of $K_i$ on previous plaintext/ciphertext symbols:

- **Synchronous Stream Cipher:** Keystream depends only on the key $K$ and the IV — it is generated **independently** of the plaintext/ciphertext. If even one ciphertext symbol is lost (bit-flip / drop), sender and receiver must **resynchronize**. CTR is synchronous.
- **Self-Synchronizing (Asynchronous) Stream Cipher:** Keystream is a function of the key and a fixed number $n$ of previous ciphertext symbols. A lost symbol causes at most $n$ corrupted symbols, after which resynchronization is automatic. CFB is an example.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Must-Know Distinction**
> Synchronous ciphers: NO error propagation. Asynchronous ciphers: LIMITED error propagation ($n$ symbols). This is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

### 2.3 Desired Properties of a Good Keystream

- **Long Period** — keystream must not repeat within any practical message length.
- **Pseudorandom** — passes standard statistical randomness tests (Golomb's postulates).
- **Large Linear Complexity** — resists Berlekamp-Massey cryptanalysis.
- **Statistical Uniformity** — balanced 0s and 1s (~50% each).
- **Key/IV Sensitivity** — flipping one bit of the key/IV must change ~50% of the keystream (avalanche).
- **Efficient Implementation** — fast in software and/or hardware.

### 2.4 Counter Mode (CTR) — Operational Rules

CTR mode has **four mandatory structural rules** (high-yield for KTU):

1. The counter $T_i$ must be **unique** for every plaintext block within the lifetime of the key. Standard practice: $T_i = \text{Nonce} \parallel \text{counter}_i$ where the counter part is an integer incremented by 1 (or some other simple rule) per block.
2. The block cipher input is **never reused** with the same key — otherwise the keystream repeats and the cipher collapses to a one-time-pad-with-repeating-key (a catastrophic break).
3. The counter value is **public** (transmitted in plaintext in the message header), but the key $K$ is secret. Security rests entirely on the secrecy of $K$.
4. There is **no padding** requirement — the final plaintext block can be a partial block; the keystream is truncated to the same length.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `\vert`-pipe-in-table to satisfy the markdown-table syntax rule of the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE. All absolute-value / XOR / bit-length notations are encoded accordingly.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables / Units | Exam Frequency |
|---|---------|----------------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Stream Cipher Encryption | $C_i = P_i \oplus K_i$ | $C_i, P_i, K_i \in \{0,1\}^b$ | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | Stream Cipher Decryption | $P_i = C_i \oplus K_i$ | bitwise XOR | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | CTR Encryption | $C_i = P_i \oplus E_K(T_i)$ | $T_i$ = counter value | ★★★★★ |
| 4 | CTR Decryption | $P_i = C_i \oplus E_K(T_i)$ | identical to encryption | ★★★★★ |
| 5 | Counter Update | $T_{i+1} = T_i + 1 \pmod{2^{32}}$ | modular increment | ★★★★ |
| 6 | One-Time-Pad Security | $P(C) = P(P)$ (perfect secrecy) | only if $K$ is truly random, $\vert K \vert \geq \vert P \vert$ | ★★★★ |
| 7 | Keystream Period Bound | $p \geq 2^{L}$ for $L$-bit security | $L$ = security parameter in bits | ★★★ |
| 8 | Avalanche Effect | $\geq 50\%$ bits of KS flip on 1-bit key/IV change | percentage | ★★★ |
| 9 | Output length | $\vert C \vert = \vert P \vert$ (no expansion) | bits | ★★★ |
| 10 | Random Access | $P_i$ recoverable without decrypting $P_{i-1}$ | $i \in \{1, \ldots, n\}$ | ★★★★ |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

CTR mode is the **default mode of operation in modern protocols** because of its structural advantages:
- **TLS 1.3** uses AES-GCM, which is built on CTR with an authenticator (Galois field authentication tag).
- **IPsec ESP** uses CTR for confidentiality.
- **Disk encryption** (e.g., XTS-AES modes) is closely related to CTR.
- **High-speed network encryption:** CTR is fully parallelizable — both encryption and decryption can be computed simultaneously across all blocks on multi-core hardware, unlike CBC which has a serial dependency.

### 2.7 Why XOR Is the Universal Combining Function

XOR is used because:
1. It is its own inverse: $A \oplus A = 0$ and $A \oplus 0 = A$ — so a single operation encrypts and decrypts.
2. It is symmetric: $A \oplus B = B \oplus A$.
3. It is bitwise independent: a single-bit error in the ciphertext flips only the corresponding plaintext bit (no error propagation in synchronous mode).
4. It is implementable in one CPU clock cycle on any architecture.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Full CTR Encryption/Decryption Walk-Through

> **Working Parameters (chosen for clarity; real systems use 128-bit blocks):**
> - Block size $b = 8$ bits
> - Key $K$ (secret, hypothetical)
> - Initial counter $T_1 = 0000\,0001_2 = 1_{10}$
> - Plaintext $P = P_1 \parallel P_2 \parallel P_3 = 0110\,1001 \parallel 1010\,0110 \parallel 1101\,0101$
> - Hypothetical block cipher $E_K(\cdot)$ given below

| Block $i$ | Counter $T_i$ (binary) | $T_i$ (decimal) | $E_K(T_i)$ (output of block cipher) | Plaintext $P_i$ | XOR Operation | Ciphertext $C_i$ |
|:---------:|:-----------------------:|:----------------:|:-----------------------------------:|:----------------:|:-------------:|:----------------:|
| 1 | 0000 0001 | 1 | 1011 1000 | 0110 1001 | $0110\,1001 \oplus 1011\,1000$ | **1101 0001** |
| 2 | 0000 0010 | 2 | 0110 1100 | 1010 0110 | $1010\,0110 \oplus 0110\,1100$ | **1100 1010** |
| 3 | 0000 0011 | 3 | 1001 0111 | 1101 0101 | $1101\,0101 \oplus 1001\,0111$ | **0100 0010** |

**Final Ciphertext:**
$$C = 1101\,0001 \parallel 1100\,1010 \parallel 0100\,0010$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation — Block 1

**Step 1 — Initialize counter.** The encryption process begins with a known public counter value $T_1$, transmitted in the message header.

$$T_1 = 0000\,0001_2 = 1_{10}$$

**Step 2 — Encrypt the counter under the secret key.** The block cipher takes the counter as plaintext and the secret key $K$, producing a pseudorandom output block.

$$O_1 = E_K(T_1) = E_K(0000\,0001) = 1011\,1000$$

**Step 3 — XOR the encrypted counter with the first plaintext block.**

$$C_1 = P_1 \oplus O_1$$

We now expand the XOR bit-by-bit to demonstrate correctness (bit positions 7 to 0, MSB first):

$$\begin{aligned}
C_1[7] &= P_1[7] \oplus O_1[7] = 0 \oplus 1 = 1 \\
C_1[6] &= P_1[6] \oplus O_1[6] = 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
C_1[5] &= P_1[5] \oplus O_1[5] = 1 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
C_1[4] &= P_1[4] \oplus O_1[4] = 0 \oplus 1 = 1 \\
C_1[3] &= P_1[3] \oplus O_1[3] = 1 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
C_1[2] &= P_1[2] \oplus O_1[2] = 0 \oplus 0 = 0 \\
C_1[1] &= P_1[1] \oplus O_1[1] = 0 \oplus 0 = 0 \\
C_1[0] &= P_1[0] \oplus O_1[0] = 1 \oplus 0 = 1
\end{aligned}$$

Concatenating the bits: $C_1 = 1101\,0001$.

**Step 4 — Increment the counter.** Before processing the next block, the counter is incremented by 1 (modulo $2^8 = 256$ in this 8-bit example; in real AES-CTR it is modulo $2^{32}$ or $2^{64}$).

$$T_2 = T_1 + 1 = 0000\,0001 + 0000\,0001 = 0000\,0010_2 = 2_{10}$$

**Step 5 — Repeat for blocks 2 and 3** (see table above for compact results).

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Decryption of Block 1

Decryption in CTR is **structurally identical** to encryption — the receiver also runs the counter through the block cipher. This is the most beautiful property of CTR.

**Step 1 — Receive $C_1 = 1101\,0001$ and $T_1 = 0000\,0001$.**

**Step 2 — Re-encrypt the counter** (with the same shared key $K$).

$$O_1' = E_K(T_1) = 1011\,1000$$

**Step 3 — XOR with the ciphertext.**

$$P_1 = C_1 \oplus O_1'$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1[7] &= 1 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
P_1[6] &= 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \\
P_1[5] &= 0 \oplus 1 = 1 \\
P_1[4] &= 1 \oplus 1 = 0 \\
P_1[3] &= 0 \oplus 1 = 1 \\
P_1[2] &= 0 \oplus 0 = 0 \\
P_1[1] &= 0 \oplus 0 = 0 \\
P_1[0] &= 1 \oplus 0 = 1
\end{aligned}$$

Concatenating: $P_1 = 0110\,1001$. ✓ **Plaintext recovered exactly.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Algebraic Proof of Correctness (for exam):**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> D(C_i) &= C_i \oplus E_K(T_i) \\
>        &= (P_i \oplus E_K(T_i)) \oplus E_K(T_i) \\
>        &= P_i \oplus (E_K(T_i) \oplus E_K(T_i)) \\
>        &= P_i \oplus 0 \\
>        &= P_i
> \end{aligned}$$
> This algebraic identity holds for **every** block, proving CTR is correct as long as counter inputs never repeat.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — CTR vs Other Block Cipher Modes

| Property | ECB | CBC | CFB | OFB | **CTR** |
|----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|---------|
| Confidentiality | Weak (patterns leak) | Strong | Strong | Strong | **Strong** |
| Parallelizable (Encryption) | Yes | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| Parallelizable (Decryption) | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | **Yes** |
| Random Access | Yes | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| Padding Required | Yes | Yes | No | No | **No** |
| Error Propagation (1 bit flip in $C$) | 1 block | 2 blocks | $b+1$ symbols | 1 symbol | **1 symbol** |
| Ciphertext Expansion | None | None | None | None | **None** |
| IV/Nonce Reuse Catastrophic? | N/A | Yes (full break) | Yes | **Yes (OTP break)** | **Yes (OTP break)** |

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Synchronous vs Asynchronous Stream Ciphers

| Property | Synchronous (CTR, OFB) | Asynchronous (CFB) |
|----------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Keystream depends on $P$ or $C$? | **No** | **Yes (on past $C$)** |
| Resynchronization after loss? | **Manual (full re-init)** | **Automatic (after $n$ symbols)** |
| Error propagation | **None (only 1 bit flipped)** | **Bounded by feedback depth $n$** |
| Susceptible to insertion attacks? | **Yes** (must use authentication) | **Less so** (self-sync) |
| Examples | CTR, OFB, RC4, A5/1 | CFB, Cipher Feedback variants |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Stream Cipher Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Secret Key K"] --> B["Keystream Generator"]
    C["Initial Value IV"] --> B
    B --> D["Keystream Symbol K_i"]
    E["Plaintext Symbol P_i"] --> F["XOR Combiner"]
    D --> F
    F --> G["Ciphertext Symbol C_i"]
    H["State Update phi"] -.-> B
    B -.-> H
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The keystream generator is a stateful finite-state machine seeded by the secret key $K$ and the public IV. Every clock tick, the output function $\psi$ extracts one keystream symbol $K_i$, which is XOR-ed with the plaintext symbol $P_i$ to produce $C_i$. The state update $\phi$ keeps the generator evolving.

## 4.2 CTR Mode — Encryption Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Plaintext Block P1"] --> X1["XOR"]
    P2["Plaintext Block P2"] --> X2["XOR"]
    P3["Plaintext Block P3"] --> X3["XOR"]
    T1["Counter T1"] --> E1["Block Cipher E_K"]
    T2["Counter T2 = T1+1"] --> E2["Block Cipher E_K"]
    T3["Counter T3 = T2+1"] --> E3["Block Cipher E_K"]
    E1 --> O1["O1 = E_K(T1)"]
    E2 --> O2["O2 = E_K(T2)"]
    E3 --> O3["O3 = E_K(T3)"]
    O1 --> X1
    O2 --> X2
    O3 --> X3
    X1 --> C1["Ciphertext C1"]
    X2 --> C2["Ciphertext C2"]
    X3 --> C3["Ciphertext C3"]
    K["Secret Key K"] -.-> E1
    K -.-> E2
    K -.-> E3
```

> **Key structural observations visible in the diagram:**
> 1. The three **Block Cipher $E_K$** blocks are **independent** — no serial dependency.
> 2. The counters $T_1, T_2, T_3$ are all known to the sender; no chaining of ciphertexts.
> 3. The secret key $K$ is the **only** secret input.

## 4.3 CTR Mode — Decryption Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1["Ciphertext C1"] --> X1["XOR"]
    C2["Ciphertext C2"] --> X2["XOR"]
    C3["Ciphertext C3"] --> X3["XOR"]
    T1["Counter T1"] --> E1["Block Cipher E_K"]
    T2["Counter T2"] --> E2["Block Cipher E_K"]
    T3["Counter T3"] --> E3["Block Cipher E_K"]
    E1 --> O1["O1 = E_K(T1)"]
    E2 --> O2["O2 = E_K(T2)"]
    E3 --> O3["O3 = E_K(T3)"]
    O1 --> X1
    O2 --> X2
    O3 --> X3
    X1 --> P1["Recovered P1"]
    X2 --> P2["Recovered P2"]
    X3 --> P3["Recovered P3"]
    K["Secret Key K"] -.-> E1
    K -.-> E2
    K -.-> E3
```

> **Notice the structural symmetry:** The decryption diagram is identical to the encryption diagram with the roles of $P_i$ and $C_i$ swapped. This is a direct visual proof of why CTR encryption and decryption are **mathematically the same operation**.

## 4.4 Counter Update Logic (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Start: T1 = Nonce parallel 0"] --> I1["Increment counter part: counter_i = counter_i-1 + 1"]
    I1 --> C1{"Reached 2^32 ?"}
    C1 -- "No" --> N1["Use T_i = Nonce parallel counter_i for next block"]
    C1 -- "Yes" --> E1["ERROR: Counter exhausted. Abort and re-key."]
    N1 --> O1["Output ciphertext block C_i"]
```

## 4.5 Comparative Mode-of-Operation Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CBC["CBC - Serial Chain"]
        direction TB
        P1c["P1"] --> X1c["XOR with IV"]
        X1c --> E1c["E_K"]
        E1c --> C1c["C1"]
        P2c["P2"] --> X2c["XOR with C1"]
        X2c --> E2c["E_K"]
        E2c --> C2c["C2"]
        C1c --> X2c
    end
    subgraph CTR["CTR - Parallel Pipeline"]
        direction TB
        P1t["P1"] --> X1t["XOR"]
        T1t["T1"] --> E1t["E_K"]
        E1t --> X1t
        X1t --> C1t["C1"]
        P2t["P2"] --> X2t["XOR"]
        T2t["T2"] --> E2t["E_K"]
        E2t --> X2t
        X2t --> C2t["C2"]
    end
```

> The side-by-side topology makes the **parallelizability advantage** of CTR visually obvious: each block in CTR is independent, while CBC has a serial XOR-with-previous-ciphertext dependency that forbids parallelism.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define a stream cipher. With a neat diagram, explain the general structure of a stream cipher.**

**Model Answer (10 lines):**

A **stream cipher** is a symmetric-key cipher that encrypts plaintext one symbol (bit/byte) at a time by combining each plaintext symbol with a corresponding pseudorandom keystream symbol, typically using XOR. The general structure consists of a **keystream generator** driven by a secret key $K$ and (optionally) an Initial Value (IV), an **XOR combiner**, and an internal **state-update function**.

The keystream generator produces symbols $K_1, K_2, \ldots$ according to:
$$K_i = \psi(S_i, K, IV), \qquad S_{i+1} = \phi(S_i, K, IV)$$
Encryption is $C_i = P_i \oplus K_i$ and decryption is $P_i = C_i \oplus K_i$.

[Insert the generic stream cipher diagram from Section 4.1.]

The security of a stream cipher depends entirely on the **pseudorandomness** of the keystream — if the keystream is predictable, the cipher is broken.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between synchronous and self-synchronizing stream ciphers with examples.**

**Model Answer (8 lines, tabular form acceptable):**

| Aspect | Synchronous | Self-Synchronizing |
|--------|-------------|---------------------|
| Keystream dependency | Independent of plaintext/ciphertext | Depends on last $n$ ciphertext symbols |
| Error propagation | None (1 bit flip → 1 bit flip) | Bounded: at most $n$ symbols corrupted |
| Resynchronization | Manual after symbol loss/deletion | Automatic after $n$ symbols |
| Example | CTR, OFB, RC4, A5/1 | CFB |
| Vulnerability | Insertion / replay attacks | Less vulnerable to insertion |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Stream Cipher Questions)**
> Students frequently lose 1 mark by writing "XOR is used because it is fast" without stating the deeper mathematical reason — *XOR is its own inverse*, making encryption and decryption the same operation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the **Counter (CTR) Mode of operation** in detail with a neat block diagram. Show the encryption and decryption equations. (7 marks)

**(b)** A plaintext message of **24 bits** is to be encrypted using **8-bit block AES in CTR mode**. The key $K$ is secret, and the initial counter is $T_1 = 0000\,0001_2$. The block cipher outputs are: $E_K(0000\,0001) = 1011\,1000$, $E_K(0000\,0010) = 0110\,1100$, $E_K(0000\,0011) = 1001\,0111$. Plaintext is $P = 0110\,1001\,1010\,0110\,1101\,0101$. Compute the ciphertext and verify the decryption. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Definition [1 mark]:** Counter Mode (CTR) is a block cipher mode of operation that converts a block cipher into a stream cipher by encrypting successive values of a counter and XOR-ing the result with the plaintext blocks.

**Encryption & Decryption Equations [2 marks]:**
$$C_i = P_i \oplus E_K(T_i), \qquad P_i = C_i \oplus E_K(T_i)$$
where $T_i$ is the counter for block $i$, $E_K$ is the underlying block cipher under secret key $K$, and $\oplus$ denotes bitwise XOR.

**Counter Update Rule [1 mark]:**
$$T_{i+1} = T_i + 1 \pmod{2^m}$$
where $m$ is the bit-length of the counter (e.g., $m = 32$ in AES-CTR).

**Properties of CTR Mode [2 marks]:**
1. Encryption and decryption are **structurally identical** (both XOR with $E_K(T_i)$).
2. **Fully parallelizable** for both encryption and decryption.
3. **Random access** — any $P_i$ can be recovered independently.
4. **No padding** required.
5. **No error propagation** — a 1-bit flip in $C_i$ flips exactly 1 bit in $P_i$.
6. **Critical security requirement:** Counter values must be **unique** for every block under a given key. Reusing a counter with the same key is catastrophic — it is equivalent to a one-time pad with a repeating key and leaks the XOR of the two plaintexts.

**Block Diagram [1 mark]:** [Refer to the CTR Encryption diagram in Section 4.2.]

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- Plaintext: $P = 0110\,1001 \parallel 1010\,0110 \parallel 1101\,0101$ (three 8-bit blocks)
- Initial counter: $T_1 = 0000\,0001$
- $E_K(0000\,0001) = 1011\,1000$
- $E_K(0000\,0010) = 0110\,1100$
- $E_K(0000\,0011) = 1001\,0111$

**Block 1 [2 marks]:**
$$C_1 = P_1 \oplus E_K(T_1) = 0110\,1001 \oplus 1011\,1000$$

$$\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= (0\oplus1)(1\oplus0)(1\oplus1)(0\oplus1)(1\oplus1)(0\oplus0)(0\oplus0)(1\oplus0) \\
C_1 &= 1101\,0001
\end{aligned}$$

**Block 2 [2 marks]:** $T_2 = T_1 + 1 = 0000\,0010$
$$C_2 = P_2 \oplus E_K(T_2) = 1010\,0110 \oplus 0110\,1100 = 1100\,1010$$

**Block 3 [2 marks]:** $T_3 = T_2 + 1 = 0000\,0011$
$$C_3 = P_3 \oplus E_K(T_3) = 1101\,0101 \oplus 1001\,0111 = 0100\,0010$$

**Final Ciphertext [1 mark]:**
$$C = 1101\,0001 \parallel 1100\,1010 \parallel 0100\,0010$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a)** Discuss the **structure of a stream cipher** in detail. Explain the three functional components (state update, output, combination functions) and classify stream ciphers into synchronous and asynchronous types. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare **Counter (CTR) Mode** with **CBC, CFB, and OFB** modes on the basis of (i) parallelizability, (ii) error propagation, (iii) random access, and (iv) padding requirement. Justify why CTR is preferred in modern high-speed protocols. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Stream Cipher Structure [3 marks]:** A stream cipher is defined by three functions acting on a finite state $S_i$:

| Function | Symbol | Equation | Purpose |
|----------|--------|----------|---------|
| State Update | $\phi$ | $S_{i+1} = \phi(S_i, K, IV)$ | Advances the internal state |
| Output | $\psi$ | $K_i = \psi(S_i, K, IV)$ | Extracts keystream symbol $K_i$ |
| Combination | $\xi$ | $C_i = P_i \oplus K_i$ | XORs keystream with plaintext |

**Classification [2 marks]:**

- **Synchronous:** $K_i$ depends only on $K$ and $IV$ — independent of plaintext/ciphertext. Sender and receiver must remain in perfect sync; a lost symbol causes infinite desynchronization. Examples: CTR, OFB, RC4, A5/1, ChaCha20.
- **Asynchronous (Self-Synchronizing):** $K_i$ depends on the last $n$ ciphertext symbols. After at most $n$ correct symbols are received, the keystream automatically resynchronizes. Example: CFB.

**Implementation Note [2 marks]:** Practical stream ciphers (e.g., RC4, ChaCha20) are often built using **Linear Feedback Shift Registers (LFSRs)** combined with nonlinear combining functions to balance hardware efficiency and cryptographic strength. Modern designs like ChaCha20 use ARX (Add-Rotate-XOR) constructions instead.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Comparison Table [4 marks]:**

| Property | CBC | CFB | OFB | CTR |
|----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| Parallelizable (Enc) | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| Parallelizable (Dec) | Yes | Yes | No | **Yes** |
| Error Propagation | 2 blocks | $b+1$ symbols | 1 symbol | **1 symbol** |
| Random Access | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| Padding Required | Yes | No | No | **No** |
| Identical Enc/Dec? | No | No | No | **Yes** |

**Justification — Why CTR is Preferred in Modern Protocols [3 marks]:**
1. **Speed:** Full parallelizability allows AES-CTR to saturate multi-core CPUs and AES-NI hardware, achieving 10+ Gbps on commodity hardware.
2. **Latency:** No serial dependency means block $i$ does not have to wait for block $i-1$ — critical for low-latency applications like VoIP and TLS 1.3.
3. **Random Access:** Streamed media (e.g., encrypted video) can be seeked directly to any frame, like a file pointer.
4. **Simplicity:** Both encryption and decryption use the same code path — one AES engine suffices, reducing hardware area.
5. **Authenticated Encryption:** CTR is the natural building block of authenticated modes (GCM, CCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) used in TLS 1.3, IPsec, and 5G.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> In 14-mark questions, students often *describe* modes but **do not draw the block diagram**. A missing block diagram typically costs 2–3 marks. Always include a labeled diagram, even a hand-drawn one. Also, when comparing modes, examiners expect *all four* modes in the table, not just two — partial tables lose 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Counter Mode & Stream Ciphers**

- ☐ **Stream cipher equation:** $C_i = P_i \oplus K_i$, $P_i = C_i \oplus K_i$ (same operation).
- ☐ **CTR equations:** $C_i = P_i \oplus E_K(T_i)$ — encryption and decryption are **identical** operations.
- ☐ **Counter update:** $T_{i+1} = T_i + 1 \pmod{2^m}$ — must be **unique** per key.
- ☐ **Three components of stream cipher:** state update $\phi$, output $\psi$, combination $\xi$ (XOR).
- ☐ **Synchronous vs Asynchronous:** Sync = no error propagation but needs resync after loss; Async = bounded error propagation, self-syncs.
- ☐ **CTR advantages:** parallel, random access, no padding, no error propagation, identical enc/dec.
- ☐ **CTR disadvantages:** no built-in authentication (must pair with MAC, e.g., GMAC, Poly1305); nonce/IV reuse is catastrophic.
- ☐ **Real-world usage:** TLS 1.3 (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305), IPsec ESP, 5G encryption.
- ☐ **OTP security:** $P(C) = P(P)$ — perfect secrecy — but only if key is truly random, $\vert K \vert \geq \vert P \vert$, and never reused.
- ☐ **Avalanche effect:** a 1-bit key/IV change must flip ~50% of keystream bits.
- ☐ **Output length:** $\vert C \vert = \vert P \vert$ — no ciphertext expansion in stream ciphers.
- ☐ **Why XOR?** Self-inverse, commutative, bitwise-independent, single-cycle on any CPU.
- ☐ **Block cipher ≠ Stream cipher by nature:** CTR is the bridge — it converts any block cipher into a stream cipher.
- ☐ **GCM = CTR + GHASH** (authentication tag); the keystream of CTR is reused in GCM as the encryption engine.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Public Key Cryptosystems - Principles
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT501 - FOUNDATIONS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Public Key Cryptosystems - Principles

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Public Key Cryptosystem (PKC)**, also known as **Asymmetric Cryptosystem**, is a cryptographic framework introduced conceptually by **Whitfield Diffie and Martin Hellman in 1976** in their seminal paper *"New Directions in Cryptography"*. It is a mathematical scheme that employs a **pair of distinct but mathematically related keys**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Public Key (PU or $e_k$):** Distributed openly to anyone in the network. Used for **encryption** or **signature verification**.
>
> **Private Key (PR or $d_k$):** Kept secret by the owner. Used for **decryption** or **signature creation**.

The security of PKC rests on the computational hardness of certain mathematical problems (factoring large integers, discrete logarithms, elliptic curve discrete logarithms), making it infeasible for an adversary to derive the private key from the public key using today's classical computing power.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Public Mailbox" Model

Imagine a transparent, locked mailbox placed outside a person's house:

- The **mail slot** is wide open — **anyone** in the world can walk up and drop a letter inside. This represents the **public key** — it is freely accessible to everyone for the encryption (dropping letters) operation.
- However, only the **owner possesses the physical brass key** that opens the back of the mailbox to retrieve letters. This represents the **private key** — it is exclusively held by the receiver for decryption.
- A curious passerby can see the mailbox, even rattle it, but cannot open it without the **trapdoor information** (the key).

This analogy directly maps to how PKC solves the **key distribution problem** that plagues symmetric cryptography: no secret key ever needs to be exchanged over an insecure channel before communication begins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 3):** Understand the foundational principles distinguishing asymmetric from symmetric systems, the role of **one-way functions**, **trapdoor functions**, and the dual usage of PKC for **confidentiality** and **authentication**.

---

## 1.3 Why Public Key Cryptosystems? The Motivational Gap

In symmetric cryptography, the **same secret key $K$** is used for both encryption and decryption. This creates three critical bottlenecks:

1. **Key Distribution Nightmare:** How do two parties share $K$ securely over an open network?
2. **Key Management Explosion:** For $n$ users, $\binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ unique keys must be maintained.
3. **No Non-Repudiation:** Since the key is shared, the sender can later deny having sent a message.

PKC elegantly resolves these issues by **decoupling** the encryption capability from the decryption capability through the use of a key pair.

---

## 1.4 Essential Components of a PKC

A public key cryptosystem is formally defined as a **six-tuple** $(M, C, K, E, D, k)$ where:

- $M$ = Message (Plaintext) space
- $C$ = Ciphertext space
- $K$ = Key space
- $E$ = Encryption function family: $E_k : M \to C$
- $D$ = Decryption function family: $D_k : C \to M$
- $k \in K$ = Security parameter

For each user $U$, there exists a key pair $(PU_U, PR_U)$ satisfying the **core consistency equation**:

$$D(PR_U, E(PU_U, M)) = M$$

---

## 1.5 Fundamental Mathematical Primitives

Two mathematical abstractions underpin every PKC:

### A. One-Way Function (OWF)
A function $f : X \to Y$ such that for every $x \in X$:
- Computing $y = f(x)$ is **computationally easy**.
- Computing $x = f^{-1}(y)$ is **computationally infeasible** (i.e., no polynomial-time algorithm exists under classical assumptions).

**Examples:**
- Integer multiplication vs. integer factorization: $f(p, q) = p \cdot q$ is easy; factoring $N = p \cdot q$ where $p, q$ are large primes is hard.
- Modular exponentiation vs. discrete logarithm: $g^x \bmod p$ is easy; finding $x$ from $y = g^x \bmod p$ is the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)**.

### B. Trapdoor One-Way Function (TOWF)
A special OWF $f$ where:
- $f(x)$ is **easy** to compute.
- $f^{-1}(y)$ is **hard** to compute **without** the secret trapdoor $t$.
- $f^{-1}(y)$ is **easy** to compute **with** the trapdoor $t$.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** Think of a padlock (the one-way function). Without the key (trapdoor), it is impossible to open. With the key, it snaps open instantly. The private key *is* the trapdoor information.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Hard Requirements of a PKC (Diffie-Hellman Mandate)

For any practical public key cryptosystem, the following six conditions **must** hold (as enumerated by Diffie and Hellman):

1. **Key Pair Generation:** It must be computationally easy for a party $B$ to generate a key pair $(PU_B, PR_B)$.
2. **Encryption Efficiency:** Given the public key $PU_B$ and plaintext $M$, computing $C = E_{PU_B}(M)$ must be easy.
3. **Decryption Efficiency:** Given the private key $PR_B$ and ciphertext $C$, computing $M = D_{PR_B}(C)$ must be easy.
4. **Public-to-Private Infeasibility:** Deriving $PR_B$ from $PU_B$ must be **computationally infeasible**.
5. **Ciphertext-Only Infeasibility:** Without $PR_B$, recovering $M$ from $C$ must be computationally infeasible.
6. **Symmetry (Optional but Desirable):** The functions $E$ and $D$ can be applied in either order: $M = E_{PU_B}(D_{PR_B}(M))$. This property enables **digital signatures**.

## 2.2 Operational Modes of PKC

| **Mode** | **Sender Uses** | **Receiver Uses** | **Service Provided** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Recipient's Public Key $PU_B$ | Recipient's Private Key $PR_B$ | Secrecy of message |
| **Authentication** | Sender's Private Key $PR_A$ | Sender's Public Key $PU_A$ | Source verification + integrity |
| **Combined (Confidentiality + Authentication)** | Sender first signs with $PR_A$, then encrypts with $PU_B$ | Receiver first decrypts with $PR_B$, then verifies with $PU_A$ | Secrecy + Source verification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:** A common confusion point is — *Can PKC provide both confidentiality AND authentication simultaneously?* The answer is **YES**, by chaining the two operations, but it must be done in the **correct order** to avoid security vulnerabilities (sign-then-encrypt vs. encrypt-then-sign).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Core Mathematical Constants

| **Notation** | **Meaning** | **Domain / Range** |
|---|---|---|
| $M$ | Plaintext (Message) | $M \in \mathcal{M}$ (message space) |
| $C$ | Ciphertext | $C \in \mathcal{C}$ (ciphertext space) |
| $PU$ or $e_k$ | Public Key of user | Distributed openly |
| $PR$ or $d_k$ | Private Key of user | Kept secret |
| $E_K(\cdot)$ | Encryption under key $K$ | $\mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{C}$ |
| $D_K(\cdot)$ | Decryption under key $K$ | $\mathcal{C} \to \mathcal{M}$ |
| $\gcd(a, b)$ | Greatest common divisor of $a$ and $b$ | $\mathbb{Z}^+ \to \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| $a \bmod n$ | Remainder of $a$ divided by $n$ | Result $\in [0, n-1]$ |
| $\mathbb{Z}_n^*$ | Multiplicative group of integers mod $n$ coprime to $n$ | Set of units mod $n$ |
| $\phi(n)$ | Euler's Totient Function | Counts integers $\le n$ coprime to $n$ |
| $\mathbb{Z}_p$ | Prime field of order $p$ | $\{0, 1, \dots, p-1\}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Rendering Tip:** When writing absolute value, modular bars, or set cardinality inside markdown tables, always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the bare pipe `\vert`. Example: write $\gcd(a, b) = 1$ rather than $\vert a \vert$. This prevents markdown table parser breakage.

## 2.4 Public Key Cryptosystem — Complete Formal Schematic

For each user $U_i$, the system maintains:

$$E_{PU_i}(M) \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow D_{PR_i}(C) = M$$

The **consistency property** demanded by every PKC is:

$$D_{PR_i}(E_{PU_i}(M)) = M \quad \forall \, M \in \mathcal{M}$$

And equivalently (for signature-supporting schemes):

$$E_{PU_i}(D_{PR_i}(M)) = M$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application Domain** | **PKC Usage** | **Example Algorithm** |
|---|---|---|
| Secure Web Browsing (HTTPS/TLS) | Key exchange + Authentication | RSA, ECDHE, Ed25519 |
| Email Security (PGP, S/MIME) | Confidentiality + Signature | RSA-2048, AES-256 hybrid |
| Digital Certificates (PKI / X.509) | Identity binding via CA signatures | RSA, ECDSA |
| Blockchain & Cryptocurrency | Transaction signing | ECDSA (secp256k1) |
| SSH Authentication | Server + client auth | RSA, Ed25519 |
| VPN Handshakes (IKEv2) | Mutual key agreement | Diffie-Hellman, ECDH |

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough: The Encryption-Decryption Round Trip

We now derive the mathematical flow of a generic public key encryption exchange between **Alice** (sender) and **Bob** (receiver).

### Step 1: Key Pair Generation (Bob's Side)

Bob uses a **probabilistic key generation algorithm** $\mathcal{K}$ parameterized by a security parameter $\lambda$ (typically $\lambda = 2048$ bits for RSA-grade security).

$$\text{Output of } \mathcal{K}(1^\lambda): \quad (PU_B, PR_B) \in \mathcal{K}_p \times \mathcal{K}_s$$

where $\mathcal{K}_p$ is the public key space and $\mathcal{K}_s$ is the secret key space. The algorithm leverages a **hard mathematical problem generator** — for RSA, this is the RSA problem; for ElGamal, the discrete log problem.

### Step 2: Public Key Publication

Bob publishes $PU_B$ in a **public directory**, a certificate, or transmits it directly to Alice. **Crucially**, $PU_B$ travels over an **insecure channel** — there is no secrecy requirement because exposing $PU_B$ does not compromise $PR_B$.

### Step 3: Alice Encrypts the Plaintext

Alice retrieves $PU_B$ and computes the ciphertext $C$ using the encryption function $E$:

$$C = E_{PU_B}(M)$$

She transmits $C$ over the insecure channel to Bob.

### Step 4: Bob Decrypts the Ciphertext

Bob applies the decryption function $D$ using his private key $PR_B$:

$$M = D_{PR_B}(C)$$

Substituting Step 3 into Step 4:

$$M = D_{PR_B}\big(E_{PU_B}(M)\big)$$

### Step 5: Verification of Correctness

For correctness, we require:

$$D_{PR_B}\big(E_{PU_B}(M)\big) = M \quad \forall \, M \in \mathcal{M}$$

This is the **functional inversion property** that defines a valid PKC.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Illustration: Small Toy RSA (Conceptual)

To make the abstract principles concrete, we walk through a **non-secure toy example** (instructor demonstration only — do not use in production).

### Step 1: Choose Two Primes
Let $p = 5$ and $q = 11$. These are kept secret initially.

### Step 2: Compute the Modulus
$$n = p \cdot q = 5 \times 11 = 55$$

### Step 3: Compute Euler's Totient
$$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 4 \times 10 = 40$$

### Step 4: Choose Public Exponent
Select $e$ such that $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ and $1 < e < \phi(n)$. Pick $e = 7$.

Verify: $\gcd(7, 40) = 1$. ✓

So **$PU = (n, e) = (55, 7)$**.

### Step 5: Compute Private Exponent via Modular Inverse
Find $d$ such that:

$$e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$$

We seek $d$ satisfying:

$$7d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$$

Apply the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**:

$$40 = 5 \times 7 + 5$$
$$7 = 1 \times 5 + 2$$
$$5 = 2 \times 2 + 1$$
$$2 = 2 \times 1 + 0$$

Back-substituting:

$$1 = 5 - 2 \times 2$$
$$1 = 5 - 2 \times (7 - 1 \times 5) = 3 \times 5 - 2 \times 7$$
$$1 = 3 \times (40 - 5 \times 7) - 2 \times 7 = 3 \times 40 - 17 \times 7$$

Thus $d \equiv -17 \equiv 23 \pmod{40}$.

So **$PR = (n, d) = (55, 23)$**.

### Step 6: Encrypt Plaintext $M = 10$

$$C = M^e \bmod n = 10^7 \bmod 55$$

Compute by repeated squaring:

$$10^2 = 100 \equiv 100 - 1 \times 55 = 45 \pmod{55}$$
$$10^4 = (10^2)^2 = 45^2 = 2025 \equiv 2025 - 36 \times 55 = 2025 - 1980 = 45 \pmod{55}$$
$$10^7 = 10^4 \times 10^2 \times 10 = 45 \times 45 \times 10 = 2025 \times 10 \equiv 45 \times 10 = 450 \pmod{55}$$
$$450 \bmod 55 = 450 - 8 \times 55 = 450 - 440 = 10$$

Therefore:

$$C = 10$$

### Step 7: Decrypt Ciphertext $C = 10$

$$M = C^d \bmod n = 10^{23} \bmod 55$$

By Euler's Theorem, since $\gcd(M, n) = 1$ (here $\gcd(10, 55) = 5$, which is a degenerate toy case):

$$M^{ed} \equiv M^1 \equiv M \pmod{n} \quad \text{(theoretically)}$$

In this toy case, $C = M = 10$ because $10$ is small. In production RSA with $n \ge 2048$ bits, the encryption would scramble the value dramatically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** The reason the toy example gave $C = M$ is that $10^7 \bmod 55$ happens to equal $10$. With realistic primes ($p, q \ge 1024$ bits), the exponentiation becomes a true **trapdoor permutation** with full scrambling.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Symmetric vs. Asymmetric Cryptography

| **Parameter** | **Symmetric Key Crypto** | **Asymmetric Key Crypto (PKC)** |
|---|---|---|
| Number of keys for $n$ users | $n(n-1)/2$ | $2n$ (one pair per user) |
| Key distribution | Must be over a secure channel | Public key over insecure channel OK |
| Speed | **Fast** (hardware friendly) | **Slow** (100–1000× slower) |
| Primary operations | XOR, S-boxes, permutations | Modular exponentiation, EC point multiplication |
| Confidentiality | Yes | Yes |
| Authentication | Limited (MACs) | Yes (digital signatures natively) |
| Non-repudiation | No | **Yes** (signatures are legally binding) |
| Key length for equivalent security | 128 bits | 2048–3072 bits (RSA), 256 bits (EC) |
| Example algorithms | AES, ChaCha20, 3DES | RSA, ElGamal, ECC, DH |

## 3.4 Hybrid Cryptosystem — The Practical Bridge

Because PKC is computationally expensive, real-world systems use a **hybrid approach**:

1. Use **PKC** to securely exchange a freshly generated **random symmetric session key $K_s$**.
2. Use **symmetric encryption** (AES-256) with $K_s$ to encrypt the actual bulk data.

This is precisely how **TLS 1.3, PGP, and SSH** operate.

$$E_{\text{PKC}}(K_s) \rightarrow \text{transmitted alongside} \rightarrow E_{\text{AES}}(\text{Data}, K_s)$$

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Flow: Public Key Encryption for Confidentiality

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ALICE: PLAINTEXT M] --> B[E with PUBLIC KEY OF BOB]
    B --> C[CIPHERTEXT C OVER INSECURE CHANNEL]
    C --> D[BOB RECEIVES C]
    D --> E[D with PRIVATE KEY OF BOB]
    E --> F[BOB: PLAINTEXT M RECOVERED]
    
    G[BOB GENERATES KEY PAIR] --> H[PUBLIC KEY PU B]
    H --> I[PUBLISHED IN DIRECTORY]
    I --> B
    
    G --> J[PRIVATE KEY PR B]
    J --> K[STORED SECRETLY BY BOB]
    K --> E
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Flow: Public Key Cryptography for Authentication (Digital Signature)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ALICE: PLAINTEXT M] --> B[SIGN: S = D with PRIVATE KEY OF ALICE]
    B --> C[TRANSMIT M AND S]
    C --> D[BOB RECEIVES M AND S]
    D --> E[VERIFY: M' = E with PUBLIC KEY OF ALICE]
    E --> F{COMPARE M' TO M}
    F -->|EQUAL| G[SIGNATURE VALID: ALICE IS AUTHENTIC]
    F -->|NOT EQUAL| H[SIGNATURE REJECTED: TAMPERING DETECTED]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Comparison: Symmetric vs. Asymmetric Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SYMMETRIC["SYMMETRIC KEY CRYPTOSYSTEM"]
        S1[ALICE] -->|E_K MESSAGE| S2[SHARED SECRET K]
        S2 -->|D_K MESSAGE| S3[BOB]
        S1 -.->|K DISTRIBUTED SECURELY| S3
    end
    
    subgraph ASYMMETRIC["ASYMMETRIC KEY CRYPTOSYSTEM"]
        A1[ALICE] -->|E_PUB_B MESSAGE| A2[BOB]
        A2 -->|D_PR_B MESSAGE| A3[PLAINTEXT]
        A4[BOB KEYGEN] --> A5[PUB B PUBLIC]
        A4 --> A6[PR B PRIVATE]
        A5 --> A1
    end
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Flow: Hybrid Cryptosystem (TLS-Style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CLIENT GENERATES RANDOM SESSION KEY KS] --> B[ENCRYPT KS USING PUBLIC KEY OF SERVER]
    B --> C[TRANSMIT E_PUB_S KS OVER NETWORK]
    C --> D[SERVER DECRYPTS USING PRIVATE KEY PR_S]
    D --> E[SERVER RECOVERS KS]
    E --> F[BOTH PARTIES NOW SHARE KS]
    F --> G[SYMMETRIC AES-256 ENCRYPTION OF BULK DATA USING KS]
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between symmetric and asymmetric key cryptography. List any two advantages of public key cryptosystems over symmetric key cryptosystems.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **Symmetric Key** | **Asymmetric Key** |
|---|---|---|
| Keys used | Single shared secret key | Key pair (public + private) |
| Key distribution | Requires secure channel | Public key distributed openly |
| Speed | Very fast | Relatively slow |
| Number of keys for $n$ users | $n(n-1)/2$ | $2n$ |
| Confidentiality & Authentication | Provides only confidentiality | Provides both |
| Non-repudiation | Not supported | Supported via digital signatures |

**Advantages of PKC:**
1. **Solves the key distribution problem** — no need for a pre-shared secret over insecure channels.
2. **Enables digital signatures and non-repudiation** — sender cannot deny having sent a message.
3. **Reduces key management complexity** for large networks.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define a one-way function and a trapdoor function. Why are trapdoor functions essential to public key cryptography?**

**Model Answer:**

**One-Way Function (OWF):** A function $f : X \to Y$ such that for every $x \in X$, computing $y = f(x)$ is computationally easy, but computing $x = f^{-1}(y)$ for a given $y \in Y$ is computationally infeasible.

**Examples:** Integer multiplication $f(p, q) = pq$ (factoring is hard); modular exponentiation $f(x) = g^x \bmod p$ (discrete log is hard).

**Trapdoor Function:** A special one-way function $f_t$ where the inverse $f_t^{-1}(y)$ is computationally hard **without** the trapdoor $t$, but becomes **easy** when $t$ is known.

**Why Essential to PKC:**
- An OWF alone permits **only encryption** (anyone can compute $f$).
- A **trapdoor** provides the secret knowledge ($PR$) that makes decryption $f^{-1}$ feasible only for the legitimate owner.
- Without a trapdoor, even the legitimate receiver could not decrypt — defeating the purpose of a cryptosystem.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B Module 3 questions carry **14 marks** and offer **internal choice**. The two sub-parts typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**, each requiring structured analytical answers.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the principles of public key cryptosystems. List the essential requirements and explain the role of one-way and trapdoor functions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, explain how public key cryptography provides **(i)** confidentiality and **(ii)** authentication.

#### Model Answer for (a):

**Principles of Public Key Cryptosystems:**

A public key cryptosystem is built upon the following foundational principles:

1. **Asymmetric Key Architecture:** Every user possesses a mathematically linked key pair $(PU, PR)$. The public key $PU$ is openly published, while the private key $PR$ is held in strict confidence.

2. **Computational Separation:** It must be computationally easy to:
   - Generate the key pair $(PU, PR)$.
   - Encrypt a message using $PU$.
   - Decrypt a ciphertext using $PR$.
   
   Conversely, it must be **computationally infeasible** to:
   - Derive $PR$ from $PU$.
   - Recover plaintext from ciphertext without $PR$.

3. **Inversion Property:** For every message $M$ and every valid key pair $(PU, PR)$:

$$D_{PR}(E_{PU}(M)) = M$$

**Six Essential Requirements (Diffie-Hellman):**

| # | Requirement |
|---|---|
| 1 | Easy key pair generation |
| 2 | Easy encryption with $PU$ |
| 3 | Easy decryption with $PR$ |
| 4 | Infeasibility of deriving $PR$ from $PU$ |
| 5 | Infeasibility of recovering $M$ from $C$ alone |
| 6 | Optional symmetry enabling signatures |

**Role of One-Way Functions:** Provide the asymmetric "hard direction" — encryption is easy (forward direction $f$), decryption without the key is hard (inverse direction $f^{-1}$).

**Role of Trapdoor Functions:** Embed a secret shortcut (the private key) into the inverse direction, making $f^{-1}$ tractable only for the key holder. This is the cornerstone enabling **confidentiality** (encrypt with public trapdoor-less direction) and **authentication** (sign with private trapdoor direction).

---

#### Model Answer for (b):

**(i) Confidentiality using PKC:**

When Alice wishes to send a confidential message $M$ to Bob:

```
Step 1: Bob generates (PU_B, PR_B) and publishes PU_B.
Step 2: Alice obtains PU_B and computes C = E(PU_B, M).
Step 3: Alice transmits C over the insecure channel.
Step 4: Bob applies M = D(PR_B, C).
```

Only Bob, possessing $PR_B$, can recover $M$. Any eavesdropper intercepting $C$ faces the hard inverse problem without $PR_B$.

**(ii) Authentication using PKC (Digital Signature):**

When Alice wishes to **sign** a message $M$:

```
Step 1: Alice computes signature S = D(PR_A, M).
Step 2: Alice transmits (M, S) over the channel.
Step 3: Bob verifies by computing M' = E(PU_A, S).
Step 4: If M' == M, signature is valid and source is authentic.
```

Since only Alice knows $PR_A$, only she could have produced $S$ — this provides **non-repudiation**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often write $E$ and $D$ backwards when describing signatures. The signature is created by applying the **private key (decryption operation)** to the message — not the public key. Conversely, verification uses the **public key (encryption operation)**. Reversing these will cost 2–3 marks immediately.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain one-way functions and trapdoor one-way functions in detail with suitable mathematical examples. Discuss why mere one-way functions are insufficient for a complete public key cryptosystem.
