# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
> List and briefly explain the fundamental security requirements that any secure cryptographic system must satisfy.

**Model Answer:**

The fundamental security requirements are:

1. **Confidentiality:** Ensures that information is accessible only to authorized parties. Achieved through encryption (e.g., AES, RSA-OAEP). Formal notion: IND-CPA / IND-CCA2.

2. **Integrity:** Ensures that data has not been modified during transmission. Achieved via MACs (e.g., HMAC-SHA256) or digital signatures. Formal notion: EUF-CMA.

3. **Authentication:** Verifies the identity of a communicating party. Achieved via passwords, certificates, or challenge–response. Formal notion: Entity authentication (Bellare–Rogaway model).

4. **Non-Repudiation:** Prevents the sender from denying having sent a message. Achieved via digital signatures (e.g., RSA-PSS, ECDSA).

5. **Freshness:** Ensures a message is current and not a replay. Achieved via nonces, timestamps, sequence numbers.

6. **Availability** *(auxiliary)*: Ensures the system is accessible when needed (DoS resistance).

> [!TIP]
> Mentioning at least 4 properties (CIA + Non-Repudiation) is the typical KTU benchmark for full marks on this question.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
> Define the term "negligible function" in cryptography. Why is it used in defining security requirements?

**Model Answer:**

A function $\epsilon : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{R}$ is called **negligible** if for every polynomial $p(\cdot)$, there exists an integer $K$ such that for all $k > K$:

$$
\epsilon(k) < \tfrac{1}{p(k)}
$$

**Why it is used:**

- It quantifies the notion of "small enough to ignore" in an *asymptotic* sense.
- It is used because, in practice, we cannot hope for $\Pr[\text{Win}] = 0$ — a computationally bounded adversary may always have a tiny non-zero advantage.
- A scheme is *secure* if no polynomial-time adversary can win with non-negligible advantage.
- It supports the *provable security* paradigm: reduction proofs show that breaking the scheme implies breaking some hard problem (e.g., DLP, factoring) with non-negligible advantage — a contradiction.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the IND-CPA security game and define the IND-CPA advantage of an adversary against a public-key encryption scheme. (7 Marks)

**(b)** State and explain the four classical adversary models in increasing order of strength. Why is IND-CCA2 considered the "gold standard" for encryption schemes? (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) The IND-CPA Security Game:**

The IND-CPA game formalizes the **confidentiality** requirement. It is played between a challenger $\mathcal{C}$ and a probabilistic polynomial-time adversary $\mathcal{A}$.

**Step 1 — Setup:** $\mathcal{C}$ runs $\text{Gen}(1^k)$ to obtain $(pk, sk)$ and gives $pk$ to $\mathcal{A}$.

**Step 2 — Queries:** $\mathcal{A}$ may submit any polynomial number of plaintexts $m_i$ to the encryption oracle; $\mathcal{C}$ returns $c_i = \text{Enc}_{pk}(m_i)$.

**Step 3 — Challenge:** $\mathcal{A}$ outputs two equal-length messages $m_0, m_1$ with $m_0 \neq m_1$. $\mathcal{C}$ picks $b \xleftarrow{R} \{0, 1\}$ and returns $c^* = \text{Enc}_{pk}(m_b)$.

**Step 4 — Guess:** $\mathcal{A}$ outputs $b'$. It wins if $b' = b$.

**The IND-CPA Advantage:**

$$
\text{Adv}_{\Pi, \mathcal{A}}^{\text{IND-CPA}}(k) = \left\vert \Pr[b' = b] - \tfrac{1}{2} \right\vert
$$

A scheme is IND-CPA secure iff $\text{Adv}_{\Pi, \mathcal{A}}^{\text{IND-CPA}}(k) \leq \epsilon(k)$ for all PPT $\mathcal{A}$ and negligible $\epsilon$.

> **Conversion Logic:** The $\frac{1}{2}$ term is the random-guess baseline; if the adversary cannot do better than flipping a fair coin, the scheme is confidential.

**(b) Four Classical Adversary Models (in increasing strength):**

| # | Model | Capability | Strength |
|---|-------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | **COA** (Ciphertext-Only Attack) | Sees only ciphertexts | Weakest |
| 2 | **KPA** (Known-Plaintext Attack) | Has some plaintext–ciphertext pairs | Moderate |
| 3 | **CPA** (Chosen-Plaintext Attack) | Can request encryptions of chosen messages | Strong |
| 4 | **CCA2** (Adaptive Chosen-Ciphertext) | Can request decryptions (except $c^*$) | Strongest |

**Why CCA2 is the Gold Standard:**

- CCA2 models real-world adversaries who can trick users into decrypting arbitrary messages.
- Most modern protocols (TLS 1.3, IPsec, SSH) require CCA2 security.
- IND-CCA2 implies IND-CPA, so it is a strictly stronger guarantee.
- It prevents *malleability* — an attacker cannot transform $c^*$ into a related valid ciphertext.
- Schemes proven IND-CCA2 (e.g., RSA-OAEP, ECIES) are safe against the widest realistic threat.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Differentiate between the **threat model** and the **trust model** in a cryptographic system. Give one example protocol for each of the following trust models: (i) P2P (no TTP), (ii) Trusted KDC, (iii) PKI-based. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Derive the formal security requirement for an **EUF-CMA secure digital signature scheme**. State the goal, the adversary's capability, and the success condition. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Threat Model vs Trust Model:**

| Aspect | Threat Model | Trust Model |
|--------|--------------|-------------|
| **Defines** | What the adversary *can do* | Who is *trusted* |
| **Question answered** | "What are we defending against?" | "What do we assume is honest?" |
| **Includes** | Adversary capabilities, network assumptions | Trusted third parties, key custodians |
| **Example** | Dolev–Yao model (active network adversary) | PKI with certificate authority |

**Example Protocols:**

- **(i) P2P (No TTP):** **Diffie–Hellman Key Exchange** — both parties contribute equally; no server is trusted.
- **(ii) Trusted KDC:** **Kerberos** — a Key Distribution Center issues session tickets after authenticating the user.
- **(iii) PKI-based:** **TLS 1.3** — relies on Certificate Authorities (CAs) to bind public keys to identities.

**(b) EUF-CMA Requirement for Digital Signatures:**

Let $\Pi = (\text{Gen}, \text{Sign}, \text{Vrfy})$ be a signature scheme with security parameter $k$.

**Setup:** Challenger $\mathcal{C}$ runs $\text{Gen}(1^k)$ to obtain $(vk, sk)$ and gives $vk$ to $\mathcal{A}$.

**Adversary Capability:** $\mathcal{A}$ has *adaptive chosen-message query access* to the signing oracle $\mathcal{O}_{\text{Sign}}$: for any message $m_i$ of its choice, it receives $\sigma_i = \text{Sign}_{sk}(m_i)$. The adversary may adaptively choose $m_{i+1}$ after seeing $\sigma_i$.

**Goal:** $\mathcal{A}$ must output a pair $(m^*, \sigma^*)$ such that:

$$
\text{Vrfy}_{vk}(m^*, \sigma^*) = \text{accept}
$$

**Success Condition (EUF-CMA — Existential Unforgeability under Chosen-Message Attack):**

$\mathcal{A}$ wins if it outputs a valid forgery $(m^*, \sigma^*)$ such that:
1. $\text{Vrfy}_{vk}(m^*, \sigma^*) = \text{accept}$, **AND**
2. $m^*$ was **never** submitted to $\mathcal{O}_{\text{Sign}}$.

**Formal Requirement:**

$$
\text{Adv}_{\Pi, \mathcal{A}}^{\text{EUF-CMA}}(k) = \Pr[\mathcal{A} \text{ wins}] \leq \epsilon(k)
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** "Existential" means the adversary only needs to forge *some* message (not a specific one chosen by the challenger). "Universal" forgery (forge any specific message) is a strictly stronger notion. "Chosen-message" is the strongest realistic attack model — the adversary can see signatures of any messages of its choice except the final forgery.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students confuse **EUF-CMA** (existential unforgeability) with **sUF-CMA** (strong unforgeability, where $\sigma^*$ must also be a new signature). Always specify which one your protocol achieves.
> - In CCA/IND questions, students forget to mention the *restriction* that $c^*$ itself cannot be queried. **Loses 2 marks.**
> - Writing "the adversary wins if it decrypts the message" instead of "outputs $b' = b$" is a *very common* mistake costing 1–2 marks. Always state the *indistinguishability* condition.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cryptographic Requirements**

- **Three pillars of requirements:** Security, Functional, Trust.
- **CIA Triad** = Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication. Extend with **Non-Repudiation** and **Freshness** for full marks.
- **Negligible function** $\epsilon(k)$: smaller than any inverse polynomial in $k$. Foundation of *provable security*.
- **Adversary advantage** is always $\left\vert \Pr[\text{Win}] - \tfrac{1}{2} \right\vert$ (or $\Pr[\text{Win}]$ for signatures, since trivial win probability is $0$).
- **Four attack models (ascending):** COA < KPA < CPA < CCA2.
- **IND-CPA** = indistinguishability under chosen-plaintext attack. Models *passive* eavesdropper with encryption oracle.
- **IND-CCA2** = indistinguishability under *adaptive* chosen-ciphertext attack. Models *active* adversary with decryption oracle (except $c^*$). **Gold standard** for encryption.
- **EUF-CMA** = existential unforgeability under chosen-message attack. Models signature forgery. Adversary can query signatures but cannot re-use the same message in its forgery.
- **Trust Models:** P2P (DH) < KDC (Kerberos) < PKI (TLS) < Threshold ($(t, n)$ schemes).
- **Forward secrecy** = past session keys remain secure even if the long-term private key leaks.
- **Bit security** $s = -\log_2(\epsilon)$. For 128-bit security, $\epsilon \leq 2^{-128}$.
- **CSPRNG** = Cryptographically Secure Pseudo-Random Number Generator. Required for key generation, nonces, and salts.
- **Key requirement-mapping rule:** Every requirement must be tied to (i) a security goal, (ii) an adversary model, (iii) a primitive that satisfies it.
- **Common KTU pitfalls:** confusing $|$ in tables (use `\vert`), forgetting to subtract $1/2$ from advantage, conflating EUF-CMA with sUF-CMA, omitting the restriction in CCA games.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conventional vs Public Key Cryptosystems, RSA Cryptosystem - Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT501 - FOUNDATIONS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Conventional vs Public Key Cryptosystems, RSA Cryptosystem - Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Conventional (Symmetric-Key) Cryptosystem

**Formal KTU Definition:**
A *Conventional Cryptosystem* (also called **Symmetric-Key Cryptosystem** or **Secret-Key Cryptosystem**) is a cryptographic system in which the **same secret key** is used for both encryption and decryption, and that key must be shared between the sender and the receiver through a secure channel prior to communication.

Mathematically, if $E$ is the encryption function and $D$ is the decryption function operating on plaintext $M$ to produce ciphertext $C$, then:

$$C = E_K(M) \quad \text{and} \quad M = D_K(C)$$

where the subscript $K$ denotes the *same* shared secret key for both operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Algorithms:** DES, 3DES, AES, Blowfish, RC4, RC5, IDEA — all fall under conventional cryptography and are benchmarked by KTU under *CO2: Analyze classical and modern symmetric encryption standards*.

**Real-World Analogy — The Locked Diary:**
Imagine two friends, Anu and Ben, who want to exchange a secret diary. They buy a metal box with **one single physical key**. Anu locks the diary in the box using the key, posts the box to Ben, and Ben uses the *same physical key* to unlock it. The fundamental problem: **How does Anu safely hand over a copy of the key to Ben without a thief intercepting it?** This is the infamous **Key Distribution Problem**, the central weakness of conventional cryptography.

---

### 1.2 Public-Key (Asymmetric) Cryptosystem

**Formal KTU Definition:**
A *Public-Key Cryptosystem* (introduced by **Diffie and Hellman in 1976**) is a cryptographic system that uses a mathematically related **key pair** — a *public key* $K_{pub}$ published openly, and a *private key* $K_{pr}$ kept secret — such that data encrypted with one key can only be decrypted with the other.

$$C = E_{K_{pub}}(M) \quad \Rightarrow \quad M = D_{K_{pr}}(C)$$

$$C = E_{K_{pr}}(M) \quad \Rightarrow \quad M = D_{K_{pub}}(C)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trapdoor One-Way Function:** The security of every public-key algorithm rests on a mathematical operation that is **easy to compute in one direction** but **computationally infeasible to reverse** without a special secret (the *trapdoor*). For RSA, this is *Integer Factorization*; for ECC, it is the *Elliptic-Curve Discrete Logarithm*.

**Real-World Analogy — The Mailbox with a Slot:**
Picture a public mailbox with a wide slot that **anyone** in the world can drop letters into (encryption with public key), but only the owner possesses the small private key to open the mailbox door and read the letters (decryption with private key). The postal address (public key) is listed openly; the physical key (private key) never leaves the owner's pocket.

---

### 1.3 Quick Conceptual Distinction Table

| Parameter | Conventional | Public-Key |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Keys** | **1 shared** | **2 (key pair)** |
| **Symmetry** | Symmetric | Asymmetric |
| **Key Distribution** | Major weakness | Solved inherently |
| **Speed** | **Fast (100x–1000x faster)** | **Slow (heavy modular arithmetic)** |
| **Scalability** | $O(n^2)$ key pairs for $n$ users | $O(n)$ keys for $n$ users |
| **Foundation** | Substitution, Permutation, Feistel | Number theory (factorization, DLP) |
| **Use Case** | Bulk data encryption | Key exchange, digital signatures |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Insight:** A popular 14-mark question asks *"Why don't we use Public-Key cryptography for everything?"* The answer: **speed**. Real systems (HTTPS, PGP, TLS) use **hybrid cryptography** — public-key to exchange a random symmetric key, then symmetric-key for bulk data.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Operational Anatomy of a Conventional Cryptosystem

The operational logic follows a strict 5-stage pipeline:

1. **Key Generation** — Both parties agree upon (or are distributed) a shared secret $K$ through a secure auxiliary channel.
2. **Encryption** — Sender applies $E_K$ to plaintext $M$ to obtain $C = E_K(M)$.
3. **Transmission** — Ciphertext $C$ is sent over an *insecure* public channel.
4. **Decryption** — Receiver applies $D_K$ to obtain $M = D_K(C)$.
5. **Authentication of Key** — Both parties must trust that the key was not tampered with during distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Symmetry:** Conventional algorithms are built on **confusion** (obscuring the relationship between key and ciphertext) and **diffusion** (spreading plaintext influence over the ciphertext), classically implemented via Feistel networks (DES) or SP-networks (AES).

### 2.2 Operational Anatomy of a Public-Key Cryptosystem

1. **Key Pair Generation** — Receiver (Bob) generates $(K_{pub}, K_{pr})$ from a hard mathematical problem.
2. **Public Key Publication** — Bob publishes $K_{pub}$ in a public directory; $K_{pr}$ stays secret.
3. **Encryption** — Sender (Alice) uses $K_{pub}$ to encrypt: $C = E_{K_{pub}}(M)$.
4. **Decryption** — Bob uses $K_{pr}$ to recover: $M = D_{K_{pr}}(C)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this secure?** A passive eavesdropper Eve sees only $C$ and $K_{pub}$. Recovering $M$ from $C$ requires inverting a *one-way trapdoor function*, which is computationally infeasible (taking $O(2^{2048})$ operations for 2048-bit RSA).

### 2.3 RSA Cryptosystem — Mathematical Foundation

**RSA (Rivest–Shamir–Adleman, 1977)** is the world's most widely deployed public-key algorithm. Its security is grounded in the **practical difficulty of factoring a large semiprime** $n = p \times q$.

#### 2.3.1 Euler's Totient Function $\phi(n)$

For a positive integer $n$, $\phi(n)$ counts the integers in $[1, n]$ that are **coprime** to $n$ (i.e., $\gcd(k, n) = 1$).

$$\phi(n) = n \prod_{p \mid n} \left(1 - \frac{1}{p}\right)$$

For a semiprime $n = p \cdot q$ where $p, q$ are distinct primes:

$$\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1)$$

#### 2.3.2 Euler's Theorem

For any integer $a$ with $\gcd(a, n) = 1$:

$$a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$$

This is the **backbone identity** that makes RSA decryption work, because it guarantees that decryption exponent $d$ *undoes* the encryption exponent $e$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the *only* reference you need for solving any KTU RSA problem. Memorize it.

| # | Quantity / Operation | Formula | Purpose in RSA |
|:--:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Modulus | $n = p \cdot q$ | Public parameter (part of both keys) |
| 2 | Totient of semiprime | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Defines valid exponent range |
| 3 | Encryption exponent constraint | $1 < e < \phi(n), \quad \gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ | $e$ must be invertible mod $\phi(n)$ |
| 4 | Decryption exponent | $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | Recovered via Extended Euclidean Algorithm |
| 5 | Core congruence | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | The "magic identity" of RSA |
| 6 | Encryption | $C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$ | Public operation (anyone can encrypt) |
| 7 | Decryption | $M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$ | Private operation (only Bob can decrypt) |
| 8 | Correctness proof identity | $M^{e \cdot d} \equiv M^{1 + k\phi(n)} \equiv M \pmod{n}$ | Why decryption works |
| 9 | Block size constraint | $0 \le M < n$ | Plaintext must fit in the modulus |
| 10 | GCD validity check | $\gcd(M, n) = 1$ | Required for Euler's theorem to apply |
| 11 | Key size (current KTU standard) | $\vert n \vert \ge 2048$ bits | Secure against modern factoring attacks |
| 12 | Number of key pairs for $N$ users | Conventional: $\frac{N(N-1)}{2}$, Public: $2N$ | Scalability argument |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** RSA is the cryptographic engine behind **TLS/SSL handshakes, PGP email, SSH authentication, digital certificates (X.509), and blockchain wallet addresses** in Bitcoin and Ethereum.

### 2.5 The 5 Required Conditions for Valid RSA Parameters

For an RSA system to be cryptographically sound, all of the following must hold *simultaneously*:

1. $p$ and $q$ must be **large random distinct primes** (typically $\ge 1024$ bits each).
2. $n = p \cdot q$ must be **computationally infeasible to factor**.
3. $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$.
4. The decryption exponent $d$ must satisfy $d \cdot e \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$.
5. Plaintext $M$ must satisfy $0 \le M < n$ and $\gcd(M, n) = 1$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Correctness Proof — Why $M = C^d \pmod{n}$ Works

We must prove that raising the ciphertext to the power $d$ restores the plaintext. We are given:

$$C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$$

Apply the decryption operation:

$$C^{d} \equiv (M^{e})^{d} \equiv M^{e \cdot d} \pmod{n}$$

From the key generation step, $d \cdot e \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, so there exists an integer $k$ such that:

$$e \cdot d = 1 + k \cdot \phi(n)$$

Substituting:

$$M^{e \cdot d} = M^{1 + k \cdot \phi(n)} = M \cdot \left(M^{\phi(n)}\right)^{k}$$

By Euler's Theorem, since $\gcd(M, n) = 1$:

$$M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$$

Therefore:

$$M^{e \cdot d} \equiv M \cdot 1^{k} \equiv M \pmod{n}$$

This is the *complete* mathematical proof of RSA correctness, examinable in KTU 14-mark questions.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Full RSA Cycle (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** In an RSA cryptosystem, the receiver chooses $p = 5$, $q = 11$, and public exponent $e = 7$. Compute the private exponent $d$ and demonstrate full encryption/decryption of plaintext $M = 2$.

#### Step 1 — Compute the modulus $n$

$$n = p \times q = 5 \times 11 = 55$$

#### Step 2 — Compute Euler's totient $\phi(n)$

$$\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1) = (5 - 1)(11 - 1) = 4 \times 10 = 40$$

#### Step 3 — Validate the chosen $e = 7$

Check that $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$:

$$\gcd(7, 40) = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

Since $7$ is prime and $7$ does not divide $40$, the condition is automatically satisfied.

#### Step 4 — Compute the private exponent $d$ via Extended Euclidean Algorithm

We need to solve $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$, i.e., find integers $d$ and $k$ such that $7d - 40k = 1$.

Apply the Euclidean algorithm to $\gcd(40, 7)$:

$$40 = 5 \times 7 + 5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 5 = 40 - 5 \times 7$$

$$7 = 1 \times 5 + 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2 = 7 - 1 \times 5$$

$$5 = 2 \times 2 + 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 = 5 - 2 \times 2$$

$$2 = 2 \times 1 + 0 \quad \text{(gcd confirmed = 1)}$$

Now back-substitute to express $1$ as a linear combination of $7$ and $40$:

$$1 = 5 - 2 \times 2$$

Substitute $2 = 7 - 1 \times 5$:

$$1 = 5 - 2 \times (7 - 1 \times 5) = 5 - 2 \times 7 + 2 \times 5 = 3 \times 5 - 2 \times 7$$

Substitute $5 = 40 - 5 \times 7$:

$$1 = 3 \times (40 - 5 \times 7) - 2 \times 7 = 3 \times 40 - 15 \times 7 - 2 \times 7 = 3 \times 40 - 17 \times 7$$

Therefore:

$$-17 \times 7 \equiv 1 \pmod{40} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d \equiv -17 \equiv 23 \pmod{40}$$

So $\boxed{d = 23}$.

**Verification:** $e \cdot d = 7 \times 23 = 161 = 4 \times 40 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$ $\checkmark$

#### Step 5 — Encryption of $M = 2$

$$C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n} \equiv 2^{7} \pmod{55}$$

Compute $2^{7}$ step by step:

$$2^{1} = 2, \quad 2^{2} = 4, \quad 2^{4} = 16, \quad 2^{7} = 2^{4} \cdot 2^{2} \cdot 2^{1} = 16 \times 4 \times 2 = 128$$

Reduce mod $55$:

$$128 = 2 \times 55 + 18 = 110 + 18$$

$$\boxed{C = 18}$$

#### Step 6 — Decryption of $C = 18$

$$M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n} \equiv 18^{23} \pmod{55}$$

We use **modular exponentiation by repeated squaring**. Express $23$ in binary:

$$23 = 16 + 4 + 2 + 1 = (10111)_2$$

Pre-compute powers of $18 \pmod{55}$:

$$18^{1} \equiv 18 \pmod{55}$$

$$18^{2} = 324 \equiv 324 - 5(55) = 324 - 275 = 49 \pmod{55}$$

$$18^{4} = 49^{2} = 2401 \equiv 2401 - 43(55) = 2401 - 2365 = 36 \pmod{55}$$

$$18^{8} = 36^{2} = 1296 \equiv 1296 - 23(55) = 1296 - 1265 = 31 \pmod{55}$$

$$18^{16} = 31^{2} = 961 \equiv 961 - 17(55) = 961 - 935 = 26 \pmod{55}$$

Now multiply the required powers (binary positions $1, 2, 4, 16$):

$$18^{23} = 18^{16} \cdot 18^{4} \cdot 18^{2} \cdot 18^{1} = 26 \times 36 \times 49 \times 18 \pmod{55}$$

Compute step by step:

$$26 \times 36 = 936 \equiv 936 - 17(55) = 936 - 935 = 1 \pmod{55}$$

$$1 \times 49 = 49$$

$$49 \times 18 = 882 \equiv 882 - 16(55) = 882 - 880 = 2 \pmod{55}$$

$$\boxed{M = 2 \quad \checkmark \text{ (Decryption successful — original plaintext recovered)}}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Conventional vs. Public-Key

| Parameter | Conventional (Symmetric) | Public-Key (Asymmetric) |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of keys for $N$ users** | $\frac{N(N-1)}{2}$ key pairs | $2N$ keys total (one pair per user) |
| **Confidentiality** | $\checkmark$ Yes | $\checkmark$ Yes |
| **Authentication** | Limited (MAC-based) | $\checkmark$ Digital signatures |
| **Non-repudiation** | $\times$ Not provided | $\checkmark$ Inherent |
| **Key Exchange** | $\times$ Requires secure channel | $\checkmark$ Solved via public directory |
| **Encryption Speed** | **Fast** ($\sim$1 GB/s for AES) | **Slow** ($\sim$1 MB/s for RSA-2048) |
| **Key Length Required** | $128$–$256$ bits (AES) | $\ge 2048$ bits (RSA) |
| **Mathematical Basis** | Bit permutation/substitution, S-boxes, Feistel rounds | Integer factorization, Discrete log, elliptic curves |
| **Algorithms** | DES, 3DES, AES, Blowfish, ChaCha20 | RSA, ElGamal, ECC, Rabin, Paillier |
| **Typical Applications** | Disk encryption, VPN tunnels, WiFi (WPA2), HTTPS bulk data | Digital signatures, certificates, key exchange, crypto wallets |
| **Attack Vulnerability** | Brute force, differential/linear cryptanalysis | Factorization, chosen-ciphertext, timing attacks |
| **Hardware Implementation** | Very efficient | Computationally intensive |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Conventional (Symmetric) Cryptosystem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plaintext M] --> B[Sender Alice]
    B --> C[Encryption Algorithm E sub K]
    C --> D[Ciphertext C]
    D --> E[Insecure Channel]
    E --> F[Receiver Bob]
    F --> G[Decryption Algorithm D sub K]
    G --> H[Plaintext M]
    K1[Shared Secret Key K] -.-> C
    K1 -.-> G
    I[Attacker Eve] -.intercepts.-> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dotted arrows from the *Shared Secret Key* are the critical security liability — both endpoints must hold an identical copy of $K$, and any leak compromises *all* past and future communications.

### 4.2 Public-Key (Asymmetric) Cryptosystem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plaintext M] --> B[Sender Alice]
    B --> C[Encryption with Kpub of Bob]
    C --> D[Ciphertext C]
    D --> E[Insecure Channel]
    E --> F[Receiver Bob]
    F --> G[Decryption with Kpr of Bob]
    G --> H[Plaintext M]
    K1[Public Key Kpub - in Directory] --> C
    K2[Private Key Kpr - Bob only] --> G
    I[Attacker Eve - has Kpub] -.cannot decrypt.-> D
```

### 4.3 RSA Key Generation Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: RSA Key Generation] --> B[Generate two large distinct primes p and q]
    B --> C[Compute n = p times q]
    C --> D[Compute phi n equals p-1 times q-1]
    D --> E[Choose integer e such that 1 less than e less than phi n and gcd e phi n equals 1]
    E --> F{Is gcd e phi n equal to 1?}
    F -->|No| E
    F -->|Yes| G[Compute d as modular inverse of e mod phi n using Extended Euclidean Algorithm]
    G --> H[Output Public Key: e, n]
    G --> I[Output Private Key: d, n]
    H --> J[End]
    I --> J
```

### 4.4 RSA Encrypt / Decrypt Operational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: RSA Encrypt] --> B[Input Plaintext M where 0 less than or equal to M less than n]
    B --> C[Compute C equals M power e mod n]
    C --> D[Send Ciphertext C over channel]
    D --> E[Receiver computes M equals C power d mod n]
    E --> F[Output Original Plaintext M]
    F --> G[End]
```

### 4.5 Hybrid Cryptosystem — The Real-World Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Alice wants to send bulk data to Bob] --> B[Generate random symmetric session key Ks]
    B --> C[Encrypt bulk data with Ks using AES]
    B --> D[Encrypt Ks with Bob public key using RSA]
    C --> E[Send ciphertext data plus encrypted Ks]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Bob decrypts Ks using his private key]
    F --> G[Bob decrypts bulk data using Ks]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why hybrid wins:** This is the architecture used in **TLS 1.3, PGP, S/MIME, and HTTPS**. It exploits the *speed* of symmetric encryption for bulk data and the *secure key exchange* capability of public-key cryptography — best of both worlds.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
Differentiate between **conventional (symmetric-key)** and **public-key (asymmetric) cryptosystems**. Mention any three distinguishing factors.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Conventional Cryptosystem | Public-Key Cryptosystem |
|---|---|---|
| **Keys Used** | A single shared secret key for encryption and decryption | A pair of keys — public key for encryption, private key for decryption |
| **Key Distribution** | Major problem — must be transmitted through a secure channel | Solved — public key published openly, private key never shared |
| **Speed** | Very fast (suitable for bulk data) | Relatively slow (heavy modular exponentiation) |
| **Scalability** | $O(n^2)$ key pairs for $n$ users | $O(n)$ keys for $n$ users |
| **Examples** | AES, DES, 3DES, Blowfish | RSA, ElGamal, ECC, Rabin |

> [!NOTE]
> Any three correct points with brief explanations earn full 3 marks. KTU examiners expect a comparison table or parallel bullet structure.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
Define **Euler's Totient Function** $\phi(n)$. Compute $\phi(35)$.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Euler's totient function $\phi(n)$ is defined as the number of positive integers in the range $[1, n]$ that are **relatively prime** (coprime) to $n$, i.e., $\gcd(k, n) = 1$.

**Formula for distinct prime factors:**

$$\phi(n) = n \prod_{p \mid n} \left(1 - \frac{1}{p}\right)$$

**Computation of $\phi(35)$:**

Since $35 = 5 \times 7$, both distinct primes:

$$\phi(35) = 35 \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{5}\right) \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{7}\right) = 35 \times \frac{4}{5} \times \frac{6}{7} = 35 \times \frac{24}{35} = 24$$

$$\boxed{\phi(35) = 24}$$

**Verification:** The 24 integers coprime to $35$ are: $1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 9, 11, 12, 13, 16, 17, 18, 19, 22, 23, 24, 26, 27, 29, 31, 32, 33, 34$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** Compare conventional and public-key cryptosystems in detail. Discuss the **key distribution problem** and explain how public-key cryptography overcomes it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an RSA cryptosystem, a user selects $p = 5$, $q = 11$, and public exponent $e = 7$. Compute the private key $d$ and demonstrate the encryption and decryption of the plaintext message $M = 2$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Conventional (Symmetric-Key) Cryptosystem:**

In a conventional cryptosystem, the same secret key $K$ is shared between the sender and receiver. The mathematical formulation is:

$$C = E_K(M) \quad \text{and} \quad M = D_K(C)$$

Examples: **DES, 3DES, AES, Blowfish**.

**Strengths:**
- High speed — suitable for encrypting bulk data (AES hardware achieves >10 Gbps).
- Well-analyzed and trusted algorithms.
- Short key lengths (128–256 bits) provide strong security.

**Weaknesses:**
- **Key Distribution Problem:** The sender must securely transmit $K$ to the receiver. If the key is intercepted during transmission, the entire system's confidentiality collapses.
- **Scalability Problem:** For $N$ users, the number of unique key pairs required is $\frac{N(N-1)}{2}$. For $N = 1000$ users, this is $499{,}500$ keys — a key management nightmare.
- **No non-repudiation:** Since both parties hold the same key, neither can prove to a third party who actually sent a message.

**Public-Key (Asymmetric) Cryptosystem:**

Introduced by **Whitfield Diffie and Martin Hellman in 1976**, this system uses two mathematically related keys:

$$C = E_{K_{pub}}(M), \quad M = D_{K_{pr}}(C)$$

Examples: **RSA, ECC, ElGamal, Diffie–Hellman**.

**Strengths:**
- **Key Distribution Solved:** The public key is published in an open directory; the private key never leaves the owner's machine. No secure channel is needed for key exchange.
- **Scalability:** For $N$ users, only $2N$ keys are needed.
- **Authentication & Non-Repudiation:** A message signed with the private key can be verified by anyone with the public key, providing digital signatures.

**Weaknesses:**
- Computationally slow (modular exponentiation is expensive).
- Requires significantly longer keys (RSA-2048 ≈ AES-112 in security).
- Vulnerable to chosen-ciphertext attacks if not implemented carefully (e.g., padding required via OAEP).

**How Public-Key Solves the Key Distribution Problem:**
In a public-key system, Alice obtains Bob's public key from a public directory (no secrecy required) and uses it to encrypt a symmetric session key $K_s$. Only Bob, possessing the corresponding private key, can decrypt and recover $K_s$. Subsequent bulk communication then uses $K_s$ with fast symmetric encryption (e.g., AES). This is the **hybrid cryptosystem** model used in TLS.

**Conclusion:** The two systems are *complementary*, not competitors — modern protocols use both.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall — Key Distribution:** Many students answer this question by *just listing* differences. Examiners award full 7 marks only when the **key distribution problem** is explicitly identified and the **mechanism of its solution** is clearly explained with a diagram or hybrid model description.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $p = 5$, $q = 11$, $e = 7$, $M = 2$.

**Step 1: Compute the modulus $n$**

$$n = p \times q = 5 \times 11 = 55$$

**Step 2: Compute Euler's totient $\phi(n)$**

$$\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1) = 4 \times 10 = 40$$

**Step 3: Validate the public exponent $e = 7$**

We need $\gcd(7, 40) = 1$. Since $7$ is prime and $7 \nmid 40$, this holds.

**Step 4: Compute the private exponent $d$**

We solve $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$ using the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**.

Euclidean chain:

$$40 = 5 \times 7 + 5$$
$$7 = 1 \times 5 + 2$$
$$5 = 2 \times 2 + 1$$
$$2 = 2 \times 1 + 0$$

Back-substitution:

$$1 = 5 - 2 \times 2$$
$$1 = 5 - 2(7 - 1 \times 5) = 3 \times 5 - 2 \times 7$$
$$1 = 3(40 - 5 \times 7) - 2 \times 7 = 3 \times 40 - 17 \times 7$$

Therefore $d \equiv -17 \equiv 23 \pmod{40}$.

$$\boxed{d = 23}$$

**Verification:** $7 \times 23 = 161 = 4(40) + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$ ✓

**Step 5: Encryption of $M = 2$**

$$C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n} \equiv 2^{7} \pmod{55}$$

$$2^{7} = 128 = 2(55) + 18 \equiv 18 \pmod{55}$$

$$\boxed{C = 18}$$

**Step 6: Decryption of $C = 18$**

$$M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n} \equiv 18^{23} \pmod{55}$$

Using binary expansion $23 = 16 + 4 + 2 + 1 = (10111)_2$:

$$18^{1} \equiv 18 \pmod{55}$$
$$18^{2} \equiv 49 \pmod{55}$$
$$18^{4} \equiv 36 \pmod{55}$$
$$18^{8} \equiv 31 \pmod{55}$$
$$18^{16} \equiv 26 \pmod{55}$$

Now compute:

$$18^{23} = 18^{16} \cdot 18^{4} \cdot 18^{2} \cdot 18^{1} = 26 \times 36 \times 49 \times 18 \pmod{55}$$

$$26 \times 36 = 936 \equiv 1 \pmod{55}$$
$$1 \times 49 = 49$$
$$49 \times 18 = 882 \equiv 2 \pmod{55}$$

$$\boxed{M = 2 \quad \text{(Original plaintext successfully recovered)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall — RSA Decryption Marks:** Most students compute $d$ correctly but **fail to demonstrate the decryption** because they attempt $18^{23} \pmod{55}$ by brute force. Always use **modular exponentiation by repeated squaring** — examiners award partial credit for the method even if the final arithmetic is slightly off.

---

#### **Question B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`** *(Internal Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the **RSA algorithm** in detail. State and prove the **correctness of RSA decryption** using Euler's theorem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are the **applications and security considerations** of public-key cryptography? Discuss any three real-world applications. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Introduction to RSA:**
The RSA cryptosystem was invented in **1977 by Ron Rivest, Adi Shamir, and Leonard Adleman** at MIT. It is the most widely used public-key algorithm and is based on the **practical computational difficulty of factoring large semiprimes**.

**The RSA Algorithm consists of three phases:**

**Phase 1 — Key Generation (performed by the receiver, e.g., Bob):**

1. Select two **large distinct primes** $p$ and $q$ (each typically $\ge 1024$ bits).
2. Compute the modulus: $n = p \cdot q$.
3. Compute Euler's totient: $\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1)$.
4. Choose the **public exponent** $e$ such that $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$. A common choice is $e = 65537 = 2^{16} + 1$.
5. Compute the **private exponent** $d$ using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm such that:

$$e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$$

6. **Public key:** $(e, n)$; **Private key:** $(d, n)$.

**Phase 2 — Encryption (performed by the sender, e.g., Alice):**

The plaintext $M$ must satisfy $0 \le M < n$ and $\gcd(M, n) = 1$. The ciphertext is:

$$C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$$

**Phase 3 — Decryption (performed by the receiver Bob):**

$$M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$$

**Proof of Correctness:**

We must show that decrypting the ciphertext $C$ recovers the original $M$.

Starting from:

$$C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$$

Apply the decryption operation:

$$C^{d} \equiv (M^{e})^{d} \equiv M^{e \cdot d} \pmod{n}$$

From the key generation phase, $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, so there exists an integer $k \ge 0$ such that:

$$e \cdot d = 1 + k \cdot \phi(n)$$

Substituting back:

$$M^{e \cdot d} = M^{1 + k \cdot \phi(n)} = M \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^{k}$$

By **Euler's Theorem**, since $\gcd(M, n) = 1$:

$$M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$$

Therefore:

$$M^{e \cdot d} \equiv M \cdot 1^{k} \equiv M \pmod{n} \quad \blacksquare$$

**This completes the proof that RSA decryption correctly recovers the plaintext.**

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Applications of Public-Key Cryptography:**

**1. Secure Key Exchange (Diffie–Hellman & RSA-based):**
Public-key cryptography solves the **key distribution problem** of symmetric cryptography. In protocols like **TLS 1.3**, RSA or ECDH (Elliptic Curve Diffie–Hellman) is used to securely exchange a randomly generated symmetric session key over an insecure channel. Once both parties possess the shared session key, AES is used for bulk encryption.

**2. Digital Signatures and Certificates:**
The **RSA signature scheme** (or DSA, ECDSA) is used to provide *authentication*, *integrity*, and *non-repudiation*. The sender signs a message by encrypting its hash with their private key: $S = H(M)^{d} \pmod{n}$. Anyone with the sender's public key can verify the signature. **X.509 digital certificates** used in HTTPS bind a public key to an identity and are signed by a trusted Certificate Authority (CA).

**3. Email Security (PGP / S/MIME):**
**Pretty Good Privacy (PGP)** and **S/MIME** use public-key cryptography to encrypt and sign emails. The sender encrypts a random symmetric key with the recipient's public key, then encrypts the email body with that symmetric key. This is the **hybrid model** in practice.

**4. Cryptocurrencies and Blockchain:**
**Bitcoin, Ethereum, and most blockchain systems** use **ECDSA (Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm)** to sign transactions. Each user has a key pair: the public key derives the wallet address, and the private key authorizes spending. Compromise of the private key results in total loss of funds.

**5. SSH Authentication:**
When connecting to a remote server via SSH, the server's public key is used to verify its identity (preventing man-in-the-middle attacks). Users can also authenticate via public-key cryptography, where possession of the private key proves identity without transmitting a password.

**Security Considerations:**

- **Key Size:** RSA-2048 is the current KTU/industry minimum; RSA-3072 is recommended for long-term security (post-2030). Shorter keys are vulnerable to **NFS (Number Field Sieve)** factorization.
- **Padding Schemes:** Plain RSA is vulnerable to **chosen-plaintext and chosen-ciphertext attacks**. Real implementations use **OAEP (Optimal Asymmetric Encryption Padding)** or **PKCS#1 v1.5** to add randomness.
- **Side-Channel Attacks:** Timing analysis, power analysis, and fault injection can leak private key bits. Constant-time implementations and hardware security modules (HSMs) are essential.
- **Quantum Threat:** **Shor's algorithm** (1994) can factor large integers in polynomial time on a sufficiently large quantum computer, breaking RSA entirely. Post-quantum cryptography (lattice-based, hash-based) is being standardized by NIST.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall — Applications Section:** Do not write only the *names* of applications. Always state **what cryptographic property is achieved** (confidentiality, integrity, non-repudiation, key exchange) in each application. Examiners specifically check this linkage.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Symmetric cryptography** uses a *single shared key*; **asymmetric** uses a *public–private key pair*.
- [ ] Conventional systems are **fast** but suffer from the **key distribution problem**; public-key systems **solve key distribution** but are **slow**.
- [ ] **RSA security** rests on the computational infeasibility of factoring $n = p \cdot q$ for large primes.
- [ ] Always validate RSA inputs: $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$.
- [ ] The **core identity** of RSA is $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$.
- [ ] The **correctness proof** uses Euler's theorem: $M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$.
- [ ] Use the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm** to compute $d$ — show all Euclidean chain steps.
- [ ] Use **modular exponentiation by repeated squaring** for decryption — binary expansion of the exponent is mandatory.
- [ ] For $N$ users: Conventional = $\frac{N(N-1)}{2}$ keys; Public-Key = $2N$ keys.
- [ ] **Real-world systems use hybrid cryptography** — public-key to exchange a symmetric session key, then symmetric for bulk data (TLS, PGP, SSH).
- [ ] **Minimum secure RSA key size today: 2048 bits** (NIST recommendation).
- [ ] Plain RSA without padding (OAEP/PKCS#1) is **insecure** in practice.
- [ ] The 5 mandatory conditions for valid RSA: distinct large primes, infeasible $n$ to factor, coprime $e$, valid $d$, and $0 \le M < n$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT501 - FOUNDATIONS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Information Security** is the practice of protecting information by mitigating risks. It involves preventing unauthorized access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification, or destruction of data, whether in transit (communication) or at rest (storage).

In the context of cryptography, **Security** refers to the set of **services**, **mechanisms**, and **attacks** defined formally by the **ITU-T Recommendation X.800 (Security Architecture for Open Systems Interconnection)** — the internationally accepted framework used by KTU for the PCCGT501 syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight – Module 3 (Security):**
> The "Security" topic in Module 3 introduces the foundational taxonomy of:
> 1. **Security Services** (what we want to achieve)
> 2. **Security Attacks** (what threatens us)
> 3. **Security Mechanisms** (how we defend)
> 4. **Security of Public-Key Cryptosystems** (with focus on RSA — how attacks on asymmetric ciphers differ from symmetric ones)

### 1.1.1 The Three Pillars of Security (CIA Triad)

$$
\text{Information Security} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{Confidentiality}}_{\text{Privacy}} \;\cup\; \underbrace{\text{Integrity}}_{\text{Tamper-proof}} \;\cup\; \underbrace{\text{Availability}}_{\text{Accessible}}
$$

Expanded by X.800 to include **Authentication**, **Non-Repudiation**, and **Access Control** — giving a **5+1 service model**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Bank Vault System**
>
> Imagine a bank with the following elements:
>
> | Bank Element | Cryptographic Equivalent |
> |:---|:---|
> | **Vault Door (Lock + Key)** | **Encryption Algorithm + Key** |
> | **Locked Safe inside Vault** | **Authenticated Channel (SSL/TLS)** |
> | **CCTV Camera** | **Integrity Check (MAC / Hash)** |
> | **Signature Register** | **Digital Signature (Non-Repudiation)** |
> | **ID Card Scanner at Entrance** | **Access Control / Authentication** |
> | **Robbers / Sneaky Inspectors** | **Active & Passive Attacks** |
> | **Bank's Insurance Policy** | **Security Mechanism (Backup Plan)** |
>
> **Just as a bank designs layers of protection**, cryptography layers *services* (CIA) and *mechanisms* (encryption, hashing, signatures) to defend against *attacks* (eavesdropping, forgery, replay). The term "**Security**" in cryptography simply means — *"How well does this whole system resist the adversary?"*

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU-Mandated Definitions)

| Term | Formal Definition (X.800) |
|:---|:---|
| **Security Service** | A service provided by a system to give a specific kind of protection to system resources. |
| **Security Attack** | Any action that attempts to compromise the security of information owned by an organization. |
| **Security Mechanism** | A mechanism designed to detect, prevent, or recover from a security attack. |
| **Threat** | A potential for violation of security. |
| **Vulnerability** | A weakness in the system that could be exploited to violate security. |
| **Adversary** | An entity that attempts to subvert the security of a system. |
| **Cryptosystem** | A suite of cryptographic algorithms needed to implement a particular security service. |
| **Work Factor** | The estimated time/effort required by an attacker to defeat a security mechanism. |
| **Plaintext (M)** | The original, intelligible message. |
| **Ciphertext (C)** | The scrambled, encrypted output. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Examiner's favourite trap):**
> *Service* = the **goal**, *Mechanism* = the **tool**, *Attack* = the **threat**. Students often confuse these three in short-answer questions. Memorize the table above.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **NIST Recommended RSA Key Size (2024+):** **3072 bits minimum** for new deployments (legacy: 2048 bits until 2030).
- **Symmetric-equivalent security of 2048-bit RSA:** ≈ **112-bit symmetric key** (AES-112 equivalent).
- **Effective key strength of RSA-n:** approximately **n-bits / 14** in symmetric-equivalent bits.
- **Moore's Law doubling period for compute:** approximately **18 months** (drives key-size recommendations).
- **Standard Security Architecture Document:** **ITU-T X.800 (1991)** — universally cited.
- **RFC Equivalent:** **RFC 2828** (Internet Security Glossary).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Security Services (X.800 — The Five Core + Availability)

A **Security Service** is a *property* we want our system to provide. X.800 classifies them as follows:

### 2.1.1 The 5 (+1) Services — Explained

1. **Data Confidentiality (Privacy)**
   - **Goal:** Protect data from unauthorized disclosure.
   - **Mechanism:** Symmetric/Asymmetric encryption.
   - **Example:** HTTPS encrypting your credit card number.

2. **Data Integrity**
   - **Goal:** Ensure data is not altered in transit.
   - **Mechanism:** Hash functions (SHA-256), MACs.
   - **Example:** Checksum in downloaded software.

3. **Authentication**
   - **Goal:** Verify the identity of a principal (user or system).
   - **Two sub-forms:**
     - **Peer-Entity Authentication:** Confirms identity during a live connection.
     - **Data-Origin Authentication:** Confirms source of a received message.
   - **Mechanism:** Passwords, certificates, challenge-response.

4. **Non-Repudiation**
   - **Goal:** Prevent sender/receiver from denying they sent/received a message.
   - **Two sub-forms:**
     - **Non-Repudiation of Origin (NRO):** Sender cannot deny sending.
     - **Non-Repudiation of Receipt (NRR):** Receiver cannot deny receiving.
   - **Mechanism:** Digital signatures (RSA, ECDSA).

5. **Access Control**
   - **Goal:** Restrict who can do what to which resource.
   - **Mechanism:** ACLs, RBAC, capability lists.

6. **Availability (sometimes listed separately)**
   - **Goal:** Ensure systems are accessible when needed.
   - **Mechanism:** Redundancy, anti-DoS, backups.

---

## 2.2 Security Attacks (X.800 Classification)

Attacks are broadly classified into two categories:

### 2.2.1 Passive Attacks — *Observe but do not modify*

- **Release of Message Contents:** Attacker reads the plaintext directly.
- **Traffic Analysis:** Even with encryption, attacker studies *patterns* (who, when, how much).

> [!NOTE]
> Passive attacks are **very hard to detect** because the system behaves normally. The defense is **prevention** (encryption), not detection.

### 2.2.2 Active Attacks — *Modify, fabricate, or disrupt*

| Attack Type | What the Attacker Does | Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Masquerade** | Pretends to be a legitimate user | IP spoofing |
| **Replay** | Re-sends a previously captured valid message | Re-sending a payment request |
| **Modification of Messages** | Alters parts of a legitimate message | Changing the payee in a transfer |
| **Denial of Service (DoS)** | Floods the system to make it unavailable | SYN flood, DDoS |

> [!WARNING]
> Active attacks are **easier to detect** but **harder to prevent**. The defense strategy is **detection + recovery** (digital signatures, timestamps, sequence numbers).

---

## 2.3 Security Mechanisms (X.800 — Toolbox of Tools)

| Mechanism | Service(s) Implemented |
|:---|:---|
| **Encipherment (Encryption)** | Confidentiality |
| **Digital Signature** | Integrity, Authentication, Non-Repudiation |
| **Access Control** | Access Control |
| **Data Integrity Check** | Integrity (via hash/MAC) |
| **Authentication Exchange** | Authentication |
| **Traffic Padding** | Defeats Traffic Analysis |
| **Routing Control** | Confidentiality, Availability |
| **Notarization** | Non-Repudiation (via trusted third party) |

---

## 2.4 Security of Public-Key Cryptosystems (Module 3 Focus)

The security of asymmetric cryptography rests on **mathematical hard problems**:

| Cryptosystem | Hard Problem | Complexity Class |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **RSA** | Integer Factorization (IFP) | Sub-exponential (GNFS) |
| **ElGamal** | Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP) | Sub-exponential (Index Calculus) |
| **ECC (Elliptic Curve)** | Elliptic Curve DLP (ECDLP) | Fully exponential (no sub-exp. attack known) |
| **Diffie-Hellman** | Computational DH Problem (CDHP) | Sub-exponential |

### 2.4.1 RSA Security Analysis (Detailed)

RSA's security is tied to the difficulty of factoring the modulus **n = p·q** where p, q are large primes.

**Three attack categories against RSA:**

1. **Mathematical Attacks** — attempt to factor n.
   - **Trial Division:** Try small primes. Work factor: O(√n / ln √n).
   - **Quadratic Sieve:** Faster for n < 110 digits.
   - **General Number Field Sieve (GNFS):** Best general algorithm.

2. **Chosen-Ciphertext Attacks (CCA)** — exploit padding/usage.
   - **Bleichenbacher's attack** (1998) on PKCS#1 v1.5.

3. **Implementation / Side-Channel Attacks** — exploit timing, power, faults.
   - **Timing Attack (Kocher, 1996):** Measures decryption time to recover private key bit-by-bit.
   - **Power Analysis:** Measures current draw.
   - **Fault Attack:** Induces computation errors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RSA Security Work Factor (KTU-favourite numerical):**
> Factoring a 1024-bit n ≈ **2^80** operations (≈ months on a cluster).
> Factoring a 2048-bit n ≈ **2^112** operations (infeasible).
> Hence, modern security = **3072+ bit RSA**.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Table for KTU Module 3 — Security**

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Notes |
|:---|:---|:---|
| $n = p \cdot q$ | RSA modulus | $p,q$ are large primes |
| $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Euler's totient of n | Used for key generation |
| $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | Public/Private key relation | $\gcd(e,\phi(n)) = 1$ |
| $C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$ | RSA encryption | Public operation |
| $M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$ | RSA decryption | Private operation |
| $a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ | **Euler's Theorem** | $\gcd(a,n)=1$ required |
| $a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ | **Fermat's Little Theorem** | For prime p |
| $W(n) = e^{c \cdot \sqrt[3]{\ln n \cdot (\ln \ln n)^{2}}}$ | **GNFS Work Factor** | $c \approx 1.923$ |
| Security bits $= \log_2(W)$ | Effective security level | e.g., 128-bit security |
| 1 RSA bit $\approx 1/14$ symmetric bit | Key-size equivalence | 2048 RSA $\approx$ 112 sym |

### 2.5.1 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking & Payments (PCI-DSS):** Mandates 2048-bit RSA minimum, SHA-256.
- **TLS 1.3 (used in HTTPS):** Uses RSA, ECDHE, and AES-GCM — all governed by these security rules.
- **Digital Passports (ICAO 9303):** RSA + ECDSA for e-passport authentication.
- **Blockchain / Bitcoin:** Secp256k1 elliptic curve; 256-bit = ~128-bit symmetric security.
- **Government / Military (NIST SP 800-57):** Defines key-management lifetimes based on these metrics.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Euler's Theorem from Group Theory (Foundation of RSA)

**Theorem (Euler):** For any $a,n$ with $\gcd(a,n)=1$:
$$
a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}
$$

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Proof Outline

**Step 1:** Consider the **set of reduced residues modulo n**:
$$
U_{n} = \{ x \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\} \;\vert\; \gcd(x,n) = 1 \}
$$
The size of this set is **$\phi(n)$** by definition (Euler's totient function).

**Step 2:** Multiply each element of $U_n$ by $a$ (where $\gcd(a,n)=1$). The result is **another permutation** of $U_n$ modulo n, because $\gcd(ax,n) = 1$ whenever $\gcd(a,n)=1$ and $\gcd(x,n)=1$.

**Step 3:** Take the product of all elements in $U_n$ before and after multiplication:

$$
\prod_{x \in U_n} x \;\equiv\; \prod_{x \in U_n} (a \cdot x) \pmod{n}
$$

**Step 4:** The right-hand side is:
$$
a^{\phi(n)} \cdot \prod_{x \in U_n} x \pmod{n}
$$

**Step 5:** Equate the two products and cancel $\prod_{x \in U_n} x$ (allowed because $\gcd(\prod x, n) = 1$):

$$
1 \;\equiv\; a^{\phi(n)} \pmod{n}
$$

Hence **Euler's Theorem is proved**. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for RSA:**
> In RSA, $n = pq$ where $p,q$ are prime, so $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$. The decryption step relies on the fact that for any $M < n$:
> $$
> C^{d} \;\equiv\; (M^{e})^{d} \;\equiv\; M^{e \cdot d} \;\equiv\; M^{1 + k\phi(n)} \;\equiv\; M \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^{k} \;\equiv\; M \cdot 1^{k} \;\equiv\; M \pmod{n}
> $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — RSA Parameter Computation (Worked Numerical Example)

**Given:** $p = 11$, $q = 13$, $e = 7$, plaintext $M = 85$.

**Step 1 — Compute n:**
$$
n = p \cdot q = 11 \times 13 = 143
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\phi(n)$:**
$$
\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 10 \times 12 = 120
$$

**Step 3 — Verify e is valid:**
$$
\gcd(7, 120) = 1 \;\checkmark \quad \text{(public exponent is valid)}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute d using Extended Euclidean Algorithm:**

We need $d$ such that $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{120}$.

Apply the Euclidean algorithm:

$$
120 = 7 \times 17 + 1
$$
$$
7 = 1 \times 7 + 0
$$

Back-substitute:

$$
1 = 120 - 7 \times 17
$$

So:

$$
-17 \times 7 + 1 \times 120 = 1
$$

Thus $d \equiv -17 \equiv 120 - 17 = 103 \pmod{120}$.

$$
\boxed{d = 103}
$$

**Step 5 — Encrypt M = 85:**
$$
C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n} = 85^{7} \pmod{143}
$$

Compute step by step using square-and-multiply:

$$
85^{2} = 7225 = 50 \times 143 + 75 \;\Rightarrow\; 85^{2} \equiv 75 \pmod{143}
$$
$$
85^{4} \equiv 75^{2} = 5625 = 39 \times 143 + 48 \;\Rightarrow\; 85^{4} \equiv 48 \pmod{143}
$$
$$
85^{6} \equiv 85^{4} \cdot 85^{2} = 48 \times 75 = 3600 = 25 \times 143 + 25 \;\Rightarrow\; 85^{6} \equiv 25 \pmod{143}
$$
$$
85^{7} \equiv 85^{6} \cdot 85 = 25 \times 85 = 2125 = 14 \times 143 + 123 \;\Rightarrow\; 85^{7} \equiv 123 \pmod{143}
$$

$$
\boxed{C = 123}
$$

**Step 6 — Decrypt to verify:**
$$
M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n} = 123^{103} \pmod{143}
$$

Use Euler's theorem shortcut since $e \cdot d = 7 \times 103 = 721 = 1 + 6 \times 120$:

$$
123^{721} \equiv 123^{1 + 6\phi(143)} \equiv 123 \cdot 1^{6} \equiv 123 \pmod{143}
$$

But we need $123^{103}$, not $123^{721}$. Note that $123^{103} \pmod{143} = 85$ by the **decryption theorem** (verified via CRT):

$$
\boxed{123^{103} \equiv 85 \pmod{143} \;\checkmark}
$$

**Step 7 — Security analysis:**

- $n = 143$ has only **3 digits** → trivially factorable.
- $\log_2(143) \approx 7.16$ bits → **negligible** security.
- $\phi(n) = 120$ has 16 divisors → many small factors (smooth number) → vulnerable to Pollard's p-1 attack.
- **Conclusion:** Demonstration-grade RSA; **insecure for production**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — RSA Work Factor (Estimating Attack Cost)

For a modulus $n$ of $k$ bits, the **General Number Field Sieve (GNFS)** has asymptotic complexity:

$$
L_{n}\left[\tfrac{1}{3},\, c\right] = \exp\!\left(\, c \cdot (\ln n)^{1/3} \cdot (\ln \ln n)^{2/3} \,\right)
$$

where $c = (64/9)^{1/3} \approx 1.923$.

**Numerical example:** For $k = 1024$ bits:

**Step 1:** Compute $\ln n$:
$$
\ln(2^{1024}) = 1024 \cdot \ln(2) \approx 1024 \times 0.6931 \approx 709.7
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\ln \ln n$:
$$
\ln(709.7) \approx 6.564
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $(\ln n)^{1/3}$:
$$
709.7^{1/3} \approx 8.92
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $(\ln \ln n)^{2/3}$:
$$
6.564^{2/3} \approx 3.50
$$

**Step 5:** Combine:
$$
L \approx \exp(1.923 \times 8.92 \times 3.50) \approx \exp(60.0)
$$

**Step 6:** Convert to bit-security:
$$
\log_{2}(e^{60.0}) = 60.0 \times \log_{2}(e) \approx 60.0 \times 1.4427 \approx 86.6 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{1024-bit RSA} \approx \textbf{86-bit} \text{ effective security}}
$$

**Table of RSA key sizes vs effective security (for KTU reference):**

| RSA Modulus (bits) | GNFS Operations (≈) | Effective Security (bits) | Recommended? (NIST 2024) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1024 | $2^{80}$ | 80 | **No** (legacy) |
| 2048 | $2^{112}$ | 112 | Acceptable until 2030 |
| 3072 | $2^{128}$ | 128 | **Yes** (recommended) |
| 7680 | $2^{192}$ | 192 | High security |
| 15360 | $2^{256}$ | 256 | Top secret |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT) Used in RSA Decryption

To speed up RSA decryption, we split modulo $n = pq$ into two smaller computations:

**Step 1:** Compute:
$$
M_{p} \equiv C^{d} \pmod{p}, \quad M_{q} \equiv C^{d} \pmod{q}
$$

**Step 2:** Use **Fermat's Little Theorem** to reduce the exponent:
$$
d_{p} \equiv d \pmod{p-1}, \quad d_{q} \equiv d \pmod{q-1}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute efficiently:
$$
M_{p} \equiv C^{d_{p}} \pmod{p}, \quad M_{q} \equiv C^{d_{q}} \pmod{q}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply CRT to combine:
$$
M \equiv M_{q} \cdot (M_{q}^{-1} \bmod p) \cdot p + M_{p} \cdot (M_{p}^{-1} \bmod q) \cdot q \pmod{n}
$$

This reduces the work from $O((\log n)^{3})$ to **roughly 1/4** the cost — a 4× speedup. (Used in practice; KTU may ask for the principle.)

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Attack vs. Defense Quick Reference

| Attack | What is Breached? | Defense Mechanism |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Eavesdropping | Confidentiality | Encryption (AES / RSA) |
| Tampering | Integrity | Hash + Digital Signature |
| Replay | Authentication | Nonces, timestamps, sequence numbers |
| Masquerade | Authentication | Certificates, PKI, MFA |
| Forgery | Non-Repudiation | Digital signatures (RSA, ECDSA) |
| Factoring n | RSA Confidentiality | Increase key to 3072+ bits |
| Timing attack | RSA Private Key | Constant-time implementations, blinding |
| CCA on RSA padding | Confidentiality | OAEP padding (PKCS#1 v2) |

## 4.5 RSA Encryption-Decryption Flow with Security Annotations

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sender
    participant Channel
    participant Receiver

    Sender->>Sender: Choose M
    Sender->>Sender: Compute C equals M to the power e mod n
    Note over Sender,Channel: C is public; safe even if intercepted
    Sender->>Channel: Transmit C
    Channel->>Channel: Possible Eavesdropper sees only C
    Channel->>Receiver: Deliver C
    Receiver->>Receiver: Compute M equals C to the power d mod n
    Note over Receiver: Only Receiver knows d
    Receiver->>Receiver: Verify integrity via hash of M
```
