# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout (Module 3 – Security):**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Service" with "Mechanism"** in 3-mark short-answer questions. *Service = goal* (e.g., Confidentiality). *Mechanism = tool* (e.g., Encipherment).
> 2. **Active vs Passive attacks**: Many students write only one example. Mention *both* — passive (release, traffic analysis) **and** active (masquerade, replay, modification, DoS).
> 3. **RSA numerical problems**: Forgetting to verify $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ loses 1–2 marks. Always show this check.
> 4. **Work Factor**: Writing "RSA-1024 is unbreakable" is wrong. State its **effective security** (~80 bits) — KTU expects numerical awareness.
> 5. **X.800** is the *only* acceptable reference framework. Do not write "SSL provides security" without naming the service.
> 6. **Timing Attack**: Many students write "we can prevent timing attacks by adding noise" — the correct answer is **constant-time implementation** and **blinding**.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Differentiate between passive and active security attacks with one example each.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Passive Attack | Active Attack |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Nature** | Monitors / observes only | Alters / fabricates data |
| **Detection** | Very difficult | Easier, but prevention is hard |
| **Effect on system** | No modification | Modifies / disrupts |
| **Defense strategy** | Prevention (encryption) | Detection + Recovery |
| **Example 1** | Release of message contents (eavesdropping) | Masquerade (IP spoofing) |
| **Example 2** | Traffic analysis | Replay attack |

Passive attacks compromise **confidentiality**; active attacks compromise **integrity, authenticity, availability, and non-repudiation**.

---

### **Q2. List any three security services defined by the OSI security architecture X.800 and the corresponding mechanism used to implement each.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| # | Security Service | Mechanism |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Data Confidentiality** | Encipherment (Encryption) |
| 2 | **Data Integrity** | Data Integrity Check (Hash + MAC) |
| 3 | **Authentication** | Authentication Exchange (challenge-response) |

*Alternative answers accepted:* Non-Repudiation → Digital Signature; Access Control → Access Control Mechanism.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU End-Semester Examination (ESE) follows **Module Internal Choice**. Two alternatives are provided below — students answer **ONE**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**With suitable examples, explain in detail the various types of passive and active security attacks as defined by the X.800 framework.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Passive Attacks** — the attacker only monitors / observes transmissions. The system is not modified.

1. **Release of Message Contents:**
   - The attacker intercepts and reads the contents of a message (e.g., reading an unencrypted email or a phone call).
   - *Defense:* Use encryption (symmetric / asymmetric).

2. **Traffic Analysis:**
   - Even if messages are encrypted, the attacker can deduce patterns — *who* is communicating, *when*, *how frequently*, and *how much data*.
   - *Defense:* Traffic padding (sending dummy messages), onion routing, mix networks.

> **Why passive attacks matter:** They are stealthy. The victim does not know they are being watched.

**Active Attacks** — the attacker modifies data, fabricates messages, or disrupts the system.

1. **Masquerade:**
   - The attacker pretends to be an authorized user.
   - *Example:* IP spoofing — forging the source IP of a packet.
   - *Defense:* Authentication mechanisms (certificates, passwords, biometrics).

2. **Replay Attack:**
   - The attacker captures a valid message and re-sends it later to get the same effect.
   - *Example:* Re-sending a previously authorized bank transaction.
   - *Defense:* Nonces, sequence numbers, timestamps in protocols.

3. **Modification of Messages:**
   - The attacker alters portions of a legitimate message in transit.
   - *Example:* Changing the destination account number in a fund transfer.
   - *Defense:* Message Authentication Codes (MAC), digital signatures, hash integrity checks.

4. **Denial of Service (DoS):**
   - The attacker floods the system with requests, making it unavailable to legitimate users.
   - *Example:* SYN flood, distributed DoS (DDoS).
   - *Defense:* Rate limiting, firewalls, redundant servers, scrubbing centers.

**Summary table:**

| Attack Type | Category | Detection Difficulty | Defense |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Release of Contents | Passive | Hard | Encryption |
| Traffic Analysis | Passive | Very hard | Padding, mix nets |
| Masquerade | Active | Medium | Authentication |
| Replay | Active | Medium | Nonces, timestamps |
| Modification | Active | Easier | MAC, digital signature |
| DoS | Active | Easiest | Rate-limit, redundancy |

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Describe the OSI Security Architecture X.800. List and explain all the security services and security mechanisms defined by it.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**The OSI Security Architecture (ITU-T X.800, 1991)** provides a systematic framework for defining security requirements and standardizing the services/mechanisms within OSI networks. It is the **foundational reference** for security in telecommunications and is the standard cited by the KTU syllabus.

**1. Security Services (Goals we want to achieve):**

| # | Service | Purpose |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Data Confidentiality** | Protect data from unauthorized disclosure. |
| 2 | **Data Integrity** | Ensure data is unchanged during transmission. |
| 3 | **Authentication** | Verify the identity of the communicating parties. |
| 4 | **Non-Repudiation** | Prevent denial of having sent or received a message. |
| 5 | **Access Control** | Restrict unauthorized access to resources. |
| 6 | **Availability** *(sometimes added)* | Ensure services remain accessible. |

**2. Security Mechanisms (Tools we use to achieve services):**

| # | Mechanism | Service(s) Implemented |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Encipherment** | Confidentiality |
| 2 | **Digital Signature** | Integrity, Authentication, Non-Repudiation |
| 3 | **Access Control** | Access Control |
| 4 | **Data Integrity** | Integrity (via hash/MAC) |
| 5 | **Authentication Exchange** | Authentication |
| 6 | **Traffic Padding** | Defeats Traffic Analysis |
| 7 | **Routing Control** | Confidentiality, Availability |
| 8 | **Notarization** | Non-Repudiation (trusted 3rd party) |

**3. Relationship between Services and Mechanisms:**

> Services are the **"WHAT"**, mechanisms are the **"HOW"**. X.800 maps them: e.g., the *Confidentiality service* is implemented by the *Encipherment mechanism*; the *Non-Repudiation service* is implemented by *Digital Signature* and *Notarization*.

**4. Significance of X.800:**

- Provides a **common vocabulary** for security discussions.
- Forms the basis for protocols like **TLS, IPSec, Kerberos**.
- Referenced in **NIST SP 800-series** publications and **ISO 27001** compliance.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the security of the RSA cryptosystem. Discuss in detail the various attacks on RSA and the corresponding defenses.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Why RSA is Considered Secure:**

The security of RSA rests on the **Integer Factorization Problem (IFP)** — given $n = p \cdot q$, finding $p$ and $q$ is computationally infeasible for large primes. Without factoring $n$, an attacker cannot derive $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ and hence cannot find the private key $d$.

**2. Types of Attacks on RSA:**

### **A. Mathematical Attacks** — Attempt to break the math.

- **Trial Division:** Test small primes. Infeasible for 1024+ bit $n$.
- **Fermat's Method:** Splits $n$ as $n = a^{2} - b^{2}$. Fast only if $p$ and $q$ are close.
- **Pollard's rho:** $O(\sqrt{p})$ — works if one factor is small.
- **Pollard's p-1:** Works if $p-1$ has only small prime factors.
- **Quadratic Sieve (QS):** Best for $n < 110$ digits.
- **General Number Field Sieve (GNFS):** Asymptotically fastest known algorithm. Sub-exponential complexity:
  $$
  L_{n}\left[\tfrac{1}{3}, c\right] = \exp\!\left(c \cdot (\ln n)^{1/3} (\ln \ln n)^{2/3}\right)
  $$

**Defense:** Use primes $p,q$ that are "safe" (e.g., $p = 2q + 1$) and of **3072+ bits** for new systems.

### **B. Chosen-Ciphertext Attacks (CCA):**

- The attacker submits chosen ciphertexts and observes plaintexts.
- **Bleichenbacher's attack (1998)** broke PKCS#1 v1.5 padding.
- **Defense:** Use **OAEP (Optimal Asymmetric Encryption Padding)** — provably secure against CCA in the random oracle model.

### **C. Side-Channel / Implementation Attacks:**

- **Timing Attack (Kocher, 1996):** Measures the time taken by RSA decryption to recover $d$ bit-by-bit.
- **Power Analysis:** Monitors current draw on smart cards.
- **Fault Attack:** Induces computation errors (e.g., voltage glitching) to extract $d$.

**Defense:** Use **constant-time algorithms** (no data-dependent branches), **blinding** (multiply ciphertext by $r^{e}$ for random $r$), and **hardware shielding**.

### **D. Protocol-Level Attacks:**

- **Common Modulus Attack:** Two users sharing $n$ but different $e$.
- **Low Exponent Attack (Håstad):** When $e = 3$ and the same message is sent to $e$ different recipients.
- **Broadcast Attack:** Recovers plaintext via CRT.

**Defense:** Use **random padding (OAEP)** and **unique $n$ per user**.

**3. Summary Table of Defenses:**

| Attack | Recommended Defense |
|:---|:---|
| Factoring | Large 3072+ bit key, safe primes |
| CCA | OAEP padding |
| Timing | Constant-time + blinding |
| Low exponent | Random padding per encryption |
| Reuse of $n$ | Unique modulus per user |

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**In an RSA cryptosystem, $p = 11$, $q = 17$, $e = 7$. Compute $n$, $\phi(n)$, and $d$. Encrypt $M = 88$ and verify by decryption. Comment on the security of this RSA system.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute n:**
$$
n = p \cdot q = 11 \times 17 = 187
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\phi(n)$:**
$$
\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 10 \times 16 = 160
$$

**Step 3 — Verify $e$ is valid:**
$$
\gcd(7, 160) = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$
(7 is a valid public exponent.)

**Step 4 — Compute $d$ using Extended Euclidean Algorithm:**

We need $d$ such that $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{160}$.

Euclidean steps:
$$
160 = 22 \times 7 + 6
$$
$$
7 = 1 \times 6 + 1
$$
$$
6 = 6 \times 1 + 0
$$

Back-substitute:
$$
1 = 7 - 1 \times 6 = 7 - 1 \times (160 - 22 \times 7) = 23 \times 7 - 1 \times 160
$$

So $d \equiv 23 \pmod{160}$.
$$
\boxed{d = 23}
$$

**Public Key:** $(n, e) = (187, 7)$ | **Private Key:** $d = 23$.

**Step 5 — Encrypt M = 88:**

$$
C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n} = 88^{7} \pmod{187}
$$

Compute by square-and-multiply:

- $88^{2} = 7744 = 41 \times 187 + 77 \;\Rightarrow\; 88^{2} \equiv 77 \pmod{187}$
- $88^{4} \equiv 77^{2} = 5929 = 31 \times 187 + 132 \;\Rightarrow\; 88^{4} \equiv 132 \pmod{187}$
- $88^{6} \equiv 88^{4} \cdot 88^{2} = 132 \times 77 = 10164 = 54 \times 187 + 66 \;\Rightarrow\; 88^{6} \equiv 66 \pmod{187}$
- $88^{7} \equiv 88^{6} \cdot 88 = 66 \times 88 = 5808 = 31 \times 187 + 11 \;\Rightarrow\; 88^{7} \equiv 11 \pmod{187}$

$$
\boxed{C = 11}
$$

**Step 6 — Decrypt to verify:**

$$
M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n} = 11^{23} \pmod{187}
$$

By Euler's theorem: $11^{160} \equiv 1 \pmod{187}$. So $11^{23}$ is computed directly:

- $11^{2} = 121$
- $11^{4} = 121^{2} = 14641 = 78 \times 187 + 55 \;\Rightarrow\; 11^{4} \equiv 55 \pmod{187}$
- $11^{8} \equiv 55^{2} = 3025 = 16 \times 187 + 33 \;\Rightarrow\; 11^{8} \equiv 33 \pmod{187}$
- $11^{16} \equiv 33^{2} = 1089 = 5 \times 187 + 154 \;\Rightarrow\; 11^{16} \equiv 154 \pmod{187}$

Now $23 = 16 + 4 + 2 + 1$:
$$
11^{23} = 11^{16} \cdot 11^{4} \cdot 11^{2} \cdot 11^{1}
$$
$$
= 154 \times 55 \times 121 \times 11 \pmod{187}
$$

Step by step:
- $154 \times 55 = 8470 = 45 \times 187 + 55 \;\Rightarrow\; 8470 \equiv 55 \pmod{187}$
- $55 \times 121 = 6655 = 35 \times 187 + 110 \;\Rightarrow\; 6655 \equiv 110 \pmod{187}$
- $110 \times 11 = 1210 = 6 \times 187 + 88 \;\Rightarrow\; 1210 \equiv 88 \pmod{187}$

$$
\boxed{11^{23} \equiv 88 \pmod{187} \;\checkmark}
$$

**Decryption verified — original plaintext recovered!**

**Step 7 — Security Comment:**

- $n = 187$ has only **3 digits** (~8 bits).
- Trivially factorable: $187 = 11 \times 17$.
- $\phi(n) = 160$ has factors: 1, 2, 4, 5, 8, 10, 16, 20, 32, 40, 80, 160 — making it a **smooth number**, vulnerable to Pollard's $p-1$ attack.
- **Effective security:** $< 30$ bits. **Completely insecure.**
- **Production requirement:** RSA needs **2048+ bit keys** (preferably 3072) to achieve $\geq 112$-bit security.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Security):**

- [x] **X.800** is the **canonical reference** for security services, attacks, and mechanisms.
- [x] **5 (+1) Security Services:** Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Non-Repudiation, Access Control (+ Availability).
- [x] **2 Attack categories:** Passive (release, traffic analysis) — hard to detect, prevent with encryption. Active (masquerade, replay, modification, DoS) — easier to detect, prevent with signatures + nonces.
- [x] **8 Security Mechanisms** (X.800): Encipherment, Digital Signature, Access Control, Data Integrity, Authentication Exchange, Traffic Padding, Routing Control, Notarization.
- [x] **RSA Security** rests on **Integer Factorization Problem (IFP)**.
- [x] **GNFS** is the best known factoring algorithm; complexity is **sub-exponential**.
- [x] **Effective security of 1024-bit RSA ≈ 80 bits** → insecure. **2048 ≈ 112 bits**; **3072 ≈ 128 bits** (recommended).
- [x] **Three attack classes on RSA:** Mathematical (factor $n$), Chosen-Ciphertext (Bleichenbacher), Side-Channel (timing, power, fault).
- [x] **Defenses:** Larger keys, OAEP padding, constant-time algorithms, blinding.
- [x] **Euler's Theorem** $a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ — the mathematical foundation of RSA correctness.
- [x] **CRT** speeds up RSA decryption by 4×.
- [x] **Key equivalence:** 1 RSA bit ≈ 1/14 symmetric bit (rough rule of thumb).
- [x] **Standard:** **NIST SP 800-57** governs key management lifetimes; **FIPS 186-4** governs digital signatures.
- [x] **Remember:** "Service" is the *goal*, "Mechanism" is the *tool*, "Attack" is the *threat*. Never confuse them in short answers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For any RSA problem, **always show**:
> (i) computation of $n$,
> (ii) computation of $\phi(n)$,
> (iii) **verification that $\gcd(e, \phi(n))=1$**,
> (iv) Extended Euclidean algorithm for $d$,
> (v) encryption,
> (vi) decryption verification,
> (vii) **a security comment** linking key size to effective security bits. This 7-step structure is what KTU examiners reward with full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Attacks, ElGamal Cryptosystem - Algorithm, Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange - Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT501 - FOUNDATIONS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Attacks, ElGamal Cryptosystem - Algorithm, Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange - Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cryptographic Attacks — The Adversarial Model

A **cryptographic attack** is any attempt by an adversary (Eve) to break a cryptosystem by exploiting weaknesses in the algorithm, the key, the protocol, or the implementation. The security of a cryptosystem is evaluated against well-defined attack models that specify what information the adversary has access to.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** For Module 3, you must clearly distinguish between the *attack types* (classified by what Eve knows) and the *attack goals* (interruption, interception, modification, fabrication — the classic **STRIDE** model from network security).

### The Six Classical Attack Categories

| # | Attack Type | What Eve Has Access To | Difficulty for Eve |
|---|------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Ciphertext-Only Attack (COA)** | Only the ciphertext(s) | Hardest for Eve |
| 2 | **Known-Plaintext Attack (KPA)** | Several (plaintext, ciphertext) pairs | Moderate |
| 3 | **Chosen-Plaintext Attack (CPA)** | Ciphertexts for plaintexts of *her choice* | Easier for Eve |
| 4 | **Chosen-Ciphertext Attack (CCA)** | Plaintexts for ciphertexts of *her choice* | Even easier |
| 5 | **Adaptive Chosen-Plaintext Attack (ACPA)** | CPA + can adapt queries based on prior answers | Very strong |
| 6 | **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** | Intercepts, reads, and *modifies* messages in transit | Protocol-level |

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy: "The Locked Mailbox"

Imagine Alice and Bob communicate through a **locked mailbox** (the cryptosystem).

- **COA**: Eve is a *garbage collector* — she only sees the sealed envelopes thrown away.
- **KPA**: Eve is a *post office clerk* who has read a few old letters AND their sealed versions, so she begins to spot patterns.
- **CPA**: Eve has *briefly borrowed the key* in the past and can now lock her own forged letters and watch how Alice seals them.
- **CCA**: Eve has a *duplicate mailbox*; she can submit locked envelopes to Bob's twin and read what comes out before forwarding the real one.
- **MITM**: Eve sits *on the road between* Alice and Bob, impersonating each to the other.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Definition:** A cryptosystem is said to be **semantically secure** if a *Chosen-Plaintext Attack* (CPA) cannot, in polynomial time, distinguish between the encryptions of two chosen plaintexts with probability significantly better than $\frac{1}{2}$. ElGamal (with random $k$) achieves IND-CPA security under the **Decisional Diffie-Hellman (DDH)** assumption.

---

## 1.2 ElGamal Cryptosystem — Definition

The **ElGamal Cryptosystem** is a public-key encryption scheme proposed by Taher Elgamal in **1985**. Its security relies on the **hardness of the Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** in a finite cyclic group. Unlike RSA, ElGamal is **probabilistic** — encrypting the same plaintext twice yields *different ciphertexts*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (verbatim style):**
> *"The ElGamal public-key cryptosystem is a non-deterministic encryption algorithm defined over a finite cyclic group $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$, where the security of the system is reducible to the intractability of computing discrete logarithms modulo a large prime $p$."*

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy: "The Magic Inkwell"

- The **public key** is a *magical inkwell* everyone can use. Anyone can dip a quill in it and write a message.
- But the ink is **chameleon-colored** — every dip produces a *different shade* (because of random $k$), so two identical messages look different.
- Only the person with the **secret formula** (private key $x$) can reverse the chameleon process and recover the original ink.

---

## 1.3 Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange — Definition

The **Diffie-Hellman (DH) Key Exchange** is a *key agreement protocol* (not an encryption scheme) that allows two parties, **Alice and Bob**, over an *insecure channel*, to establish a **shared secret key** without ever transmitting the key itself. Invented by Whitfield Diffie and Martin Hellman in **1976**, it is the conceptual ancestor of all modern public-key cryptography.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Fact:** Diffie-Hellman is a **key-exchange** (agreement) protocol, *not* an encryption or signing scheme. It produces a *shared symmetric key* that is then used with a fast cipher like AES.

### 🧠 Intuition: "The Paint-Mixing Trick"

- Alice and Bob publicly agree on a common **yellow paint** (the public parameters $p, g$).
- Alice secretly mixes yellow with her **private red** $\Rightarrow$ **orange** (sends to Bob).
- Bob secretly mixes yellow with his **private blue** $\Rightarrow$ **green** (sends to Alice).
- Alice takes Bob's green + her private red $\Rightarrow$ **brown** (shared secret).
- Bob takes Alice's orange + his private blue $\Rightarrow$ **brown** (same shared secret).
- A spy (Eve) sees yellow, orange, green — but cannot reverse the mix to get red or blue because of the **mixing problem** (Diffie-Hellman problem).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deeper Look at Attack Types — The Adversary's Power

A proper KTU answer must explain *what Eve knows*, *what Eve can do*, and *what Eve is trying to achieve*.

### 2.1.1 Attacks Classified by **Information Available to Eve**

1. **Ciphertext-Only Attack (COA)** — Eve observes $C_1, C_2, \dots, C_n$. She must recover $M_i$ or the key $K$. *Example: Classical Caesar cipher attack by frequency analysis.*
2. **Known-Plaintext Attack (KPA)** — Eve has pairs $(M_i, C_i)$ for several $i$. *Example: WWII cryptanalysis of Enigma using known "Heil Hitler" salutes.*
3. **Chosen-Plaintext Attack (CPA)** — Eve can *temporarily* obtain encryptions of her chosen plaintexts. *Example: WWII US Navy code-breaking against Japanese PURPLE.*
4. **Chosen-Ciphertext Attack (CCA)** — Eve submits chosen ciphertexts to a *decryption oracle* and gets the plaintexts back. *Example: RSA padding-oracle attacks (Bleichenbacher, 1998).*
5. **Adaptive Attacks** — Eve's queries depend on previous oracle replies.
6. **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** — Eve *inserts herself* into the channel, impersonating each party to the other. *Example: DH key exchange without authentication.*

### 2.1.2 Attacks Classified by **Goal**

- **Total Break** — Recover the secret key.
- **Universal Forgery** — Forge a valid ciphertext for any plaintext.
- **Selective Forgery** — Forge a valid ciphertext for a chosen plaintext.
- **Existential Forgery** — Forge *some* (plaintext, ciphertext) pair.
- **Distinguishing Attack** — Tell encryptions of $M_0$ from $M_1$ (breaks IND-CPA).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important for ElGamal?** ElGamal *without* message authentication is **malleable**: given $C = (c_1, c_2)$, an attacker can compute $C' = (c_1, 2 \cdot c_2 \bmod p)$ which decrypts to $2m$. Hence ElGamal is **not IND-CCA secure** in its basic form, but it *is* IND-CPA secure under the **DDH assumption**.

### 2.1.3 The **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** — Foundation of Both Schemes

For a prime $p$, generator $g$ of $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$, and $y \in \mathbb{Z}_p^*$:

$$\text{Given } y = g^x \bmod p, \text{ find } x.$$

This is conjectured to be **computationally infeasible** for primes $p \geq 2048$ bits (or more precisely, $|p| \geq 224$ bits for special-form primes and $\geq 2048$ for safe primes).

### 2.1.4 Related Hardness Assumptions

| Assumption | Statement | Used In |
|------------|-----------|---------|
| **DL (Discrete Log)** | $x \leftarrow \log_g y$ is hard | ElGamal, DH |
| **CDH (Computational DH)** | Given $(g^a, g^b)$, compute $g^{ab}$ | DH security |
| **DDH (Decisional DH)** | $(g^a, g^b, g^{ab})$ indistinguishable from $(g^a, g^b, g^c)$ | ElGamal IND-CPA |

---

## 2.2 The ElGamal Cryptosystem — Algorithm Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Key Generation (Run by Bob)

1. Generate a large prime $p$.
2. Choose a generator $g$ of the multiplicative group $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$.
3. Select a random integer $x$ where $1 \leq x \leq p-2$ (this is the **private key**).
4. Compute $y \equiv g^x \pmod{p}$.
5. Public key = $(p, g, y)$; Private key = $x$.

### 2.2.2 Encryption (Run by Alice, who wants to send $m$ to Bob)

1. Obtain Bob's authentic public key $(p, g, y)$.
2. Choose a fresh random integer $k$ where $1 \leq k \leq p-2$, with $\gcd(k, p-1) = 1$.
3. Compute $c_1 \equiv g^k \pmod{p}$.
4. Compute $c_2 \equiv m \cdot y^k \pmod{p}$.
5. Ciphertext $C = (c_1, c_2)$.

### 2.2.3 Decryption (Run by Bob)

1. Use private key $x$ to compute $s \equiv c_1^{\,x} \pmod{p}$.
2. Compute the modular inverse $s^{-1} \bmod p$.
3. Recover $m \equiv c_2 \cdot s^{-1} \pmod{p}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does decryption work?** Because $s \equiv c_1^x \equiv g^{kx} \equiv (g^x)^k \equiv y^k \pmod{p}$, so $c_2 \cdot s^{-1} \equiv m \cdot y^k \cdot (y^k)^{-1} \equiv m \pmod{p}$.

---

## 2.3 The Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange — Algorithm Breakdown

### 2.3.1 Public Parameters (Agreed by Alice and Bob over an *insecure* channel)

- A large prime $p$.
- A generator $g$ of $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$ (or a suitable subgroup of prime order $q$).

### 2.3.2 Private and Public Values

| Party | Private (Secret) | Public (Transmitted) |
|-------|------------------|----------------------|
| **Alice** | $a$ (random, $1 \leq a \leq p-2$) | $A = g^a \bmod p$ |
| **Bob**   | $b$ (random, $1 \leq b \leq p-2$) | $B = g^b \bmod p$ |

### 2.3.3 Key Computation

- Alice computes: $K = B^a \bmod p = g^{ba} \bmod p$.
- Bob computes:   $K = A^b \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p$.
- They now share the **same symmetric key** $K$.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this table — these equations appear in nearly every KTU question on Module 3.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Notation Notes |
|---|---------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Discrete Logarithm | $y = g^x \bmod p \Rightarrow x = \log_g y$ | $x \in \mathbb{Z}_{p-1}$ |
| 2 | ElGamal Public Key | $y = g^x \bmod p$ | $x$ is private, $y$ is public |
| 3 | ElGamal Encryption | $c_1 = g^k \bmod p$ | $k$ is a **fresh random** integer |
| 4 | ElGamal Encryption | $c_2 = m \cdot y^k \bmod p$ | $m$ is plaintext message |
| 5 | ElGamal Ciphertext | $C = (c_1, c_2)$ | Size is $2 \cdot \lvert p \rvert$ bits |
| 6 | ElGamal Shared Secret | $s = c_1^{\,x} = g^{kx} = y^k \bmod p$ | Used to undo the mask |
| 7 | ElGamal Decryption | $m = c_2 \cdot s^{-1} \bmod p$ | $s^{-1}$ is modular inverse of $s$ mod $p$ |
| 8 | DH Public Value (Alice) | $A = g^a \bmod p$ | $a$ kept secret |
| 9 | DH Public Value (Bob) | $B = g^b \bmod p$ | $b$ kept secret |
| 10 | DH Shared Secret (Alice) | $K = B^a \bmod p$ | Equals $g^{ab} \bmod p$ |
| 11 | DH Shared Secret (Bob) | $K = A^b \bmod p$ | Same value as Alice's $K$ |
| 12 | CDH Problem | Given $(g^a, g^b)$, compute $g^{ab}$ | Conjectured hard |
| 13 | DDH Problem | Distinguish $(g^a, g^b, g^{ab})$ from $(g^a, g^b, g^c)$ | Easier than CDH |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ElGamal** is the cryptographic core of **GNU Privacy Guard (GPG)**, digital signature standards (DSA — which is *ElGamal signatures*), and many blockchain voting schemes.
- **Diffie-Hellman** is used in **TLS 1.2/1.3 handshakes** (DHE and ECDHE cipher suites), **IPsec IKEv2**, and **SSH key agreement**. Modern variants use **Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman (ECDH)** for smaller key sizes with equivalent security.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 ElGamal — Complete Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU exam tip:** ElGamal questions with *small primes* (like $p = 23$ or $p = 71$) are extremely common. Practice the full key-gen → encrypt → decrypt cycle with these.

### Problem
Use the ElGamal public-key cryptosystem with public parameters $p = 23$, $g = 5$, and Bob's public key $y = 10$. (Assume Bob's private key is $x = 3$, which you can verify.)
Alice wants to send the plaintext $m = 7$ using the random integer $k = 7$.
Show the encryption and decryption steps.

---

### Step 1: Verify Bob's Public Key (Sanity Check)

Bob's public key is computed as:

$$y \equiv g^x \pmod{p}$$

$$\Rightarrow y \equiv 5^{3} \pmod{23}$$

$$\Rightarrow 5^1 = 5, \quad 5^2 = 25 \equiv 2, \quad 5^3 = 5^2 \cdot 5 \equiv 2 \cdot 5 = 10 \pmod{23}$$

$$\therefore y = 10 \pmod{23} \quad \checkmark$$

This confirms Bob's private key is $x = 3$.

---

### Step 2: Alice's Encryption of $m = 7$

**Sub-step 2a — Compute $c_1$:**

$$c_1 \equiv g^k \pmod{p} = 5^{7} \pmod{23}$$

We compute powers of $5$ modulo $23$ by repeated squaring:

$$5^1 \equiv 5 \pmod{23}$$

$$5^2 \equiv 25 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}$$

$$5^4 \equiv (5^2)^2 \equiv 2^2 = 4 \pmod{23}$$

$$5^7 = 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 5^1 \equiv 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 = 40 \pmod{23}$$

$$40 = 23 \cdot 1 + 17 \Rightarrow 5^7 \equiv 17 \pmod{23}$$

$$\therefore c_1 = 17$$

**Sub-step 2b — Compute $c_2$:**

$$c_2 \equiv m \cdot y^k \pmod{p} = 7 \cdot 10^{7} \pmod{23}$$

Compute $10^7 \bmod 23$ by repeated squaring:

$$10^1 \equiv 10 \pmod{23}$$

$$10^2 \equiv 100 \equiv 100 - 4 \cdot 23 = 100 - 92 = 8 \pmod{23}$$

$$10^4 \equiv (10^2)^2 \equiv 8^2 = 64 \equiv 64 - 2 \cdot 23 = 18 \pmod{23}$$

$$10^7 = 10^4 \cdot 10^2 \cdot 10^1 \equiv 18 \cdot 8 \cdot 10 = 1440 \pmod{23}$$

$$1440 \div 23 = 62 \text{ remainder } 14 \quad (\text{since } 62 \cdot 23 = 1426, \; 1440 - 1426 = 14)$$

$$10^7 \equiv 14 \pmod{23}$$

Now:

$$c_2 = 7 \cdot 14 = 98 \pmod{23}$$

$$98 = 4 \cdot 23 + 6 \Rightarrow 98 \equiv 6 \pmod{23}$$

$$\therefore c_2 = 6$$

**Ciphertext transmitted:** $C = (c_1, c_2) = (17, 6)$.

---

### Step 3: Bob's Decryption of $C = (17, 6)$

**Sub-step 3a — Compute the shared secret $s$:**

$$s \equiv c_1^{\,x} \pmod{p} = 17^{3} \pmod{23}$$

$$17^2 = 289 = 12 \cdot 23 + 13 \equiv 13 \pmod{23}$$

$$17^3 = 17^2 \cdot 17 \equiv 13 \cdot 17 = 221 \pmod{23}$$

$$221 = 9 \cdot 23 + 14 \quad (\text{since } 9 \cdot 23 = 207, \; 221 - 207 = 14)$$

$$\therefore s = 14$$

**Sub-step 3b — Compute $s^{-1} \bmod 23$:**

We need $14 \cdot s^{-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{23}$.

Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:

$$23 = 1 \cdot 14 + 9$$

$$14 = 1 \cdot 9 + 5$$

$$9 = 1 \cdot 5 + 4$$

$$5 = 1 \cdot 4 + 1$$

Back-substitute:

$$1 = 5 - 1 \cdot 4$$

$$= 5 - 1 \cdot (9 - 1 \cdot 5) = 2 \cdot 5 - 9$$

$$= 2 \cdot (14 - 9) - 9 = 2 \cdot 14 - 3 \cdot 9$$

$$= 2 \cdot 14 - 3 \cdot (23 - 14) = 5 \cdot 14 - 3 \cdot 23$$

Therefore:

$$14 \cdot 5 \equiv 1 \pmod{23} \Rightarrow s^{-1} = 5$$

**Sub-step 3c — Recover $m$:**

$$m \equiv c_2 \cdot s^{-1} \pmod{23} = 6 \cdot 5 = 30 \pmod{23}$$

$$30 = 1 \cdot 23 + 7$$

$$\therefore m = 7 \quad \checkmark \text{ (Original plaintext recovered!)}$$

---

## 3.2 Diffie-Hellman — Complete Worked Numerical Example

### Problem
Alice and Bob agree on public parameters $p = 23$ and $g = 5$. Alice chooses $a = 6$ and Bob chooses $b = 15$. Compute the shared secret key $K$ from both sides.

---

### Step 1: Verify $g = 5$ is a Generator of $\mathbb{Z}_{23}^*$

The order of $\mathbb{Z}_{23}^*$ is $\phi(23) = 22$. So 5 must generate all 22 non-zero residues. (For exam purposes, this is given; for a rigorous check, one verifies $5^{22/q} \not\equiv 1 \pmod{23}$ for all prime divisors $q$ of 22, i.e., $q = 2, 11$.)

$$5^{11} \pmod{23} = ?$$

We had $5^2 \equiv 2$ and $5^4 \equiv 4$ earlier. Continuing:

$$5^8 \equiv (5^4)^2 \equiv 4^2 = 16 \pmod{23}$$

$$5^{11} = 5^8 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 5^1 \equiv 16 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 = 160 \pmod{23}$$

$$160 = 6 \cdot 23 + 22 \Rightarrow 160 \equiv 22 \equiv -1 \pmod{23}$$

Since $5^{11} \equiv -1 \neq 1$ and $5^{2} = 2 \neq 1$, **5 is a generator of $\mathbb{Z}_{23}^*$**.

---

### Step 2: Alice Computes Her Public Value $A$

$$A \equiv g^a \pmod{p} = 5^{6} \pmod{23}$$

$$5^6 = 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \equiv 4 \cdot 2 = 8 \pmod{23}$$

$$\therefore A = 8$$

Alice **sends $A = 8$ to Bob** over the insecure channel.

---

### Step 3: Bob Computes His Public Value $B$

$$B \equiv g^b \pmod{p} = 5^{15} \pmod{23}$$

$$5^{15} = 5^8 \cdot 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 5^1 \equiv 16 \cdot 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 = 640 \pmod{23}$$

$$640 = 27 \cdot 23 + 19 \quad (\text{since } 27 \cdot 23 = 621, \; 640 - 621 = 19)$$

$$\therefore B = 19$$

Bob **sends $B = 19$ to Alice** over the insecure channel.

---

### Step 4: Alice Computes the Shared Secret

$$K_A \equiv B^a \pmod{p} = 19^{6} \pmod{23}$$

Repeated squaring of 19 mod 23:

$$19^1 \equiv 19 \equiv -4 \pmod{23}$$

$$19^2 \equiv 16 \pmod{23}$$

$$19^4 \equiv (19^2)^2 \equiv 16^2 = 256 \equiv 256 - 11 \cdot 23 = 256 - 253 = 3 \pmod{23}$$

$$19^6 = 19^4 \cdot 19^2 \equiv 3 \cdot 16 = 48 \equiv 48 - 2 \cdot 23 = 2 \pmod{23}$$

$$\therefore K_A = 2$$

---

### Step 5: Bob Computes the Shared Secret

$$K_B \equiv A^b \pmod{p} = 8^{15} \pmod{23}$$

Repeated squaring of 8 mod 23:

$$8^1 \equiv 8 \pmod{23}$$

$$8^2 = 64 \equiv 64 - 2 \cdot 23 = 18 \pmod{23}$$

$$8^4 \equiv 18^2 = 324 \equiv 324 - 14 \cdot 23 = 324 - 322 = 2 \pmod{23}$$

$$8^8 \equiv (8^4)^2 \equiv 2^2 = 4 \pmod{23}$$

$$8^{15} = 8^8 \cdot 8^4 \cdot 8^2 \cdot 8^1 \equiv 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 18 \cdot 8 \pmod{23}$$

$$= (4 \cdot 2) \cdot (18 \cdot 8) = 8 \cdot 144 \pmod{23}$$

$$144 = 6 \cdot 23 + 6 \equiv 6 \pmod{23}$$

$$8 \cdot 6 = 48 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}$$

$$\therefore K_B = 2 \quad \checkmark$$

**Both parties now share the secret key $K = 2$**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: ElGamal vs. Diffie-Hellman

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's gold:** A side-by-side comparison table is a *favourite 7-mark sub-question* in KTU. Memorize the distinctions below.

| Feature | ElGamal | Diffie-Hellman |
|---------|---------|----------------|
| **Purpose** | Public-key *encryption* | Public-key *agreement* (key exchange) |
| **Output** | Ciphertext for a single message | A shared symmetric key |
| **Randomness per session** | Yes — fresh $k$ each encryption | Yes — fresh $a, b$ each run |
| **Direction of communication** | One-way ($A \to B$) | Two-way ($A \leftrightarrow B$) |
| **Output size vs. plaintext** | $C = (c_1, c_2)$ is *twice* the size of $m$ | $K$ is one group element |
| **Hardness assumption** | Discrete Log (and DDH for IND-CPA) | Computational Diffie-Hellman (CDH) |
| **Verifies integrity / authenticates?** | No (malleable) | No — vulnerable to MITM |
| **Ciphertext recovery formula** | $m = c_2 \cdot (c_1^{\,x})^{-1} \bmod p$ | N/A — produces a key, not a message |
| **Inverse exponent needed?** | Yes — modular inverse of $c_1^x$ | No — both sides use their *own* secret |
| **Year / Inventor** | Taher Elgamal, 1985 | Diffie & Hellman, 1976 |
| **Modern descendant** | DSA signatures, GPG | ECDHE in TLS 1.3 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Cryptographic Attacks

```mermaid
graph TD
    attacksRoot["Cryptographic Attacks"]
    attacksRoot --> infoRoot["Classified by INFORMATION Available to Eve"]
    attacksRoot --> goalRoot["Classified by GOAL of Eve"]

    infoRoot --> coaNode["COA - Ciphertext Only"]
    infoRoot --> kpaNode["KPA - Known Plaintext"]
    infoRoot --> cpaNode["CPA - Chosen Plaintext"]
    infoRoot --> ccaNode["CCA - Chosen Ciphertext"]
    infoRoot --> acpaNode["ACPA - Adaptive CPA"]
    infoRoot --> mitmNode["MITM - Man in the Middle"]

    goalRoot --> totNode["Total Break - Recover Key"]
    goalRoot --> univNode["Universal Forgery"]
    goalRoot --> selNode["Selective Forgery"]
    goalRoot --> exisNode["Existential Forgery"]
    goalRoot --> distNode["Distinguishing Attack IND-CPA"]

    coaNode -.-> freq["Example - Frequency Analysis on Caesar Cipher"]
    kpaNode -.-> enigma["Example - WWII Enigma using known salutes"]
    cpaNode -.-> purp["Example - US Navy vs Japanese PURPLE"]
    ccaNode -.-> rsapa["Example - Bleichenbacher RSA padding attack"]
    mitmNode -.-> dhwarn["Example - DH key exchange without authentication"]

    classDef infoClass fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef goalClass fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000
    classDef examClass fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    class coaNode,kpaNode,cpaNode,ccaNode,acpaNode,mitmNode infoClass
    class totNode,univNode,selNode,exisNode,distNode goalClass
    class freq,enigma,purp,rsapa,dhwarn examClass
```

**Reading the diagram:** The taxonomy splits attacks into two orthogonal axes — *what Eve knows* (information) and *what Eve wants* (goal). The dashed arrows show classic historical examples.

---

## 4.2 ElGamal Cryptosystem — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph setupNode["KEY GENERATION - Bob"]
        p1["Pick large prime p"]
        g1["Pick generator g of Zp*"]
        x1["Pick secret x in 2..p-2"]
        y1["Compute y = g to the x mod p"]
        pkOut["PUBLIC KEY - p, g, y"]
        skOut["PRIVATE KEY - x"]
        p1 --> g1 --> x1 --> y1
        y1 --> pkOut
        y1 --> skOut
    end

    subgraph encNode["ENCRYPTION - Alice"]
        mIn["Plaintext m in Zp*"]
        kPick["Pick fresh random k with gcd k, p-1 = 1"]
        c1c["c1 = g to the k mod p"]
        c2c["c2 = m times y to the k mod p"]
        cOut["CIPHERTEXT - c1, c2"]
        mIn --> kPick --> c1c
        mIn --> c2c
        c1c --> cOut
        c2c --> cOut
    end

    subgraph decNode["DECRYPTION - Bob"]
        cIn["Receive c1, c2"]
        sComp["s = c1 to the x mod p"]
        invComp["s to the -1 mod p using Extended Euclid"]
        mRec["m = c2 times s to the -1 mod p"]
        mOut["RECOVERED m"]
        cIn --> sComp --> invComp --> mRec --> mOut
    end

    pkOut -- "Alice fetches via authenticated channel" --> kPick
    skOut -- "Bob uses only locally" --> sComp
    cOut -- "Sent over insecure channel" --> cIn
```

**Reading the diagram:** Three dashed-line trust boundaries separate key generation (Bob's local), encryption (Alice's side, using Bob's public key), and decryption (Bob's side, using his private key). Note that $y$ is the *only* piece Bob exposes; $x$ never leaves his machine.

---

## 4.3 Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange — Sequence Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant AlicePub as Alice
    participant EveNode as Eve - Insecure Channel
    participant BobPub as Bob

    Note over AlicePub, BobPub: BOTH AGREE PUBLICLY on p and g

    AlicePub->>AlicePub: Pick private a in 2..p-2
    BobPub->>BobPub: Pick private b in 2..p-2

    AlicePub->>AlicePub: Compute A = g to the a mod p
    BobPub->>BobPub: Compute B = g to the b mod p

    AlicePub-->>EveNode: Send A
    EveNode-->>BobPub: Forward A
    BobPub-->>EveNode: Send B
    EveNode-->>AlicePub: Forward B

    Note over EveNode: Eve sees p, g, A, B but NOT a, b, K

    AlicePub->>AlicePub: K = B to the a mod p
    BobPub->>BobPub: K = A to the b mod p

    Note over AlicePub, BobPub: BOTH share the SAME secret K = g to the ab mod p
```

**Reading the diagram:** The sequence is intentionally *symmetric* — both Alice and Bob perform identical arithmetic in their own columns. Eve, drawn in the middle, can *observe* $A$ and $B$ but cannot compute $K$ without solving the CDH problem.

> [!WARNING]
> **MITM Vulnerability:** In the diagram above, if Eve is **active** (not just passive), she can substitute her own $A'$ for Alice's $A$ and her own $B'$ for Bob's $B$, ending up with a *shared secret with Alice* and *another shared secret with Bob* — completely breaking the protocol. The fix is **authenticated DH**, where $A$ and $B$ are signed with RSA or ECDSA certificates (as in TLS 1.3 with ECDHE).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

> **Differentiate between Ciphertext-Only Attack (COA) and Known-Plaintext Attack (KPA). Give one real-world example of each.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks distribution)

| Component | Marks | Content |
|-----------|-------|---------|
| COA definition | 1 | "In a Ciphertext-Only Attack, the adversary has access only to a set of intercepted ciphertexts and attempts to deduce the plaintexts or the key." |
| KPA definition | 1 | "In a Known-Plaintext Attack, the adversary possesses both the plaintext and its corresponding ciphertext for several messages, which allows recovery of the key or algorithm." |
| Examples | 1 | COA: frequency analysis on Caesar cipher. KPA: Cryptanalysis of the German Enigma using known phrases like "Heil Hitler." |

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **List any three security attacks possible on the Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange algorithm. How is the Man-in-the-Middle attack prevented in practice?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks distribution)

| Component | Marks | Content |
|-----------|-------|---------|
| Three attacks | 1.5 | 1. **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** 2. **Replay Attack** 3. **Small Subgroup Attack** (forcing values into a small subgroup so discrete log becomes easy) |
| MITM prevention | 1.5 | By using **authenticated DH** — each party signs its public value $A$ or $B$ with a long-term private key (e.g., RSA, ECDSA), or uses **certificates** issued by a trusted CA, as in TLS 1.3 with ECDHE. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]`

> **(a)** Explain the ElGamal Public-Key Cryptosystem. Describe the key generation, encryption, and decryption algorithms with suitable mathematical expressions. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Perform ElGamal encryption and decryption for the following parameters: prime $p = 17$, generator $g = 3$, Bob's private key $x = 4$, Alice's plaintext $m = 10$, and Alice's random integer $k = 5$. Verify the result. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** ElGamal is a probabilistic public-key encryption scheme proposed by Taher ElGamal (1985). Its security is based on the hardness of the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** in a finite cyclic group $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$.

**Key Generation (Run by Bob) (2 marks):**

1. Generate a large prime $p$.
2. Choose a primitive root $g$ of $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$.
3. Choose integer $x$ with $1 \le x \le p-2$ as the **private key**.
4. Compute $y \equiv g^x \pmod{p}$.
5. Publish public key $(p, g, y)$; keep $x$ secret.

**Encryption (Run by Alice, message $m$) (2 marks):**

1. Fetch Bob's authentic public key $(p, g, y)$.
2. Pick a fresh random $k$ with $\gcd(k, p-1) = 1$.
3. $c_1 \equiv g^k \pmod{p}$.
4. $c_2 \equiv m \cdot y^k \pmod{p}$.
5. Send $C = (c_1, c_2)$.

**Decryption (Run by Bob) (2 marks):**

1. $s \equiv c_1^{\,x} \pmod{p}$.
2. $m \equiv c_2 \cdot s^{-1} \pmod{p}$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute Bob's public key $y$ (1 mark):**

$$y \equiv g^x \pmod{p} = 3^4 \pmod{17}$$

$$3^2 = 9, \quad 3^4 = 9^2 = 81 \equiv 81 - 4 \cdot 17 = 81 - 68 = 13 \pmod{17}$$

$$\therefore y = 13$$

**Step 2: Encryption of $m = 10$ with $k = 5$ (3 marks):**

$$c_1 \equiv g^k \pmod{p} = 3^5 \pmod{17}$$

$$3^5 = 3^4 \cdot 3 = 13 \cdot 3 = 39 \equiv 39 - 2 \cdot 17 = 5 \pmod{17}$$

$$\therefore c_1 = 5$$

$$c_2 \equiv m \cdot y^k \pmod{p} = 10 \cdot 13^5 \pmod{17}$$

Compute $13^5 \bmod 17$:

$$13 \equiv -4 \pmod{17} \Rightarrow 13^5 \equiv (-4)^5 = -1024 \pmod{17}$$

$$1024 \div 17 = 60 \text{ remainder } 4 \Rightarrow 1024 \equiv 4 \pmod{17}$$

$$-1024 \equiv -4 \equiv 13 \pmod{17}$$

$$\therefore 13^5 \equiv 13 \pmod{17}$$

$$c_2 = 10 \cdot 13 = 130 \pmod{17}$$

$$130 = 7 \cdot 17 + 11 \Rightarrow 130 \equiv 11 \pmod{17}$$

$$\therefore c_2 = 11$$

**Ciphertext:** $C = (5, 11)$.

**Step 3: Decryption (3 marks):**

$$s \equiv c_1^{\,x} \pmod{p} = 5^4 \pmod{17}$$

$$5^2 = 25 \equiv 8 \pmod{17}$$

$$5^4 = (5^2)^2 = 8^2 = 64 \equiv 64 - 3 \cdot 17 = 64 - 51 = 13 \pmod{17}$$

$$\therefore s = 13$$

Find $s^{-1} \bmod 17$:

We need $13 \cdot s^{-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{17}$.

Try $s^{-1} = 4$: $13 \cdot 4 = 52 = 3 \cdot 17 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{17} \checkmark$

$$\therefore s^{-1} = 4$$

Recover $m$:

$$m \equiv c_2 \cdot s^{-1} \pmod{17} = 11 \cdot 4 = 44 \equiv 44 - 2 \cdot 17 = 10 \pmod{17}$$

$$\therefore m = 10 \quad \checkmark \text{ — Original plaintext recovered!}$$

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]`

> **(a)** Explain the Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange algorithm in detail, with a clear sequence of messages exchanged. Discuss its vulnerability to the Man-in-the-Middle attack and how it can be prevented. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Two users Alice and Bob agree on $p = 11$ and $g = 2$. Alice chooses $a = 3$ and Bob chooses $b = 4$. Compute the shared secret key on both sides. Identify the security of the chosen prime $p$ and suggest a suitable safe prime for production use. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Algorithm Description (4 marks):**

The Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange allows two parties to derive a **shared symmetric secret** over an *insecure* channel without ever sending the key itself. It is based on the **Diffie-Hellman Problem (DHP)** — given $g^a \bmod p$ and $g^b \bmod p$, computing $g^{ab} \bmod p$ is hard.

**Sequence of steps:**

1. Alice and Bob publicly agree on a large prime $p$ and a generator $g$ of $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$.
2. Alice picks a secret integer $a$ and computes $A = g^a \bmod p$. She sends $A$ to Bob.
3. Bob picks a secret integer $b$ and computes $B = g^b \bmod p$. He sends $B$ to Alice.
4. Alice computes $K = B^a \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p$.
5. Bob computes $K = A^b \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p$.
6. Both share the key $K$.

**MITM Vulnerability (2 marks):**

An active adversary Eve can intercept $A$ and $B$, substitute her own $A'$ (pretending to be Bob to Alice) and $B'$ (pretending to be Alice to Bob), and end up with two *different* shared secrets — one with Alice, one with Bob. She can read/modify all subsequent traffic.

**Prevention (1 mark):**

Use **authenticated DH** — sign $A$ and $B$ with a long-term private key (RSA or ECDSA), or use **certificates** from a trusted CA. Modern TLS 1.3 uses **ECDHE** with certificate-based authentication.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Alice computes $A$ (1 mark):**

$$A = g^a \bmod p = 2^3 \bmod 11 = 8$$

**Step 2: Bob computes $B$ (1 mark):**

$$B = g^b \bmod p = 2^4 \bmod 11 = 16 \bmod 11 = 5$$

**Step 3: Alice computes shared secret (1.5 marks):**

$$K_A = B^a \bmod p = 5^3 \bmod 11$$

$$5^2 = 25 \equiv 3 \pmod{11}, \quad 5^3 = 5^2 \cdot 5 \equiv 3 \cdot 5 = 15 \equiv 4 \pmod{11}$$

$$\therefore K_A = 4$$

**Step 4: Bob computes shared secret (1.5 marks):**

$$K_B = A^b \bmod p = 8^4 \bmod 11$$

$$8 \equiv -3 \pmod{11}, \quad 8^2 \equiv 9 \pmod{11}, \quad 8^4 \equiv 9^2 = 81 \equiv 4 \pmod{11}$$

$$\therefore K_B = 4 \quad \checkmark$$

**Shared secret:** $K = 4$.

**Step 5: Security analysis of $p = 11$ (1 mark):**

$p = 11$ is a prime, but $p - 1 = 10 = 2 \cdot 5$ has only small factors. This makes $p = 11$ **unsafe** for production because an attacker can use the **Pohlig-Hellman algorithm** to compute discrete logs in subgroups of order 2 or 5, breaking the scheme in milliseconds.

**Step 6: Safe prime recommendation (1 mark):**

Use a **safe prime** of the form $p = 2q + 1$ where both $p$ and $q$ are prime. For example, the RFC 3526 MODP group with $p = 2^{2048} - 2^{1984} - 1 + 2^{64} \cdot (\text{constant})$, or simply a 2048-bit safe prime such that the smallest subgroup has order at least $2^{224}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify $x$ in ElGamal key-gen**: If asked "Bob's public key is $y$ with private key $x$", you must **first check $y = g^x \bmod p$**. Skipping this costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Reusing $k$ in ElGamal**: Never use the same $k$ for two different messages — it leaks $\frac{m_1}{m_2}$. State this explicitly in viva.
> 3. **Forgetting modular inverse step in ElGamal decryption**: $m = c_2 \cdot s^{-1}$, *not* $c_2 \cdot s$. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 4. **Confusing DH and ElGamal**: DH produces a **shared key**, not a ciphertext. ElGamal produces a **ciphertext**, not a key agreement. Examiners will fail a 7-mark answer that confuses the two.
> 5. **Ignoring the MITM threat on DH**: Always mention authentication when describing DH — it's the single most-asked 2-mark follow-up question in KTU exams.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section and stick it on your wall the night before the exam.**

- 🔑 **ElGamal Public Key:** $(p, g, y)$ where $y = g^x \bmod p$; private key is $x$.
- 🔑 **ElGamal Encryption:** $C = (g^k \bmod p, \; m \cdot y^k \bmod p)$ — the random $k$ must be **fresh per encryption**.
- 🔑 **ElGamal Decryption:** $m = c_2 \cdot (c_1^{\,x})^{-1} \bmod p$.
- 🔑 **ElGamal is IND-CPA secure** under the **DDH assumption**, but **malleable** (not IND-CCA secure).
- 🔑 **Diffie-Hellman Setup:** Public parameters $p$ (large prime) and $g$ (generator of $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$).
- 🔑 **DH Public Values:** $A = g^a \bmod p$, $B = g^b \bmod p$.
- 🔑 **DH Shared Secret:** $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$, computed as $B^a$ by Alice and $A^b$ by Bob.
- 🔑 **DH is a key-agreement protocol**, *not* encryption. Output is a symmetric key.
- 🔑 **DH's main weakness** is the **Man-in-the-Middle attack** — always authenticate with certificates or digital signatures.
- 🔑 **Use a safe prime** $p = 2q + 1$ to defeat the **Pohlig-Hellman attack** on small subgroups.
- 🔑 **Six attack types to remember (in increasing power for Eve):** COA → KPA → CPA → CCA → ACPA → MITM.
- 🔑 **Three hardness assumptions to remember (increasing strength):** Discrete Log (DL) → Computational DH (CDH) → Decisional DH (DDH).
- 🔑 **ElGamal ciphertext size is double the plaintext size**, because $C = (c_1, c_2)$ — a common KTU trick question.
- 🔑 **Modern descendants:** ElGamal → DSA signatures; DH → ECDHE in TLS 1.3.
- 🔑 **Real-world constants:** Use $p \geq 2048$ bits (or 256-bit curve for ECDH) for production security beyond year 2030.
- 🔑 **Mnemonic for ElGamal:** *"Alice masks with $y^k$, Bob unmasks with $x$"* — $y$ is Bob's public mask exponent, $x$ is Bob's private unmask exponent.
- 🔑 **Mnemonic for DH:** *"Yellow + Red = Orange public; Orange + Blue = Brown secret"* — Eve sees yellow, orange, green but not brown.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) Attack

### Formal Definition
A **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack** is a classical cryptographic assault in which an adversary, say **Eve**, secretly intercepts, relays, and potentially alters the communication between two legitimate parties, **Alice** and **Bob**, who mistakenly believe they are communicating directly with each other. The attacker fools both parties into thinking the channel is secure and authentic.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, MITM is classified as an **active eavesdropping attack** that violates both **confidentiality** and **integrity** (and potentially **authenticity**) of communication.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Impostor Postman"
Imagine Alice writes a love letter to Bob and gives it to the "postman" (Eve). Eve opens the letter, reads it, **modifies the content**, reseals it with a new envelope that *looks identical*, and delivers it to Bob. Bob thinks Alice sent it, and Alice thinks Bob received her original message. Worse — Bob's reply also goes through Eve, who again tampers with it. Alice and Bob are conversing normally; Eve is the silent ghost in the middle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** MITM is the canonical reason why **Diffie-Hellman key exchange** is insecure *by itself*. Without an authentication mechanism (like digital signatures or certificates), DH is vulnerable to MITM.

### MITM Against Diffie-Hellman — The Classic Attack

Assume the public parameters are a prime $p$ and generator $g$. Alice and Bob want to establish a shared secret.

1. **Alice → Bob:** Sends $A = g^{a} \bmod p$. **Eve intercepts.**
2. **Eve → Bob (pretending to be Alice):** Sends $A' = g^{e_1} \bmod p$ where $e_1$ is Eve's chosen secret.
3. **Bob → Alice:** Sends $B = g^{b} \bmod p$. **Eve intercepts.**
4. **Eve → Alice (pretending to be Bob):** Sends $B' = g^{e_2} \bmod p$.
5. **Alice computes:** Shared key = $B'^{\,a} = g^{e_2 a} \bmod p$.
6. **Bob computes:** Shared key = $A'^{\,b} = g^{e_1 b} \bmod p$.
7. **Eve knows both:** $A^{e_1} = g^{a e_1}$ and $B^{e_2} = g^{b e_2}$, so Eve can decrypt and re-encrypt all traffic.

### Countermeasures to MITM
| Countermeasure | Mechanism | Effectiveness |
|---|---|---|
| **Digital Certificates + PKI** | Bind identity to public key via trusted CA | High |
| **Digital Signatures** | Sign DH values with long-term private keys | High |
| **Mutual Authentication** | Both parties verify each other's identity | High |
| **Key Exchange with Authentication** | e.g., STS (Station-to-Station) protocol | High |
| **Out-of-band verification** | Compare hash of shared key via another channel | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Favorite Question:** *"Why is unauthenticated Diffie-Hellman vulnerable to MITM?"* — Answer in 2 lines: Because DH provides no authentication of the participants; an attacker can establish two separate DH exchanges (one with Alice, one with Bob) without either party detecting the impersonation.

---

## 1.2 Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC) — Foundation

### Formal Definition
**Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)** is a public-key cryptosystem based on the algebraic structure of **elliptic curves over finite fields**. The security of ECC rests on the **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)** — given a base point $P$ and a public point $Q = kP$, it is computationally infeasible to recover the integer $k$.

### The Elliptic Curve Equation
An elliptic curve over a finite field $\mathbb{F}_p$ (where $p$ is a large prime) is defined by the **Weierstrass equation**:

$$E: y^2 \equiv x^3 + ax + b \pmod{p}$$

with the **non-singularity discriminant** condition:

$$4a^3 + 27b^2 \not\equiv 0 \pmod{p}$$

The set of points $(x, y)$ satisfying this equation, together with a special **point at infinity** $\mathcal{O}$ (also called the identity element), forms an **abelian group** under the chord-and-tangent addition law.

### Intuitive Analogy — "Billiard on a Curved Table"
Imagine an infinitely thin pool table shaped like the elliptic curve $y^2 = x^3 + ax + b$. A ball is shot from point $P$ toward point $Q$. It travels in a straight line, **hits the boundary** of the table, and **bounces off elastically** (reflection rule). It then continues until it hits another boundary, bounces again, and so on. After $k$ bounces, the ball lands at the point $kP$. The "discrete log problem" is: given $P$ and the final landing point $kP$, **how many bounces were there?** This is the trapdoor that makes ECC secure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standardized Curves used in practice (KTU must-know):**
> - **NIST P-256** (secp256r1) — used in TLS, Bitcoin (secp256k1 variant)
> - **Curve25519** — used in Signal, WhatsApp, SSH
> - **secp192k1** — older, 192-bit security equivalent
> - **brainpoolP256r1** — European alternative

### Key Sizes: ECC vs RSA (Security Equivalence)

| Symmetric Security (bits) | RSA / DH Key Size (bits) | ECC Key Size (bits) |
|---|---|---|
| **80** | 1024 | 160 |
| **128** | 3072 | 256 |
| **192** | 7680 | 384 |
| **256** | 15360 | 512 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** ECC-256 provides the same security as RSA-3072 but with **~12× smaller keys** and **~10× faster computation**. This is why mobile devices, IoT, and blockchain heavily favor ECC.

---

## 1.3 ElGamal on Elliptic Curves (EC-ElGamal)

### Formal Definition
**EC-ElGamal** is the elliptic-curve analogue of the classical ElGamal public-key encryption scheme. The plaintext message is encoded as a point on the elliptic curve, and encryption produces a pair of points. Security reduces to the **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Two Sealed Envelopes on a Curved Path"
Imagine Alice wants to send Bob a secret message represented as a point $M$ on the elliptic curve. Bob has published a public key $Q_B = n_B \cdot G$. Alice picks a random integer $k$, computes a "blinding factor" point $C_1 = kG$, and then "folds" the message $M$ into the curve by computing $C_2 = M + k \cdot Q_B$. She sends the **two-envelope pair** $(C_1, C_2)$ to Bob. Bob, knowing his private key $n_B$, can "unfold" by computing $C_2 - n_B C_1 = M$. An eavesdropper Eve sees the envelopes but cannot undo the curve operations without solving ECDLP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EC-ElGamal is a probabilistic encryption scheme** — encrypting the same message $M$ twice yields *different* ciphertexts because of the random $k$. This provides **semantic security** (IND-CPA) under standard assumptions.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Elliptic Curve Group Operation — The Heart of ECC

### Point Addition: Geometric Rule
Let $P = (x_1, y_1)$ and $Q = (x_2, y_2)$ be two distinct points on $E$ (over $\mathbb{R}$ for intuition; the same formula works over $\mathbb{F}_p$).

1. Draw the line through $P$ and $Q$.
2. The line intersects the curve at exactly **one more point** $R' = (x_3, -y_3)$ (by Bezout's theorem, a line meets a cubic in 3 points).
3. **Reflect** $R'$ across the x-axis to get $R = P + Q = (x_3, y_3)$.

### Point Doubling: $P + P = 2P$
1. Draw the **tangent** to the curve at $P$.
2. The tangent meets the curve at one other point $R'$.
3. Reflect to get $2P = R$.

### Algebraic Formulas (over $\mathbb{F}_p$)

**Case 1: $P \neq Q$, $P \neq -Q$**

$$\lambda \equiv \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \pmod{p}$$

$$x_3 \equiv \lambda^2 - x_1 - x_2 \pmod{p}$$

$$y_3 \equiv \lambda(x_1 - x_3) - y_1 \pmod{p}$$

**Case 2: $P = Q$ (Point Doubling)**

$$\lambda \equiv \frac{3x_1^2 + a}{2y_1} \pmod{p}$$

$$x_3 \equiv \lambda^2 - 2x_1 \pmod{p}$$

$$y_3 \equiv \lambda(x_1 - x_3) - y_1 \pmod{p}$$

**Case 3: $P = -Q$ (Inverse)** — Result is $\mathcal{O}$ (point at infinity).

**Case 4: $P = \mathcal{O}$ or $Q = \mathcal{O}$** — Result is the other point (identity behavior).

### Scalar Multiplication
The operation $kP = P + P + \cdots + P$ ($k$ times) is the fundamental building block. Computed efficiently via **double-and-add** in $O(\log_2 k)$ operations.

**Algorithm: Double-and-Add**
```
Input: k = (k_{t-1}, ..., k_1, k_0)_2, point P
Q ← O
for i from t-1 down to 0:
    Q ← 2Q              // double
    if k_i = 1:
        Q ← Q + P        // add
return Q
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is fast:** Computing $kP$ needs at most $t$ doublings and $t$ additions where $t = \lfloor \log_2 k \rfloor + 1$. For $k \approx 2^{256}$, this is only ~384 operations — trivial for a computer. But **reversing** the process (finding $k$ from $kP$) is the ECDLP and is **exponentially hard**.

---

## 2.2 The Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)

**Definition.** Given an elliptic curve $E$ over $\mathbb{F}_p$, a base point $G$ of order $n$, and a point $Q = kG$, find the integer $k \in [1, n-1]$.

**Best known attacks:**
- **Pollard's rho algorithm:** $O(\sqrt{n})$ — needs ~$\sqrt{n}$ operations.
- **Baby-step giant-step:** $O(\sqrt{n})$ — needs $O(\sqrt{n})$ memory.
- **MOV / Frey-Rück attack** (for *supersingular* curves): reduces ECDLP to DLP in $\mathbb{F}_{p^k}$.
- **Index calculus / Number Field Sieve:** NOT effective on general ECDLP.

For a 256-bit curve, $\sqrt{n} \approx 2^{128}$ operations — computationally infeasible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Point:** The choice of curve matters. **Anomalous curves** (where $\#E(\mathbb{F}_p) = p$) and **supersingular curves** are weak because the ECDLP can be reduced to DLP in the extension field. Always use **standardized safe curves** (NIST P-256, Curve25519, secp256k1).

---

## 2.3 EC-ElGamal — Full Protocol

### System Parameters (Public)
- Elliptic curve $E$ over $\mathbb{F}_p$.
- A base point $G$ of large prime order $n$ in $E(\mathbb{F}_p)$.
- Hash function $H$ (optional, for message embedding).

### Key Generation (by Bob — the receiver)
1. Choose a random integer $n_B \in [1, n-1]$ — Bob's **private key**.
2. Compute $Q_B = n_B \cdot G$ — Bob's **public key**.
3. Publish $Q_B$. Keep $n_B$ secret.

### Encryption (by Alice — the sender)
Given plaintext message $M$ encoded as a point on $E(\mathbb{F}_p)$:

1. Choose a random integer $k \in [1, n-1]$ — Alice's **ephemeral secret**.
2. Compute $C_1 = kG$.
3. Compute $C_2 = M + k Q_B = M + k (n_B G) = M + (k n_B) G$.
4. Send ciphertext $(C_1, C_2)$ to Bob.

### Decryption (by Bob)
1. Compute $n_B C_1 = n_B (kG) = (n_B k) G$.
2. Compute $M = C_2 - n_B C_1 = M + (k n_B) G - (n_B k) G = M + \mathcal{O} = M$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why decryption works (verification step):**
> $$C_2 - n_B C_1 = \big(M + k Q_B\big) - n_B(kG) = M + k(n_B G) - (n_B k)G = M + (kn_B - kn_B)G = M + \mathcal{O} = M$$

### Message Encoding (Koblitz Method or simple)
Plaintexts are integers, not curve points. **Koblitz's method** for encoding $m$ into a point $M$:

1. Compute $x = mk + j$ for $j = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$
2. Test if $x^3 + ax + b$ is a quadratic residue mod $p$.
3. Take the first $j$ for which it is, and set $x_m = x$.
4. Compute $y_m = \sqrt{x_m^3 + a x_m + b} \bmod p$.
5. Set $M = (x_m, y_m)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Point — Message Expansion:** EC-ElGamal doubles the message size. A 256-bit message becomes a pair of two 256-bit points, totaling ~512 bits. This is a known **disadvantage** of EC-ElGamal compared to RSA-OAEP.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Elliptic Curve Equation | $y^2 \equiv x^3 + ax + b \pmod p$ | Weierstrass form |
| 2 | Discriminant (non-singular) | $\Delta = -16(4a^3 + 27b^2) \not\equiv 0 \pmod p$ | Ensures smooth group |
| 3 | Point Addition slope | $\lambda = (y_2 - y_1)(x_2 - x_1)^{-1} \bmod p$ | When $P \neq Q$ |
| 4 | Point Doubling slope | $\lambda = (3x_1^2 + a)(2y_1)^{-1} \bmod p$ | When $P = Q$ |
| 5 | $x_3$ coordinate | $x_3 = \lambda^2 - x_1 - x_2 \bmod p$ | Addition result |
| 6 | $y_3$ coordinate | $y_3 = \lambda(x_1 - x_3) - y_1 \bmod p$ | Addition result |
| 7 | Identity element | $\mathcal{O}$ — point at infinity | Group identity |
| 8 | Inverse of $P$ | $-P = (x, -y \bmod p)$ | Reflection across x-axis |
| 9 | Order of group | $\#E(\mathbb{F}_p) \approx p$ (Hasse's bound) | Hasse: $\vert p+1 - \#E \vert \le 2\sqrt p$ |
| 10 | ECDLP | Given $Q = kG$, find $k$ | $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt n)$ best attack |
| 11 | EC-ElGamal Public Key | $Q_B = n_B G$ | Bob's public key |
| 12 | EC-ElGamal $C_1$ | $C_1 = kG$ | Random ephemeral |
| 13 | EC-ElGamal $C_2$ | $C_2 = M + k Q_B$ | Message masked |
| 14 | EC-ElGamal Decryption | $M = C_2 - n_B C_1$ | Use private key |
| 15 | Security of 256-bit ECC | Equivalent to 3072-bit RSA | Same as AES-128 |
| 16 | MITM on DH | Eve creates $A' = g^{e_1}$, $B' = g^{e_2}$ | No authentication needed |

---

## 2.5 Why ECC and EC-ElGamal Matter in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Deployment Footprint:**
> - **HTTPS/TLS 1.3:** Uses X25519 (Curve25519 ECDH) by default.
> - **Bitcoin/Ethereum:** Use **secp256k1** for digital signatures (ECDSA).
> - **WhatsApp/Signal:** X25519 for key agreement + AES for symmetric layer.
> - **Smart Cards / IoT:** ECC-192/ECC-256 because of low memory footprint.
> - **Government / Defense:** Suite B Cryptography mandates ECC (P-256, P-384).

For a B.Tech student: **every modern protocol you'll encounter in industry uses ECC in some form** — understand it well, and you understand the backbone of current cryptography.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Point Addition and Doubling on a Curve

### Setup
Consider the elliptic curve over $\mathbb{F}_{23}$:

$$E: y^2 \equiv x^3 + x + 1 \pmod{23}$$

with $a = 1$, $b = 1$. Discriminant: $4(1)^3 + 27(1)^2 = 31 \not\equiv 0 \pmod{23}$ ✓ (non-singular).

### Step 1 — Verify a Point Lies on the Curve
Check $P = (0, 1)$:
- RHS: $0^3 + 0 + 1 = 1$
- LHS: $1^2 = 1$
- $1 \equiv 1 \pmod{23}$ ✓ So $P = (0,1) \in E$.

Check $Q = (3, 10)$:
- RHS: $3^3 + 3 + 1 = 27 + 3 + 1 = 31 \equiv 31 - 23 = 8 \pmod{23}$
- LHS: $10^2 = 100 \equiv 100 - 4(23) = 100 - 92 = 8 \pmod{23}$ ✓ So $Q = (3,10) \in E$.

### Step 2 — Compute $P + Q$

$$P = (x_1, y_1) = (0, 1), \quad Q = (x_2, y_2) = (3, 10)$$

**Slope:**
$$\lambda = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \frac{10 - 1}{3 - 0} = \frac{9}{3} = 3 \pmod{23}$$

Since $\gcd(3, 23) = 1$, the modular inverse is $3^{-1} \equiv 8 \pmod{23}$ (because $3 \cdot 8 = 24 \equiv 1 \pmod{23}$).

$$\lambda = 9 \cdot 8 = 72 \equiv 72 - 3(23) = 72 - 69 = 3 \pmod{23}$$

**$x_3$ coordinate:**
$$x_3 = \lambda^2 - x_1 - x_2 = 3^2 - 0 - 3 = 9 - 3 = 6 \pmod{23}$$

**$y_3$ coordinate:**
$$y_3 = \lambda(x_1 - x_3) - y_1 = 3(0 - 6) - 1 = 3(-6) - 1$$

$$3 \cdot (-6) = -18 \equiv -18 + 23 = 5 \pmod{23}$$

$$y_3 = 5 - 1 = 4 \pmod{23}$$

**Result:** $P + Q = (6, 4)$

### Step 3 — Verify the Result
Check $(6, 4)$ is on the curve:
- LHS: $4^2 = 16$
- RHS: $6^3 + 6 + 1 = 216 + 6 + 1 = 223$
- $223 \bmod 23$: $223 = 9 \cdot 23 + 16 = 207 + 16$ → $223 \equiv 16 \pmod{23}$ ✓

### Step 4 — Compute $2P$ (Point Doubling)

$$P = (0, 1)$$

**Slope (doubling formula):**
$$\lambda = \frac{3x_1^2 + a}{2y_1} = \frac{3(0)^2 + 1}{2(1)} = \frac{1}{2} \pmod{23}$$

Modular inverse: $2^{-1} \equiv 12 \pmod{23}$ (since $2 \cdot 12 = 24 \equiv 1$).

$$\lambda = 1 \cdot 12 = 12 \pmod{23}$$

**$x_3$ coordinate:**
$$x_3 = \lambda^2 - 2x_1 = 12^2 - 0 = 144 \pmod{23}$$
$$144 = 6 \cdot 23 + 6 = 138 + 6 \Rightarrow x_3 \equiv 6 \pmod{23}$$

**$y_3$ coordinate:**
$$y_3 = \lambda(x_1 - x_3) - y_1 = 12(0 - 6) - 1 = 12 \cdot (-6) - 1$$
$$12 \cdot (-6) = -72 \equiv -72 + 4(23) = -72 + 92 = 20 \pmod{23}$$
$$y_3 = 20 - 1 = 19 \pmod{23}$$

**Result:** $2P = (6, 19)$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Notice that $P + Q = (6, 4)$ and $2P = (6, 19)$. Both have $x_3 = 6$ but different $y$. Geometrically this is consistent: they lie vertically aligned (since $19 \equiv -4 \pmod{23}$ because $-4 + 23 = 19$). So $(6, 4) + (6, 19) = \mathcal{O}$, meaning they are **inverses of each other**. ✓

### Step 5 — Compute $3P = 2P + P$

$$2P = (6, 19), \quad P = (0, 1)$$

**Slope:**
$$\lambda = \frac{1 - 19}{0 - 6} = \frac{-18}{-6} = 3 \pmod{23}$$

**$x_3$:**
$$x_3 = 3^2 - 6 - 0 = 9 - 6 = 3 \pmod{23}$$

**$y_3$:**
$$y_3 = 3(6 - 3) - 19 = 3 \cdot 3 - 19 = 9 - 19 = -10 \equiv 13 \pmod{23}$$

**Result:** $3P = (3, 13)$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Full EC-ElGamal Encryption/Decryption

### Setup
Use the curve $E: y^2 \equiv x^3 + x + 1 \pmod{23}$ with generator $G = (0, 1)$ of order $n = 28$ (we will assume this for the example; real curves have prime order).

### Key Generation (Bob)
- Bob picks $n_B = 5$ (private key).
- Computes $Q_B = 5G = 5 \cdot (0,1)$.

From our earlier computation: $5G = 2(2G) + G$ etc. Let us compute step by step using scalar multiplication.

From the previous steps: $G = (0,1)$, $2G = (6, 19)$, $3G = (3, 13)$, $4G = 3G + G = (3,13) + (0,1)$.

**Compute $4G = 3G + G = (3, 13) + (0, 1)$:**
- $\lambda = (1 - 13)/(0 - 3) = -12 / -3 = 4 \pmod{23}$
- $x_3 = 4^2 - 3 - 0 = 16 - 3 = 13$
- $y_3 = 4(3 - 13) - 13 = 4(-10) - 13 = -40 - 13 = -53 \equiv -53 + 3(23) = 16 \pmod{23}$
- So $4G = (13, 16)$.

**Compute $5G = 4G + G = (13, 16) + (0, 1)$:**
- $\lambda = (1 - 16)/(0 - 13) = -15 / -13 = 15 \cdot 13^{-1} \pmod{23}$
- $13^{-1} \pmod{23}$: $13 \cdot x \equiv 1 \pmod{23}$. Try $x$: $13 \cdot 16 = 208 = 9 \cdot 23 + 1 = 207 + 1$ → $13^{-1} \equiv 16 \pmod{23}$.
- $\lambda = 15 \cdot 16 = 240 \equiv 240 - 10(23) = 240 - 230 = 10 \pmod{23}$
- $x_3 = 10^2 - 13 - 0 = 100 - 13 = 87 \equiv 87 - 3(23) = 87 - 69 = 18 \pmod{23}$
- $y_3 = 10(13 - 18) - 16 = 10(-5) - 16 = -50 - 16 = -66 \equiv -66 + 3(23) = 3 \pmod{23}$
- So $Q_B = 5G = (18, 3)$.

**Public key:** $Q_B = (18, 3)$. Private key: $n_B = 5$.

### Encryption (Alice)
Suppose Alice wants to encrypt message point $M = (3, 10)$ (recall $Q = (3, 10)$ is on the curve). She picks random $k = 3$.

**Compute $C_1 = kG = 3G = (3, 13)$** (from our earlier work).

**Compute $k Q_B = 3 Q_B = 3 \cdot (18, 3)$:**

$Q_B = (18, 3)$, so $2Q_B$:
- $\lambda = (3 \cdot 18^2 + 1)/(2 \cdot 3) = (3 \cdot 324 + 1)/6 = (972 + 1)/6 = 973/6 \pmod{23}$
- $324 \bmod 23$: $324 = 14 \cdot 23 + 2 = 322 + 2$ → $324 \equiv 2$
- $3 \cdot 2 = 6$, $+1 = 7$ (numerator)
- $2 \cdot 3 = 6$ (denominator)
- $\lambda = 7 \cdot 6^{-1} \pmod{23}$; $6^{-1} \equiv 4$ (since $6 \cdot 4 = 24 \equiv 1$)
- $\lambda = 7 \cdot 4 = 28 \equiv 5 \pmod{23}$
- $x_3 = 5^2 - 2(18) = 25 - 36 = -11 \equiv 12 \pmod{23}$
- $y_3 = 5(18 - 12) - 3 = 5 \cdot 6 - 3 = 30 - 3 = 27 \equiv 4 \pmod{23}$
- $2Q_B = (12, 4)$.

$3Q_B = 2Q_B + Q_B = (12, 4) + (18, 3)$:
- $\lambda = (3 - 4)/(18 - 12) = -1/6 \equiv -1 \cdot 4 = -4 \equiv 19 \pmod{23}$
- $x_3 = 19^2 - 12 - 18 = 361 - 30 = 331 \pmod{23}$
- $331 = 14 \cdot 23 + 9 = 322 + 9$ → $x_3 \equiv 9 \pmod{23}$
- $y_3 = 19(12 - 9) - 4 = 19 \cdot 3 - 4 = 57 - 4 = 53 \pmod{23}$
- $53 = 2 \cdot 23 + 7$ → $y_3 \equiv 7 \pmod{23}$
- $3Q_B = (9, 7)$.

**Compute $C_2 = M + k Q_B = (3, 10) + (9, 7)$:**
- $\lambda = (7 - 10)/(9 - 3) = -3/6 = -3 \cdot 6^{-1} \equiv -3 \cdot 4 = -12 \equiv 11 \pmod{23}$
- $x_3 = 11^2 - 3 - 9 = 121 - 12 = 109 \pmod{23}$
- $109 = 4 \cdot 23 + 17 = 92 + 17$ → $x_3 \equiv 17 \pmod{23}$
- $y_3 = 11(3 - 17) - 10 = 11 \cdot (-14) - 10 = -154 - 10 = -164 \pmod{23}$
- $-164 = -8 \cdot 23 + 20 = -184 + 20$ → $y_3 \equiv 20 \pmod{23}$
- $C_2 = (17, 20)$.

**Ciphertext:** $(C_1, C_2) = ((3, 13),\; (17, 20))$.

### Decryption (Bob)
Bob knows $n_B = 5$.

**Compute $n_B C_1 = 5 C_1 = 5 \cdot (3, 13)$:**

We need $5 \cdot (3, 13)$. From our doubling chain on $(0,1)$ we computed $3G = (3, 13)$ — interestingly the same point. Let us double-add.

$2(3, 13)$:
- $\lambda = (3 \cdot 9 + 1)/(2 \cdot 13) = 28/26 \pmod{23}$
- $28 \equiv 5$, $26 \equiv 3$ (since $26 = 23 + 3$)
- $\lambda = 5 \cdot 3^{-1} \pmod{23}$; $3^{-1} \equiv 8$
- $\lambda = 5 \cdot 8 = 40 \equiv 40 - 23 = 17 \pmod{23}$
- $x_3 = 17^2 - 6 = 289 - 6 = 283 \pmod{23}$
- $283 = 12 \cdot 23 + 7 = 276 + 7$ → $x_3 \equiv 7 \pmod{23}$
- $y_3 = 17(3 - 7) - 13 = 17(-4) - 13 = -68 - 13 = -81 \pmod{23}$
- $-81 = -4 \cdot 23 + 11 = -92 + 11$ → $y_3 \equiv 11 \pmod{23}$
- $2(3,13) = (7, 11)$.

$3(3,13) = (7,11) + (3,13)$:
- $\lambda = (13 - 11)/(3 - 7) = 2/(-4) = -1/2 \equiv -1 \cdot 12 = -12 \equiv 11 \pmod{23}$
- $x_3 = 11^2 - 7 - 3 = 121 - 10 = 111 \pmod{23}$
- $111 = 4 \cdot 23 + 19 = 92 + 19$ → $x_3 \equiv 19 \pmod{23}$
- $y_3 = 11(7 - 19) - 11 = 11(-12) - 11 = -132 - 11 = -143 \pmod{23}$
- $-143 = -7 \cdot 23 + 18 = -161 + 18$ → $y_3 \equiv 18 \pmod{23}$
- $3(3,13) = (19, 18)$.

$4(3,13) = 2 \cdot 2(3,13) = 2(7,11)$:
- $\lambda = (3 \cdot 49 + 1)/(2 \cdot 11) = 148/22 \pmod{23}$
- $148 \bmod 23$: $148 = 6 \cdot 23 + 10$ → $148 \equiv 10$
- $22 \equiv -1$
- $\lambda = 10/(-1) = -10 \equiv 13 \pmod{23}$
- $x_3 = 13^2 - 14 = 169 - 14 = 155 \pmod{23}$
- $155 = 6 \cdot 23 + 17$ → $x_3 \equiv 17 \pmod{23}$
- $y_3 = 13(7 - 17) - 11 = 13(-10) - 11 = -130 - 11 = -141 \pmod{23}$
- $-141 = -7 \cdot 23 + 20 = -161 + 20$ → $y_3 \equiv 20 \pmod{23}$
- $4(3,13) = (17, 20)$.

$5(3,13) = 4(3,13) + (3,13) = (17, 20) + (3, 13)$:
- $\lambda = (13 - 20)/(3 - 17) = -7/(-14) = 1/2 \equiv 12 \pmod{23}$
- $x_3 = 12^2 - 17 - 3 = 144 - 20 = 124 \pmod{23}$
- $124 = 5 \cdot 23 + 9$ → $x_3 \equiv 9 \pmod{23}$
- $y_3 = 12(17 - 9) - 20 = 12 \cdot 8 - 20 = 96 - 20 = 76 \pmod{23}$
- $76 = 3 \cdot 23 + 7$ → $y_3 \equiv 7 \pmod{23}$
- $5C_1 = (9, 7)$.

**Compute $M = C_2 - n_B C_1 = C_2 - 5C_1 = (17, 20) - (9, 7) = (17, 20) + (9, -7)$:**

$-7 \bmod 23 = 16$. So $-(9, 7) = (9, 16)$.

$(17, 20) + (9, 16)$:
- $\lambda = (16 - 20)/(9 - 17) = -4/(-8) = 1/2 \equiv 12 \pmod{23}$
- $x_3 = 12^2 - 17 - 9 = 144 - 26 = 118 \pmod{23}$
- $118 = 5 \cdot 23 + 3$ → $x_3 \equiv 3 \pmod{23}$
- $y_3 = 12(17 - 3) - 20 = 12 \cdot 14 - 20 = 168 - 20 = 148 \pmod{23}$
- $148 \equiv 10 \pmod{23}$
- $M = (3, 10)$ ✓

**Decryption successful — original message recovered!**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — EC-ElGamal vs Classical ElGamal vs RSA

| Property | Classical ElGamal | EC-ElGamal | RSA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mathematical Group** | $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$ (multiplicative) | $E(\mathbb{F}_p)$ (elliptic curve) | $\mathbb{Z}_n^*$ |
| **Hard Problem** | DLP | ECDLP | Integer Factorization |
| **Public Key Size (256-bit security)** | 3072 bits | 512 bits | 3072 bits |
| **Ciphertext Size** | $2 \times 3072 = 6144$ bits | $2 \times 512 = 1024$ bits | 3072 bits |
| **Encryption Speed** | Slow | Fast | Fast |
| **Decryption Speed** | Slow | Fast | Fast |
| **Plaintext Space** | Integer mod $p$ | Point on curve | Integer mod $n$ |
| **Probabilistic?** | Yes | Yes | No (deterministic) |
| **Key Generation** | Simple | Curve point multiplication | Random prime generation |
| **Standardization** | ElGamal, DH | NIST P-256, Curve25519, secp256k1 | PKCS#1, RSA-OAEP |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Man-in-the-Middle Attack — Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph legit["Legitimate Parties (Belief)"]
        A["ALICE<br/>Wants shared key with Bob"]
        B["BOB<br/>Wants shared key with Alice"]
    end

    subgraph channel["Untrusted Network Channel"]
        E["EVE - ATTACKER<br/>Intercepts, Forwards, Tampers"]
    end

    A -- "A = g^a mod p" --> E
    E -- "A' = g^e1 mod p (fake Alice)" --> B
    B -- "B = g^b mod p" --> E
    E -- "B' = g^e2 mod p (fake Bob)" --> A

    E -. "Decrypts / Re-encrypts" .-> E

    A -. "Thinks: shared key with Bob" .-> A
    B -. "Thinks: shared key with Alice" .-> B

    classDef attacker fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef victim fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class A,B victim
    class E attacker
```

**Read this diagram:** Notice that Alice and Bob never exchange messages directly. The red Eve node sits *between* them, intercepting outbound traffic from each and injecting fake traffic back. Each victim believes they are directly connected to the other.

---

## 4.2 EC-ElGamal — Encryption & Decryption Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph setup["PUBLIC PARAMETERS (E, p, G, n)"]
        params["Curve E: y^2 = x^3 + ax + b mod p<br/>Base point G of order n"]
    end

    subgraph keygen["BOB'S KEY GENERATION"]
        priv["Private Key: nB in 1..n-1<br/>chosen randomly"]
        pub["Public Key: QB = nB * G<br/>(point on curve)"]
        priv --> pub
    end

    subgraph encrypt["ALICE'S ENCRYPTION (Plaintext M)"]
        randk["Random k in 1..n-1"]
        c1["C1 = k * G"]
        c2["C2 = M + k * QB"]
        ctext["Send ciphertext (C1, C2)"]
        randk --> c1
        randk --> c2
        c1 --> ctext
        c2 --> ctext
    end

    subgraph decrypt["BOB'S DECRYPTION"]
        nbc1["nB * C1 = nB * k * G = k * nB * G"]
        recov["M = C2 - nB * C1<br/>= M + k*nB*G - k*nB*G"]
        nbc1 --> recov
    end

    params --> keygen
    params --> encrypt
    pub --> c2
    ctext --> nbc1
    ctext --> c2

    classDef secret fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:1px
    classDef public fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:1px
    classDef crypto fill:#e1e1ff,stroke:#000066,stroke-width:1px

    class priv,randk,nbc1 secret
    class params,pub,ctext public
    class c1,c2,recov crypto
```

**Read this diagram:** The orange boxes are *secrets* (private key, random $k$, intermediate $n_B C_1$). The green boxes are *public* (parameters, public key, transmitted ciphertext). The blue boxes are *cryptographic operations*. Notice how $n_B$ from key generation is reused in decryption.

---

## 4.3 Elliptic Curve Point Operations — Geometric Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph geom["GEOMETRIC VIEW (Over Real Numbers for Intuition)"]
        P["P = (x1, y1)<br/>on curve"]
        Q["Q = (x2, y2)<br/>on curve"]
        line["Draw line through P and Q<br/>(chord)"]
        Rprime["Find third intersection R'<br/>with the cubic curve"]
        reflect["Reflect R' across x-axis<br/>to get P + Q"]
        R["P + Q = R = (x3, y3)"]

        P --> line
        Q --> line
        line --> Rprime
        Rprime --> reflect
        reflect --> R
    end

    subgraph doubl["DOUBLING (P = Q)"]
        P2["P = (x1, y1)"]
        tan["Draw TANGENT at P"]
        Rp2["Find other intersection R'"]
        ref2["Reflect to get 2P"]
        R2["2P = (x3, y3)"]

        P2 --> tan
        tan --> Rp2
        Rp2 --> ref2
        ref2 --> R2
    end

    subgraph scal["SCALAR MULTIPLICATION (kP)"]
        repeat["Repeat addition/doubling k times<br/>using double-and-add algorithm"]
        kp["Result: kP = P + P + ... + P"]

        repeat --> kp
    end

    classDef geo fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#660066,stroke-width:1px
    classDef alg fill:#e1f7ff,stroke:#003355,stroke-width:1px

    class P,Q,line,Rprime,reflect,R,P2,tan,Rp2,ref2,R2 geo
    class repeat,kp alg
```

**Read this diagram:** Three operations are illustrated separately. (1) Top: $P + Q$ via chord method. (2) Middle: $2P$ via tangent. (3) Bottom: scalar multiplication $kP$ by repeated addition. All operations are over the elliptic curve group.

---

## 4.4 MITM Countermeasure — Station-to-Station (STS) Protocol

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Alice
    participant E as Eve (attacker)
    participant B as Bob

    Note over A,B: Long-term signing keys: SigA, SigB

    A->>E: g^a mod p
    Note right of E: Eve can intercept
    E->>B: g^e mod p (impersonating Alice)
    B->>E: g^b mod p, SigB(g^a, g^b)
    Note right of E: Eve cannot forge SigB
    E--xB: Cannot forward valid Bob's signature!
    Note over A,B: MITM fails because Eve cannot forge digital signatures
```

**Read this diagram:** The key insight is that Eve, despite intercepting the DH values, **cannot forge Bob's digital signature** (because she doesn't have Bob's private signing key). When Bob signs the DH exchange transcript, the signature binds his identity to the session — any tampering by Eve is detected. This is the **Station-to-Station (STS) protocol** — a standard defense against MITM.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]
> **Explain Man-in-the-Middle attack with respect to Diffie-Hellman key exchange. Mention one countermeasure.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack is one in which an adversary Eve secretly intercepts the communication between Alice and Bob. In the context of Diffie-Hellman, Eve intercepts Alice's public value $g^a \bmod p$ and sends her own value $g^{e_1} \bmod p$ to Bob. Similarly, Eve intercepts Bob's $g^b \bmod p$ and sends $g^{e_2} \bmod p$ to Alice. Alice computes a shared key with Eve (thinking it's with Bob), and Bob computes a different shared key with Eve (thinking it's with Alice). Eve now sits transparently in the middle, decrypting and re-encrypting all traffic.

**Countermeasure (1 mark):** Authenticate the DH values using **digital signatures** (e.g., Station-to-Station protocol) or **digital certificates via a PKI**. This binds the public values to the participants' identities, preventing impersonation.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
> **State the Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP). Why is ECC preferred over RSA for equivalent security levels?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **ECDLP** states: given an elliptic curve $E$ over a finite field $\mathbb{F}_p$, a base point $G$ of order $n$, and a point $Q = kG$ on the curve, it is computationally infeasible to determine the integer $k$ for sufficiently large $n$.

**Why ECC preferred (2 marks):**
1. **Smaller key sizes** — ECC-256 provides security equivalent to RSA-3072 (about 12× smaller keys).
2. **Faster computation** — Scalar multiplication $kP$ is more efficient than RSA modular exponentiation, especially on resource-constrained devices (IoT, smartphones).
3. **Lower bandwidth & storage** — Smaller signatures (e.g., ECDSA signatures ~512 bits vs RSA-3072 ~3072 bits).

---
