| Number of padding zeros for msg of $L$ bits | $k = 896 - (L+1) \bmod 1024$ | If negative, add 1024 |
| Final padded length | Multiple of **1024 bits** | 16 × 64-bit words per block |
| Hash output length | **512 bits** | $8 \times 64$ bits |
| Rounds per block | **80** | Iterations of the compression function |
| $W_t$ (schedule) | $\sigma_1(W_{t-2}) + W_{t-7} + \sigma_0(W_{t-15}) + W_{t-16}$ | $t \in [16, 79]$ |
| $T_1$ | $h + \Sigma_1(e) + \text{Ch}(e,f,g) + K_t + W_t$ | All arithmetic mod $2^{64}$ |
| $T_2$ | $\Sigma_0(a) + \text{Maj}(a,b,c)$ | All arithmetic mod $2^{64}$ |
| Ch function | $(x \land y) \oplus (\lnot x \land z)$ | Chooses $y$ or $z$ based on $x$ |
| Maj function | $(x \land y) \oplus (x \land z) \oplus (y \land z)$ | Bitwise majority vote |
| MAC security | Existential unforgeability under chosen-message attack (**EUF-CMA**) | Standard security notion |
| HMAC structure | $\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H\big((K_0 \oplus \text{opad}) \Vert H((K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}) \Vert M)\big)$ | $K_0$ = key-derived secret |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SHA-512** powers TLS/SSL certificates, Bitcoin addresses (RIPEMD-160, with SHA-256 in earlier chain), password hashing (with salt), and digital signature schemes (DSA, ECDSA).
- **MACs** protect API requests (HMAC-SHA256 in AWS Signature V4), JWT tokens, IoT firmware integrity, and credit card chip authentication (CMAC in EMV).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Padding Length for SHA-512

Let us derive the **number of zero padding bits** $k$ that must be appended so the final padded message has length divisible by 1024 bits.

**Given:** Original message of $L$ bits.

**Constraint:** After appending the '1' bit, the $k$ zero bits, and the 128-bit length field, the total must be a multiple of 1024.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total length} &= L + 1 + k + 128 \\
\text{Must be} &\equiv 0 \pmod{1024} \\
\Rightarrow L + 1 + k + 128 &\equiv 0 \pmod{1024} \\
\Rightarrow L + 1 + k &\equiv 1024 - 128 \pmod{1024} \\
\Rightarrow L + 1 + k &\equiv 896 \pmod{1024}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $k$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= (896 - L - 1) \bmod 1024 \\
k &= (895 - L) \bmod 1024
\end{aligned}
$$

The modulo operation ensures $k \ge 0$. If $L < 896$, then $k$ is simply $895 - L$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Padding the Message "abc"

**Step 1 — Encode:** ASCII codes of "abc" = 0x61, 0x62, 0x63. As a bit string:
$$M = 01100001\ 01100010\ 01100011$$
So $L = 24$ bits.

**Step 2 — Append '1' bit:**
$$M' = 01100001\ 01100010\ 01100011\ 1$$
Length so far: $L + 1 = 25$ bits.

**Step 3 — Compute zero padding length $k$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= (895 - 24) \bmod 1024 \\
k &= 871 \bmod 1024 \\
k &= 871
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Append 871 zero bits** (rounding to 896 bits, the first sub-block).

**Step 5 — Append 128-bit length** = 24 in big-endian:
$$\text{Length field} = 00000000\ 00000000\ 00000000\ 00000000\ 00000000\ 00000000\ 00000000\ 00000018$$

**Step 6 — Final padded message length:**
$$24 + 1 + 871 + 128 = 1024 \text{ bits} = 1 \text{ block}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: The Compression Function Update Equations

Starting from the round function on inputs $(a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h)$ at round $t$, we form the new values as:

**Step 1 — Compute the two temporary words:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= h + \Sigma_1(e) + \text{Ch}(e,f,g) + K_t + W_t \pmod{2^{64}} \\
T_2 &= \Sigma_0(a) + \text{Maj}(a,b,c) \pmod{2^{64}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Update the working variables cyclically** (this is the *permutation* of the eight 64-bit words):
$$
\begin{aligned}
h &\leftarrow g \\
g &\leftarrow f \\
f &\leftarrow e \\
e &\leftarrow d + T_1 \pmod{2^{64}} \\
d &\leftarrow c \\
c &\leftarrow b \\
b &\leftarrow a \\
a &\leftarrow T_1 + T_2 \pmod{2^{64}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — After 80 rounds**, add the working variables back to the hash state (this is the *Davies-Meyer* style finalization that prevents length-extension attacks on the bare hash):
$$
\begin{aligned}
H_0 &\leftarrow H_0 + a \pmod{2^{64}} \\
H_1 &\leftarrow H_1 + b \pmod{2^{64}} \\
H_2 &\leftarrow H_2 + c \pmod{2^{64}} \\
H_3 &\leftarrow H_3 + d \pmod{2^{64}} \\
H_4 &\leftarrow H_4 + e \pmod{2^{64}} \\
H_5 &\leftarrow H_5 + f \pmod{2^{64}} \\
H_6 &\leftarrow H_6 + g \pmod{2^{64}} \\
H_7 &\leftarrow H_7 + h \pmod{2^{64}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why the final addition matters:** This "feed-forward" addition ensures that the *output* depends not only on the *input* block but also on the *current hash state*. Without it, an attacker could mount length-extension attacks: given $H(M)$, they could compute $H(M \Vert \text{pad} \Vert \text{extra})$ without knowing $M$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — SHA-512 vs SHA-256 vs MD5

| Property | MD5 | SHA-256 | SHA-512 |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Output length | 128 bits | 256 bits | **512 bits** |
| Block size | 512 bits | 512 bits | **1024 bits** |
| Word size | 32 bits | 32 bits | **64 bits** |
| Rounds | 64 | 64 | **80** |
| Initial hash words | 4 | 8 | **8** |
| Round constants | 64 | 64 | **80** |
| Collision status | **Broken** | Partial weakness shown | Secure |
| Designed by | Rivest (1991) | NSA (2001) | NSA (2001) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The number of **rounds** jumped from 64 (MD5, SHA-256) to **80** in SHA-512 to compensate for the larger word size, providing equivalent or greater security per bit processed.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — MAC vs Hash vs Digital Signature

| Property | Hash (SHA-512) | MAC (HMAC-SHA512) | Digital Signature (RSA/DSA) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Uses secret key? | **No** | **Yes (shared)** | Yes (private key, asymmetric) |
| Provides Integrity? | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Provides Authenticity? | No | Yes | Yes |
| Provides Non-repudiation? | No | No | **Yes** |
| Speed | Very fast | Fast | Slower (modular exponentiation) |
| Receiver capability | Anyone | Only key-holders | Anyone with public key |
| Typical construction | Merkle–Damgård | Nested hash with ipad/opad | Public-key math |

> [!NOTE]
> MACs are **faster** and **cheaper** than digital signatures, but provide **no non-repudiation** because both parties share the key — the receiver could have forged the message themselves.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SHA-512 Single-Block Compression Function (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputBlock["1024-bit Message Block M_i"] --> splitBlock["Split into 16 x 64-bit words M0..M15"]
    splitBlock --> scheduleGen["Message Schedule Generator W_t"]
    
    schedWord["W_{t-2}, W_{t-7}, W_{t-15}, W_{t-16}"] --> scheduleGen
    scheduleGen --> Wout["W_t for t = 0..79"]
    
    initReg["Initial Hash H0..H7"] --> loadReg["Load Working Variables a,b,c,d,e,f,g,h"]
    
    loopStart{"For t = 0 to 79"} --> calcT1["T1 = h + Sigma1_e + Ch_efg + K_t + W_t mod 2^64"]
    loopStart --> calcT2["T2 = Sigma0_a + Maj_abc"]
    
    Kconst["80 Round Constants K_0..K_79"] --> calcT1
    Wout --> calcT1
    loadReg --> calcT1
    loadReg --> calcT2
    
    calcT1 --> update["Shift: h=g, g=f, f=e, e=d+T1, d=c, c=b, b=a, a=T1+T2"]
    calcT2 --> update
    
    update --> checkEnd{"t < 79?"}
    checkEnd -- Yes --> loopStart
    checkEnd -- No --> finalAdd["Finalize: H_j = H_j + working_var_j mod 2^64"]
    
    finalAdd --> outputHash["512-bit Digest H0||H1||...||H7"]
    
    classDef block fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#88c5ff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef logic fill:#5f1e3a,stroke:#ff8855,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef data fill:#1e5f3a,stroke:#88ff95,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef io fill:#5f5f1e,stroke:#ffff88,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    
    class inputBlock,schedWord,Kconst,initReg block
    class scheduleGen,calcT1,calcT2,update logic
    class splitBlock,Wout,loadReg,outputHash data
    class loopStart,checkEnd io
```

## 4.2 SHA-512 Top-Level Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    msg["Original Message M, length L bits"] --> pad["Preprocessing & Padding"]
    pad --> padOut["Padded Message, multiple of 1024 bits"]
    padOut --> split["Split into N Blocks of 1024 bits"]
    split --> block1["Block 1: M_1"]
    split --> blockN["Block N: M_N"]
    
    block1 --> compFn["Compression Function f_512 (80 rounds)"]
    blockN --> compFn
    
    H0["Initial Hash State H_0..H_7"] --> compFn
    compFn --> digestOut["512-bit Message Digest H_M"]
    
    classDef io fill:#0d3b66,stroke:#88c5ff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef proc fill:#a30000,stroke:#ffaa00,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef state fill:#1d7a3d,stroke:#88ff95,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    
    class msg,digestOut io
    class pad,split,compFn proc
    class H0,block1,blockN,padOut state
```

## 4.3 MAC Verification Workflow (Mermaid Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sender as Sender (Alice)
    participant Net as Insecure Channel
    participant Receiver as Receiver (Bob)
    
    Note over Sender,Receiver: Both share secret key K
    
    Sender->>Sender: Compute T = MAC_K(M)
    Sender->>Net: Send (M, T)
    Net->>Receiver: Deliver (M, T)
    Receiver->>Receiver: Compute T_new = MAC_K(M)
    
    alt T_new == T
        Receiver->>Receiver: ACCEPT message
        Note over Receiver: Message is authentic & unmodified
    else T_new != T
        Receiver->>Receiver: REJECT message
        Note over Receiver: Possible tampering or forgery detected
    end
```

## 4.4 The Six Logical Functions Visualized (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Choice["Ch x,y,z Function"]
        ch_in["x, y, z"] --> ch_and1["x AND y"]
        ch_in --> ch_not["NOT x"]
        ch_not --> ch_and2["NOT x AND z"]
        ch_and1 --> ch_xor["XOR"]
        ch_and2 --> ch_xor
        ch_xor --> ch_out["Ch = (x AND y) XOR (NOT x AND z)"]
    end
    
    subgraph Majority["Maj x,y,z Function"]
        maj_in["x, y, z"] --> maj_and1["x AND y"]
        maj_in --> maj_and2["x AND z"]
        maj_in --> maj_and3["y AND z"]
        maj_and1 --> maj_xor["XOR Tree"]
        maj_and2 --> maj_xor
        maj_and3 --> maj_xor
        maj_xor --> maj_out["Maj = (x AND y) XOR (x AND z) XOR (y AND z)"]
    end
    
    classDef blue fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#88c5ff,color:#ffffff
    classDef red fill:#5f1e3a,stroke:#ff8855,color:#ffffff
    classDef green fill:#1e5f3a,stroke:#88ff95,color:#ffffff
    
    class ch_in,ch_and1,ch_not,ch_and2,ch_xor,ch_out blue
    class maj_in,maj_and1,maj_and2,maj_and3,maj_xor,maj_out red
```

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid is used here to render **block-level functional architecture flows** that capture the logical interactions of SHA-512 and MAC operations, providing a clear view of data flow that is reproducible in any markdown-aware toolchain.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define the term "Message Authentication Code" and list any three of its requirements.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Message Authentication Code (MAC)** is a cryptographic checksum computed over a message $M$ using a **shared secret key $K$**, producing a fixed-size tag $T = \text{MAC}_K(M)$ that assures the receiver of both the **integrity** and **authenticity** of $M$.

**Three requirements (1 mark each):**

1. **Ease of Computation** — For a known key $K$ and message $M$, the MAC tag must be efficiently computable.
2. **Computational Resistance to Forgery** — Without knowing $K$, an attacker must find it computationally infeasible to forge a valid tag for any new message $M'$.
3. **Compression** — The MAC function must map an arbitrarily long message $M$ to a fixed-length tag of $n$ bits.

> [!TIP]
> Any 3 of the 5 requirements from §2.3 will fetch full 3 marks.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Mention the hash output size, block size, and number of rounds in SHA-512.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Hash output size | **512 bits** |
| Block (message chunk) size | **1024 bits** |
| Number of rounds per block | **80** |

(1 mark per correct entry.)

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**(a)** Describe the complete preprocessing stage of SHA-512 for a message of length $L$ bits. Show how the padding length $k$ is computed and what the final padded message structure looks like. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Ch** and **Maj** functions used in SHA-512 with their logical expressions. What is the role of the message schedule $W_t$ and how is it computed for $t \in [16, 79]$? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 marks

The preprocessing stage of SHA-512 consists of three mandatory steps:

**Step 1 — Append a '1' bit** to the end of the original message. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Append $k$ zero bits**, where $k$ is the smallest non-negative integer satisfying:
$$L + 1 + k \equiv 896 \pmod{1024}$$

Therefore, $k = (895 - L) \bmod 1024$. (2 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> The value 896 comes from the fact that the final 128-bit length field occupies the last 128 bits of a 1024-bit block, leaving $1024 - 128 = 896$ bits for the message plus its 1-bit '1' marker.

**Step 3 — Append a 128-bit big-endian representation of the original message length $L$.** (1 mark)

**Final Structure (3 marks):**
$$\underbrace{\text{Original Message}}_{L \text{ bits}} \Vert \underbrace{1}_{1 \text{ bit}} \Vert \underbrace{0 \ldots 0}_{k \text{ bits}} \Vert \underbrace{L \text{ in 128-bit binary}}_{128 \text{ bits}}$$

The total length becomes a multiple of 1024. The padded message is then split into $N$ blocks of 1024 bits each, where $N = \lceil (L + 1 + k + 128)/1024 \rceil$.

**Worked Numerical Example (bonus, for clarity):**
For $M =$ "abc", $L = 24$ bits. Then $k = (895 - 24) \bmod 1024 = 871$. Final length = $24+1+871+128 = 1024$ bits, i.e., exactly **1 block**.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 marks

**The Ch Function (Choice):** (2 marks)

$$\text{Ch}(x, y, z) = (x \land y) \oplus (\lnot x \land z)$$

It "chooses" between $y$ and $z$: when $x=1$, the result is $y$; when $x=0$, the result is $z$. This is essentially a hardware multiplexer.

**The Maj Function (Majority):** (2 marks)

$$\text{Maj}(x, y, z) = (x \land y) \oplus (x \land z) \oplus (y \land z)$$

It outputs the bitwise majority of the three inputs — i.e., 1 if at least two of the three are 1.

**Message Schedule $W_t$:** (3 marks)

The 1024-bit block is divided into 16 × 64-bit words which directly form $W_0$ through $W_{15}$. For the remaining 64 rounds ($t = 16$ to $79$), the schedule is **extended** by:
$$W_t = \sigma_1(W_{t-2}) + W_{t-7} + \sigma_0(W_{t-15}) + W_{t-16} \pmod{2^{64}}$$

where:
$$\sigma_0(x) = \text{ROTR}^1(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^8(x) \oplus \text{SHR}^7(x)$$
$$\sigma_1(x) = \text{ROTR}^{19}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{61}(x) \oplus \text{SHR}^6(x)$$

The schedule **diffuses** the message bits across all 80 rounds, ensuring that even a single bit change in the original message propagates throughout the digest (avalanche effect).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the compression function of SHA-512. List the initial hash values $H_0 \ldots H_7$ and the origin from which they are derived. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a Message Authentication Code? Explain in detail the **security requirements** that a MAC must satisfy. Differentiate between a MAC and a hash function. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 marks

**Block Diagram (4 marks):** Refer to §4.1 Mermaid diagram and reproduce the following textual flow:

- **Inputs to compression function:** Current hash state $H_0 \ldots H_7$ and current message block $M^{(i)}$ (1024 bits).
- **Step 1:** Load $(a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h) = (H_0, H_1, \ldots, H_7)$.
- **Step 2:** Generate message schedule $W_0, W_1, \ldots, W_{79}$ (16 from block + 64 extended).
- **Step 3:** For $t = 0$ to $79$ compute $T_1, T_2$ and update working variables.
- **Step 4:** Add working variables back to hash state.
- **Output:** Updated $H_0 \ldots H_7$.

**Initial Hash Values (2 marks):**

| Register | Hex Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| $H_0$ | 6a09e667f3bcc908 |
| $H_1$ | bb67ae8584caa73b |
| $H_2$ | 3c6ef372fe94f82b |
| $H_3$ | a54ff53a5f1d36f1 |
| $H_4$ | 510e527fade682d1 |
| $H_5$ | 9b05688c2b3e6c1f |
| $H_6$ | 1f83d9abfb41bd6b |
| $H_7$ | 5be0cd19137e2179 |

**Origin (1 mark):** These values are the **first 64 bits of the fractional parts of the square roots of the first eight prime numbers** (2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19).

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 marks

**MAC Definition (2 marks):** A Message Authentication Code is a short piece of information (tag) generated by a keyed hash algorithm, used to authenticate a message and confirm both its integrity and that it came from the stated sender (who shares the key $K$).

**Security Requirements of MAC (3 marks):**

1. **Resistance to forgery (existential unforgeability, EUF-CMA):** Without the secret key, an adversary should not be able to produce a valid $(M, T)$ pair, even after observing many valid pairs.
2. **Key recovery resistance:** Knowing many input–output pairs $(M_i, \text{MAC}_K(M_i))$ must not let an attacker recover $K$.
3. **Length-extension resistance:** For schemes like HMAC, the design must prevent an attacker from forging tags for $M' = M \Vert \text{extra}$ using only $\text{MAC}_K(M)$.
4. **Compression and Determinism:** The MAC must be a deterministic, fixed-length function of $M$ and $K$.

**MAC vs Hash Function (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Hash (SHA-512) | MAC (HMAC-SHA512) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Key involvement | None | Shared secret key $K$ |
| Authenticity? | No (anyone can compute) | Yes (only key-holders) |
| Collision resistance needed? | Yes | Yes (plus forgery resistance) |
| Example use | File integrity, blockchain | API requests, TLS records |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the bit-lengths:** Students often write SHA-512 has a 1024-bit hash (it does NOT — 1024 bits is the *block size*; the *hash* is 512 bits). This loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the final feed-forward addition:** The step $H_j \leftarrow H_j + a$ (etc.) at the end of every block is **mandatory** in board answers. Skipping it costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up big-Sigma and small-sigma:** Big-Sigma ($\Sigma_0, \Sigma_1$) is for round computations $T_1, T_2$; small-sigma ($\sigma_0, \sigma_1$) is for the message schedule $W_t$. Reversing them is a frequent mistake.
> 4. **Writing the padding length formula without mod 1024:** Always include the modular condition; otherwise the answer is incomplete.
> 5. **Listing "confidentiality" as a MAC property:** MACs do **NOT** provide confidentiality — they only guarantee integrity and authenticity. This is a common conceptual trap.
> 6. **Forgetting the message length field:** In the padding step, the 128-bit length field is **mandatory** and must be present in any diagram.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **SHA-512 is part of SHA-2 family**, standardized in FIPS 180-4.
- [x] **Hash length = 512 bits**, block length = 1024 bits, word size = 64 bits, **rounds = 80**.
- [x] **Padding formula:** $L + 1 + k \equiv 896 \pmod{1024}$, i.e., $k = (895 - L) \bmod 1024$.
- [x] **Padding steps:** Append '1' → Append $k$ zeros → Append 128-bit big-endian $L$.
- [x] **Initial hash values $H_0 \ldots H_7$** come from first 64 bits of fractional parts of $\sqrt{p}$ for primes 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19.
- [x] **Round constants $K_0 \ldots K_{79}$** come from first 64 bits of fractional parts of $\sqrt[3]{p}$ for first 80 primes.
- [x] **Six logical functions:** Ch, Maj, $\Sigma_0, \Sigma_1, \sigma_0, \sigma_1$ — big-Sigma for rounds, small-sigma for schedule.
- [x] **Message schedule $W_t$:** $W_t = \sigma_1(W_{t-2}) + W_{t-7} + \sigma_0(W_{t-15}) + W_{t-16}$ for $t \in [16, 79]$.
- [x] **Compression function update:** Compute $T_1, T_2$, cyclically shift working variables.
- [x] **Final feed-forward addition** $H_j \leftarrow H_j + \text{variable}$ is **crucial** (prevents length-extension).
- [x] **MAC = Keyed hash** that provides **integrity + authenticity**, but **not confidentiality or non-repudiation**.
- [x] **MAC requirements:** Ease of computation, compression, forgery resistance, key non-recoverability, collision-style resistance.
- [x] **HMAC** is the most popular MAC, built using a nested hash with ipad (0x36) and opad (0x5C).
- [x] **MAC vs Digital Signature:** MACs are symmetric (faster, no non-repudiation); Signatures are asymmetric (slower, provide non-repudiation).
- [x] **MD5 is broken**; SHA-256/SHA-512 remain secure as of 2024.
- [x] **Avalanche effect:** A 1-bit change in input completely transforms the 512-bit output — a property of all good hash functions.
- [x] **KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw the block diagram — it typically earns 2–3 marks on its own in 14-mark questions.
- [x] **Don't confuse SHA-512 with SHA-512/256** — the latter is a truncated variant producing 256-bit output.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Uses
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT501 - FOUNDATIONS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Uses

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCGT501 - Foundations of Cryptography, Module 4)**, the topic **"Uses"** refers to the **practical applications and deployment scenarios of Cryptographic Hash Functions**. A cryptographic hash function $H(\cdot)$ is a deterministic mathematical algorithm that maps an arbitrary-length input message $M$ to a fixed-length output $h$, called the **message digest** or **hash value**, formally expressed as:

$$h = H(M)$$

where $H : \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ for some fixed output length $n$ (e.g., $n = 256$ bits for SHA-256).

The **"Uses"** topic enumerates the engineering and security applications for which these primitives are deployed across modern computing infrastructure, ranging from **message integrity verification** to **blockchain consensus**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students are expected to enumerate, classify, and justify the use of hash functions in **(a) Message Authentication, (b) Digital Signatures, (c) Password Storage, (d) Data Integrity, and (e) Key Derivation**. A common KTU board question asks: *"List and explain any four applications of cryptographic hash functions."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **unique digital fingerprint** for every document, email, or file on your computer. Just as a human fingerprint uniquely identifies a person regardless of their size or age, a **cryptographic hash** uniquely identifies a digital message regardless of its length.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Tamper-Evident Seal":**
> Think of a cryptographic hash as a **numbered tamper-evident wax seal** used on medieval royal letters. When the king sends a decree, he presses his unique seal (a fixed-size stamp) onto the document (arbitrary length). Three critical guarantees follow:
> 1. **Fingerprinting** — Every document gets a unique seal pattern (the hash).
> 2. **Tamper Detection** — If a thief alters even a single word in the letter, the seal pattern changes completely when re-applied, instantly revealing the forgery.
> 3. **Compactness** — Instead of safeguarding the entire long document, the recipient only needs to verify the small fixed-size seal to confirm the document's authenticity.
>
> This is *exactly* how modern hash functions enable secure digital communication, password storage, and blockchain transactions.

## 1.3 The Three Foundational Security Properties

Every legitimate **use case** of a hash function relies on three core security properties:

| # | Property | Formal Statement | Engineering Use |
|---|----------|------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Pre-image Resistance** | Given $h$, it is computationally infeasible to find $M$ such that $H(M) = h$. | Password storage |
| 2 | **Second Pre-image Resistance** | Given $M_1$, it is infeasible to find $M_2 \neq M_1$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$. | Data integrity |
| 3 | **Collision Resistance** | It is computationally infeasible to find any $M_1 \neq M_2$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$. | Digital signatures |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** If a KTU question asks "Which property ensures…", map **password storage** to **pre-image resistance**, **virus/malware integrity** to **second pre-image resistance**, and **digital signature forgery** to **collision resistance**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Classification of Hash Function Uses

The applications of cryptographic hash functions can be systematically classified into **seven primary engineering use-cases**. Each use-case exploits a specific combination of the three security properties.

### Use-Case 1: Message Authentication (via MACs and HMAC)

A **Message Authentication Code (MAC)** uses a hash function combined with a secret key to provide both **integrity** and **authentication**. The **Hash-based MAC (HMAC)** construction is:

$$\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H\Big((K \oplus \text{opad}) \parallel H((K \oplus \text{ipad}) \parallel M)\Big)$$

where:
- $K$ is the secret key
- $\text{opad} = 0x5C5C\ldots5C$ (outer pad, 64 bytes)
- $\text{ipad} = 0x3636\ldots36$ (inner pad, 64 bytes)
- $\oplus$ denotes XOR
- $\parallel$ denotes bitwise concatenation

> [!NOTE]
> **Why HMAC and not plain $H(K \parallel M)$?** Because of **length-extension attacks** that affect raw hash constructions like SHA-256. HMAC's nested structure defeats this attack class.

### Use-Case 2: Digital Signatures (Hash-and-Sign Paradigm)

Digital signature schemes (RSA, ECDSA, EdDSA) do **not** sign the message directly due to performance constraints. Instead, they sign the **hash** of the message:

$$\text{Sign}(M) = \text{Sign}_{\text{private}}(H(M))$$

$$\text{Verify}(M, S) = \text{Verify}_{\text{public}}(H(M), S)$$

This dramatically reduces the computational cost when $M$ is large (e.g., a software update of **several GB**), since hashing produces a fixed-size output (e.g., **256 bits for SHA-256**), regardless of input size.

### Use-Case 3: Password Storage

Operating systems and web servers **never store passwords in plaintext**. Instead, they store the hash:

$$\text{Stored Credential} = H(\text{password} \parallel \text{salt})$$

where **salt** is a per-user random nonce that prevents **rainbow table attacks**. Modern systems use **memory-hard** hash functions like **bcrypt**, **scrypt**, or **Argon2id** to resist GPU/ASIC brute-force attacks.

### Use-Case 4: Data Integrity Verification (Checksums & File Fingerprinting)

Software distributors publish the hash of a file so users can verify the download was not corrupted or tampered with:

$$\text{Integrity Check} : H(\text{downloaded file}) \stackrel{?}{=} \text{published hash}$$

**Examples:** Linux ISO distributions publish SHA-256 checksums; Git uses **SHA-1** (now migrating to SHA-256) to identify commits uniquely.

### Use-Case 5: Key Derivation (KDFs and HKDF)

Hash functions are the building blocks of **Key Derivation Functions (KDFs)** that convert low-entropy secrets (like passwords) into cryptographically strong symmetric keys:

$$K_{\text{derived}} = \text{HKDF}(\text{IKM}, \text{salt}, \text{info})$$

where:
- $\text{IKM}$ = Input Keying Material
- $\text{salt}$ = optional non-secret random value
- $\text{info}$ = context/application-specific info

This is used in **TLS 1.3** handshake, **Wi-Fi WPA3** (using **SAE**), and **disk encryption (LUKS)**.

### Use-Case 6: Commitment Schemes

A cryptographic commitment scheme allows a party to "lock in" a value $x$ without revealing it, using:

$$\text{Commit}(x, r) = H(x \parallel r)$$

where $r$ is a random nonce. Later, the committer reveals $x$ and $r$, and verifiers recompute the hash to confirm the value was not changed. Used in **e-voting protocols**, **zero-knowledge proofs**, and **blockchain sealed-bid auctions**.

### Use-Case 7: Proof-of-Work and Consensus (Blockchain)

In **Bitcoin** and similar cryptocurrencies, miners must find a **nonce** $N$ such that:

$$H(\text{block header} \parallel N) < \text{target difficulty}$$

This is the **Proof-of-Work (PoW)** puzzle that secures the blockchain. The **average work** to find such a nonce is governed by:

$$E[\text{hashes to solve}] = 2^d$$

where $d$ is the number of leading zero bits required (the **mining difficulty**).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Application | Core Construction | Key Property Exploited | Typical Hash Used |
|---|-------------|-------------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Message Authentication** | $\text{HMAC}(K, M)$ | Pre-image + Key secrecy | HMAC-SHA-256 |
| 2 | **Digital Signatures** | $\text{Sig}_{\text{priv}}(H(M))$ | Collision Resistance | SHA-256, SHA-3 |
| 3 | **Password Storage** | $H(\text{pwd} \parallel \text{salt})$ | Pre-image Resistance | Argon2, bcrypt, PBKDF2 |
| 4 | **Data Integrity** | Compare $H(\text{file})$ with published | Second Pre-image | SHA-256, MD5 (legacy) |
| 5 | **Key Derivation** | $\text{HKDF}(S, \text{salt}, \text{info})$ | Pseudorandomness | HKDF-SHA-256 |
| 6 | **Commitment** | $H(x \parallel r)$ with $r$ random | Hiding + Binding | SHA-256 |
| 7 | **Proof-of-Work** | $H(\text{header} \parallel N) < T$ | Pre-image on partial output | SHA-256 (double) |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Relevance (KTU Placement Context):**
> - **Banking (RBI, SWIFT):** HMAC-SHA-256 secures API transactions.
> - **Cloud (AWS, Azure):** SHA-256 fingerprints every stored object (S3 ETag).
> - **DevOps (Docker, Kubernetes):** Image layers are content-addressed by SHA-256 digests.
> - **Cybersecurity (Kerala Police Cyberdome):** Forensic disk imaging uses SHA-1/SHA-256 to prove evidence integrity in court.
> - **Networking (TLS 1.3, IPsec):** HKDF derives session keys from handshake secrets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Security Reduction for Hash-Based Password Storage

We derive the **work factor** for an attacker attempting to recover a password $P$ given only the stored hash $h = H(P \parallel s)$ with a per-user random salt $s$ of length $b$ bits.

### Step 1 — Define the Attack Model

Assume the attacker has:
- Access to the password hash database containing $\{h_i, s_i\}$ for all $N$ users.
- A dictionary $\mathcal{D}$ of $D$ candidate passwords (where typically $D \approx 10^9$ to $10^{14}$ for well-curated dictionaries).
- Computation budget allowing $Q$ hash evaluations.

### Step 2 — Formulate the Probability of Compromise

For a single user, the probability that a random guess $P' \in \mathcal{D}$ matches the true password $P$ (assuming unique passwords) is:

$$P_{\text{match}} = \frac{1}{D}$$

### Step 3 — Work Factor with Salt

Because the salt $s$ is **per-user random** of $b$ bits, the attacker cannot pre-compute a **rainbow table** (a single table valid for all users). Instead, they must compute:

$$H(P' \parallel s) \quad \text{for each } (P', s) \text{ pair}$$

The expected number of trials to crack a single password is therefore:

$$E[\text{trials}] = D$$

This is independent of the number of users $N$, but the **storage cost** of a per-user rainbow table grows as $D \times 2^b$, which is computationally infeasible for $b \geq 128$ bits.

### Step 4 — Introduce Memory-Hard Functions

Modern password hashing schemes like **Argon2id** add a *memory* dimension $m$ (in KB) and *time* dimension $t$ (iterations). The attacker's work factor becomes:

$$W_{\text{attacker}} = D \times t \times m$$

For example, Argon2id with $t = 3$ iterations and $m = 64$ MB raises the cost of one guess to roughly **3 SHA-256 equivalents spread across 64 MB of memory**, defeating GPU/ASIC parallelization.

### Step 5 — Conclude the Security Bound

The probability of cracking at least one of $N$ user passwords in a dictionary attack is:

$$P_{\text{any cracked}} = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{Q}{D}\right)^N \approx \frac{N \cdot Q}{D} \quad \text{for } Q \ll D$$

This shows that **increasing $D$ (via password policy)** and **increasing $t, m$ (via slow hashing)** are mathematically equivalent in raising the work factor.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Uses to Hash Properties

The following matrix rigorously maps each use-case to the security property it most critically depends on:

| Use Case | Pre-image | 2nd Pre-image | Collision | Pseudorandom |
|----------|:---------:|:-------------:|:---------:|:------------:|
| HMAC | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ |
| Digital Signature | ✓ | ✓ | ✓✓✓ | — |
| Password Storage | ✓✓✓ | — | — | — |
| Integrity Check | — | ✓✓✓ | ✓ | — |
| HKDF | ✓✓ | — | ✓ | ✓✓✓ |
| Commitment | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ |
| Proof-of-Work | ✓✓✓ | — | — | ✓ |

*(Legend: ✓✓✓ = primary dependency, ✓✓ = strong dependency, ✓ = used, — = not directly required)*

## 3.3 Worked Example: Verifying a File's Integrity (KTU Numerical Style)

**Problem:** A software vendor publishes `installer.exe` along with the SHA-256 digest `d = 7d38c8b1...e9a2`. A user downloads the file and recomputes the hash. If the hash function is collision-resistant and outputs 256 bits, calculate:

(a) The probability that a corrupted file accidentally produces the same digest as the original.
(b) The expected number of trials an attacker would need to forge a colliding file via brute force (birthday bound).

**Solution:**

**(a) Probability of accidental match:** Since SHA-256 produces $2^{256}$ possible outputs, the probability that a random corrupted file collides with the original digest is:

$$P_{\text{accidental}} = \frac{1}{2^{256}} \approx 10^{-77}$$

This is astronomically smaller than the probability of a **hardware bit-flip error** ($10^{-15}$), confirming the integrity guarantee.

**(b) Expected trials via Birthday Attack:** By the **Birthday Paradox**, an attacker needs approximately $\sqrt{2^{256}} = 2^{128}$ trials to find any collision, because:

$$\sqrt{N} \approx 1.177 \times \sqrt{2^n} \quad \text{for output size } n = 256$$

$$E[\text{brute-force trials}] = 2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$$

At $10^{18}$ hashes/second on a nation-state supercomputer, this would take approximately $10^{13}$ years — vastly exceeding the age of the universe ($1.38 \times 10^{10}$ years). Hence, **SHA-256 is computationally secure for digital signatures and integrity checks.**

## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation: HMAC Construction Security

We derive the **pseudorandom advantage** of HMAC over a raw hash.

### Step 1 — Key Padding

The secret key $K$ (assumed shorter than the block size $B = 64$ bytes for SHA-256) is zero-padded to length $B$:

$$K' = K \parallel 0^{B - \vert K \vert}$$

### Step 2 — Inner and Outer Key Derivation

$$K_{\text{inner}} = K' \oplus \text{ipad} = K' \oplus (0x36)^{B}$$

$$K_{\text{outer}} = K' \oplus \text{opad} = K' \oplus (0x5C)^{B}$$

### Step 3 — Nested Hash Computation

$$\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H_{\text{outer}}\Big(K_{\text{outer}} \parallel H_{\text{inner}}(K_{\text{inner}} \parallel M)\Big)$$

### Step 4 — Security Bound

The Bellare-Canetti-Krawczyk (1996) result proves that if $H$ is a **pseudorandom function (PRF)**, then HMAC's advantage against a distinguishing adversary making $q$ queries and running in time $T$ is bounded by:

$$\text{Adv}_{\text{HMAC}}^{\text{PRF}}(q, T) \leq \text{Adv}_{H}^{\text{PRF}}(q, T') + \frac{q^2}{2^n}$$

where $T' \approx T + O(q \cdot B)$ and $n$ is the hash output size. For SHA-256 ($n = 256$), the birthday term $q^2 / 2^{256}$ is negligible even for $q = 2^{64}$ queries.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Classification of Hash Function Uses

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hash Function H of M] --> B[7 Primary Use Cases]
    B --> C1[Message Authentication]
    B --> C2[Digital Signatures]
    B --> C3[Password Storage]
    B --> C4[Data Integrity Checks]
    B --> C5[Key Derivation KDFs]
    B --> C6[Commitment Schemes]
    B --> C7[Proof of Work Blockchain]
    C1 --> D1[HMAC SHA 256]
    C2 --> D2[Sig priv H of M]
    C3 --> D3[H pwd plus salt]
    C4 --> D4[Compare H file with published digest]
    C5 --> D5[HKDF IKM salt info]
    C6 --> D6[H x parallel r random nonce]
    C7 --> D7[Find nonce N such that H header N less than target]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: HMAC Operational Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Message M] --> B[Concatenate with K inner XOR ipad]
    K1[Secret Key K] --> C[Pad K to block size 64 bytes]
    C --> D1[Compute K XOR ipad]
    C --> D2[Compute K XOR opad]
    D1 --> B
    B --> E[Apply inner hash H]
    E --> F[Concatenate with K outer XOR opad]
    D2 --> F
    F --> G[Apply outer hash H]
    G --> H[Output HMAC Tag]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Hash-and-Sign Digital Signature Paradigm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Original Message M arbitrary length] --> H[HASH]
    H --> D[Fixed size digest e.g. 256 bits]
    D --> E[Sign with private key e.g. RSA or ECDSA]
    E --> S[Digital Signature S]
    M --> T[Transmit M plus S]
    T --> V[Verifier receives]
    V --> H2[HASH M to recompute]
    H2 --> D2[Compare with decrypted S]
    S --> D3[Decrypt S using public key]
    D3 --> D4[Recover claimed digest]
    D2 --> Match{Digests match?}
    D4 --> Match
    Match -->|Yes| OK[Signature VALID message INTACT]
    Match -->|No| Fail[Signature INVALID or message TAMPERED]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Password Storage Using Salted Hash

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[User enters password P] --> A[Generate random salt s of 128 bits]
    A --> B[Compute h = H P parallel s]
    B --> C[Store h and s in database]
    C --> D[Later: user enters P prime]
    D --> E[Retrieve stored s]
    E --> F[Compute h prime = H P prime parallel s]
    F --> G{Compare h prime with stored h}
    G -->|Match| Login[Authentication SUCCESS]
    G -->|No match| Deny[Authentication FAILED]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Proof-of-Work Mining Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Miner collects transactions] --> B[Construct block header]
    B --> C[Set initial nonce N = 0]
    C --> D[Compute h = H SHA 256 header parallel N]
    D --> E{h less than target difficulty d?}
    E -->|No| F[Increment N by 1]
    F --> D
    E -->|Yes| G[Broadcast valid block]
    G --> H[Network verifies and accepts]
    H --> I[Miner receives block reward]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any four applications of cryptographic hash functions and briefly explain the security property each application relies on.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Message Authentication using HMAC** — Relies on *pre-image resistance* and *key secrecy*; the receiver recomputes HMAC$(K, M)$ and compares to detect tampering.
2. **Digital Signatures (Hash-and-Sign)** — Relies on *collision resistance*; the signer signs $H(M)$, and any forgery would require finding a colliding message.
3. **Password Storage** — Relies on *pre-image resistance*; the server stores $H(\text{pwd} \parallel \text{salt})$ so that recovering the password from the hash is computationally infeasible.
4. **Data Integrity Verification** — Relies on *second pre-image resistance*; the receiver recomputes $H(\text{file})$ and compares with the published digest to detect corruption or tampering.

*(Any four well-explained uses with correct property mapping = full 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is a Message Authentication Code (MAC)? How is HMAC constructed from a hash function?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Message Authentication Code (MAC)** is a cryptographic checksum $T = \text{MAC}(K, M)$ produced using a secret key $K$ shared between sender and receiver. It guarantees both **data integrity** and **data origin authentication** — any modification to $M$ or substitution by an attacker without knowledge of $K$ is detected.

**HMAC Construction:**

$$\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H\Big((K \oplus \text{opad}) \parallel H((K \oplus \text{ipad}) \parallel M)\Big)$$

where $K$ is zero-padded to the hash block size (64 bytes for SHA-256), $\text{ipad} = 0x36$ repeated 64 times, and $\text{opad} = 0x5C$ repeated 64 times. The outer hash is computed over the concatenation of $K \oplus \text{opad}$ with the inner hash output. This nested design defeats **length-extension attacks** that would otherwise compromise the simpler $H(K \parallel M)$ construction.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **role of hash functions in digital signatures**. With a neat diagram, describe the **hash-and-sign paradigm** and justify why the message is hashed before signing.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the use of hash functions in **password storage**. Derive the expression for the work factor of a dictionary attack against a salted password scheme, and explain how memory-hard functions (Argon2) improve security.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

**Step 1 — Why hash before signing?**
Public-key signature schemes (RSA, ECDSA) operate on integers of bounded size. A direct signature over a long message $M$ (e.g., a 1 GB software update) is computationally infeasible. Hashing reduces arbitrary-length input to a fixed-size digest (e.g., 256 bits), making signing tractable and constant-time.

**Step 2 — Hash-and-Sign Paradigm:**

1. **Signing:** Compute $d = H(M)$, then signature $S = \text{Sig}_{\text{priv}}(d)$.
2. **Transmit:** Send $(M, S)$ over an insecure channel.
3. **Verification:** Recompute $d' = H(M)$ from received $M$, recover $d = \text{Verify}_{\text{pub}}(S)$, and check $d' \stackrel{?}{=} d$.

**Step 3 — Diagram (as in Section 4.3 above).**

**Step 4 — Security Justification:**
If $H$ is collision-resistant, an attacker cannot construct $M' \neq M$ with $H(M') = H(M)$, which would let them reuse the signature $S$ on a different message. The collision-resistance property is therefore **essential** to prevent existential forgery.

**Step 5 — Real-world example:** RSA-PSS (Probabilistic Signature Scheme) used in TLS 1.3 mandates SHA-256 or SHA-384 as the hash primitive before signing.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Step 1 — Plaintext password storage is insecure** because a database breach exposes all credentials. The standard defense is to store $h = H(P \parallel s)$ where $s$ is a per-user random **salt**.

**Step 2 — Work factor derivation:**

For a dictionary of $D$ passwords, an attacker performing a brute-force search against a single salted user must compute:

$$E[\text{hash evaluations}] = D$$

Without salt, the attacker builds one **rainbow table** of size $D$ valid for all $N$ users, so expected work to crack any user is still $D$. With a per-user $b$-bit salt, rainbow table reuse is impossible, and the table must be recomputed per user, costing $D \cdot 2^b$ pre-computation.

**Step 3 — Memory-hard functions:**
Argon2id raises the cost per guess by adding a memory parameter $m$ and time parameter $t$:

$$W_{\text{per guess}} = t \times m$$

For $m = 64$ MB and $t = 3$, the attacker needs 64 MB of fast memory *per parallel guess*, which devastates GPU throughput (GPUs have limited memory per core) and ASIC efficiency. This raises the practical dictionary attack cost by **3–4 orders of magnitude** compared to unsalted SHA-256.

**Step 4 — Concluding security bound:**
The probability of cracking at least one password out of $N$ users with $Q$ attacker queries is:

$$P_{\text{breach}} \approx 1 - \left(1 - \frac{Q}{D}\right)^N$$

For $N = 10^6$ users, $Q = 10^9$ attacker guesses, and $D = 10^{12}$ (strong password policy), we get $P_{\text{breach}} \approx 10^{-3}$, which is acceptable when combined with **multi-factor authentication (MFA)** and **account lockout policies**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the construction of **HMAC** with a neat diagram. Explain why HMAC uses a nested (double-hash) construction rather than a single $H(K \parallel M)$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain how hash functions are used in **blockchain Proof-of-Work** consensus. Derive the expected number of hash trials required to find a valid nonce for a target difficulty $d$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**Step 1 — HMAC Construction Recap:**

$$\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H\Big((K \oplus \text{opad}) \parallel H((K \oplus \text{ipad}) \parallel M)\Big)$$

(See diagram in Section 4.2.)

**Step 2 — Length-Extension Attack on Naive Construction:**
If we use $\text{MAC} = H(K \parallel M)$, an attacker who knows $(M, \text{MAC})$ can compute $\text{MAC}' = H(K \parallel M \parallel \text{pad} \parallel M')$ for any chosen $M'$, *without knowing $K$*. This works because Merkle–Damgård hash functions (SHA-256, SHA-512) process data in blocks and expose internal state after $H(K \parallel M)$.

**Step 3 — How HMAC Defeats Length Extension:**
The outer hash consumes the secret $K$ *twice* (once via $K \oplus \text{ipad}$ feeding the inner hash, once via $K \oplus \text{opad}$ feeding the outer hash). An attacker who extends the inner hash output cannot append further blocks and re-feed them through the outer hash without knowing $K$.

**Step 4 — Security Proof Outline:**
By the Bellare-Canetti-Krawczyk (BCK 1996) theorem, if the underlying compression function $f$ of $H$ is a **pseudorandom function**, then HMAC is a **secure PRF** with advantage bounded by $q^2 / 2^n$ for $q$ queries against an $n$-bit output. For SHA-256 ($n = 256$), this is negligible for all practical $q$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Step 1 — Proof-of-Work Puzzle:**
A Bitcoin miner must find a nonce $N$ such that:

$$H(\text{block header} \parallel N) \leq T$$

where $T$ is the **target threshold** corresponding to difficulty $d$. The target is typically represented as requiring the hash to begin with $d$ leading zero bits.

**Step 2 — Probability of a Single Trial Succeeding:**
Each hash output is uniformly distributed over $2^{256}$ values (for SHA-256). The probability that a random nonce produces a hash with $d$ leading zeros is:

$$P_{\text{success}} = \frac{2^{256 - d}}{2^{256}} = 2^{-d}$$

**Step 3 — Expected Number of Trials:**
Since each trial is independent and identically distributed, the number of trials $X$ to success follows a **Geometric Distribution** with success probability $p = 2^{-d}$:

$$E[X] = \frac{1}{p} = 2^d$$

**Step 4 — Work for a Specific Difficulty:**
For Bitcoin's target difficulty $d \approx 74$ (as of 2024), the expected number of SHA-256 trials per block is approximately $2^{74} \approx 1.9 \times 10^{22}$. The entire network, operating at roughly $5 \times 10^{19}$ hashes/sec, finds a block on average every **10 minutes**, confirming the protocol's design intent.

**Step 5 — Why This Secures the Blockchain:**
- **Asymmetric cost:** A honest miner with $h$ hash-rate contributes fraction $h/H$ of the network work.
- **Sybil resistance:** Creating a fraudulent longer chain requires more than $50\%$ of the network's hash-rate (the "51% attack" threshold).
- **Difficulty adjustment:** Bitcoin re-targets $d$ every 2016 blocks to maintain the 10-minute interval.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Uses" with "Properties"** — A question asking for "applications of hash functions" expects 4–7 *engineering use-cases* (HMAC, signatures, password storage, integrity, KDF, commitment, PoW), not the three security properties. Listing only "pre-image, second pre-image, collision resistance" = 0 marks.
> 2. **Missing the property mapping** — Each use-case must explicitly name the security property it depends on. Simply writing "hash is used for passwords" without stating *pre-image resistance* loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting the salt in password storage** — Writing $H(\text{password})$ instead of $H(\text{password} \parallel \text{salt})$ is a serious omission worth 1 mark deduction.
> 4. **Confusing HMAC with $H(K \parallel M)$** — In 14-mark questions, the examiner expects you to derive the nested construction and *justify* why the nested form defeats length-extension attacks.
> 5. **Skipping diagrams** — KTU 2024 scheme mandates a diagram in any 7-mark sub-question on digital signatures or HMAC. Not drawing the block flow costs 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Wrong formula in PoW** — Writing $E[X] = d$ instead of $E[X] = 2^d$ is a common error. Always include the $2^d$ derivation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**
>
> ✅ **Seven core uses of hash functions to memorize:**
> 1. **HMAC** (Message Authentication) → pre-image + key secrecy
> 2. **Digital Signatures** (Hash-and-Sign) → collision resistance
> 3. **Password Storage** → pre-image resistance + salt
> 4. **Data Integrity** → second pre-image resistance
> 5. **Key Derivation (HKDF)** → pseudorandomness
> 6. **Commitment Schemes** → hiding + binding
> 7. **Proof-of-Work (Blockchain)** → pre-image on partial output
>
> ✅ **Key formulas to memorize:**
> - $h = H(M) : \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$
> - $\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H((K \oplus \text{opad}) \parallel H((K \oplus \text{ipad}) \parallel M))$
> - $E[\text{trials in PoW}] = 2^d$
> - $P_{\text{breach}} \approx 1 - (1 - Q/D)^N$
>
> ✅ **Standard hash algorithms (recall output sizes):**
> - **MD5** — 128 bits (broken, legacy only)
> - **SHA-1** — 160 bits (broken since 2017, deprecated)
> - **SHA-256** — 256 bits (current KTU standard)
> - **SHA-3 / Keccak** — sponge construction, NIST standard 2015
> - **Argon2id** — memory-hard, for password storage
>
> ✅ **Property-to-Use mapping (memorize this table):**
> - **Pre-image** ↔ Password storage, PoW
> - **2nd Pre-image** ↔ Integrity, virus detection
> - **Collision** ↔ Digital signatures, commitments
> - **Pseudorandom** ↔ HMAC, HKDF, stream ciphers
>
> ✅ **Engineering-context examples to cite in answers:**
> - **TLS 1.3** uses HKDF + HMAC-SHA-256
> - **Bitcoin** uses double SHA-256 for PoW
> - **Git** uses SHA-1 (migrating to SHA-256) for commit IDs
> - **Linux distros** publish SHA-256 checksums for ISOs
> - **AWS S3** uses MD5 (ETag) and SHA-256 for object integrity

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hash-based MAC (HMAC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT501 - FOUNDATIONS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hash-based MAC (HMAC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code)** is a specific, mathematically rigorous construction for computing a Message Authentication Code (MAC) by combining a **cryptographic hash function** $H$ with a **secret cryptographic key** $K$. It simultaneously guarantees **data integrity** (the message has not been altered) and **data origin authentication** (the message truly originates from a party who possesses the shared secret key $K$).

HMAC is formally standardized in:
- **IETF RFC 2104** (Internet Standard)
- **FIPS PUB 198-1** (Federal Information Processing Standard – U.S. NIST)
- **ISO/IEC 9797-2** (International Standard)

Mathematically, HMAC is defined as a two-level nested hashing structure:

$$
\text{HMAC}_K(m) = H\!\left(\left(K_0 \oplus \text{opad}\right) \;\Vert\; H\!\left(\left(K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}\right) \;\Vert\; m\right)\right)
$$

where the symbols carry the following KTU-board meanings:
- $H$ = Approved cryptographic hash function (e.g., **SHA-256**, **SHA-1**, **MD5** – though MD5 is cryptographically broken).
- $m$ = Arbitrary-length input message.
- $K$ = Secret shared symmetric key.
- $K_0$ = Key normalized to the hash function's **block length** $B$ bytes.
- $\text{ipad}$ = **Inner pad** = the byte $\mathbf{0x36}$ repeated $B$ times.
- $\text{opad}$ = **Outer pad** = the byte $\mathbf{0x5C}$ repeated $B$ times.
- $\Vert$ = Bitwise/bytewise concatenation.
- $\oplus$ = Bitwise XOR.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The Sealed Wax Envelope System

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain Hashing is a "Transparent Glass Box"** – anyone can see the contents and even produce their own identical hash (the **length-extension vulnerability**).
> 
> **HMAC is a "Sealed Wax-Envelope with a Secret Stamp"** – even if an attacker sees the outer envelope, they cannot reproduce the unique royal seal imprint without possessing the *shared royal signet ring* (the secret key $K$).

**Intuition Workflow:**
1. The **sender** and the **receiver** share a **secret key** (the signet ring).
2. The sender **inverts and seals** the message twice (two nested envelopes) using key-derived inner and outer padding.
3. The receiver, who holds the same secret key, can **unseal and re-stamp** the message to verify the imprint matches.
4. An outsider holding the envelope **cannot forge the imprint** because they lack the signet.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded for KTU Board Recall)

| Metric | Standard Value (SHA-256 example) |
|---|---|
| Block size $B$ | **64 bytes (512 bits)** |
| Output size $L$ | **32 bytes (256 bits)** |
| Recommended key length | $\geq L$ bytes; truncation allowed if $K > B$ |
| Key derivation cost | **One hash evaluation** (only if key $> B$) |
| Computation cost | **Two hash evaluations** per HMAC invocation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic – Broken Into 6 Discrete Steps

> [!NOTE]
> The HMAC construction is deliberately a **two-pass** nested scheme. The double pass is what immunizes HMAC against the famous **length-extension attack** that plagues raw $H(\text{secret} \Vert m)$ designs (e.g., naïve MACs built using $H(K \Vert m)$).

**Step 1 – Key Pre-processing (Normalization to Block Size $B$):**
- If $\text{len}(K) > B$: Compute $K_0 = H(K)$, then right-pad with **zero bytes** to reach $B$ bytes.
- If $\text{len}(K) < B$: Right-pad $K$ with **zero bytes** to reach $B$ bytes (this becomes $K_0$).
- **Why?** The hash function $H$ operates on fixed-size blocks; the XOR-with-pad step is only valid when the operand is exactly $B$ bytes long.

**Step 2 – Inner Key Stream ($S_i$):**
- $S_i = K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}$
- $S_i$ is a $B$-byte block that is *XOR-mixed* with the constant byte 0x36. The result still has length $B$.

**Step 3 – First (Inner) Hash Invocation:**
- Compute the inner hash: $H_{\text{inner}} = H(S_i \Vert m)$
- Why two different padding constants? If $\text{ipad} = \text{opad}$, the construction would collapse into a single $H((K_0 \oplus c) \Vert (K_0 \oplus c) \Vert m)$, opening doors for algebraic attacks. The two distinct constants $\mathbf{0x36}$ and $\mathbf{0x5C}$ provide **cryptographic separation** between the two stages.

**Step 4 – Outer Key Stream ($S_o$):**
- $S_o = K_0 \oplus \text{opad}$
- A fresh $B$-byte block, this time XOR-mixed with the constant byte 0x5C.

**Step 5 – Second (Outer) Hash Invocation:**
- Compute the outer hash: $H_{\text{outer}} = H(S_o \Vert H_{\text{inner}})$

**Step 6 – Optional Trunculation (Standard Compliant):**
- If an application needs a shorter tag (e.g., 96-bit MAC for IPsec/ESP), it may **truncate** $H_{\text{outer}}$ to its leftmost $t$ bytes, where $t \geq L/2$ is the KTU/FIPS-recommended lower bound.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **board-exam-ready reference**. Memorize the columns verbatim. Note the use of $\mid$ instead of $\vert$ symbol for safer markdown rendering when delimiters are mandatory.

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Typical Value (SHA-256) |
|---|---|---|
| $H(\cdot)$ | Cryptographic hash function | SHA-256 |
| $B$ | Block length of $H$ (bytes) | **64** |
| $L$ | Output length of $H$ (bytes) | **32** |
| $K$ | Secret shared key | $\geq L$ bytes (recommended) |
| $K_0$ | Normalized key of length $B$ | 64 bytes |
| $\text{ipad}$ | Inner pad: byte $0x36$ repeated $B$ times | 64 bytes |
| $\text{opad}$ | Outer pad: byte $0x5C$ repeated $B$ times | 64 bytes |
| $\text{HMAC}_K(m)$ | Final tag | 32 bytes |
| $t$ | Truncated output length | $\geq 16$ bytes |

**Canonical Master Equation (must be memorized):**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{HMAC}_K(m) = H\!\left(\left(K_0 \oplus \text{opad}\right) \;\Vert\; H\!\left(\left(K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}\right) \;\Vert\; m\right)\right)\;}
$$

**Inner-Hash Sub-Equation:**

$$
H_{\text{inner}} = H\!\left(\left(K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}\right) \;\Vert\; m\right)
$$

**Outer-Hash Sub-Equation:**

$$
H_{\text{outer}} = H\!\left(\left(K_0 \oplus \text{opad}\right) \;\Vert\; H_{\text{inner}}\right)
$$

**Key Normalization (full form):**

$$
K_0 = 
\begin{cases}
H(K) \;\Vert\; 0^{B-L}, & \text{if } \text{len}(K) > B \\
K \;\Vert\; 0^{B - \text{len}(K)}, & \text{if } \text{len}(K) \leq B
\end{cases}
$$

where $0^{x}$ denotes $x$ zero bytes.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> HMAC is **not a theoretical curiosity** — it is the workhorse MAC of the global internet:
> - **TLS 1.2 / 1.3** uses HMAC-SHA-256 (and HMAC-SHA-384) for its PRF (Pseudo-Random Function) in the key derivation of every HTTPS session.
> - **IPsec (ESP and AH)** uses HMAC-SHA-256-128 for packet authentication.
> - **JSON Web Tokens (JWT)** uses HMAC-SHA-256 in the HS256 algorithm.
> - **API Authentication (AWS Signature V4, OAuth 2.0)** uses HMAC-SHA-256.
> - **FIDO2 / WebAuthn** uses HMAC-SHA-256 in some device-bound credential schemes.

Thus, mastering HMAC is essential for any KTU student targeting careers in **cybersecurity, cloud engineering, blockchain, or secure software development**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walk-Through of the HMAC Algorithm (with Numerical Hash Substitution)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> To eliminate every form of "black box", we now mechanically trace **every byte** of an HMAC-SHA-1 computation with a *toy* key and message. SHA-1 is used here *only for instructional clarity* (its block size $B = 64$ bytes and output size $L = 20$ bytes are pedagogically simple). For production, SHA-256 or SHA-3 is mandated.

### 3.1.1 Given Inputs (Hypothetical KTU Numerical)

Let the underlying hash be **SHA-1**, so:
- $B = 64$ bytes
- $L = 20$ bytes
- Hash function: $H(\cdot) = \text{SHA-1}(\cdot)$

Let the secret key be:

$$
K = \text{"KTU2024"} = \texttt{0x4B 54 55 32 30 32 34}
$$

so $\text{len}(K) = 7$ bytes.

Let the message be:

$$
m = \text{"EXAM"} = \texttt{0x45 58 41 4D}
$$

so $\text{len}(m) = 4$ bytes.

### 3.1.2 Step 1 – Key Normalization (Compute $K_0$)

Since $\text{len}(K) = 7 < B = 64$, we right-pad with $(B - \text{len}(K)) = 57$ zero bytes:

$$
K_0 = K \;\Vert\; 0^{57}
$$

Concretely:

$$
K_0 = \texttt{0x4B 54 55 32 30 32 34 00 00 00 \ldots 00} \quad (\text{64 bytes total})
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 – Compute $\text{ipad}$ and $\text{opad}$ Strings

$$
\text{ipad} = \underbrace{\texttt{0x36} \; \texttt{0x36} \; \ldots \; \texttt{0x36}}_{64 \text{ bytes}}
$$

$$
\text{opad} = \underbrace{\texttt{0x5C} \; \texttt{0x5C} \; \ldots \; \texttt{0x5C}}_{64 \text{ bytes}}
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 – Inner Key Stream

$$
S_i = K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}
$$

Per-byte XOR logic: for each byte position $j$ (with $0 \leq j \leq 63$):

$$
S_i[j] = K_0[j] \oplus \text{ipad}[j]
$$

**Sample bytes (position 0 to 6 – the non-zero key portion):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_i[0] &= \texttt{0x4B} \oplus \texttt{0x36} = \texttt{0x7D} \\
S_i[1] &= \texttt{0x54} \oplus \texttt{0x36} = \texttt{0x62} \\
S_i[2] &= \texttt{0x55} \oplus \texttt{0x36} = \texttt{0x63} \\
S_i[3] &= \texttt{0x32} \oplus \texttt{0x36} = \texttt{0x04} \\
S_i[4] &= \texttt{0x30} \oplus \texttt{0x36} = \texttt{0x06} \\
S_i[5] &= \texttt{0x32} \oplus \texttt{0x36} = \texttt{0x04} \\
S_i[6] &= \texttt{0x34} \oplus \texttt{0x36} = \texttt{0x02}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sample bytes (positions 7 to 63 – the zero-padded key portion):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_i[7] &= \texttt{0x00} \oplus \texttt{0x36} = \texttt{0x36} \\
S_i[8] &= \texttt{0x00} \oplus \texttt{0x36} = \texttt{0x36} \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
S_i[63] &= \texttt{0x00} \oplus \texttt{0x36} = \texttt{0x36}
\end{aligned}
$$

So $S_i$ has the form: a *one-time key mask* in the first 7 positions and the constant byte 0x36 in the remaining 57 positions.

### 3.1.5 Step 4 – Inner Hash Computation

Form the inner preimage:

$$
P_{\text{inner}} = S_i \;\Vert\; m
$$

Substituting lengths:

$$
\text{len}(P_{\text{inner}}) = 64 + 4 = 68 \text{ bytes}
$$

Apply SHA-1 (one block of 64 bytes is processed in the first iteration; the second iteration processes the 4-byte tail plus the SHA-1 length-padding block):

$$
H_{\text{inner}} = \text{SHA-1}(P_{\text{inner}})
$$

Hypothetical output (illustrative; actual SHA-1 of arbitrary 68-byte input gives some 20-byte value):

$$
H_{\text{inner}} = \texttt{a1 b2 c3 d4 \ldots} \quad (\text{20 bytes})
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 5 – Outer Key Stream

$$
S_o = K_0 \oplus \text{opad}
$$

Per-byte logic (first 7 positions):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_o[0] &= \texttt{0x4B} \oplus \texttt{0x5C} = \texttt{0x17} \\
S_o[1] &= \texttt{0x54} \oplus \texttt{0x5C} = \texttt{0x08} \\
S_o[2] &= \texttt{0x55} \oplus \texttt{0x5C} = \texttt{0x09} \\
S_o[3] &= \texttt{0x32} \oplus \texttt{0x5C} = \texttt{0x6E} \\
S_o[4] &= \texttt{0x30} \oplus \texttt{0x5C} = \texttt{0x6C} \\
S_o[5] &= \texttt{0x32} \oplus \texttt{0x5C} = \texttt{0x6E} \\
S_o[6] &= \texttt{0x34} \oplus \texttt{0x5C} = \texttt{0x68}
\end{aligned}
$$

And for positions 7 to 63 (zero-padded region):

$$
S_o[j] = \texttt{0x00} \oplus \texttt{0x5C} = \texttt{0x5C}, \quad \text{for } 7 \leq j \leq 63
$$

### 3.1.7 Step 6 – Outer Hash Computation

Form the outer preimage:

$$
P_{\text{outer}} = S_o \;\Vert\; H_{\text{inner}}
$$

Substituting lengths:

$$
\text{len}(P_{\text{outer}}) = 64 + 20 = 84 \text{ bytes}
$$

Apply SHA-1:

$$
\text{HMAC}_K(m) = \text{SHA-1}(P_{\text{outer}})
$$

Hypothetical final tag (20 bytes):

$$
\text{HMAC}_K(m) = \texttt{f8 91 2a 7c 0b 9e 6f \ldots}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Why Two Hashes and Not One? (Security Justification)

Suppose a naïve designer proposed:

$$
\text{Naïve MAC} = H(K \;\Vert\; m)
$$

The **length-extension attack** works because, given $H(K \Vert m)$ and $\text{len}(m)$, an attacker can compute $H(K \Vert m \Vert \text{pad} \Vert m')$ for any chosen suffix $m'$, without knowing $K$. This violates the **existential unforgeability** requirement of a MAC.

In HMAC, the **outer hash** acts as a *final one-way gate*. Even if an attacker can length-extend the inner computation (which only yields $H(S_i \Vert m \Vert \text{pad} \Vert m')$), they cannot manipulate the outer $H(S_o \Vert \cdot)$ invocation without knowing $K_0$, because the outer hash re-mixes the secret $K_0$ *after* the inner has finalized.

Therefore, the security of HMAC reduces to the **pseudorandom function (PRF) property** of the underlying hash $H$ when keyed via the two-key derived stream $(S_i, S_o)$. Bellare's 2006 security proof (revisited in RFC 2104) established that HMAC's security is provably equivalent to the **collision resistance** of $H$ up to a factor of $2^L / \text{msg-length}$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Properties of HMAC vs Other MAC Schemes

> [!NOTE]
> This KTU-style comparison matrix is a frequent **2-mark short answer** on board exams.

| Property | HMAC | CMAC | GMAC | Poly1305 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Underlying primitive | Hash function | Block cipher (AES) | Hash in Galois mode | Stream cipher |
| Standard | FIPS 198, RFC 2104 | NIST SP 800-38B | NIST SP 800-38D | RFC 8439 |
| Block-size dependent | Yes ($B$) | Yes (16 bytes for AES) | No (counter-based) | No (16-byte key) |
| Vulnerable to length-extension | **No** (immune) | No | No (if nonce unique) | No |
| Provable security reduction | Yes (Bellare 2006) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Output length | $L$ (truncatable) | $L$ | 128 bits | 128/256 bits |
| Used in TLS 1.3 | **Yes (HMAC-SHA-256)** | No | **Yes (AES-GCM)** | Yes (ChaCha20-Poly1305) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram – Complete HMAC Functional Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The following flowchart captures the **byte-level data path** of HMAC. The left spine is the **inner-hash branch**; the right spine is the **outer-hash branch**. The block-cipher-style "round" indicators are Mermaid-safe alphanumeric IDs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    keyIn[Secret Key K] --> keyChk{Key length vs Block B}
    msgIn[Input Message m] --> innerConcat
    padIn[Inner pad ipad 0x36 x B] --> innerXOR
    padOut[Outer pad opad 0x5C x B] --> outerXOR
    keyChk -->|Longer than B| hashK[Hash H of K]
    keyChk -->|Shorter than B| zeroPad[Zero pad to B bytes]
    hashK --> zeroPad
    zeroPad --> kZero[Normalized key K0 of B bytes]
    kZero --> innerXOR[XOR K0 with ipad]
    kZero --> outerXOR[XOR K0 with opad]
    innerXOR --> innerConcat[Concatenate Si with message m]
    innerConcat --> innerHash[Apply H first pass]
    innerHash --> innerOut[Inner digest H inner L bytes]
    innerOut --> outerConcat[Concatenate So with inner digest]
    outerXOR --> outerConcat
    outerConcat --> outerHash[Apply H second pass]
    outerHash --> truncOpt{Output truncation needed}
    truncOpt -->|No| finalTag[Final HMAC tag L bytes]
    truncOpt -->|Yes t less than L| truncFn[Truncate to t bytes]
    truncFn --> finalTag
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram – Attack-Defense Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    naive["Naive MAC: H K concat m"] --> vuln[Length Extension Attack Possible]
    hmac["HMAC: nested H with ipad and opad"] --> safe[Attack Mitigated by outer key stream]
    vuln --> bad[Forgery possible without K]
    safe --> good[Forgery requires breaking H or knowing K]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture (Engineering Schematic)

```
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|                     HMAC ENGINE                           |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|  +--------+      +-----------------+      +-----------+   |
|  | Key K  | ---> | Key Normalizer  | ---> |   K_0     |   |
|  |  (any) |      | (hash+zero-pad) |      | (B bytes) |   |
|  +--------+      +-----------------+      +-----+-----+   |
|                                            |             |
|                          +-----+   +-------+             |
|                          |     v   v                     |
|                          |  +------------------+         |
|                          |  | XOR with ipad     |        |
|                          |  | (0x36 repeated)   |        |
|                          |  +--------+---------+         |
|                          |           |                   |
|                          |           v                   |
|   m (input) -------------+---->  H (inner SHA)           |
|                          |           |                   |
|                          |           v                   |
|                          |  +------------------+         |
|                          |  | XOR with opad     |        |
|                          |  | (0x5C repeated)   |        |
|                          |  +--------+---------+         |
|                          |           |                   |
|                          |           v                   |
|                          +--->   H (outer SHA)           |
|                                      |                   |
|                                      v                   |
|                              +---------------+           |
|                              | HMAC tag (L)  |           |
|                              +---------------+           |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
```
