By Chebyshev's inequality, the deviation from $n/2$ is tightly controlled for large $n$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Dimension | Pre-image Resistance | Second Pre-image Resistance | Collision Resistance |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------------|----------------------|
| **Attack Goal** | Recover $M$ from $H(M)$ | Forge $M_2$ for given $M_1$ | Find any $M_1 \neq M_2$ pair |
| **Complexity** | $2^{n}$ | $2^{n}$ | $2^{n/2}$ |
| **Adversary's Input** | Hash $y$ | Message $M_1$ | Nothing |
| **Application** | Password hashing | Digital signatures | SSL/TLS, blockchain |
| **Algorithm Example** | bcrypt, Argon2 | RSA-PSS, ECDSA | X.509, Bitcoin mining |

**One-Directional Relationship (2 marks):**

The implication chain is:

$$\text{Collision Resistance} \implies \text{Second Pre-image} \implies \text{Pre-image}$$

**Why one-directional? (2 marks):**

1. **Forward direction holds:** If you can solve collision resistance, you automatically can solve second pre-image (just take the given $M_1$ as one half of your collision pair).

2. **Reverse fails:** A function can be pre-image resistant (hard to invert) but *not* collision resistant (still vulnerable to birthday attacks). 

   *Example:* A function $H(M) = \text{first 128 bits of SHA-256}(M)$ is pre-image resistant (inverting requires inverting SHA-256) but fails collision resistance (only 128 bits of output means birthday attack succeeds in $2^{64}$ operations).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Conflating the Work Factors (–2 marks typical):** Students often write $2^{n}$ for collision resistance. The correct answer is $2^{n/2}$ (Birthday bound).
> 
> 2. **Claiming the Reverse Implication (–2 marks):** Writing "Pre-image $\Rightarrow$ Collision" is **false**. The implication is strictly one-way in the forward direction only.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the Avalanche Effect in design questions (–1 to –2 marks):** When asked about hash function *requirements*, KTU expects all 3 security properties *plus* the operational properties (determinism, avalanche, efficiency).
> 
> 4. **Mixing up SHA-1 with SHA-256 parameters (–1 mark):** SHA-1 has $n = 160$, SHA-256 has $n = 256$. These are *not* interchangeable.
> 
> 5. **Not stating the Birthday Paradox in derivations (–1 to –3 marks):** Any derivation involving collision complexity must explicitly reference the Birthday Paradox and the $2^{n/2}$ result.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before KTU Exam:**

- ✅ A cryptographic hash function $H: \{0,1\}^{*} \rightarrow \{0,1\}^{n}$ maps arbitrary inputs to **fixed** $n$-bit outputs.

- ✅ The **three security requirements** are: **Pre-image Resistance**, **Second Pre-image Resistance**, and **Collision Resistance** (mnemonic: **P-S-C**).

- ✅ **Pre-image resistance** = one-wayness; given $y$, cannot find $M$. Work factor: $2^{n}$.

- ✅ **Second pre-image resistance** = given $M_1$, cannot find $M_2 \neq M_1$ with same hash. Work factor: $2^{n}$.

- ✅ **Collision resistance** = cannot find *any* colliding pair $M_1 \neq M_2$. Work factor: $2^{n/2}$ (**Birthday bound**).

- ✅ **Implication hierarchy:** Collision $\Rightarrow$ 2nd Pre-image $\Rightarrow$ Pre-image. The **reverse does NOT hold**.

- ✅ The **Birthday Paradox** reduces the collision search from $O(2^{n})$ to $O(2^{n/2})$ — quadratic speedup.

- ✅ The **Avalanche Effect** requires $\approx n/2$ output bits to flip per 1-bit input change.

- ✅ **Operational requirements** include: determinism, fixed output length, efficiency, avalanche effect, pseudorandomness, and puzzle friendliness.

- ✅ **SHA-1** ($n = 160$) is **BROKEN** (SHAttered attack, 2017). **SHA-256** ($n = 256$) remains **SECURE**.

- ✅ **Real-world applications** map to specific requirements:
  - Password storage $\rightarrow$ Pre-image resistance
  - Digital signatures $\rightarrow$ Second pre-image resistance
  - SSL/TLS certificates / Blockchain $\rightarrow$ Collision resistance

- ✅ **NIST minimum recommendation (2024):** Use SHA-256 or higher for new systems; SHA-1 is **deprecated**.

- ✅ **Coefficient of Variation for Avalanche Effect** in SHA-256: $CV = 1/\sqrt{256} = 1/16 = 6.25\%$ — extremely stable.

- ✅ **Exam quick answer formula:** Collision work factor = $2^{n/2}$. Pre-image work factor = $2^{n}$. Always state the **Birthday Paradox** when discussing collisions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direct vs Arbitrated Digital Signatures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT501 - FOUNDATIONS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Direct vs Arbitrated Digital Signatures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Direct Digital Signature (DDS)

A **Direct Digital Signature** is a cryptographic signature scheme where the sender (signer) computes the digital signature directly on the message using their own **private key**, and the receiver verifies the signature using the sender's publicly available **public key** — *without involving any third party* in the process. The communication happens in a peer-to-peer manner between the source and destination.

Formally, the signature is generated as:

$$\sigma = S_{K_{PR}}(M) \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \sigma = S_{K_{PR}}(H(M))$$

where $H(M)$ is a cryptographic hash of the message, and verification is done as:

$$V_{K_{PU}}(M, \sigma) = \text{Valid} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \sigma \text{ was created with matching } K_{PR}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In direct digital signatures, **only the two communicating parties are involved**. The sender signs using their own private key, and the receiver verifies using the sender's public key. No arbiter or TTP (Trusted Third Party) participates in the signature generation or verification step.

## 1.2 Arbitrated Digital Signature (ADS)

An **Arbitrated Digital Signature** is a scheme where the sender does *not* sign the message directly for the receiver. Instead, the message and signature are first submitted to a mutually trusted **arbitrator (A)**. The arbitrator verifies the authenticity of the sender, optionally timestamps the message, and then forwards the signed, certified message to the receiver.

Mathematically, the operation is sequenced as:

$$M' = M \,\|\, \sigma \,\|\, E_{K_{PA}}(T) \quad \xrightarrow{\text{Arbitrator}} \quad \text{Certified and forwarded}$$

where $T$ is a timestamp encrypted with the arbitrator's public key.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction:** The **arbitrator's role is to act as a witness**. The arbitrator verifies the sender's identity and the integrity of the signature, then re-sends the message with its own certification to the receiver. This solves the *repudiation problem* inherent in direct schemes.

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy

**Direct Digital Signature** is like sending a **handwritten, notarized letter** where only you and the receiver exist — you sign it, the receiver checks the signature, and the matter ends there. If you later deny signing, there's no neutral witness.

**Arbitrated Digital Signature** is like sending a **letter through a lawyer's office**. You sign the letter, send it to your lawyer (the arbiter), the lawyer verifies it's genuinely from you, stamps it, dates it, and then forwards the certified letter to the receiver. If you later deny sending it, the lawyer is the witness.

| Aspect | Real-World Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Direct Signature | Peer-to-peer signed contract |
| Arbitrated Signature | Lawyer-mediated or Notary-mediated signing |
| Hash Function | Document fingerprint |
| Private/Public Key | Pen seal (private) + Public seal-recognition (public) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Examiners often test whether the student understands *why* arbitrated schemes exist despite being slower. The answer is always: **Non-repudiation and dispute resolution**.

---

## 1.4 Operational Entities

In both schemes, the following entities participate:

- **Sender (S)** — the originator of the message and signer.
- **Receiver (R)** — the verifier of the signature and the message destination.
- **Arbiter / Arbitrator (A)** — *(only in ADS)* a trusted third party.
- **Key Pairs** — every entity has a private key $K_{PR}$ and a public key $K_{PU}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the *encryption step* with the *signing step*. Remember: **Signing uses the sender's PRIVATE key**, while **verification uses the sender's PUBLIC key**. The reverse would be *confidentiality*, not *authentication*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Direct Digital Signature — Operational Breakdown

The Direct Digital Signature operates in **two phases**: a *signing phase* and a *verification phase*.

### Phase 1 — Signing (at Sender)
1. The sender creates the message $M$.
2. The sender computes a hash: $h = H(M)$ using a secure cryptographic hash function such as **SHA-256**, **SHA-3**, or **MD5** *(deprecated)*.
3. The sender encrypts the hash with their **own private key** $K_{PR_S}$ to obtain the signature:

$$\sigma = S_{K_{PR_S}}(h) = S_{K_{PR_S}}(H(M))$$

4. The sender transmits the tuple $(M, \sigma)$ to the receiver.

### Phase 2 — Verification (at Receiver)
1. The receiver receives $(M, \sigma)$.
2. The receiver computes the hash of the received message: $h' = H(M)$.
3. The receiver decrypts the signature using the sender's **public key** $K_{PU_S}$:

$$h = V_{K_{PU_S}}(\sigma)$$

4. The receiver compares $h \stackrel{?}{=} h'$. If equal → signature is **valid**; else → **rejected**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why hash instead of the full message?** Most messages are larger than the hash output. Hashing first produces a **fixed-size, compact** value. It also provides **integrity protection** — any change in $M$ will result in a completely different hash.

## 2.2 Arbitrated Digital Signature — Operational Breakdown

There are **three classical variants** of arbitrated signatures. We examine the most common one.

### Variant 1: Conventional Encryption + Arbiter
1. Sender $\to$ Arbiter: $S \to A: \;\big(E_{K_{PA}}(M), E_{K_{SA}}(ID_S)\big)$
2. Arbiter decrypts both, verifies $ID_S$, then re-encrypts for receiver.
3. Arbiter $\to$ Receiver: $A \to R: \;\big(E_{K_{PR_A}}(ID_S \,\|\, M), E_{K_{SR}}(M)\big)$

### Variant 2: Public-Key + Arbiter (Most Common in KTU syllabus)
1. Sender computes: $M_1 = E_{K_{PR_S}}(ID_S \,\|\, M)$
2. Sender $\to$ Arbiter: $S \to A: \; M_1 \,\|\, E_{K_{PU_A}}(M)$
3. Arbiter decrypts both using $K_{PU_S}$ and $K_{PR_A}$, verifies $ID_S$.
4. Arbiter appends timestamp: $M_2 = E_{K_{PR_A}}(ID_S \,\|\, M \,\|\, T)$
5. Arbiter $\to$ Receiver: $A \to R: \; M_2$

### Phase 3 — Receiver Verification
1. Receiver decrypts using $K_{PU_A}$: recovers $ID_S, M, T$.
2. Verifies timestamp freshness and sender's identity.
3. Optionally verifies the original signature by decrypting $M_1$ with $K_{PU_S}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the timestamp T?** A timestamp prevents **replay attacks**, where an adversary captures an old signed message and resends it as if it were new.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Operation | Mathematical Expression | Purpose / Output |
|---|---|---|
| Hashing | $h = H(M)$ | Fixed-size digest of message $M$ |
| Signing (Direct) | $\sigma = S_{K_{PR}}(H(M))$ | Generates signature $\sigma$ |
| Verification (Direct) | $H(M) \stackrel{?}{=} V_{K_{PU}}(\sigma)$ | Checks if signature matches |
| Arbiter Certify | $C = E_{K_{PR_A}}(ID_S \;\vert\; M \;\vert\; T)$ | Arbiter's certified token |
| Timestamp check | $T_{\text{now}} - T \le \Delta t_{\max}$ | Replay-attack prevention |
| Encryption | $C = E_K(P)$ | Confidentiality of plaintext $P$ |
| Decryption | $P = D_K(C)$ | Recovery of plaintext |
| Symmetric relation | $D_{K_{PU}}(E_{K_{PR}}(M)) = M$ | Signature round-trip property |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize that signature is **$S_{K_{PR}}$** and verification is **$V_{K_{PU}}$**. Reversing these is the #1 cause of full-mark losses in KTU valuation.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Direct Signatures**: Used in **PGP email**, **SSH key authentication**, **Git commit signing**, **TLS certificate chains**, and **blockchain transaction signing** (Bitcoin ECDSA).
- **Arbitrated Signatures**: Used in **legal e-contracts**, **government digital identity systems** (e.g., India's eSign), **financial clearing houses**, and **regulatory compliance scenarios** where dispute resolution is mandated.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Direct Digital Signature Round-Trip

We prove mathematically that the verification of a direct signature recovers the original message hash **iff** the correct key pair is used.

**Given:**
- Message $M$ (arbitrary length)
- Hash function $H: \{0,1\}^* \to \{0,1\}^n$ (e.g., $n = 256$ for SHA-256)
- Sender's private key $K_{PR_S}$ and public key $K_{PU_S}$
- Signature algorithm pair $(S, V)$ forming an asymmetric trapdoor pair

**Step 1:** Sender computes the hash of the message.

$$h = H(M) \quad \text{where} \quad h \in \{0,1\}^{256}$$

This step is deterministic, public, and collision-resistant.

**Step 2:** Sender applies the signing function with the private key.

$$\sigma = S_{K_{PR_S}}(h)$$

**Step 3:** Sender transmits the pair $(M, \sigma)$ over an insecure channel.

**Step 4:** Receiver applies the verification function with the sender's public key.

$$h' = V_{K_{PU_S}}(\sigma)$$

**Step 5:** By the mathematical property of asymmetric key pairs:

$$V_{K_{PU_S}}(S_{K_{PR_S}}(h)) = h$$

Therefore:

$$h' = V_{K_{PU_S}}(\sigma) = V_{K_{PU_S}}(S_{K_{PR_S}}(h)) = h$$

**Step 6:** Receiver independently hashes the received message.

$$h'' = H(M)$$

**Step 7:** Receiver compares.

$$\text{Valid} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; h' = h''$$

**Conclusion:** If no adversary tampered with $M$ during transit, $h' = h'' = h$, and the signature is accepted. Otherwise, even a single bit change in $M$ causes $h''$ to differ from $h'$ with overwhelming probability $1 - 2^{-n}$.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Arbitrated Signature with Timestamping

**Given:**
- Sender $S$, Arbiter $A$, Receiver $R$
- Public keys: $K_{PU_S}, K_{PU_A}, K_{PU_R}$
- Private keys: $K_{PR_S}, K_{PR_A}, K_{PR_R}$
- Timestamp $T$ at the moment of arbitration

**Step 1:** Sender constructs the signed inner block.

$$X = ID_S \;\|\; M$$

$$Y = E_{K_{PR_S}}(X) = E_{K_{PR_S}}(ID_S \;\|\; M)$$

The vertical bar $\|$ denotes concatenation.

**Step 2:** Sender encrypts the message for the arbiter's eyes only.

$$Z = E_{K_{PU_A}}(M)$$

**Step 3:** Sender transmits $(Y, Z)$ to the arbiter $A$.

**Step 4:** Arbiter decrypts $Y$ using $K_{PU_S}$ (public-key operation).

$$X' = D_{K_{PU_S}}(Y) = D_{K_{PU_S}}(E_{K_{PR_S}}(ID_S \,\|\, M)) = ID_S \,\|\, M$$

**Step 5:** Arbiter decrypts $Z$ using its own private key $K_{PR_A}$.

$$M' = D_{K_{PR_A}}(Z) = D_{K_{PR_A}}(E_{K_{PU_A}}(M)) = M$$

**Step 6:** Arbiter verifies that the right-half of $X'$ matches $M'$.

$$\text{Condition: } X'_{\text{right}} \stackrel{?}{=} M'$$

If true, sender's identity and message are confirmed.

**Step 7:** Arbiter creates the certified package with timestamp.

$$W = E_{K_{PR_A}}(ID_S \;\|\; M \;\|\; T)$$

**Step 8:** Arbiter transmits $W$ to the receiver $R$.

**Step 9:** Receiver decrypts using the arbiter's public key.

$$(ID_S, M, T) = D_{K_{PU_A}}(W)$$

**Step 10:** Receiver validates:
- Is $T$ within the acceptable window? $\;T_{\text{current}} - T \le \Delta t_{\max}$
- Is $ID_S$ a trusted identity?
- Is $M$ the expected message?

**Final Validation:** The arbiter's digital signature on $(ID_S, M, T)$ is **non-repudiable**, since only the arbiter possesses $K_{PR_A}$.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Direct vs Arbitrated

| Parameter | Direct Digital Signature | Arbitrated Digital Signature |
|---|---|---|
| **Parties Involved** | 2 (Sender + Receiver) | 3 (Sender + Arbiter + Receiver) |
| **Trusted Third Party** | Not required | **Mandatory** |
| **Speed** | Fast (no intermediate hop) | Slower (extra hop through arbiter) |
| **Non-Repudiation** | Weak — sender can deny | **Strong** — arbiter is witness |
| **Replay Attack Defense** | No inherent defense | **Timestamp provides defense** |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Lower | Higher |
| **Single Point of Failure** | No | **Yes** — arbiter's compromise breaks all |
| **Key Compromise Impact** | Sender's key leaked → forgeable | Sender's key leaked → harder to forge due to arbiter's stamp |
| **Public Key Required by Receiver** | Yes (sender's $K_{PU_S}$) | Yes (sender's $K_{PU_S}$ and arbiter's $K_{PU_A}$) |
| **Timestamp Used** | Optional | **Standard** |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | Very High | Very High |
| **Example Use Case** | Bitcoin ECDSA, SSH | E-contracts, Court-admissible e-signatures |
| **Mathematical Anchor** | $V_{K_{PU}}(S_{K_{PR}}(M)) = M$ | $D_{K_{PU_A}}(E_{K_{PR_A}}(ID_S \mid M \mid T))$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Pattern:** When asked to "compare," examiners expect a **tabular answer with at least 6–8 distinct points**, each carrying 1 mark. The above matrix provides a 14-point comparison suitable for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Direct Digital Signature Scheme

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Message M] --> B[Hash Function H]
    B --> C[Hash Value h]
    C --> D[Sign with Private Key Kprs]
    D --> E[Signature Sigma]
    A --> F[Transmit M and Sigma]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Receiver receives pair]
    G --> H[Compute Hash h2 from M]
    G --> I[Verify Sigma using Public Key Kpus]
    I --> J[Recovered Hash h3]
    H --> K{Compare h2 and h3}
    J --> K
    K -->|Equal| L[Signature Accepted]
    K -->|Not Equal| M[Signature Rejected]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The message $M$ is hashed and the hash is signed using the sender's private key. The receiver independently hashes $M$ and decrypts the signature with the sender's public key. A successful comparison implies a valid signature.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Arbitrated Digital Signature Scheme

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Sender S creates Message M] --> P2[Sign: Y = E_KprS of IDs and M]
    P1 --> P3[Encrypt: Z = E_KpuA of M]
    P2 --> P4[Transmit Y and Z to Arbiter A]
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5[Arbiter decrypts Y with KpuS to recover IDs and M]
    P4 --> P6[Arbiter decrypts Z with KprA to recover M]
    P5 --> P7[Cross-verify IDs and message match]
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Arbiter attaches Timestamp T]
    P8 --> P9[Arbiter signs: W = E_KprA of IDs, M, T]
    P9 --> P10[Transmit W to Receiver R]
    P10 --> P11[Receiver decrypts W with KpuA]
    P11 --> P12[Receiver checks timestamp freshness]
    P11 --> P13[Receiver checks sender identity IDs]
    P12 --> P14{Valid and Fresh}
    P13 --> P14
    P14 -->|Yes| P15[Accept Arbitrated Signature]
    P14 -->|No| P16[Reject]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Notice the **three distinct trust boundaries** — the sender, the arbiter, and the receiver. Each boundary involves its own cryptographic operation. The arbiter is the linchpin providing non-repudiation.

---

## 4.3 Side-by-Side Comparative Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DIRECT["DIRECT SIGNATURE"]
        D1[Sender signs with own private key] --> D2[Receiver verifies with sender public key]
        D2 --> D3[Done, no third party]
    end
    subgraph ARBITRATED["ARBITRATED SIGNATURE"]
        A1[Sender signs and sends to Arbiter] --> A2[Arbiter verifies and timestamps]
        A2 --> A3[Arbiter re-signs and forwards to Receiver]
        A3 --> A4[Receiver verifies Arbiter signature]
    end
    DIRECT --- X[COMPARISON AXIS] --- ARBITRATED
```

**Reading the diagram:** The Direct scheme is a **2-step linear flow**, while the Arbitrated scheme is a **4-step flow with a TTP checkpoint**. This single diagram is often awarded 2–3 marks in KTU exams for the visual contrast.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between Direct Digital Signature and Arbitrated Digital Signature. Mention any three points.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Third-Party Involvement:** Direct digital signatures involve only the sender and the receiver, while arbitrated digital signatures require a trusted third party called the *arbiter* to authenticate the message and signature.

2. **Non-Repudiation:** Direct schemes offer weak non-repudiation because the sender can later deny signing the message. Arbitrated schemes offer strong non-repudiation since the arbiter acts as a witness.

3. **Timestamp:** Arbitrated schemes typically include a timestamp $T$ encrypted by the arbiter to prevent replay attacks, while direct schemes usually do not include timestamps inherently.

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the role of a cryptographic hash function in a direct digital signature scheme? Why is it preferred over signing the full message?**

**Model Answer:**

The hash function $H(M)$ converts an arbitrary-length message $M$ into a **fixed-size digest** (e.g., 256 bits for SHA-256). The sender signs *only* this digest using the private key, producing $\sigma = S_{K_{PR}}(H(M))$. The receiver hashes the received message and compares it with the decrypted signature digest.

It is preferred over signing the full message because:
- **Efficiency:** Signing a small fixed-size hash is computationally faster than signing a large variable-length message.
- **Integrity:** Any tampering with $M$ produces a wildly different hash, immediately invalidating the signature.
- **Security:** Hashing provides a clean, collision-resistant representation suitable for asymmetric operations.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] — **OPTION 1**

**(a)** Explain the operation of a **Direct Digital Signature scheme** with a neat block diagram. Mention the mathematical steps for both signing and verification. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of direct digital signatures in real-world applications. Compare with a scenario where arbitrated signatures would be more appropriate. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

A **Direct Digital Signature** is a cryptographic mechanism in which the sender $S$ signs the message directly using their own private key, and the receiver $R$ verifies it using the sender's public key — *without any intermediate trusted party*.

**Step 1 — Signing at Sender:**
The sender first applies a cryptographic hash function $H(\cdot)$ (such as **SHA-256**) to the message $M$ to obtain a fixed-size hash $h$:

$$h = H(M)$$

The sender then encrypts this hash using their **own private key** $K_{PR_S}$ to produce the digital signature $\sigma$:

$$\sigma = S_{K_{PR_S}}(h) = S_{K_{PR_S}}(H(M))$$

The sender transmits the pair $(M, \sigma)$ to the receiver over an insecure channel.

**Step 2 — Verification at Receiver:**
The receiver independently computes the hash of the received message:

$$h_1 = H(M)$$

The receiver then decrypts the signature using the sender's **public key** $K_{PU_S}$:

$$h_2 = V_{K_{PU_S}}(\sigma) = V_{K_{PU_S}}(S_{K_{PR_S}}(H(M))) = H(M)$$

**Step 3 — Comparison:**
The receiver checks:

$$h_1 \stackrel{?}{=} h_2$$

If equal, the signature is **valid** and the message is accepted as authentic. If not, the signature is **rejected** and the message may have been tampered with.

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 above for the full schematic.

**Key Property:** The verification operation $V_{K_{PU}}(S_{K_{PR}}(h)) = h$ is the foundational property of any asymmetric key pair.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Advantages of Direct Digital Signatures:**

1. **Speed and Efficiency:** No intermediate party is involved, so the message and signature are exchanged in a single hop, making it faster than arbitrated schemes.

2. **Simpler Architecture:** No trusted third party needs to be provisioned, reducing infrastructure complexity and operational cost.

3. **Scalability:** Direct schemes scale well with the number of users since they do not depend on a central arbiter that could become a bottleneck.

4. **Suitable for Bandwidth-Limited Systems:** Low-latency applications like IoT, mobile authentication, and blockchain transactions benefit from direct signing.

**Limitations of Direct Digital Signatures:**

1. **Weak Non-Repudiation:** If the sender's private key is compromised or the sender deliberately denies the transaction, there is no neutral witness to resolve the dispute.

2. **Vulnerability to Replay Attacks:** Without a timestamp, an adversary can capture a valid $(M, \sigma)$ pair and replay it later.

3. **Key Compromise Risk:** A single private key compromise allows the attacker to forge signatures indefinitely, since there is no third-party checkpoint.

4. **No Time Validation:** Direct schemes do not inherently validate *when* the message was signed.

**Scenarios Where Arbitrated Signatures Are Better:**

- **Legal Contracts:** Court-admissible signatures require a neutral witness (the arbiter) to certify the signing event with a trusted timestamp.
- **Financial Transactions:** High-value banking transfers often require a trusted intermediary for regulatory compliance.
- **Government Identity Systems:** E-Passports, e-Sign services, and digital identity programs use arbitrated schemes to provide audit trails.

---

### Question B-B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — **OPTION 2**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **Arbitrated Digital Signature scheme**. Explain the role of the arbiter and the timestamp in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A company wants to digitally sign legal contracts between two parties. Recommend whether they should use a direct or arbitrated digital signature scheme. Justify your answer with at least five technical reasons. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

An **Arbitrated Digital Signature** involves a trusted third party called the *arbiter* $A$ who acts as an intermediary between the sender $S$ and the receiver $R$. The arbiter verifies the sender's identity, optionally adds a timestamp, and re-sends the certified message to the receiver.

**Operational Steps:**

1. **Sender prepares the signed message:**
   $S$ computes $Y = E_{K_{PR_S}}(ID_S \,\|\, M)$, where $ID_S$ is the sender's identity and $M$ is the message.

2. **Sender encrypts for the arbiter:**
   $S$ computes $Z = E_{K_{PU_A}}(M)$ and sends $(Y, Z)$ to the arbiter $A$.

3. **Arbiter verifies the sender:**
   $A$ decrypts $Y$ using $K_{PU_S}$ to recover $ID_S$ and $M$, and decrypts $Z$ using $K_{PR_A}$ to recover $M$. The arbiter cross-checks both values of $M$.

4. **Arbiter adds timestamp and signs:**
   $A$ computes $W = E_{K_{PR_A}}(ID_S \,\|\, M \,\|\, T)$, where $T$ is the current timestamp.

5. **Arbiter forwards to receiver:**
   $A$ sends $W$ to $R$.

6. **Receiver verifies:**
   $R$ decrypts $W$ using $K_{PU_A}$ and checks the freshness of $T$ and the validity of $ID_S$.

**Role of the Arbiter:**

- **Authentication:** The arbiter verifies that the message genuinely came from $ID_S$ by checking the sender's signature.
- **Non-Repudiation:** Since the arbiter has a copy of the signed package, the sender cannot later deny the transaction.
- **Time Stamping:** The arbiter provides an authoritative timestamp $T$ that prevents replay attacks.
- **Trust Anchor:** The receiver trusts the arbiter's public key, simplifying the trust model.

**Role of the Timestamp:**

The timestamp $T$ is crucial for:
- **Replay Attack Prevention:** An old captured message cannot be reused since $T$ will be stale.
- **Audit Trail:** Regulators and courts can verify *when* the contract was signed.
- **Ordering of Events:** In multi-party transactions, $T$ establishes the chronological order.

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2 above.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Recommendation:** The company should use an **Arbitrated Digital Signature** scheme for legal contracts.

**Five Technical Justifications:**

1. **Strong Non-Repudiation:** Legal contracts demand that neither party can later deny signing. The arbiter's certified signature $W = E_{K_{PR_A}}(ID_S \,\|\, M \,\|\, T)$ serves as court-admissible evidence.

2. **Replay Attack Defense:** The timestamp $T$ embedded by the arbiter prevents an adversary from reusing a captured contract in a different temporal context. The freshness check $T_{\text{current}} - T \le \Delta t_{\max}$ guarantees the contract is current.

3. **Dispute Resolution Mechanism:** In the event of a legal dispute, the arbiter holds an independent, verifiable record of the signing event. Direct schemes offer no such witness.

4. **Regulatory Compliance:** Many jurisdictions (e.g., India's IT Act 2000, EU eIDAS regulation) require trusted third-party certification for digital contracts to be legally enforceable.

5. **Authentication of Both Parties:** The arbiter verifies the sender's identity and the receiver can independently verify the arbiter's public key, reducing reliance on a possibly compromised sender's public key infrastructure.

6. **Audit Logging:** Arbiters typically maintain immutable logs of all arbitrated signatures, providing an audit trail for compliance, taxation, and forensic analysis.

7. **Trust Distribution:** The trust is anchored in the arbiter, not in either contracting party, eliminating the "who do we trust" problem inherent in direct schemes.

**Conclusion:** Although arbitrated schemes are slower and more complex, the trade-off is justified for legal contracts where authenticity, non-repudiation, and regulatory compliance outweigh performance concerns.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Reversing keys:** Writing $E_{K_{PU}}$ for signing instead of $E_{K_{PR}}$ — costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Skipping the hash step:** Not mentioning the role of $H(M)$ in a direct scheme — costs 1 mark.
> 3. **No timestamp mention:** Failing to explain why $T$ is essential in arbitrated schemes — costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Missing the arbiter's verification:** Writing the arbiter as a "postman" who just forwards — the arbiter MUST verify the sender's signature first. Costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **No block diagram:** For a 7-mark sub-part, a diagram is **mandatory** and worth 2 marks. Skipping it is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 6. **Conflating encryption with signing:** Many students describe confidentiality (encryption) when asked about authentication (signing). Read the question carefully!

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist (Read 30 minutes before the exam):**

- **Direct Digital Signature:** No third party; sender signs with **$K_{PR}$**, receiver verifies with **$K_{PU}$**; fast but weak non-repudiation.
- **Arbitrated Digital Signature:** Trusted third party (arbiter) involved; provides **non-repudiation** and **timestamping**; slower but legally robust.
- **Hashing is mandatory** for practical direct signatures — never sign the full message.
- **Signature = $S_{K_{PR}}(H(M))$** ; **Verification = $V_{K_{PU}}(\sigma)$**.
- **Timestamp $T$** is the arbiter's weapon against replay attacks.
- **Key reversal = wrong answer** — private key signs, public key verifies.
- **Three variants of arbitrated schemes** exist; KTU most commonly tests the public-key + arbiter variant.
- **Direct schemes are used in:** Bitcoin, SSH, Git, TLS.
- **Arbitrated schemes are used in:** Legal e-contracts, e-Sign, government IDs.
- **Comparison table must include:** parties, speed, non-repudiation, replay defense, complexity, key compromise impact.
- **Block diagrams are worth 2 marks** in 7-mark sub-parts — never skip them.
- **Mathematical property to remember:** $V_{K_{PU}}(S_{K_{PR}}(h)) = h$ — this is the bedrock identity.
- **Replay attack** = reusing an old signed message in a new context; defended by timestamp.
- **Non-repudiation** = the signer cannot later deny their signature; guaranteed by the arbiter in ADS.
- **Single point of failure** in arbitrated schemes = the arbiter itself. If $K_{PR_A}$ is leaked, the entire system collapses.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RSA Digital Signature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT501 - FOUNDATIONS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** RSA Digital Signature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# RSA Digital Signature Scheme

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition

The **RSA Digital Signature Scheme (RSA-DSS)** is an asymmetric-key cryptographic primitive that uses the algebraic properties of the **RSA public-key cryptosystem** to provide **authenticity, integrity, and non-repudiation** of digital messages. It is formally defined as a triple of probabilistic polynomial-time algorithms $(\mathcal{G}, \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{V})$ operating over a security parameter $\lambda$:

- **Key Generation** $\mathcal{G}(1^{\lambda})$: Outputs a public verification key $pk = (n, e)$ and a private signing key $sk = (n, d)$.
- **Signing** $\mathcal{S}_{sk}(m)$: Produces a signature $\sigma$ on a message $m \in \mathbb{Z}_n^{\ast}$ such that $\sigma = m^{d} \bmod n$.
- **Verification** $\mathcal{V}_{pk}(m, \sigma)$: Outputs $1$ (accept) iff $m \equiv \sigma^{e} \pmod{n}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** RSA Digital Signature is a high-weight topic in **Module 4** and is frequently asked in Part B (14-mark) questions involving full numerical computation (key generation $\rightarrow$ signing $\rightarrow$ verification).

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **personal wax seal** in ancient times:
- **Your unique seal (private key $d$)** can only be pressed by **YOU**.
- **Anyone in the kingdom** owns an **imprint reference (public key $e$)** that lets them verify whether the wax imprint came from your official ring.
- Once sealed, **you cannot deny** having sealed the document (non-repudiation), and **nobody can tamper** with the message inside without breaking the wax (integrity).

In RSA Digital Signature:
- The **signing key $d$** is your "private wax seal."
- The **verification key $e$** is the public reference imprint that the entire world holds.
- Signing means **raising the message to power $d$ modulo $n$**, and verification means **raising the signature to power $e$ modulo $n$** to recover the message.

> [!NOTE]
> **Direction Reversal vs. RSA Encryption:**
> - **RSA Encryption:** Receiver uses **their own private key $d$** to decrypt: $m = c^{d} \bmod n$.
> - **RSA Signing:** Sender uses **their own private key $d$** to sign: $\sigma = m^{d} \bmod n$.
> The mathematical operation is the **same**, but the **owner of the key** is different. This is the most common point of confusion in the KTU exam.

---

### Critical Distinction: Three Algorithms

| Algorithm | Operation | Key Used | Computes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Encryption** (RSA) | $c = m^{e} \bmod n$ | Receiver's **public** key $(e, n)$ | Confidentiality |
| **Decryption** (RSA) | $m = c^{d} \bmod n$ | Receiver's **private** key $(d, n)$ | Confidentiality |
| **Signing** (RSA-DSS) | $\sigma = m^{d} \bmod n$ | Sender's **private** key $(d, n)$ | Authentication |
| **Verification** (RSA-DSS) | $m = \sigma^{e} \bmod n$ | Sender's **public** key $(e, n)$ | Authentication |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "signing uses the public key" or "verification uses the private key." This is **incorrect** and costs full marks in KTU valuation.

---

### Standard Security Parameters (Bold Metrics)

For an RSA-based system to resist modern attacks, the following modulus sizes are **industry standards**:

| Security Level | Modulus $n$ (bits) | Equivalent Symmetric Key |
|---|---|---|
| **80-bit security** | **1024 bits** | ~80-bit symmetric |
| **112-bit security** | **2048 bits** | ~112-bit symmetric |
| **128-bit security** | **3072 bits** | ~128-bit symmetric |
| **192-bit security** | **7680 bits** | ~192-bit symmetric |
| **256-bit security** | **15360 bits** | ~256-bit symmetric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Expectation:** When asked "What is the recommended key size for RSA today?" the answer is **at least 2048 bits** (NIST recommendation).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2. Operational Breakdown of the RSA Signature Scheme

The RSA signature scheme consists of **three stages**: key generation, signing, and verification. Each stage is built on modular exponentiation and the computational hardness of the **Integer Factorization Problem (IFP)**.

### Stage 1: Key Generation $\mathcal{G}(1^{\lambda})$

The signer (say, Alice) generates her public/private key pair:

- **Step 1:** Pick **two large, distinct, random primes** $p$ and $q$ of approximately equal bit-length $\lambda/2$.
- **Step 2:** Compute the **RSA modulus**:
$$n = p \cdot q$$
- **Step 3:** Compute **Euler's totient**:
$$\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1)$$
- **Step 4:** Choose the **public exponent** $e$ such that:
$$1 < e < \phi(n) \quad \text{and} \quad \gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$$
- **Step 5:** Compute the **private exponent** $d$ as the modular multiplicative inverse of $e$ modulo $\phi(n)$:
$$e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$$
- **Output:** Public key $pk = (n, e)$; Private key $sk = (n, d)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this work?** Because of **Euler's Theorem**: for any $m$ coprime to $n$, $m^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$. Thus, raising any signature to $e$ recovers the original message.

---

### Stage 2: Signing $\mathcal{S}_{sk}(m)$

To sign a message $m \in \mathbb{Z}_n^{\ast}$:

- **Step 1:** Compute the signature:
$$\sigma = m^{d} \bmod n$$
- **Step 2:** Append $(m, \sigma)$ to the message or transmit $\sigma$ alongside $m$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The message $m$ must satisfy $0 \leq m < n$. If $m \geq n$, reduce it: $m = m \bmod n$ first.

---

### Stage 3: Verification $\mathcal{V}_{pk}(m, \sigma)$

The verifier (Bob) checks Alice's signature:

- **Step 1:** Receive $(m, \sigma)$.
- **Step 2:** Compute:
$$m' = \sigma^{e} \bmod n$$
- **Step 3:** **Accept** if and only if:
$$m' \equiv m \pmod{n}$$
Otherwise, **reject**.

The mathematical justification is:

$$\sigma^{e} \equiv (m^{d})^{e} \equiv m^{d \cdot e} \equiv m^{1 + k\phi(n)} \equiv m \cdot (m^{\phi(n)})^{k} \equiv m \cdot 1^{k} \equiv m \pmod{n}$$

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Modulus | $n = p \cdot q$ | $p, q$ are large distinct primes |
| 2 | Euler Totient | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Counts integers in $[1, n]$ coprime to $n$ |
| 3 | Public Exponent | $1 < e < \phi(n)$, $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ | Typically $e = 65537$ |
| 4 | Private Exponent | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | Computed via Extended Euclidean Algorithm |
| 5 | Signing | $\sigma = m^{d} \bmod n$ | Uses private key $d$ |
| 6 | Verification | $m \equiv \sigma^{e} \pmod{n}$ | Uses public key $e$ |
| 7 | Correctness | $\sigma^{e} = m^{d \cdot e} \equiv m^{1} \pmod{n}$ | From Euler's theorem |
| 8 | Hash-then-Sign | $\sigma = H(m)^{d} \bmod n$ | Hash $m$ first using SHA-256 |
| 9 | Standard | **PKCS \#1 v1.5**, **RSA-PSS** | Industry padding schemes |
| 10 | Min Key Size | **2048 bits** | NIST recommendation (as of 2024) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** RSA Digital Signatures are used in **TLS/SSL certificates**, **code signing** (Microsoft Authenticode, Apple notarization), **email security (S/MIME)**, **PDF document signing**, and **blockchain transactions**.

---

### The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why $p$ and $q$ random and large?** To make factoring $n = p \cdot q$ computationally infeasible. Best known factoring takes sub-exponential time.
- **Why $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$?** Because $p$ and $q$ are prime, every integer from $1$ to $n$ that is not a multiple of $p$ or $q$ is coprime to $n$. By inclusion-exclusion: $n - \lfloor n/p \rfloor - \lfloor n/q \rfloor + \lfloor n/(pq) \rfloor = pq - q - p + 1 = (p-1)(q-1)$.
- **Why $e = 65537$ often?** It is a Fermat prime $2^{16} + 1$, allowing very fast public-key operations with low Hamming weight (only 2 set bits).
- **Why hash before signing?** To prevent **existential forgery attacks** (raw RSA is malleable: an attacker can compute $\sigma' = \sigma \cdot r^{e} \bmod n$ and claim $m' = m \cdot r$ is signed).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3. Exhaustive Numerical Walkthrough (14-Mark KTU Pattern)

This is the **canonical KTU-style problem**. We will solve it with **every algebraic transition explicitly written out**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU Examiner Pattern:** For full marks (14/14), you must show: (i) Key Generation, (ii) Signing, (iii) Verification, and (iv) the mathematical justification of correctness.

---

### Problem Statement

Alice wants to sign a message $m = 7$ using the RSA Digital Signature Scheme. Use $p = 11$ and $q = 13$ as the primes. Show the complete key generation, signing, and verification process.

---

### Solution

#### Step A: Key Generation

**A.1.** Compute the modulus $n$:

$$n = p \cdot q = 11 \times 13 = 143$$

**A.2.** Compute Euler's totient $\phi(n)$:

$$\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1) = (11 - 1)(13 - 1) = 10 \times 12 = 120$$

**A.3.** Choose the public exponent $e$.

We need $1 < e < 120$ and $\gcd(e, 120) = 1$. Choose $e = 7$.

**Verification:** $\gcd(7, 120) = 1$ since $120 = 7 \cdot 17 + 1$, so $7 \mid 120$ is false.

**A.4.** Compute the private exponent $d$ such that $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$.

We solve:
$$7 \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{120}$$

**Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:**

- $120 = 7 \cdot 17 + 1$
- $7 = 1 \cdot 7 + 0$

Back-substitute:
$$1 = 120 - 7 \cdot 17$$

Thus:
$$1 = 120 + 7 \cdot (-17)$$

So $d \equiv -17 \equiv 120 - 17 = 103 \pmod{120}$.

**Therefore:** $d = 103$.

**A.5.** Published keys:

- **Public key:** $pk = (n, e) = (143, 7)$
- **Private key:** $sk = (n, d) = (143, 103)$

---

#### Step B: Signing

Alice signs the message $m = 7$.

**B.1.** Check feasibility: $0 \leq m < n \Rightarrow 0 \leq 7 < 143$ ✓

**B.2.** Compute the signature:

$$\sigma = m^{d} \bmod n = 7^{103} \bmod 143$$

**Compute $7^{103} \bmod 143$ using repeated squaring:**

First, write $103$ in binary: $103 = 64 + 32 + 4 + 2 + 1 = (1100111)_2$.

We compute successive squares modulo $143$:

$$7^{1} \equiv 7 \pmod{143}$$

$$7^{2} \equiv 49 \pmod{143}$$

$$7^{4} \equiv 49^{2} = 2401 = 16 \cdot 143 + 113 \equiv 113 \pmod{143}$$

$$7^{8} \equiv 113^{2} = 12769 = 89 \cdot 143 + 42 \equiv 42 \pmod{143}$$

$$7^{16} \equiv 42^{2} = 1764 = 12 \cdot 143 + 48 \equiv 48 \pmod{143}$$

$$7^{32} \equiv 48^{2} = 2304 = 16 \cdot 143 + 16 \equiv 16 \pmod{143}$$

$$7^{64} \equiv 16^{2} = 256 = 1 \cdot 143 + 113 \equiv 113 \pmod{143}$$

**Now combine the binary expansion $103 = 64 + 32 + 4 + 2 + 1$:**

$$7^{103} = 7^{64} \cdot 7^{32} \cdot 7^{4} \cdot 7^{2} \cdot 7^{1}$$

$$= 113 \cdot 16 \cdot 113 \cdot 49 \cdot 7 \pmod{143}$$

Compute step by step:

$$113 \cdot 16 = 1808 = 12 \cdot 143 + 92 \equiv 92 \pmod{143}$$

$$92 \cdot 113 = 10396 = 72 \cdot 143 + 100 \equiv 100 \pmod{143}$$

$$100 \cdot 49 = 4900 = 34 \cdot 143 + 38 \equiv 38 \pmod{143}$$

$$38 \cdot 7 = 266 = 1 \cdot 143 + 123 \equiv 123 \pmod{143}$$

**Therefore:** $\sigma = 123$.

---

#### Step C: Verification

Bob verifies the signature $\sigma = 123$ on message $m = 7$ using public key $(143, 7)$.

**C.1.** Compute:

$$m' = \sigma^{e} \bmod n = 123^{7} \bmod 143$$

**Compute $123^{7} \bmod 143$ using repeated squaring:**

$7 = 4 + 2 + 1 = (111)_2$.

$$123^{1} \equiv 123 \pmod{143}$$

$$123^{2} = 15129 = 105 \cdot 143 + 114 \equiv 114 \pmod{143}$$

$$123^{4} \equiv 114^{2} = 12996 = 90 \cdot 143 + 126 \equiv 126 \pmod{143}$$

**Combine $7 = 4 + 2 + 1$:**

$$123^{7} = 123^{4} \cdot 123^{2} \cdot 123^{1}$$

$$= 126 \cdot 114 \cdot 123 \pmod{143}$$

Compute:

$$126 \cdot 114 = 14364 = 100 \cdot 143 + 64 \equiv 64 \pmod{143}$$

$$64 \cdot 123 = 7872 = 55 \cdot 143 + 7 \equiv 7 \pmod{143}$$

**Therefore:** $m' = 7$.

**C.2.** Compare: $m' = 7$ and original $m = 7$. Since $m' \equiv m \pmod{143}$, the signature is **VALID**. ✓

---

#### Step D: Mathematical Justification of Correctness

The verification succeeds because of **Euler's Theorem**:

$$\sigma^{e} = (m^{d})^{e} = m^{d \cdot e}$$

Since $d \cdot e = 1 + k \cdot \phi(n)$ for some integer $k$:

$$m^{d \cdot e} = m^{1 + k\phi(n)} = m \cdot (m^{\phi(n)})^{k} \equiv m \cdot 1^{k} = m \pmod{n}$$

For $m = 7$, $\gcd(7, 143) = 1$, so Euler's theorem applies directly.

---

### Analytical Matrix: Worked Examples for Different Parameters

| Example | $p$ | $q$ | $n$ | $\phi(n)$ | $e$ | $d$ | $m$ | $\sigma$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | 7 | 35 | 24 | 5 | 5 | 3 | $3^{5} \bmod 35 = 33$ |
| 2 | 11 | 13 | 143 | 120 | 7 | 103 | 7 | **123** |
| 3 | 17 | 19 | 323 | 288 | 5 | 173 | 10 | $10^{173} \bmod 323$ |
| 4 | 23 | 29 | 667 | 616 | 3 | 411 | 5 | $5^{411} \bmod 667$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Tip:** When $\gcd(m, n) \neq 1$ (i.e., $m$ is a multiple of $p$ or $q$), Euler's theorem does **not** directly apply, and the verification must use **CRT-based** decomposition. This is an advanced KTU topic.

---

### Hashing Before Signing: Why and How

For **real-world** RSA signatures, we do not sign $m$ directly. We first compute:

$$h = H(m)$$

where $H$ is a cryptographic hash function (e.g., **SHA-256**), then sign:

$$\sigma = h^{d} \bmod n$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** Many students lose 2 marks by forgetting to mention that **raw RSA without hashing is insecure** (vulnerable to existential forgery, multiplicative homomorphism, etc.).

---

### RSA-PSS: The Modern Standard

**RSA-PSS (Probabilistic Signature Scheme)** is the modern padding standard (PKCS \#1 v2.1) that adds randomness to prevent attacks:

- Uses a **random salt** $r$ in the encoding.
- Applies a **Mask Generation Function (MGF)**.
- Provides **provable security** under the RSA assumption in the random oracle model.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Expectation:** If asked "What is the current standard for RSA signatures?" answer: **RSA-PSS with SHA-256**, as recommended by NIST SP 800-57.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4. RSA Digital Signature Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagrams)

### 4.1 Overall Architecture of RSA-DSS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    alice["ALICE Signer"]
    pkRegistry["PUBLIC KEY DIRECTORY\n(n=143, e=7)"]
    bob["BOB Verifier"]

    subgraph keygen["KEY GENERATION PHASE"]
        k1["Pick primes p=11, q=13"]
        k2["Compute n = p * q = 143"]
        k3["Compute phi(n) = 120"]
        k4["Choose e = 7"]
        k5["Compute d = 103 via EEA"]
        k1 --> k2 --> k3 --> k4 --> k5
    end

    subgraph signing["SIGNING PHASE"]
        s1["Input message m = 7"]
        s2["Compute sigma = m^d mod n"]
        s3["Output signature sigma = 123"]
        s1 --> s2 --> s3
    end

    subgraph verify["VERIFICATION PHASE"]
        v1["Receive m and sigma"]
        v2["Compute m' = sigma^e mod n"]
        v3{"Check m' == m ?"}
        v4["ACCEPT signature"]
        v5["REJECT signature"]
        v1 --> v2 --> v3
        v3 -- yes --> v4
        v3 -- no --> v5
    end

    alice --> keygen
    keygen --> pkRegistry
    alice --> signing
    signing --> bob
    pkRegistry --> bob
    bob --> verify
```

---

### 4.2 Detailed Signing Sub-Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    msgIn["Message m = 7"]
    checkValid["0 <= m < n?"]
    hashBlock["Optional: h = SHA-256(m)"]
    expOp["Modular Exponentiation\nsigma = m^d mod n"]
    sigOut["Signature sigma = 123"]

    msgIn --> checkValid
    checkValid -- yes --> hashBlock
    checkValid -- no --> errBlock["Error: Reduce m mod n"]
    hashBlock --> expOp
    expOp --> sigOut
```

---

### 4.3 Detailed Verification Sub-Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    recvMsg["Received m"]
    recvSig["Received sigma"]
    checkRange["sigma in Z_n*?"]
    expVerify["Modular Exponentiation\nm' = sigma^e mod n"]
    compare{"m' == m?"]
    acceptOut["VERIFIED\nAuthenticity + Integrity OK"]
    rejectOut["REJECTED\nSignature Invalid"]

    recvMsg --> compare
    recvSig --> checkRange
    checkRange -- yes --> expVerify
    checkRange -- no --> rejectOut
    expVerify --> compare
    compare -- yes --> acceptOut
    compare -- no --> rejectOut
```

---

### 4.4 Attack Model on RSA Signatures

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    attacker["ADVERSARY"]

    subgraph attacks["KNOWN ATTACKS"]
        a1["Existential Forgery\nPick sigma, compute m = sigma^e mod n"]
        a2["Chosen Message Attack\nQuery signatures, forge new one"]
        a3["Factor n Attack\nRecover d from (n, e)"]
        a4["Bleichenbacher Attack\nFaulty PKCS1 v1.5 padding"]
        a5["Hash Collision Attack\nFind m1 != m2 with H(m1)=H(m2)"]
    end

    subgraph defenses["COUNTERMEASURES"]
        d1["Apply secure padding RSA-PSS"]
        d2["Use cryptographic hash SHA-256"]
        d3["Key size >= 2048 bits"]
        d4["Use constant-time verification"]
        d5["Validate encoding strictly"]
    end

    a1 -. mitigated by .-> d1
    a2 -. mitigated by .-> d1
    a3 -. mitigated by .-> d3
    a4 -. mitigated by .-> d5
    a5 -. mitigated by .-> d2

    attacker --> attacks
```

---

### 4.5 RSA vs RSA-DSS Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph encrypt["RSA ENCRYPTION"]
        e1["Sender uses Receiver PUBLIC key (e_R, n_R)"]
        e2["c = m^e_R mod n_R"]
        e3["Receiver decrypts with PRIVATE key d_R"]
        e4["Goal: CONFIDENTIALITY"]
        e1 --> e2 --> e3 --> e4
    end

    subgraph sign["RSA DIGITAL SIGNATURE"]
        s1["Sender uses Sender PRIVATE key (d_S, n_S)"]
        s2["sigma = m^d_S mod n_S"]
        s3["Verifier checks with PUBLIC key (e_S, n_S)"]
        s4["Goal: AUTHENTICITY and INTEGRITY"]
        s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4
    end

    note["KEY DIFFERENCE:\nDirection of exponentiation\nand KEY OWNERSHIP"]
    encrypt --- note
    note --- sign
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** In KTU valuation, a clearly labeled flowchart or block diagram earns at least **1–2 extra marks** even when the numerical work is partially incorrect, because it demonstrates conceptual clarity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5. Practice Questions Modeled on KTU Past Papers

---

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Conceptual Definition
**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define a digital signature. How does the RSA digital signature scheme provide authentication and non-repudiation?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **digital signature** is a cryptographic primitive that binds the identity of a signer to a digital message, providing **authentication**, **integrity**, and **non-repudiation**.

In the **RSA Digital Signature Scheme**:
- The signer uses their **private key $d$** to compute $\sigma = m^{d} \bmod n$.
- Anyone can verify using the signer's **public key $e$** by checking $\sigma^{e} \equiv m \pmod{n}$.
- **Authentication** is achieved because only the signer knows $d$; a valid signature proves the message came from the claimed sender.
- **Non-repudiation** is achieved because the private key $d$ is uniquely held by the signer; they cannot later deny signing since forging requires $d$.
- **Integrity** is guaranteed because any modification of $m$ would invalidate the verification equation.

**(Mark Distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for authentication mechanism, 1 mark for non-repudiation explanation.)**

---

#### Question 2: Comparison
**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Distinguish between RSA encryption and RSA digital signature. Highlight the key that is used in each operation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | RSA Encryption | RSA Digital Signature |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Confidentiality | Authentication & Integrity |
| **Key used to operate** | Receiver's **public** key | Sender's **private** key |
| **Operation** | $c = m^{e} \bmod n$ | $\sigma = m^{d} \bmod n$ |
| **Reverse operation** | $m = c^{d} \bmod n$ | $m' = \sigma^{e} \bmod n$ |
| **Holder of key** | Receiver holds $d$ secretly | Signer holds $d$ secretly |

Both schemes use the **same mathematical operation** ($x^{k} \bmod n$), but the **direction and key ownership** differ. **(3 marks for clear distinction with formula.)**

---

### Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — RSA-DSS Full Numerical

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / 2024 Expected Pattern]**

**Q.A.** Consider the RSA Digital Signature Scheme with $p = 7$ and $q = 11$.

**(a)** Perform the **key generation** phase. Choose a suitable public exponent $e$ and compute the corresponding private exponent $d$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Sign the message $m = 5$ and **verify** the signature using the public key. Show all modular exponentiation steps. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a): Key Generation**

**Step 1:** Compute modulus $n$:
$$n = p \cdot q = 7 \times 11 = 77$$

**Step 2:** Compute Euler's totient $\phi(n)$:
$$\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1) = (7 - 1)(11 - 1) = 6 \times 10 = 60$$

**Step 3:** Choose public exponent $e$ such that $1 < e < 60$ and $\gcd(e, 60) = 1$.

We can try $e = 7$. Check: $60 = 7 \cdot 8 + 4$, $7 = 4 \cdot 1 + 3$, $4 = 3 \cdot 1 + 1$. So $\gcd(7, 60) = 1$. ✓

**Step 4:** Compute private exponent $d$ such that $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{60}$.

Using Extended Euclidean Algorithm:
- $60 = 7 \cdot 8 + 4$
- $7 = 4 \cdot 1 + 3$
- $4 = 3 \cdot 1 + 1$
- $3 = 1 \cdot 3 + 0$

Back-substitution:
$$1 = 4 - 3 \cdot 1$$
$$1 = 4 - (7 - 4 \cdot 1) \cdot 1 = 2 \cdot 4 - 7$$
$$1 = 2 \cdot (60 - 7 \cdot 8) - 7 = 2 \cdot 60 - 17 \cdot 7$$

Therefore: $d \equiv -17 \equiv 43 \pmod{60}$.

**Step 5:** Published keys:
- **Public key:** $(n, e) = (77, 7)$
- **Private key:** $(n, d) = (77, 43)$

**(Marks: 1 for $n$, 1 for $\phi(n)$, 1 for $e$, 3 for EEA / $d$ computation, 1 for final key pair.)**

---

**Part (b): Signing and Verification**

**Step 1 (Signing):** Compute $\sigma = m^{d} \bmod n = 5^{43} \bmod 77$.

Compute $5^{43} \bmod 77$ via repeated squaring. First, $43 = 32 + 8 + 2 + 1 = (101011)_2$.

$$5^{1} \equiv 5 \pmod{77}$$

$$5^{2} \equiv 25 \pmod{77}$$

$$5^{4} \equiv 25^{2} = 625 = 8 \cdot 77 + 9 \equiv 9 \pmod{77}$$

$$5^{8} \equiv 9^{2} = 81 = 1 \cdot 77 + 4 \equiv 4 \pmod{77}$$

$$5^{16} \equiv 4^{2} = 16 \pmod{77}$$

$$5^{32} \equiv 16^{2} = 256 = 3 \cdot 77 + 25 \equiv 25 \pmod{77}$$

Now combine $43 = 32 + 8 + 2 + 1$:

$$5^{43} = 5^{32} \cdot 5^{8} \cdot 5^{2} \cdot 5^{1}$$

$$= 25 \cdot 4 \cdot 25 \cdot 5 \pmod{77}$$

Compute step by step:
$$25 \cdot 4 = 100 = 1 \cdot 77 + 23 \equiv 23 \pmod{77}$$

$$23 \cdot 25 = 575 = 7 \cdot 77 + 46 \equiv 46 \pmod{77}$$

$$46 \cdot 5 = 230 = 2 \cdot 77 + 76 \equiv 76 \pmod{77}$$

**Therefore:** $\sigma = 76$.

**Step 2 (Verification):** Bob computes $m' = \sigma^{e} \bmod n = 76^{7} \bmod 77$.

Compute $76^{7} \bmod 77$. Note that $76 \equiv -1 \pmod{77}$.

$$76^{7} \equiv (-1)^{7} = -1 \equiv 76 \pmod{77}$$

Wait, that gives $m' = 76$, but $m = 5$. Let me recheck.

**Recheck:** $76 \equiv -1 \pmod{77}$, so $76^{7} \equiv -1 \equiv 76 \pmod{77}$. This does not equal $5$.

This means **$e = 7$ and $m = 5$ lead to an issue**. Let me recheck the computation of $d$:

$7 \cdot 43 = 301 = 5 \cdot 60 + 1$. So $7 \cdot 43 \equiv 1 \pmod{60}$. ✓

Let me recompute $5^{43} \bmod 77$ more carefully:

$$5^{2} = 25$$

$$5^{4} = 625 \bmod 77: 77 \cdot 8 = 616, 625 - 616 = 9$$. So $5^{4} \equiv 9$.

$$5^{8} = 9^{2} = 81 \bmod 77 = 4$$

$$5^{16} = 4^{2} = 16$$

$$5^{32} = 16^{2} = 256 \bmod 77: 77 \cdot 3 = 231, 256 - 231 = 25$$. So $5^{32} \equiv 25$.

$5^{43} = 5^{32} \cdot 5^{8} \cdot 5^{2} \cdot 5^{1} = 25 \cdot 4 \cdot 25 \cdot 5$

$25 \cdot 4 = 100 \bmod 77 = 23$

$23 \cdot 25 = 575 \bmod 77: 77 \cdot 7 = 539, 575 - 539 = 36$. So $\equiv 36$.

$36 \cdot 5 = 180 \bmod 77: 77 \cdot 2 = 154, 180 - 154 = 26$. So $\equiv 26$.

**Correction:** $\sigma = 26$.

**Verification:** $m' = 26^{7} \bmod 77$.

Compute $26^{2} = 676 \bmod 77: 77 \cdot 8 = 616, 676 - 616 = 60$. So $26^{2} \equiv 60$.

$26^{4} = 60^{2} = 3600 \bmod 77: 77 \cdot 46 = 3542, 3600 - 3542 = 58$. So $26^{4} \equiv 58$.

$26^{7} = 26^{4} \cdot 26^{2} \cdot 26^{1} = 58 \cdot 60 \cdot 26$.

$58 \cdot 60 = 3480 \bmod 77: 77 \cdot 45 = 3465, 3480 - 3465 = 15$. So $\equiv 15$.

$15 \cdot 26 = 390 \bmod 77: 77 \cdot 5 = 385, 390 - 385 = 5$. So $\equiv 5$. ✓

**Therefore:** $m' = 5 = m$, and the signature is **VALID**.

**(Marks: 2 for signature computation, 3 for verification computation, 2 for final conclusion.)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the initial error happened:** I made an arithmetic slip in the repeated squaring. The verification process always catches such errors, which is precisely why verification is the **integrity check**.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Theory + Numerical)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q.B.** Answer the following:

**(a)** Explain the **RSA Digital Signature Scheme** with its three algorithms. Why is hashing required before signing in practice? Discuss one attack on raw RSA signatures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For $p = 5$, $q = 11$, $e = 3$, and message $m = 4$, perform **complete key generation**, sign the message, and **verify** the signature. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a): Theoretical Explanation**

The RSA Digital Signature Scheme consists of three polynomial-time algorithms:

1. **Key Generation $\mathcal{G}(1^{\lambda})$**: Generates a key pair $(pk, sk)$ where $pk = (n, e)$ and $sk = (n, d)$.
2. **Signing $\mathcal{S}_{sk}(m)$**: Outputs $\sigma = m^{d} \bmod n$.
3. **Verification $\mathcal{V}_{pk}(m, \sigma)$**: Accepts if and only if $\sigma^{e} \equiv m \pmod{n}$.

**Why hashing is required:**

Signing the raw message $m$ directly is **insecure** due to the following attacks:

- **Existential Forgery:** An adversary picks any $\sigma$, computes $m = \sigma^{e} \bmod n$, and produces a valid-looking $(m, \sigma)$ pair without knowing $d$.
- **Multiplicative Homomorphism:** Given signatures $\sigma_1 = m_1^{d}$ and $\sigma_2 = m_2^{d}$, the adversary can forge a signature on $m_1 \cdot m_2$ as $\sigma_1 \cdot \sigma_2$.
- **Bleichenbacher's Attack (on PKCS \#1 v1.5):** Exploits parsing ambiguity in padding.

To prevent these, we hash first: $\sigma = H(m)^{d} \bmod n$, where $H$ is a collision-resistant hash like **SHA-256**. This binds the signature to a fixed-length digest and prevents algebraic manipulation.

**(Marks: 2 for algorithm listing, 2 for explanation of hashing, 2 for attack description, 1 for conclusion.)**

---

**Part (b): Numerical Computation**

**Step 1:** Compute $n = p \cdot q = 5 \times 11 = 55$.

**Step 2:** Compute $\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1) = 4 \times 10 = 40$.

**Step 3:** Verify $e = 3$ satisfies $\gcd(3, 40) = 1$. Indeed, $40 \bmod 3 = 1$, so coprime. ✓

**Step 4:** Compute $d$ such that $3d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$.

Using EEA:
- $40 = 3 \cdot 13 + 1$
- $3 = 1 \cdot 3 + 0$

Back-substitute: $1 = 40 - 3 \cdot 13$, so $d \equiv -13 \equiv 27 \pmod{40}$.

**Verification:** $3 \cdot 27 = 81 = 2 \cdot 40 + 1$. ✓ So $d = 27$.

**Public key:** $(55, 3)$. **Private key:** $(55, 27)$.

**Step 5 (Signing):** $\sigma = m^{d} \bmod n = 4^{27} \bmod 55$.

Compute using repeated squaring. $27 = 16 + 8 + 2 + 1 = (11011)_2$.

$$4^{1} \equiv 4 \pmod{55}$$

$$4^{2} \equiv 16 \pmod{55}$$

$$4^{4} \equiv 16^{2} = 256 \bmod 55: 55 \cdot 4 = 220, 256 - 220 = 36 \equiv 36 \pmod{55}$$

$$4^{8} \equiv 36^{2} = 1296 \bmod 55: 55 \cdot 23 = 1265, 1296 - 1265 = 31 \equiv 31 \pmod{55}$$

$$4^{16} \equiv 31^{2} = 961 \bmod 55: 55 \cdot 17 = 935, 961 - 935 = 26 \equiv 26 \pmod{55}$$

Combine: $4^{27} = 4^{16} \cdot 4^{8} \cdot 4^{2} \cdot 4^{1} = 26 \cdot 31 \cdot 16 \cdot 4$.

$26 \cdot 31 = 806 \bmod 55: 55 \cdot 14 = 770, 806 - 770 = 36$. So $\equiv 36$.

$36 \cdot 16 = 576 \bmod 55: 55 \cdot 10 = 550, 576 - 550 = 26$. So $\equiv 26$.

$26 \cdot 4 = 104 \bmod 55: 55 \cdot 1 = 55, 104 - 55 = 49$. So $\equiv 49$.

**Therefore:** $\sigma = 49$.

**Step 6 (Verification):** $m' = \sigma^{e} \bmod n = 49^{3} \bmod 55$.

$$49^{2} = 2401 \bmod 55: 55 \cdot 43 = 2365, 2401 - 2365 = 36 \equiv 36 \pmod{55}$$

$$49^{3} = 49^{2} \cdot 49 = 36 \cdot 49 = 1764 \bmod 55: 55 \cdot 32 = 1760, 1764 - 1760 = 4 \equiv 4 \pmod{55}$$

**Therefore:** $m' = 4 = m$. ✓ **Signature VALID.**

**(Marks: 1 for $n$, 1 for $\phi(n)$, 1 for $d$, 2 for signature, 2 for verification.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
>
> Students commonly lose marks in the following ways:
>
> 1. **Wrong key assignment** (using $e$ to sign and $d$ to verify) — costs **3 marks**.
> 2. **Skipping the EEA** (just stating $d = $ "inverse of $e$ mod $\phi(n)$" without showing computation) — costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **Arithmetic mistakes** in repeated squaring. Mitigate by **always verifying** at the end: if verification fails, recompute.
> 4. **Forgetting the message range check** ($m < n$) — costs **1 mark**.
> 5. **Not justifying correctness** using Euler's theorem — costs **2 marks**.
> 6. **Writing $m \equiv \sigma^{d}$ instead of $m \equiv \sigma^{e}$** during verification — costs **full marks** for that step.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for RSA Digital Signature**

- **Three Algorithms:** KeyGen, Sign ($m^{d} \bmod n$), Verify ($\sigma^{e} \equiv m \pmod{n}$).
- **Key Pair:** Public $pk = (n, e)$, Private $sk = (n, d)$.
- **Core Equation:** $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, where $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$.
- **Signing key** is the **PRIVATE** key $d$; **Verifying key** is the **PUBLIC** key $e$.
- **Mathematical Backbone:** **Euler's Theorem**: $m^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ when $\gcd(m, n) = 1$.
- **Correctness Chain:** $\sigma^{e} = (m^{d})^{e} = m^{de} = m^{1 + k\phi(n)} = m \cdot 1 = m \pmod{n}$.
- **Hash Before Sign:** Always compute $\sigma = H(m)^{d} \bmod n$ for security.
- **Recommended Key Size:** $\geq$ **2048 bits** for modern security (NIST).
- **Common Public Exponent:** $e = 65537$ ($2^{16} + 1$, Fermat prime).
- **Standard Padding:** **PKCS \#1 v1.5** (legacy) or **RSA-PSS** (modern, recommended).
- **Attacks to Know:** Existential forgery, multiplicative homomorphism, Bleichenbacher, factoring $n$.
- **Compute $d$ via EEA:** Always show the Extended Euclidean Algorithm steps explicitly.
- **Efficiency Tip:** Use **Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT)** for fast signing: compute mod $p$ and mod $q$ separately, then combine (gives **4× speedup**).
- **Signature Length:** Equals modulus size, e.g., **2048 bits** for $n = 2048$-bit RSA.
- **Verification Equation:** $m \stackrel{?}{=} \sigma^{e} \bmod n$ — **always compute and compare**.
- **Remember the direction:** **Sign = $m^{d}$**, **Verify = $\sigma^{e}$**, never the opposite.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** If you have only 5 minutes left in the exam, write down the three core equations: $\sigma = m^{d} \bmod n$, $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$, and $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$. This alone guarantees **partial credit** even if the numerical answer is incomplete.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ElGamal Digital Signature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT501 - FOUNDATIONS OF CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ElGamal Digital Signature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ElGamal Digital Signature Scheme (EGDSS)**
> A **probabilistic** public-key digital signature scheme proposed by **Taher Elgamal in 1985**, whose security is based on the **Computational Diffie–Hellman Problem (CDHP)** and the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** over a multiplicative cyclic group of prime order.

Unlike RSA (which is *deterministic* — the same message always produces the same signature), ElGamal is **probabilistic** because each signature depends on a freshly chosen random integer per signing operation. The scheme is an extension of the ElGamal encryption system, but applied in the *reverse* direction (signing with the private key, verifying with the public key).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sealed Treasure Chest"

Imagine a king (verifier) gives every loyal knight (signer) a special **combination lock** with three rotating dials where the final opening code is mathematically dependent on the knight's *secret* and a *one-time* secret ingredient:

- The **lock's public setting** ($y$) is open for inspection by anyone.
- The **knight's secret** ($x$) is known only to the knight.
- For every message, the knight uses a **fresh secret ingredient** ($k$) — like a pinch of saffron used only once in a royal recipe.
- The signature $(S_1, S_2)$ is a *two-part evidence* that only the knight could have produced, because reconstructing it would require knowing both the knight's private secret $x$ AND the one-time random $k$ — both protected by the hardness of the discrete logarithm problem.

Even if an eavesdropper intercepts **one thousand** signed messages, they still cannot forge the **1001st** signature, because each signature uses an *independent* random $k$. This is the heart of ElGamal's non-determinism.

## 1.3 The Three Algorithmic Phases

A complete ElGamal Digital Signature System consists of three sub-algorithms:

| Phase | Actor | Goal |
|---|---|---|
| **Key Generation** | Signer | Produce private key $x$ and public key $(p, g, y)$ |
| **Signature Generation (Sign)** | Signer | Produce signature $(S_1, S_2)$ on message $m$ |
| **Signature Verification (Verify)** | Verifier | Authenticate whether $(S_1, S_2)$ is a valid signature on $m$ |

## 1.4 System Parameters — Notation Lockdown

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard ElGamal Public Parameters**
> - **$p$** — a large prime number (≥ 1024 bits in practice)
> - **$g$** — a primitive root modulo $p$ (i.e., a generator of $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$)
> - **$m$** — the message to be signed (typically a hash $h(m)$ in real systems)
> - **$x$** — signer's **private key**, $1 \le x \le p-2$
> - **$y$** — signer's **public key**, computed as $y = g^x \bmod p$
> - **$k$** — a per-message **random integer** (ephemeral key), with $\gcd(k, p-1) = 1$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phase 1 — Key Generation (Setup by Signer)

The signer performs the following once and publishes the public key:

1. Select a large prime $p$ and a primitive root $g$ modulo $p$.
2. Choose a random integer $x$ such that $1 \le x \le p - 2$.
3. Compute the public verification key:
   $$y \equiv g^x \pmod{p}$$
4. Publish the public key tuple $(p, g, y)$; keep $x$ strictly secret.

> **Why a primitive root?**
> Because $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$ is a cyclic group of order $p-1$, and $g$ being a generator ensures that every non-zero residue modulo $p$ is expressible as $g^a \bmod p$ for some $a$. This gives the discrete-log problem its strongest form.

## 2.2 Phase 2 — Signature Generation (Signing by Signer)

For each message $m$ to be signed:

1. Choose a fresh random integer $k$ such that $1 \le k \le p-2$ and $\gcd(k, p-1) = 1$.
2. Compute the first signature component:
   $$S_1 \equiv g^k \pmod{p}$$
3. Compute the modular inverse $k^{-1} \bmod (p-1)$ using the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**.
4. Compute the second signature component using the key equation:
   $$S_2 \equiv k^{-1} \cdot (m - x \cdot S_1) \pmod{p-1}$$
5. The signature on $m$ is the ordered pair $\boldsymbol{(S_1, S_2)}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The signing equation** $k \cdot S_2 \equiv m - x \cdot S_1 \pmod{p-1}$ is the algebraic *heart* of ElGamal. It is a discrete-log-style linear relation in $m$, $x$, and $k$.

## 2.3 Phase 3 — Signature Verification (By Any Verifier)

Given the public key $(p, g, y)$, the message $m$, and the alleged signature $(S_1, S_2)$:

**Validity check** — accept the signature **if and only if**:
$$y^{S_1} \cdot S_1^{S_2} \equiv g^{m} \pmod{p}$$

Otherwise, reject it as invalid or forged.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Operation** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Conditions / Domain** |
|---|---|---|
| Public key generation | $y \equiv g^x \pmod{p}$ | $1 \le x \le p-2$ |
| Signature component 1 | $S_1 \equiv g^k \pmod{p}$ | $1 \le k \le p-2$, $\gcd(k, p-1) = 1$ |
| Signature component 2 | $S_2 \equiv k^{-1}(m - x \cdot S_1) \pmod{p-1}$ | $k^{-1}$ exists mod $p-1$ |
| Verification equation | $y^{S_1} \cdot S_1^{S_2} \equiv g^{m} \pmod{p}$ | All arithmetic mod $p$ |
| Required primitive root | $\text{ord}(g) = p - 1$ | $g$ generates $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$ |
| Randomness requirement | $k$ must be **fresh per message** | Never reuse $k$ across messages |
| Hashing convention | Use $m = H(M)$ where $H$ is a cryptographic hash | $M$ = original message |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **Application of ElGamal-like Signatures** |
|---|---|
| **Digital Certificates** | Foundation for DSA (Digital Signature Algorithm), the U.S. FIPS 186 standard — a derivative of ElGamal |
| **Blockchain / Cryptocurrencies** | Schnorr and EdDSA schemes are descendants of ElGamal-style group signature logic |
| **Secure Email (PGP)** | Conceptually related to signing primitives in OpenPGP |
| **Smart Cards & IoT Authentication** | Compact, modular arithmetic suitable for embedded devices |
| **Software Code Signing** | Authenticates publisher identity via signed hashes |

## 2.6 Security Caveats — Where Students Lose Marks

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Operational Rules**
> 1. **Never reuse the random $k$** — if two different messages $m_1, m_2$ are signed with the **same $k$**, the private key $x$ can be algebraically recovered by the attacker. (This is exactly the Sony PS3 break of 2010.)
> 2. **Never sign the raw message $M$ directly** — always sign its cryptographic hash $H(M)$ to prevent existential forgery.
> 3. **Always check that $S_1 \in [1, p-1]$** — the verifier should reject $S_1 \equiv 0 \pmod{p}$ to prevent trivial forgery.
> 4. **Use a safe prime** $p$ (where $p = 2q + 1$ with $q$ prime) to defeat Pohlig–Hellman subgroup attacks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Why the Verification Equation Works — Full Proof

This is the **mathematical correctness proof** that every KTU examiner expects. We must show:

$$y^{S_1} \cdot S_1^{S_2} \equiv g^{m} \pmod{p}$$

### Step 1 — Substitute the public key $y = g^x$

Starting with the left-hand side of the verification equation:

$$y^{S_1} \cdot S_1^{S_2} \equiv (g^x)^{S_1} \cdot (g^k)^{S_2} \pmod{p}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the power rule $(a^b)^c = a^{bc}$

$$(g^x)^{S_1} = g^{x \cdot S_1} \quad \text{and} \quad (g^k)^{S_2} = g^{k \cdot S_2}$$

Therefore:

$$y^{S_1} \cdot S_1^{S_2} \equiv g^{x \cdot S_1} \cdot g^{k \cdot S_2} \pmod{p}$$

### Step 3 — Combine exponents using $a^b \cdot a^c = a^{b+c}$

$$g^{x \cdot S_1} \cdot g^{k \cdot S_2} \equiv g^{x \cdot S_1 + k \cdot S_2} \pmod{p}$$

### Step 4 — Invoke the signing equation

From Phase 2, the signer ensures:

$$S_2 \equiv k^{-1} \cdot (m - x \cdot S_1) \pmod{p-1}$$

Multiply both sides by $k$:

$$k \cdot S_2 \equiv m - x \cdot S_1 \pmod{p-1}$$

Rearranging:

$$x \cdot S_1 + k \cdot S_2 \equiv m \pmod{p-1}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute the exponent relation into the power

$$g^{x \cdot S_1 + k \cdot S_2} \equiv g^{m + (p-1) \cdot t} \pmod{p}$$

for some integer $t$ (since the two exponents are congruent mod $p-1$).

### Step 6 — Apply Fermat's Little Theorem in group form

By Fermat's Little Theorem (since $g^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ for $\gcd(g, p) = 1$):

$$g^{m + (p-1) \cdot t} \equiv g^{m} \cdot (g^{p-1})^{t} \equiv g^{m} \cdot 1^{t} \equiv g^{m} \pmod{p}$$

### Conclusion

$$\boxed{y^{S_1} \cdot S_1^{S_2} \equiv g^{m} \pmod{p}} \quad \blacksquare$$

The verifier's check holds **if and only if** the signing equation was used during signature generation. Conversely, a forger who does not know $x$ cannot construct a valid $(S_1, S_2)$ pair without solving the Discrete Logarithm Problem.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Standard Style)

Let us illustrate the entire scheme end-to-end with small, exam-friendly numbers.

> **Public parameters:** $p = 23$, $g = 5$ (primitive root of 23).
> **Signer's private key:** $x = 6$
> **Message to sign:** $m = 7$

### Key Generation

$$y = g^x \bmod p = 5^6 \bmod 23$$

Compute: $5^2 = 25 \equiv 2$, $5^4 \equiv 2^2 = 4$, $5^6 \equiv 4 \cdot 2 = 8 \pmod{23}$.

$$\boxed{y = 8} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Public key} = (23, 5, 8)$$

### Signature Generation

Choose random $k = 5$ (with $\gcd(5, 22) = 1$ — valid).

$$S_1 = g^k \bmod p = 5^5 \bmod 23$$

Compute: $5^4 \equiv 4$, so $5^5 \equiv 4 \cdot 5 = 20 \pmod{23}$.

$$S_1 = 20$$

Compute $k^{-1} \bmod (p-1) = k^{-1} \bmod 22$. Find $k^{-1}$ such that $5 \cdot k^{-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{22}$. By trial, $5 \cdot 9 = 45 \equiv 1 \pmod{22}$, so $k^{-1} = 9$.

$$S_2 \equiv k^{-1}(m - x \cdot S_1) \pmod{p-1}$$

Compute $x \cdot S_1 = 6 \cdot 20 = 120$, then $m - x \cdot S_1 = 7 - 120 = -113$.

$$S_2 \equiv 9 \cdot (-113) \pmod{22} \equiv -1017 \pmod{22}$$

Reduce: $-1017 = -22 \cdot 47 + 17$, so $S_2 \equiv 17 \pmod{22}$.

$$\boxed{S_2 = 17} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Signature} = (20, 17)$$

### Signature Verification

The verifier must check:

$$y^{S_1} \cdot S_1^{S_2} \equiv g^m \pmod{p}$$

**Left-hand side:**

$y^{S_1} = 8^{20} \bmod 23$

Using repeated squaring: $8^2 = 64 \equiv 18 \equiv -5$, $8^4 \equiv (-5)^2 = 25 \equiv 2$, $8^8 \equiv 4$, $8^{16} \equiv 16$. Then $8^{20} = 8^{16} \cdot 8^4 \equiv 16 \cdot 2 = 32 \equiv 9 \pmod{23}$.

$S_1^{S_2} = 20^{17} \bmod 23$. Note $20 \equiv -3$, so $(-3)^{17} = -3^{17} \bmod 23$. Compute $3^2 = 9$, $3^4 = 81 \equiv 12$, $3^8 \equiv 144 \equiv 6$, $3^{16} \equiv 36 \equiv 13$. So $3^{17} \equiv 3^{16} \cdot 3 = 13 \cdot 3 = 39 \equiv 16 \pmod{23}$. Therefore $20^{17} \equiv -16 \equiv 7 \pmod{23}$.

Combine: $y^{S_1} \cdot S_1^{S_2} \equiv 9 \cdot 7 = 63 \equiv 63 - 46 = 17 \pmod{23}$.

**Right-hand side:**

$g^m = 5^7 \bmod 23$. Compute $5^4 \equiv 4$, $5^7 = 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 5 = 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 = 40 \equiv 17 \pmod{23}$.

**Comparison:** LHS $= 17$ and RHS $= 17$. ✅ **Signature is VALID.**

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Key Properties Summary

| **Property** | **ElGamal Digital Signature** |
|---|---|
| Type | **Probabilistic** (different signature per run) |
| Signature size | $2 \times \vert p \vert$ bits (twice the modulus) |
| Security basis | Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP) |
| Signature length overhead | 2× compared to RSA at equivalent security |
| Reusing $k$ across messages? | **Catastrophic — leaks $x$** |
| Existential forgery possible? | Yes if raw message (not hash) is signed |
| Standardized variant | **DSA (FIPS 186)** and **GOST R 34.10** |
| Verification cost | 2 modular exponentiations |
| Signing cost | 1 modular exponentiation + 1 inverse + arithmetic |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level System Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Params["Public Parameters p and g"] --> KeyGen
    KeyGen["Key Generation Module"] --> PrivKey["Private Key x secret"]
    KeyGen --> PubKey["Public Key y = g^x mod p"]
    PubKey --> VerifModule
    Message["Original Message M"] --> Hash["Hash Function H"]
    Hash --> HashedMsg["Hashed Message m = HM"]
    HashedMsg --> SignModule
    PrivKey --> SignModule
    RandomK["Fresh Random k gcd k p-1 = 1"] --> SignModule
    SignModule["Signature Generation"] --> SigPair["Signature S1 S2"]
    SigPair --> Channel["Insecure Channel"]
    HashedMsg --> Channel
    PubKey --> VerifModule["Signature Verification"]
    Channel --> VerifModule
    VerifModule --> Decision{"y power S1 times S1 power S2 equals g power m mod p"}
    Decision -->|Yes| Accept["Signature ACCEPTED Authentic"]
    Decision -->|No| Reject["Signature REJECTED Forged or Tampered"]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Signature Generation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input m x p g"] --> B["Choose random k with gcd k p-1 = 1"]
    B --> C["Compute S1 = g to the power k mod p"]
    C --> D["Compute inverse of k modulo p-1"]
    D --> E["Compute S2 = k inverse times m minus x times S1 mod p-1"]
    E --> F["Output Signature S1 S2"]
```

## 4.3 Detailed Signature Verification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input m S1 S2 y p g"] --> B["Check S1 in range 1 to p-1"]
    B -->|Invalid| R1["REJECT"]
    B -->|Valid| C["Compute LHS = y power S1 times S1 power S2 mod p"]
    C --> D["Compute RHS = g power m mod p"]
    D --> E{"LHS equals RHS mod p"}
    E -->|Yes| ACC["ACCEPT Signature Valid"]
    E -->|No| REJ["REJECT Signature Invalid"]
```

## 4.4 Why Reusing $k$ Breaks Security — Attack Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Normal Signing"
        N1["Message m1 with random k"] --> N2["Signature S1a S2a"]
        N3["Message m2 with random k"] --> N4["Signature S1b S2b"]
    end
    subgraph "BAD: Reusing same k"
        B1["Message m1 with random k"] --> B2["Signature S1 S2 prime"]
        B3["Message m2 with SAME random k"] --> B4["Signature S1 S2 double prime"]
    end
    subgraph "Consequence for Attacker"
        B2 --> C1["Attacker has S2 prime and S2 double prime and m1 and m2"]
        B4 --> C1
        C1 --> C2["Compute k = m1 minus m2 times inverse of S2 prime minus S2 double prime mod p-1"]
        C2 --> C3["Compute x = m1 minus k times S2 prime times inverse of S1 mod p-1"]
        C3 --> C4["PRIVATE KEY COMPROMISED"]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What are the essential requirements that a digital signature scheme must satisfy? List and briefly explain them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A digital signature scheme must provide the following three security services:
1. **Authentication** — The verifier must be convinced that the signature was created by the claimed sender (this requires the private key to remain secret).
2. **Non-repudiation** — The signer cannot later deny having signed the message, because producing a valid signature requires the private key that only the signer possesses.
3. **Integrity** — Any modification of the message after signing will cause the verification equation to fail, so the message cannot be tampered with undetected.

A valid digital signature scheme must also satisfy **unforgeability** (computationally infeasible to produce a valid signature without the private key).

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Why must the random per-message value $k$ in the ElGamal Digital Signature Scheme be chosen freshly for every signing operation, and what happens if it is reused?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
